Moral Letters to Lucilius

All 124 letters from a Stoic: philosophy as a daily practice, written to a friend

by Seneca

c. 65 AD 2026

This is an AI modernization of Moral Letters to Lucilius into contemporary English. The original is available from Wikisource. Also available as an epub.

Letters 1–6

Letter 1: On Saving Time

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Keep doing what you're doing, my dear Lucilius: claim yourself for yourself. Gather up and guard your time — the time that until now was being taken from you, or stolen outright, or simply slipping through your fingers. Believe me when I tell you how it goes: some moments are torn from us, some are quietly lifted, and some just drift out of reach. But the most shameful loss of all is the loss that comes from carelessness. Look closely and you'll see that the largest part of life slips away from people while they're doing wrong, a good share while they're doing nothing, and the whole of it while they're doing what doesn't matter.

Show me the man who puts a real price on his time, who values each day, who understands that he is dying daily. Because that's our mistake — we see death as somewhere up ahead, when most of it is already behind us. Whatever years lie behind us are in death's hands.

So do exactly what you tell me you're doing: hold every hour in your grasp. Get a grip on today and you'll depend that much less on tomorrow. While we are postponing, life speeds by. Nothing, Lucilius, is ours, except time. Nature put us in possession of this one thing — this one fleeting, slippery thing — and anyone who feels like it can push us out of it. And how foolish people are! They'll let the cheapest trifles, things easily replaced, get charged to their account once received; but when they've been given someone's time, the most precious commodity there is, they never consider themselves in debt at all. And yet time is the one loan that even a grateful borrower can never repay.

You may want to know how I'm doing myself — I, who hand out this advice so freely. I'll confess frankly: my books balance the way you'd expect from a man who spends freely but keeps careful accounts. I can't claim I waste nothing. But I can tell you what I'm wasting, and how, and why; I can give you the full accounting of my poverty. My situation is like that of many people who've been reduced to little through no fault of their own: everyone forgives them, and no one comes to their rescue.

So where does that leave us? Here: I don't consider a man poor if the little he has left is enough for him. But you — hold on to what's really yours, and you can't start too early. As our ancestors used to say, it's too late to start saving when you're down to the dregs of the cask. What's left at the bottom isn't just the smallest part — it's the worst.

Farewell.

Letter 2: On Discursiveness in Reading

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

From what you write, and from what I hear, I'm forming a good opinion of your future. You don't run around from place to place, unsettling yourself with every change of address — that kind of thrashing about is the sign of a disordered spirit. The first proof of a well-ordered mind, in my view, is the ability to stand still and keep your own company.

But be careful that all this reading — many authors, every kind of book — doesn't leave you scattered and unsteady. If you want ideas that will take root and hold, you have to stay awhile with a few master thinkers and digest their work. To be everywhere is to be nowhere. People who spend their lives traveling end up with many acquaintances and no friends — and the same goes for anyone who never gets close to a single great author but skims them all at a run. Food does no good, and never becomes part of you, if it leaves the stomach as soon as it's eaten. Nothing blocks healing like constantly switching medicines. No wound closes while you're trying one salve after another. A plant that keeps getting moved never grows strong. Nothing is so useful that it can help you while it's in transit. And a crowd of books is a distraction.

So, since you can't read all the books you might own, it's enough to own only as many books as you can read. "But," you say, "I want to dip into this one first, then that one." Tasting a little from many dishes is the sign of a fussy appetite: when they're many and varied, they cloy but don't nourish. Read the proven authors, and when you crave a change, go back to the ones you read before. Every day, acquire something that will arm you against poverty, against death, against every other misfortune as well; and after ranging over many thoughts, pick out one to digest thoroughly that day.

That's my own practice: from the many things I've read, I claim some one part for myself. Today's comes from Epicurus — yes, I make a habit of crossing over into the enemy's camp. Not as a deserter, mind you; as a scout. He says: "Contented poverty is an honorable thing." Though really, if it's contented, it isn't poverty at all. It isn't the man who has too little who is poor — it's the man who craves more. What does it matter how much sits in a man's safe or his warehouses, how large his herds are or how fat his interest payments, if he's eyeing his neighbor's property and counting up not what he's gained but what he hopes to gain? You ask what the proper limit to wealth is? First, having what is necessary. Second, having what is enough.

Farewell.

Letter 3: On True and False Friendship

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You sent your letter by the hand of a "friend" — your word for him. And in your very next sentence you warn me not to discuss your affairs with him, since you don't make a habit of it yourself. In one and the same letter you've told me he is your friend and that he isn't. Now, if you were using the word loosely — the way we call every candidate for office "the honorable gentleman," or greet a man whose name escapes us as "my dear sir" — fine, so be it. But if you consider anyone a friend whom you don't trust as much as you trust yourself, you are badly mistaken, and you don't understand what real friendship means.

Talk everything over with a friend, by all means — but first, talk over the friend himself. Once friendship is settled, you must trust; before it's formed, you must judge. The people who get this backwards — who, breaking Theophrastus's rule, judge a man after making him a friend instead of making him a friend after judging him — have their duties exactly reversed. Take a long time deciding whether to admit someone into your friendship. But once you've decided to admit him, welcome him with your whole heart and soul, and speak with him as boldly as with yourself.

As for you yourself: live in such a way that you'd trust yourself with nothing you couldn't entrust even to your enemy. Still, since certain things are kept private by custom, share with your friend at least all your worries and all your plans. Treat him as loyal, and you will make him loyal. Some people, by fearing to be deceived, have taught men to deceive — their suspicion handed their friend the right to do wrong. Why should I hold back any word in my friend's presence? Why shouldn't I feel as alone in his company as I do by myself?

There's a class of people who will tell whoever happens to be nearest what should be told only to friends, unloading whatever's bothering them into any available ear. Others are afraid to confide even in those closest to them; if they could, they wouldn't trust even themselves, and they push every secret down deep inside. Do neither. It's equally a fault to trust everyone and to trust no one — though I'd say the first fault is the more innocent, the second the safer.

In the same way, there are two kinds of people who deserve a scolding: the ones who can never rest, and the ones who never do anything else. Because a love of bustle isn't industry — it's the restlessness of a hunted mind. And true rest doesn't mean writing off all motion as a nuisance; that kind of rest is slackness and inertia. So keep in mind a line I came across in Pomponius: "Some men shrink so far into dark corners that daylight itself looks dim to them." Mix the two: the resting man should act, and the acting man should rest. Take it up with Nature — she'll tell you she made both day and night.

Farewell.

Letter 4: On the Terrors of Death

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Keep on as you've begun, and hurry all you can — the sooner you finish the work, the longer you'll get to enjoy a mind that is improved and at peace with itself. There's enjoyment in the improving too, of course; but the pleasure of contemplating a mind cleansed of every stain, a mind that shines, is something else entirely. You remember, surely, the joy you felt when you put away the clothes of boyhood, put on a man's toga, and were escorted down to the forum. Expect a greater joy still when you put away the boy's mind, and wisdom enrolls you among men. Because it isn't boyhood that stays with us — it's something worse: boyishness. And it's all the more serious because we carry the authority of age together with the follies of boyhood — of infancy, even. Boys fear trifles, small children fear shadows; we fear both.

All you need to do is keep advancing, and you'll come to understand that some things are less to be dreaded precisely because they frighten us so much. No evil is great if it is the last evil of all. Death arrives; it would be worth dreading if it could stay with you. But death must either never come at all, or come and be gone.

"It's hard, though," you say, "to bring the mind to the point where it can look down on life." But look at the trivial reasons that drive people to throw life away. One man hangs himself outside his mistress's door. Another throws himself off the roof to escape a bad-tempered master's taunts. A runaway drives a sword into his own belly rather than be dragged back. Don't you think virtue can manage what runaway terror manages so easily? No one can have a peaceful life who spends it scheming to prolong it, or who counts a long string of consulships among the great blessings. Rehearse this thought every day, so that you can leave life contentedly when the time comes. Plenty of men clutch and cling to life the way people swept down rapids clutch and cling at thorns and jagged rocks.

Most men toss miserably between the fear of death and the hardships of life: they don't want to live, and they don't know how to die. So make your whole life agreeable to yourself by giving up all anxiety about keeping it. No good thing makes its owner happy unless his mind is prepared for its loss — and nothing is lost more painlessly than the thing that can't be missed once it's gone. So encourage yourself, toughen yourself, against the blows that fall even on the most powerful. Pompey's fate was decided by a boy and a eunuch; Crassus's by a cruel and insolent Parthian. Gaius Caesar ordered Lepidus to bare his neck to the tribune Dexter's axe — and then offered his own throat to Chaerea. Fortune has never raised any man so high that she didn't threaten him with everything she had granted him. Don't trust her calm; in a moment the sea is churned to its depths. The very day the ships make their brave show at the games, they're swallowed.

Consider that a highwayman or an enemy could cut your throat at any time. And short of that, every slave holds the power of life and death over you, master or no master. I'll say it plainly: whoever holds his own life cheap is master of yours. Run through the men who have died by plots inside their own houses, killed openly or by trickery, and you'll see that slaves' anger has cut down as many as kings' anger has. So what does it matter how powerful the man you fear may be, when the thing you're afraid of is a power everyone has?

"But if you fall into enemy hands," you say, "the conqueror will order you led away —" yes, led to where you were already going. Why fool yourself, and act as if you've only just now been told the fate you've been under all along? Take my word for it: since the day you were born, you have been being led there. We must turn these thoughts, and thoughts like them, over in our minds if we want to be calm as we wait for that final hour — the one whose fear poisons all the hours before it.

But I need to end this letter. Here's the saying that pleased me today — picked, once again, from another man's Garden, Epicurus's: "Poverty brought into line with the law of nature is great wealth." Do you know what limits that law of nature sets for us? To keep off hunger, thirst, and cold — that's all. To banish hunger and thirst, you don't have to wait on the doorsteps of the purse-proud, or endure the hard frown, or the kindness that humiliates; you don't have to scour the seas or follow an army. What nature demands is close at hand and easy to get. It's the extras men sweat for — the extras that wear our togas threadbare, that keep us growing old in army camps, that fling us onto foreign shores. What is enough is within reach. Whoever has made a fair peace with poverty is rich.

Farewell.

Letter 5: The Philosopher's Mean

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I applaud you, and I'm delighted that you're sticking to your studies and — setting everything else aside — working at one thing every day: becoming a better man. I don't just urge you to keep it up; I'm begging you to. But let me give you a warning: don't act like the people who want to be conspicuous rather than to improve, doing things with their dress or their way of living that are designed to draw stares. Rough clothes, unkempt hair, a slovenly beard, loud contempt for silverware, a bed on the bare ground — avoid that whole perverse wardrobe of self-display. The very name "philosophy" attracts enough sneers even when it's pursued quietly; what happens if we start seceding from ordinary human customs altogether? Inside, let us be different in every respect; outside, let us fit in with everyone else. Don't wear too fine a toga — and don't wear a shabby one either. We don't need silver plate crusted with solid gold; but let's not imagine that doing without silver and gold proves the simple life. Let's aim at a way of life better than the crowd's, not opposed to it — otherwise we frighten off the very people we're trying to improve. We also make them unwilling to imitate us in anything, for fear they'd have to imitate us in everything.

The first thing philosophy promises is fellow-feeling with all mankind — sympathy, sociability. Being unlike other people breaks that promise. We have to make sure the things we do to win admiration aren't simply ridiculous and repellent. Our motto, as you know, is "live according to Nature" — and it is flatly against nature to torture your body, to hate easy elegance, to be dirty on purpose, to eat food that isn't just plain but foul and revolting. Hunting for delicacies is decadence; avoiding ordinary things you can buy for next to nothing is madness. Philosophy calls for plain living, not for penance — and plain can perfectly well be neat. This is the middle course I approve of: a life that strikes a happy balance between the ways of the wise man and the ways of the world; everyone should admire it, but everyone should recognize it too.

"So then, shall we do just as everyone else does? Shall there be no difference between us and them?" A very great difference — but one that people discover when they look closely. A visitor to our house should admire us, not our furnishings. He's a great man who uses clay dishes as if they were silver — and just as great a man who uses silver as if it were clay. Not being able to endure wealth is the mark of an unsteady mind.

But let me share today's profit with you as well. In our own Hecato — no raid on a rival camp needed this time — I find that limiting your desires helps cure your fears too. "Cease to hope," he says, "and you will cease to fear." "But how," you'll ask, "can two such different things travel together?" It's true, my dear Lucilius: they look opposed, but they're linked. The same chain binds the prisoner and the soldier who guards him — and hope and fear, unlike as they are, keep step together. Fear follows hope. And I'm not surprised they move that way. Both belong to a mind in suspense — a mind fretting over what comes next. The chief cause of both is that we don't fit ourselves to the present but send our thoughts far ahead. And so foresight, the noblest gift the human race has, gets twisted into a curse. Wild animals run from the dangers they can see, and once they've escaped, they're at ease; we torment ourselves over what's coming and what's past alike. Many of our blessings do us harm: memory replays the agony of fear, and foresight previews it. Nobody is made wretched by the present alone.

Farewell.

Letter 6: On Sharing Knowledge

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I feel, my dear Lucilius, that I am being not merely reformed but transformed. Not that I promise myself — or even let myself hope — that there's nothing left in me that needs changing. Of course there's plenty that should be tightened up, or thinned down, or built out. But this very thing is proof that my spirit has changed for the better: it can now see faults in itself it never noticed before. With some patients, it's cause for congratulation when they realize they're sick.

I wanted to share this sudden change in myself with you, because then I could begin to trust our friendship more surely — the true friendship that hope and fear and self-interest cannot break, the friendship in which, and for the sake of which, men are willing to die. I can show you plenty of men who have lacked not a friend but a friendship. That can never happen when two souls are drawn together into partnership by a shared desire for what is honorable. And why not? Because such men know they hold everything in common — especially their troubles.

You can't imagine how much progress I see each day bringing me. "Then send me a share of these gifts you've found so helpful," you'll say. Believe me, I want to pour all of it into you; part of my joy in learning anything is that I get to teach it. Nothing will ever please me — however excellent, however beneficial — if I have to keep the knowledge of it to myself. If wisdom were offered to me on one condition, that I keep it shut away and never speak of it, I would turn it down. No good thing is any pleasure to own without a friend to share it.

So I'll send you the books themselves — and, so you won't waste time hunting through them for the profitable parts, I'll mark the passages, so you can turn straight to what I approve and admire. But the living voice, and a life lived side by side, will help you more than any written word. You have to come and see for yourself — first, because people trust their eyes more than their ears, and second, because the road is long through precepts, but short and sure through examples. Cleanthes could not have become the very image of Zeno by merely hearing his lectures: he shared his life, saw into his private purposes, watched to see whether the man lived by his own rules. Plato and Aristotle and that whole crowd of sages who would each go his own way got more from Socrates's character than from his words. It wasn't Epicurus's classroom that made Metrodorus, Hermarchus, and Polyaenus into great men — it was living under the same roof with him. So I'm calling you here not only to gain something, but to add something; we can do each other a great deal of good.

Meanwhile, I owe you your little daily wage. Here's what pleased me today in Hecato: "You ask what progress I've made? I have begun to be a friend to myself." Real progress — a man like that will never be alone. And you may be sure of this: such a man is a friend to the whole human race.

Farewell.

Letters 7–9

Letter 7: On Crowds

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You ask what you should avoid above all else? Crowds. You can't yet trust yourself in one safely. I'll confess my own weakness on this point: I never bring back home the same character that I took abroad with me. Something of the calm I've forced on myself gets shaken loose; some enemy I had driven off slips back in. Like an invalid who has been weak so long he can't be carried outdoors without a relapse — that's us, souls convalescing from a long illness. Mixing with the crowd does damage. Every single person in it will recommend some vice to us, or stamp one on us, or smear us with one before we've even noticed. And the bigger the mob we mingle with, the bigger the danger.

Nothing is as ruinous to good character as killing time at the games — that's where vice sneaks in through the open door of pleasure. What do I mean? I mean I come home greedier, more ambitious, more self-indulgent — crueler, in fact, and less human — because I've been among human beings. The other day I dropped in on the midday show, expecting some fun, some wit, a breather — the slot where the audience's eyes get a rest from human slaughter. It was the opposite. The morning's fights had been mercy itself by comparison. Now the fooling around is over, and it's butchery pure and simple. The men have nothing to defend themselves with. Every inch of them is open to the blow, and no blow ever misses. Plenty of people prefer this to the regular pairings and the special-request bouts. Of course they do — there's no helmet or shield to turn the blade. Why bother with armor? Why bother with skill? All that just delays death. In the morning men are thrown to the lions and the bears; at noon they're thrown to the spectators. The crowd orders each killer sent against the man who will kill him next, and keeps the latest winner in stock for another slaughter. The exit for every fighter is death, and fire and sword keep the show moving. And all this goes on while the arena stands practically empty.

"But he was a highway robber," you may say. "He killed a man!" So what? Granted he was a murderer and deserved the punishment — what crime did you commit, poor fellow, that you deserve to sit and watch it? All morning they scream: "Kill him! Whip him! Burn him! Why does he meet the sword like such a coward? Why does he strike so feebly? Why won't he die game? Lash him into his wounds! Make them trade blow for blow, chests bare, right into the stroke!" And when the show pauses for intermission, the announcement comes: "A little throat-cutting in the meantime, so there's still something going on!"

Come now — can't you see even this much, that a bad example comes back on the one who sets it? Thank the immortal gods that the man you're teaching cruelty to is one who can't learn it. A young character that can't yet keep its grip on the right has to be pulled out of the mob; it's too easy to go over to the majority. Even a Socrates, a Cato, a Laelius could have had their principles shaken by a crowd unlike them — that's how true it is that none of us, however hard we polish our nature, can stand the shock of vices marching in with a procession that size behind them. A single example of extravagance or greed does plenty of damage. A luxurious housemate softens and weakens us little by little; a rich neighbor keeps our envy stirred up; a spiteful companion rubs his rust off on us no matter how clean and honest we are. So what do you think happens to a character the whole world is assaulting? You will end up either imitating the world or loathing it.

Avoid both. Don't copy the bad because they're many, and don't hate the many because they're unlike you. Withdraw into yourself as far as you can. Associate with those who will make a better man of you. Welcome those you can make better yourself. It works both ways: men learn while they teach.

And don't let pride in advertising your talents lure you out before the public, reciting and lecturing. I'd tell you to go ahead if you had merchandise this mob wanted; as it is, not one of them can understand you. Maybe one or two individuals will drift your way, and even those you'll have to shape and train up to the point of understanding you. "Then who did I learn all this for?" Don't worry — nothing was wasted. You learned it for yourself.

But so that I won't have learned only for myself today, I'll share three excellent sayings that came my way, all circling the same point. One of them pays this letter's debt; the other two, accept as an advance. Democritus says: "One man means as much to me as a multitude, and a multitude as much as one man." And this was nobly said too, whoever said it (the author is disputed) — asked why he worked so hard at an art that would reach almost no one, he answered: "A few are enough for me; one is enough; none is enough." The third, and a fine one, is Epicurus, writing to a partner in his studies: "I write this not for the many but for you; each of us is audience enough for the other."

Take these to heart, Lucilius, and you'll learn to scorn the pleasure that comes from the applause of the majority. Many people praise you — but is that any reason to be pleased with yourself, if you're someone the many can understand? Let your good qualities face inward.

Farewell.

Letter 8: On the Philosopher’s Seclusion

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

"So you're telling me," you say, "to shun the crowd, withdraw from society, and settle for a clear conscience? What happened to your school's marching orders — die in the middle of active work?" Listen: the retreat I keep urging on you — my purpose in shutting myself away and locking the door is to help more people. I never spend a day in idleness; I claim part of the night for study too. I don't budget time for sleep — I surrender to it when I must, and when my eyes are worn out with waking and ready to fall shut, I hold them to the task. I've withdrawn not only from people but from business, and from my own business most of all. The work I'm doing is for later generations: writing down things that might help them. There are certain healthy counsels — think of them as recipes for useful medicines — that I'm putting into writing, because I've found them effective on my own sores, which may not be fully healed but have at least stopped spreading.

I point other men to the right road — the one I found late, worn out from wandering. I shout to them: "Avoid whatever the crowd loves, whatever Fortune tosses your way! Stop in doubt and fear before every windfall she brings — it's the dumb animals and the fish that get taken in by baited hopes. You call these things Fortune's gifts? They're traps. Anyone who wants a life of safety will steer as wide as he can of these sticky snares of her favor, which fool us — wretched in this too — into thinking we hold them, when they hold us. A career like that leads along cliff edges, and life on those heights ends in a fall. Once prosperity starts driving us off course, we can't even stand against her, or go down 'with the ship at least on her course,' or go down once and for all. Fortune doesn't capsize us — she drives the bow under and pounds us on the rocks.

"So hold fast to this sound and wholesome rule of life: indulge the body only as far as good health requires. Handle it a bit roughly, or it will stop obeying the mind. Eat to ease your hunger, drink to quench your thirst, dress to keep out the cold, and house yourself as shelter from discomfort — nothing more. Whether that house is built of turf or of colorful imported marble matters very little: understand that thatch covers a man just as well as a roof of gold. Despise everything that useless labor sets up as ornament and decoration. Keep in mind that nothing deserves wonder except the soul — and to a great soul, nothing else is great."

When I talk in these terms with myself and with future generations, don't you think I'm doing more good than when I show up as counsel in court, or press my seal into a will, or lend my voice and my hand in the senate to some candidate? Believe me, the people who seem to be doing nothing are doing the greater things: they're handling the mortal and the immortal at the same time.

But I must stop and pay the customary contribution that balances this letter. It won't come out of my own pocket — I'm still working through Epicurus. Today I read this sentence in him: "To enjoy real freedom, you must be the slave of philosophy." The man who submits and surrenders himself to her isn't kept waiting; he's freed on the spot. For the very service of philosophy is freedom.

Now, you'll probably ask why I quote so many of Epicurus's fine sayings instead of our own school's. But why should you count them as Epicurus's property rather than common property? Think how many poets say things philosophers have said — or should say. I won't even touch the tragedians or our national drama (which has its serious side too, standing halfway between comedy and tragedy). What a stock of shrewd verse lies buried in the mime! How many of Publilius's lines deserve delivery from tragic actors, not just comedians in slippers! I'll quote one verse of his that touches philosophy — the very part of it we were just discussing — where he says that Fortune's gifts should never be counted as our own:

  Whatever you have gained by coveting
  is still not yours.

I remember you put the same idea much better and tighter yourself:

  What Fortune makes yours is not really yours.

And a third of yours, better still, shouldn't be left out:

  The good that can be given can be taken away.

I won't charge these to the expense account — you got them from your own stock.

Farewell.

Letter 9: On Philosophy and Friendship

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You want to know whether Epicurus is right when, in one of his letters, he criticizes those who say the wise man is self-sufficient and therefore has no need of friendship. That's the charge he brings against Stilbo and the others who hold that the supreme good is a soul beyond feeling.

We run straight into ambiguity if we try to compress the Greek term for "no feeling" into one quick word — it can be read as the opposite of what we mean. What we mean is a soul that refuses to register any evil; what people will hear is a soul that can't endure any. Consider whether it isn't better to say "a soul that cannot be harmed," or "a soul entirely beyond the reach of suffering." Here's the difference between us and that other school: our wise man feels his troubles but overcomes them; theirs doesn't even feel them. What we share with them is this: the wise man is self-sufficient. And yet, sufficient as he is unto himself, he still wants friends, neighbors, associates.

Notice just how self-sufficient he is: at need, he can be content with a part of himself. If disease or war costs him a hand, if some accident takes one eye or both, what's left will satisfy him; maimed and diminished, he'll be as glad in his body as he was when it was whole. But not mourning what's missing doesn't mean not preferring it intact. The wise man is self-sufficient in this sense: he can do without friends — not that he wants to. And by "can" I mean this: he bears the loss of a friend with a level spirit.

He'll never actually be short of friends, though, because it lies in his own power how quickly he replaces a loss. If Phidias loses a statue, he can carve another on the spot; just so, this master of the art of making friendships can fill the place of a friend he has lost. How do you make a friend quickly? I'll tell you — provided we agree that I may pay my debt right now and square this letter's account. Hecato says: "I can show you a love potion made without drugs, without herbs, without any witch's incantation: if you want to be loved, love." And there's great pleasure not only in keeping up old, established friendships but in starting and winning new ones. The difference between winning a friend and having won one is the difference between the farmer sowing and the farmer reaping. The philosopher Attalus used to say: "It's more pleasant to make a friend than to have one, just as it's more pleasant for the artist to paint than to have painted." While the work has you absorbed, the absorption itself is a deep delight; the pleasure dulls once you lift your hand from the finished masterpiece. From then on he enjoys the fruits of his art; while he was painting, he enjoyed the art itself. With our children, their grown years bear the more abundant fruit, but their infancy was sweeter.

Now back to the question. The wise man, I say, self-sufficient though he is, still wants friends — if for no other reason, then so that he may practice friendship, so that a virtue that size doesn't lie idle. Not for the purpose Epicurus names in that letter — "so there's someone to sit by his bed when he's sick, someone to help when he's imprisoned or poor" — but so that he may have someone whose sickbed he can sit beside, someone held prisoner by an enemy whom he can set free. Whoever looks only to himself and comes to friendship for that reason has it backwards. And the end will match the beginning: he made a friend to help him out of chains; at the first rattle of the chain, that friend is gone. These are the fair-weather friendships, as people call them. Choose a friend for his usefulness and he'll please you exactly as long as he's useful. That's why prosperous men are mobbed by troops of friends while ruined men stand in a vast emptiness, their friends fleeing the very crisis that would have tested their worth. That's why we see so many shameful desertions, so many betrayals out of fear. Beginnings and endings have to agree. The man who starts being your friend because it pays will stop because it pays. If anything in a friendship attracts him besides the friendship itself, some other payoff will attract him away.

"Then what do I make a friend for?" To have someone I can die for. Someone I can follow into exile, someone whose life I'll stake my own against — and pay up, too. What you're describing is a business deal, not a friendship: it courts advantage and looks to the return. No question, the feeling of a lover has something in it akin to friendship — you might call love a friendship gone mad. So then: does anyone fall in love for profit? For advancement? For fame? Love on its own, careless of everything else, sets the soul on fire with desire for the beautiful — not without hope, yes, of affection returned. What follows? Can a cause that is more honorable produce a passion that is corrupt?

"But we're not discussing whether friendship is worth pursuing for its own sake." On the contrary — that's exactly what most needs proving. Because if it is to be sought for its own sake, then the man who is enough for himself may still seek it. "And how does he seek it?" The way you seek any thing of great beauty: not pulled toward it by hope of profit, not scared off by Fortune's mood swings. Whoever seeks friendship for its convenient occasions strips it of all its nobility.

"The wise man is self-sufficient." Many people get this sentence wrong, my dear Lucilius: they banish the wise man from the world and shut him up inside his own skin. But we have to mark carefully what it means and how far it goes. The wise man is sufficient unto himself for living happily — not for merely living. For mere living he needs a great many supports; for living happily he needs only a sound and upright soul, one that looks down on Fortune.

I'd like to show you a distinction of Chrysippus's as well. He says the wise man lacks nothing, yet needs many things; "the fool, on the other hand, needs nothing — he doesn't know how to use anything — but he lacks everything." The wise man needs hands, eyes, and countless things for daily use, but he lacks nothing. Lack implies necessity, and nothing is necessary to the wise man. So although he's self-sufficient, he still has need of friends — and wants as many as possible — but not so that he may live happily. He'll live happily even without them. The supreme good never sends out for equipment: it's raised at home, and the whole of it grows from itself. Let the good beg any part of itself from outside, and it has started taking orders from Fortune.

"But what kind of existence will the wise man have if he's left friendless in prison, or stranded among strangers, or held up on a long voyage, or cast up on an empty shore?" The kind Jupiter has when the world dissolves — the gods run together into one, Nature pauses her work for a while, and he settles into himself, given over to his own thoughts. That's more or less what the sage does: he retreats into himself; he is his own company. Still, as long as he's free to arrange his affairs by his own judgment, he is self-sufficient — and marries a wife. Self-sufficient — and raises children. Self-sufficient — and yet could not live at all if it meant living with no other human being. What draws him into friendship is no personal advantage but a natural pull. Friendship has a built-in attraction for us, the way other things do. Just as we hate solitude and reach for company, just as nature bonds man to man, so here too there's a prod that makes us hungry for friendship. Even so — though he loves his friends dearly, ranking them with himself and often above himself — the sage will keep all his good within his own borders, and he'll say what Stilbo said, the very Stilbo Epicurus's letter attacks. Stilbo's city had been captured, his children lost, his wife lost; he walked out of the general wreckage alone — and yet happy. Demetrius, whose destructions had earned him the name Sacker of Cities, asked whether he had lost anything. "I have all my goods with me," Stilbo said. There's a brave, tough man for you! The enemy conquered his city; Stilbo conquered his conqueror. "I have lost nothing" — he forced Demetrius to wonder whether he'd really won at all. "My goods are all with me": in other words, nothing that can be taken away counts as a good.

We marvel at certain animals because they can pass through fire without harm. How much more marvelous is this man, who marched out through fire and sword and rubble unhurt and unrobbed! You see now how much easier it is to conquer a whole nation than to conquer one man? Stilbo's saying is common ground with Stoicism: the Stoic, too, carries his goods intact through cities burned to ash, for he is self-sufficient. That is the boundary he draws around his own happiness.

And don't imagine that our school alone produces noble words. Epicurus himself, Stilbo's scold, said something much the same — take it as a credit to me, though I've already cleared today's account. "Whoever does not think that what he has is ample wealth is unhappy, though he be master of the whole world." Or, if this strikes you as better put (since we should aim at the meaning, not the words): "A man may rule the world and still be miserable, if he doesn't feel supremely happy." And to show you that these feelings are universal — Nature herself prompts them — you'll find this line in one of the comic poets:

  Unhappy is the man who thinks himself unhappy.

What does your condition matter, if it looks bad in your own eyes? "So then," you say, "take that man over there, rich by shameful means, or that one, master of many and slave of more — if they call themselves happy, does their own opinion make them happy?" What matters isn't what a man says but what he feels — and not what he feels on one particular day, but what he feels steadily. No need to fear this great prize falling into unworthy hands, though: only the wise man is pleased with what is his own. Folly is forever sick of itself.

Farewell.

Letters 10–13

Letter 10: On Living to Oneself

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

No, I haven't changed my mind: avoid the crowd, avoid the small circle, avoid even the single companion. There is no one I know of that I'd be willing to share you with. And notice what a compliment I'm paying you here — I trust you alone with yourself. Crates, they say, the student of that same Stilbo I mentioned in an earlier letter, once noticed a young man walking by himself and asked him what he was doing out there all alone. "I'm talking with myself," the young man answered. "Then be careful," said Crates. "Pay close attention — you're talking with a bad man."

When someone is grieving, or frightened about something, we make a point of keeping watch over them, so their solitude can't do them harm. No thoughtless person should ever be left alone. Alone, he only plots foolishness and piles up future dangers for himself or for others. Alone, his low desires come out to play; whatever fear or shame used to keep hidden, the mind now puts on display. Solitude sharpens his recklessness, stirs his lusts, and needles his temper. And the one real benefit solitude offers — no one to trust, no witnesses to fear — is completely wasted on a fool. He informs on himself.

So mark what my hopes are for you — no, mark what I am promising myself, since "hope" is just the name we give a blessing that isn't certain yet: I don't know anyone whose company I would rather you keep than your own. I remember the great-hearted way you flung out certain phrases, and how much strength was in them. I congratulated myself on the spot and said: "Those words didn't come from the edge of the lips. There's a foundation under what this man says. He's not one of the many; he has his eye on his real welfare." Speak that way, and live that way. See to it that nothing drags you down. As for your old prayers, you can release the gods from answering them — file new ones instead. Pray for a sound mind and for good health, of the soul first and then of the body. And pray for these things often, of course. Ask God boldly; you won't be asking him for anything that belongs to someone else.

But, as usual, I need to send a little gift along with this letter. Here's a true saying I found in Athenodorus: "Know that you are free of all desires when you have reached the point of asking God for nothing you couldn't ask for out loud." How foolish people are about this now! They whisper the filthiest prayers up to heaven, and the moment anyone is listening, they go silent. What they refuse to let other people hear, they tell God. So consider whether this might be the healthiest advice anyone could give you: Live among men as if God saw you; speak with God as if men were listening.

Farewell.

Letter 11: On the Blush of Modesty

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Your friend and I have had a talk. He's a man of real ability; his very first words showed what spirit and understanding he has, and how far he has already come. He gave me a foretaste of himself, and he won't fail to live up to it — because he wasn't speaking from a script. He was caught off guard, and when he tried to collect himself, he could barely shake off that flush of modesty — a good sign in a young man. The blush that spread over his face seemed to rise from somewhere deep. And I'm confident that habit of blushing will stay with him even after he has toughened his character, stripped off all his faults, and become wise. No amount of wisdom removes the body's natural weaknesses. What's planted in us at birth can be trained down, but never trained out. The steadiest public speaker often breaks into a sweat before a crowd, as if he'd worn himself out or overheated. Some men's knees shake when they stand up to speak. I know men whose teeth chatter, whose tongues stumble, whose lips tremble. Training and experience never fully shake this off; nature exerts her own power, and through a weakness like this she reminds even the strongest men that she exists. The blush, I'm sure, belongs to this family — it spreads suddenly across the faces of the most dignified men. It shows up more in the young, yes, because their blood is warmer and their faces more sensitive; but it touches veterans and old men too. Some men are at their most dangerous when they redden, as if their shame were draining out of them. Sulla was at his fiercest when the blood rushed to his cheeks. No one had a more sensitive face than Pompey; he blushed in front of every gathering, and especially at a public assembly. And I remember Fabianus reddening when he appeared as a witness before the senate — and the embarrassment suited him remarkably well. A habit like this doesn't come from weakness of mind but from the newness of a situation. An inexperienced man isn't necessarily rattled, but he's usually affected, because his body's natural bent slides him into it. Some men are simply full-blooded; others have quick, restless blood that rushes straight to the face.

As I said, wisdom can never cure this. If wisdom could erase all our flaws, she would be mistress of the universe. Whatever the terms of our birth and the mix of our constitution assign to us will stick to us, no matter how hard or how long the soul works at mastering itself. We can no more forbid these feelings than we can summon them. Actors in the theater, who imitate the emotions — who play fear and nervousness, who act out sorrow — imitate bashfulness by hanging their heads, lowering their voices, fixing their eyes on the ground. But a blush is the one thing they cannot produce. It can't be prevented, and it can't be performed. Wisdom offers no promise here and no help; the blush comes and goes on its own orders. It is a law unto itself.

But my letter is calling for its closing line. Hear this one and take it to heart, because it's useful and it's healthy: "Cherish some man of high character, and keep him always before your eyes — live as if he were watching you, and act in everything as if he saw it." That, my dear Lucilius, is Epicurus's advice, and it's exactly right: he has given us a guardian and an attendant. We would drop most of our sins if a witness stood beside us whenever we were about to go wrong. The soul should have someone it can revere — someone whose authority makes even its inner sanctuary more sacred. Happy is the man who improves other people not just when he's with them, but even when he's in their thoughts! And happy too is the man who can revere someone so deeply that merely remembering him calms and straightens him out. A man who can revere another like that will soon be worth revering himself. So choose yourself a Cato — or, if Cato seems too severe a model, choose a Laelius, a gentler spirit. Choose a master whose life, whose conversation, whose very face — the face that shows the soul — has won you over; keep him always in view, as your protector or your pattern. Because we truly do need someone to measure our characters against. You can never straighten what is crooked unless you use a ruler.

Farewell.

Letter 12: On Old Age

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Everywhere I turn, I see evidence of how old I'm getting. I visited my country place recently and complained about the money being poured into that crumbling building. My caretaker insisted the flaws weren't his fault — "he was doing everything possible, but the house was old." And this is the house that went up under my own hands! What does the future hold for me, if stones my own age are already crumbling? Annoyed, I seized the next excuse to vent at him. "It's obvious," I said, "that these plane trees are being neglected. They have no leaves. Look how gnarled and shriveled the branches are, how rough and shabby the trunks! That wouldn't happen if someone loosened the soil around them and gave them water." He swore by my guardian spirit that "he was doing everything possible, he never let up — but those trees were old." Between you and me, I planted those trees myself. I saw their first leaves. Then I turned toward the door and asked, "Who is that broken-down wreck? Good thinking, posting him at the entrance — he's clearly on his way out. Where did you get him? What possessed you to steal another man's corpse for burial?" But the slave said, "Don't you know me, sir? I'm Felicio — you used to bring me little clay figures. My father was Philositus the steward. I'm your old pet." "The man is stark raving mad," I said. "Has my little pet become a little boy again? Well, it's entirely possible — his teeth are falling out."

I owe my country place this much: it showed me my old age wherever I looked. So let's embrace old age and love it. It's full of pleasure, if you know how to use it. Fruit is sweetest just before it spoils; youth is loveliest as it's ending; for the dedicated drinker it's the last drink that delights — the one that tops him off and crowns the drunkenness. Every pleasure saves its best for last. Life is most delightful on the downward slope — before the edge, but on the way there. And I honestly believe that even the years standing at the roof's very edge have pleasures of their own. Or else this itself replaces pleasure: not needing any. How comforting it is to have worn out your appetites and left them behind! "But," you say, "it's grim to have death staring you in the face." First, death should be stared at by young and old alike — we aren't called up in the order of the census list. Second, no one is so old that he can't decently hope for one more day. And one day, mind you, is a stage on life's journey.

Our whole span of life is built of parts — larger circles enclosing smaller ones. One circle wraps around and bounds all the rest: it runs from birth to our last day. Inside it, a second circle closes off the years of our youth. A third holds the whole of childhood in its circumference. Then there's the year, a class of its own, containing every division of time whose multiplication adds up to a life. The month is ringed by a narrower circle. The smallest circle of all is the day — but even a day has its beginning and its end, its sunrise and its sunset. That's why Heraclitus, whose murky style earned him his nickname, said: "One day is equal to every day." People take this different ways. Some say days are equal in hours, which is true enough — if a "day" means twenty-four hours, then all days are equal, since the night gains whatever the day loses. But others say that one day equals all days by resemblance: even the longest stretch of time contains nothing you won't find in a single day — light and darkness — and eternity just multiplies those alternations; it doesn't make them different, shorter here and longer there. So live each day as if it closed the series — as if it rounded off and completed your life.

Pacuvius, who held Syria so long he made it his own, used to stage a funeral sacrifice in his own honor — wine, the full funeral banquet — and then have himself carried from the dining room to his bedroom while eunuchs clapped and sang in Greek to music: "He has lived his life! He has lived his life!" Not a day passed without Pacuvius carrying himself out for burial. Let's do from a good motive what he did from a rotten one. Let's go to our sleep glad and cheerful, saying:

  I have lived; the course that Fortune set me
  is finished.

And if God is pleased to add another day, let's welcome it with glad hearts. The happiest man, the most securely in possession of himself, is the one who can wait for tomorrow without anxiety. Whoever has said "I have lived" collects a bonus every morning he gets up.

But now I should close this letter. "What?" you say. "It's coming to me without any little present?" Don't worry — it carries something. More than something: a great deal. What could be nobler than the saying I'm handing it to deliver: "It is wrong to live under constraint; but no man is constrained to live under constraint." Of course not. On every side, short and simple roads to freedom lie open. Let's thank God that no one can be held prisoner in life. We are free to trample the constraints themselves. "Epicurus said that," you object. "What business do you have with someone else's property?" Whatever is true is my property. And I'm going to keep piling Epicurus on you, so that the people who swear by a name — who price the speaker instead of the words — may learn that the best ideas belong to everyone.

Farewell.

Letter 13: On Groundless Fears

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I know you have plenty of spirit. Even before you began equipping yourself with maxims — the wholesome kind, with real power over hardship — you took pride in your standing bout with Fortune; and that's truer now that you've actually grappled with her and tested your strength. Our powers can never give us solid confidence in ourselves until difficulties have crowded us from every side — until they've occasionally closed to grappling range. That is how the true spirit proves itself: the spirit that refuses to come under the jurisdiction of anything outside itself. That's its touchstone. No prizefighter can walk into the ring with high hopes if he's never been beaten black and blue. The only man who steps up with real confidence is the one who has seen his own blood, felt his teeth rattle under a fist, been tripped and taken the full weight of a charge — the one who has been knocked down in body but never in spirit, and who rises from every fall more defiant than before. So, to keep the metaphor going: Fortune has had you on the ground before, and you didn't surrender — you sprang back up and planted your feet more eagerly than ever. Courage grows when it's challenged. Still, if you'll let me, I'd like to offer a few extra defenses to fortify yourself with.

There are more things likely to frighten us, Lucilius, than there are to crush us. We suffer more often in imagination than in reality. I'm not talking to you in the Stoic strain now, but in my milder style. Our Stoic custom is to dismiss everything that draws cries and groans as trivial, beneath notice — but let's you and I drop the grand language, true as it is, God knows. Here's my actual advice: don't be miserable ahead of the crisis. The dangers that made you go pale, as if they were bearing down on you, may never arrive. They certainly haven't arrived yet. Some things torment us more than they should; some torment us before they should; and some torment us when they shouldn't torment us at all. We exaggerate our suffering, or we invent it, or we borrow it early.

Set the first of those three aside for now — the matter is disputed and the case is still in court, so to speak. What I call trivial, you'll insist is deadly serious; and I'm well aware that some men laugh under the whip while others wince at a box on the ear. We'll consider later whether such evils get their power from their own strength or from our weakness.

Do me this favor: when people crowd around you working to convince you how unhappy you are, weigh not what you hear but what you actually feel. Consult your own feelings; put the question to yourself independently — you know your own affairs better than anyone. Ask: "Why exactly are these people condoling with me? Why are they nervous, why afraid of catching something from me, as if trouble were contagious? Is there any actual evil here, or just an evil reputation?" Ask yourself, voluntarily: "Am I tormenting myself without good reason? Am I gloomy over nothing? Am I converting something that isn't an evil into one?" You'll come back at me: "How am I supposed to know whether my sufferings are real or imaginary?" Here's the rule. We are tormented by the present, by the future, or by both. The present is an easy verdict: if your body is free and healthy and nothing external is injuring you, then today there is nothing wrong. "But something will happen!" First, examine whether your evidence of coming trouble is solid. Far more often, we're troubled by our own suspicions — we get played by that great mocker, rumour, which has been known to end wars and far more often ends individuals. Yes, my dear Lucilius: we sign on too quickly to whatever people say. We never put our fears to the test, never examine them. We go pale and retreat like soldiers abandoning camp over a dust-cloud kicked up by stampeding cattle, or panicked by some story nobody has verified. And somehow it's the empty report that rattles us most. Truth has fixed boundaries; whatever comes out of uncertainty is handed over to guesswork and to the runaway license of a frightened mind. That is why no fear is so ruinous, so impossible to rein in, as panic fear. Other fears are groundless; panic is witless.

So let's look at this carefully. It's likely some trouble will come to us; but it is not a present fact. How often the unexpected has happened! How often the expected has never shown up! And even if suffering is on its way, what do you gain by running out to meet it? You'll suffer soon enough when it arrives. In the meantime, count on better things. What does that buy you? Time. Plenty will happen in the interval to postpone your trials, or cancel them, or pass them along to someone else — even the ones nearby, even the ones on top of you. A fire has opened a road to escape. Disaster has set men down gently. The sword has been stopped at the very throat. Men have outlived their own executioners. Even bad fortune is fickle. Maybe it will come, maybe it won't; in the meantime, it is not. So count on better things.

Sometimes, with no sign anywhere pointing to trouble, the mind manufactures its own false shapes of evil. It wrings the worst possible meaning out of an ambiguous word. It decides some grudge against us is graver than it is — dwelling not on how angry the enemy actually is, but on how far he might go if he were. But life is not worth living, and there is no bottom to sorrow, if we give fear everything it asks for. Here, let prudence be your help: reject fear with a resolute spirit even when it's standing in plain sight. If you can't manage that, then fight one weakness with another — temper your fear with hope. Nothing we're afraid of is so certain as this: that what we dread comes to nothing, and what we hope for makes fools of us.

So weigh your hopes and your fears together, and whenever the whole question is in doubt, rule in your own favor: believe the version you'd prefer. And if fear carries the vote anyway, lean the other way regardless, and stop harassing your soul. Keep reminding yourself that most mortals work themselves into a froth even when no trouble is at hand and none is certainly coming. No one applies the brakes once he's been given a push; no one scales his alarm to the truth. No one says: "The man who started this story is a fool; the man who believes it is a fool; so is the man who made it up." We drift with every breeze. We panic at uncertainties as if they were certainties. We keep no sense of proportion — the slightest weight tips the scale and dumps us straight into panic.

But I'm embarrassed to be lecturing you sternly, or to be coaxing you along with remedies this mild. Let somebody else say, "Maybe the worst won't happen." You are the one who should say: "And what if it does? Let's see who wins. Maybe it will happen for my benefit — maybe a death like that will do my life credit." The hemlock is what made Socrates great. Wrench from Cato's hand that sword of his, liberty's defender, and you rob him of the greater part of his glory. But I've been exhorting you far too long, when what you need is a reminder, not a sermon. The path I'm leading you down is the same one your nature is already taking; you were born for the conduct I'm describing. All the more reason, then, to build up the good in you, and make it beautiful.

Now, to close this letter, I have only to press the usual seal into it — that is, to entrust it with one noble message for you: "The fool, among all his other faults, has this one too: he is always getting ready to live." Think about what that saying means, my excellent Lucilius, and you'll see how revolting it is — this fickleness of men who lay down fresh foundations for their lives every day, who start erecting new hopes at the very brink of the grave. Search your own memory for examples and you'll think of old men busy preparing, at that very hour, for a political career, or for travel, or for business. And what is more shameful than getting ready to live when you are already old? I wouldn't bother concealing the author's name, except that this saying is little known — it isn't one of Epicurus's greatest hits, the ones I've allowed myself to praise and appropriate.

Farewell.

Letters 14–16

Letter 14: On the Reasons for Withdrawing from the World

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I'll admit it: we are all born with an affection for our own body. I'll admit we've been made its guardians, and I'm not saying it should get no consideration at all. What I'm saying is that we must not be its slaves. A man who makes his body his master will have many masters — the man who is over-anxious on its behalf, who runs every decision through the body first. We should act not as if the body were the thing we live for, but as if it were the thing we can't live without. Loving it too much rattles us with fears, loads us with worries, and leaves us open to insults. Virtue comes cheap to the man who prices his body too high. Care for it, by all means, and carefully. But be ready, when reason, self-respect, or duty demands the sacrifice, to hand it over even to the flames.

Still, as far as we can, let's avoid discomfort as well as danger, and pull back onto safe ground by thinking constantly about how to keep the things we fear at a distance. Unless I'm mistaken, they come in three classes: we fear poverty, we fear sickness, and we fear what stronger men can do to us by violence. Of these, nothing shakes us like the threat that hangs over us from another man's power, because it arrives with shouting and uproar. The natural evils I mentioned — poverty and sickness — steal in silently; they bring no terror for the eye or the ear. The other kind of evil comes as a huge parade. It travels with an escort of swords and fire and chains, and a pack of beasts kept ready to be loosed on human flesh. Picture, under this heading, the prison, the cross, the rack, the hook, the stake driven through a man's body until it comes out at his throat. Think of limbs torn apart by chariots driven in opposite directions, of the shirt woven and smeared with pitch to burn on its wearer, and of every other device cruelty has invented besides. No wonder, then, that this fate is our greatest terror: it comes in so many shapes, and its equipment is terrifying. The torturer accomplishes more the more instruments he lays out — the display breaks men who could have withstood the pain itself. In the same way, of all the forces that grip and master our minds, the most effective are the ones with something to show. The other plagues are no less serious — I mean hunger, thirst, ulcers of the stomach, fever burning us up from within — but they are hidden. They have nothing to brandish and nothing to announce them. These others, like great armies, win by sheer display of force.

So let's make sure we give no offense. Sometimes it's the people we need to fear; sometimes, if the state is run so that most business goes through the Senate, its influential men; and sometimes individuals handed power by the people and used against the people. Keeping the friendship of all such men is heavy work; it's enough not to make enemies of them. So the wise man will never provoke the anger of those in power — no, he will steer clear of it, exactly as he'd steer clear of a storm at sea. When you traveled to Sicily, you crossed the Straits. The reckless pilot laughed off the blustering south wind — the wind that roughens the Sicilian sea and churns it into chop — and made not for the shore on the left but for the strand hard by the waters where Charybdis throws the sea into confusion. Your more careful pilot asks the locals about the tides and what the clouds mean, and holds his course far from that stretch infamous for its whirlpools. The wise man does the same. He avoids the powerful man who can hurt him — while making a point of not seeming to avoid him. A large part of staying safe is not visibly seeking safety; for what we avoid, we condemn.

Next, let's look around for ways to protect ourselves from the mob. First of all, want nothing they want; rivalry breeds fights. Second, own nothing that could be snatched from you at great profit to a schemer — carry as little loot on your person as possible. Nobody sheds human blood for its own sake, or almost nobody; more murderers calculate their profits than vent their hatred. If your hands are empty, the highwayman passes you by; even on an infested road, the poor travel in peace. Then remember the old rule and take special care to avoid three things: hatred, envy, and contempt. And only wisdom can show you how. The balance is hard to strike: in dodging envy we can slide into contempt, and while declining to trample others we can make them think we're safe to trample. The power to frighten has left many men frightened. So withdraw on every side — being scorned is as harmful as being admired.

That's why you must take refuge in philosophy. In the eyes of good people — and even of the moderately bad — this pursuit is a kind of sacred emblem. Courtroom eloquence, or anything else that plays to the crowd, makes enemies; philosophy is peaceful and minds her own business. She cannot be scorned; every profession honors her, down to the lowest. Evil will never grow so strong, and good character never be so conspired against, that the name of philosophy stops being holy.

Philosophy itself, though, should be practiced with calm and moderation. "Oh really?" you shoot back. "Do you call the philosophy of Marcus Cato moderate? Cato's voice tried to stop a civil war. Cato stepped between the swords of maddened warlords. When some attacked Pompey and others attacked Caesar, Cato took on both sides at once!" Fair enough — but you can question whether a wise man should have entered public life at all just then. "What are you doing, Marcus Cato? Freedom isn't the question anymore; freedom was wrecked long ago. The question is whether Caesar or Pompey owns the state — and what is that dispute to you? Neither side is yours. A tyrant is being chosen. What does it matter to you which man wins? The better man may win, but the winner is bound to be the worse man." I've cited Cato's final act; but even in earlier years the times didn't allow a wise man to step into that looting of the state. What could Cato do but raise his voice and speak words that went nowhere? One day he was "hustled" — mobbed, spat on, dragged from the forum, marked for exile; another day he was marched from the senate-house straight to prison.

Whether the wise man should give his attention to politics at all, we'll consider later. In the meantime, I ask you to consider those Stoics who, shut out of public life, withdrew into privacy — and there set about improving human existence and framing laws for the human race, without ever offending the men in power. The wise man won't upset the customs of the people, and he won't draw the crowd's eye by some novel way of living.

"So what? Is a man who follows this plan guaranteed to be safe?" I can no more guarantee that than I can guarantee health to a man who lives with moderation — though moderation does, in fact, produce health. Ships sometimes sink in the harbor; what do you think happens on the open sea? And how much more exposed would this man be, busy with many projects, when even his leisure isn't secure! Innocent people sometimes perish — who denies it? — but the guilty perish more often. A soldier's skill isn't disproved when the blow comes through his armor. And finally: the wise man weighs the reason for everything he does, not the results. The beginning is in our power; Fortune decides the outcome — but I do not grant her the right to pass sentence on me. "But she can inflict a measure of suffering and trouble." The highwayman who kills you isn't passing sentence.

Now you're stretching out your hand for the daily gift. Golden indeed is the gift I'll load you with today — and since we've mentioned gold, let me tell you how to get more use and joy out of it. "He who needs riches least, enjoys riches most." "Author's name, please!" you say. Just to show you how generous I am, I've made it my policy to praise the sayings of the other schools: this one belongs to Epicurus, or Metrodorus, or someone else from that workshop. But what difference does it make who said it? It was said for everyone. Whoever craves riches fears for them, and no one enjoys a blessing that keeps him anxious. He's always trying to add a little more; and while he puzzles over growing his wealth, he forgets how to use it. He collects on his accounts, wears out the pavement of the forum, flips through his ledger — he stops being the master and becomes the bookkeeper.

Farewell.

Letter 15: On Brawn and Brains

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

The old Romans had a custom that survived into my own lifetime: they would open a letter with the words, "If you are well, it is well; I also am well." We would do better to write: "If you are studying philosophy, it is well." Because that is what being well actually means. Without philosophy the mind is sick — and the body, however powerful, is strong only the way a madman's is strong. So this is the health to cultivate first; the other kind comes second, and it will cost you little effort — if all you want is to be physically fit. For it is foolish, my dear Lucilius, and thoroughly unbecoming in an educated man, to spend your days building muscle, broadening your shoulders, and expanding your lungs. Even if the heavy feeding pays off and your bulk piles up, you will never match a first-class bull for strength or for weight. Meanwhile the load of food smothers the soul and slows it down. So ration the flesh as much as you can, and give the spirit free play. Devote yourself to that regimen and the inconveniences pile up. First come the workouts, where the effort drains your vital energy and leaves it unfit for the strain of serious study. Then heavy eating dulls the mind's edge. On top of that, you take orders from slaves of the worst stamp — men who divide their day between the oil-flask and the wine-jug, who count the day a success if they've worked up a good sweat and then poured in, to replace what they lost, huge drinks that sink in all the deeper on an empty stomach. Drinking and sweating — it's the life of a heartburn patient!

There are short, simple exercises that tire the body quickly and so save time — and time is something we ought to keep strict account of. Running; swinging weights; and jumping, high or long, or the kind I might politely call "the Priest's dance," or, less politely, "the laundryman's stomp." Pick any one of them and you'll find it plain and easy. But whatever you do, come back quickly from body to mind. The mind is the thing to exercise day and night: it feeds on moderate labor, and this workout is stopped by no cold weather, no hot weather, not even old age. Cultivate the good that improves with the years. I'm not ordering you to hang forever over your books and your writing tablets; the mind needs a break — but the kind of break that loosens it without unstringing it. Riding in a litter jostles the body and doesn't interrupt study: you can read, dictate, talk, or listen. Walking doesn't prevent any of these either.

Nor should you sneer at voice training — though I forbid you the drills, running your voice up and down through scales and set intonations. What next — lessons in walking? Let in the sort of coach that hunger has taught new tricks and you'll have someone regulating your stride, watching every mouthful you eat, and going exactly as far as your patience and your faith in him encourage his nerve to go. "So what, then?" you'll ask. "Is my voice supposed to start out shouting, straining my lungs to the limit?" No. The natural thing is for it to build by easy stages — even people quarreling start in ordinary conversational tones and work up to screaming at the top of their lungs. No speaker opens with "Citizens, help me!" So whenever your spirit prompts you, raise a racket, now louder, now softer, as your voice and your spirit suggest. Then, when you rein it in and call it back down, let it come down gently, not collapse; let it trail off in a middle register, not break off in the raw rustic way, straight down from its ranting. The point isn't for us to give the voice exercise; it's for the voice to give us exercise.

There — I've relieved you of no small nuisance. And I'll throw in one small bonus gift — Greek, this time. Here's the proverb, and it's an excellent one: "The fool's life is empty of gratitude and full of fears: its course lies wholly toward the future." "Who said that?" you ask. The same man as before. And what life do you suppose he means by the fool's life? That of Baba and Isio, the famous idiots? No — he means our own. Blind desire hurls us into ventures that will hurt us and will certainly never satisfy us; if anything could satisfy us, it would have done so long ago. We never stop to think how pleasant it is to ask for nothing, how noble it is to be content and not depend on Fortune. So keep reminding yourself, Lucilius, how many ambitions you have already attained. When you see how many people are ahead of you, count how many are behind. If you want to thank the gods and be grateful for your past life, contemplate how many men you have outrun. But what do the others have to do with it? You have outrun yourself.

Set a limit you wouldn't even want to pass, if you had the power. And then away with all these treacherous goods! They look better to the people hoping for them than to the people who have them. If there were anything solid in them, they would sooner or later fill you up; as it is, they only make the drinker thirstier. Away with the showpieces! As for what the future's uncertain lot has in store — why should I beg Fortune to give, rather than command myself not to crave? And why should I crave? Should I pile up winnings and forget how flimsy a man's lot is? What would I be toiling for? Look: today is the last day — and if not the last, then near it.

Farewell.

Letter 16: On Philosophy, the Guide of Life

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

It's clear to you by now, Lucilius, I'm sure, that no one can live a happy life — or even a bearable one — without the study of wisdom. You know, too, that the happy life arrives when our wisdom is brought to completion, but that life is at least endurable the moment wisdom is begun. Clear as this is, though, it has to be strengthened and driven deeper by daily reflection. Keeping the resolutions you've already made matters more than making noble new ones. You must persevere, adding strength by continuous study, until a good inclination becomes a good settled purpose. So you can spare me the long speeches and protestations: I know you've made great progress. I understand the feelings behind your words; they aren't faked or dressed up. Still, I'll tell you frankly what I think: I have hopes for you, but not yet full confidence. And I want you to take the same attitude toward yourself — there's no reason to trust yourself too quickly and easily. Examine yourself; scrutinize yourself; watch yourself from different angles. And check, before anything else, whether the progress you've made is in philosophy or merely in life. Philosophy is not a trick for the crowd; it isn't built for show. It's a matter of facts, not words. It isn't taken up so the day will pass amusingly, or so leisure won't bore us. It molds and builds the soul; it orders our life, guides our conduct, shows us what to do and what to leave undone; it sits at the helm and holds the course while we waver in the swells. Without it, no one can live fearless, no one can live at peace. Every hour, countless things happen that call for advice — and philosophy is where that advice must be sought.

Someone may say: "What good is philosophy to me if Fate exists? What good, if God rules the universe? What good, if Chance governs everything? What's already determined can't be changed, and what's undetermined can't be planned for: either God has forestalled my plans and decided what I'm to do, or Fortune leaves my plans no room to play." Whether the truth, Lucilius, lies in one of these views or in all of them, we must be philosophers. Whether Fate binds us with an unbreakable law, or God as arbiter of the universe has arranged everything, or Chance kicks human affairs around without method, philosophy must be our defense. She will encourage us to obey God cheerfully and Fortune defiantly; she will teach us to follow God and to endure Chance. But this isn't the moment to be pulled into the debate over what's in our own control — whether foreknowledge rules everything, or a chain of fated events drags us along in its grip, or the sudden and unexpected lord it over us like tyrants. I come back to my warning and my plea: don't let the impulse of your spirit weaken and grow cold. Hold onto it, and plant it firm, so that what is now an impulse becomes a habit of the mind.

If I know you at all, you've been scanning this letter from the first line to find out what little contribution it carries. Sift it and you'll find it. And don't marvel at any genius of mine — so far I'm lavish only with other men's property. Though why do I say "other men's"? Whatever anyone has said well is mine. This, too, is a saying of Epicurus: "If you live according to nature, you will never be poor; if you live according to opinion, you will never be rich." Nature's wants are slight; opinion's demands are bottomless. Suppose the property of many millionaires were heaped in your lap. Suppose fortune carried you far beyond any private income — decked you with gold, dressed you in purple, and brought you to such a pitch of luxury and wealth that you could bury the earth under your marble floors: that you didn't merely own riches, but walked on them. Add statues, paintings, and whatever any art has devised to feed luxury. All these things will teach you is to crave still bigger ones.

Natural desires have a limit; desires born of false opinion have no stopping point — the false has no boundary. When you travel a road, there's an end somewhere; wander off it, and you can stray forever. So call your steps back from empty things, and when you want to know whether what you're chasing rests on natural desire or on a blind one, test whether it can stop anywhere. If you've gone far and there is always a farther goal in view, you can be sure the thing is contrary to nature.

Farewell.

Letters 17–19

Letter 17: On Philosophy and Riches

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Toss all of that aside if you're wise — no, toss it aside so that you can be wise. Race toward a sound mind at top speed and with everything you've got, and if any tie holds you back, loosen it or cut it. "But," you say, "my estate is holding me up. I want to arrange things so that it can support me when I've retired from work — so that poverty won't be a burden to me, and I won't be a burden to anyone else." When you talk like that, you don't seem to know the strength and power of the good you're considering.
