You do grasp the big thing — the great benefit philosophy confers — but you don't yet see its workings up close; you don't yet know how much help it gives us in everything, everywhere; how (to borrow Cicero's phrase) it doesn't just come to our rescue in the greatest matters but reaches down into the smallest. Take my advice: call wisdom in for a consultation. She'll advise you not to sit forever over your ledger. Obviously what you're after, with all this postponing of your studies, is to make sure you'll never have to fear poverty. But what if poverty is something to desire? Riches have blocked many a man's road to wisdom; poverty travels light and carefree. When the trumpet sounds, the poor man knows the attack isn't aimed at him. When someone shouts "Fire!" he looks for the way out, not for what he can carry. If he has to take a sea voyage, the harbor doesn't roar and the docks don't churn with one man's entourage. No mob of slaves surrounds him — slaves with mouths that force their master to covet the harvests of lands across the sea. It's easy to fill a few stomachs when they're well trained and want nothing except to be filled. Hunger is cheap; a picky appetite is expensive. Poverty is content once its pressing needs are met.

Why, then, should you refuse Philosophy as a companion? Even the rich man imitates her ways when he's in his right mind. If you want leisure for your mind, either be poor or live like a poor man. Study does no good unless you take the trouble to live simply — and living simply is voluntary poverty. So away with all the excuses: "I don't have enough yet; once I reach the amount I've set, then I'll give myself over to philosophy completely." And yet this very thing you keep postponing and placing behind everything else is what should come first — you should begin with it. "I just want enough to live on," you answer. Fine — but learn while you're earning it. If anything forbids you to live nobly, nothing forbids you to die nobly. There is no reason poverty should call us away from philosophy — no, not even outright want. When we're hurrying after wisdom, we must be willing to endure even hunger. Men have endured hunger in besieged towns, and what did all that endurance buy them except not falling into the conqueror's power? How much greater is what's promised here: lasting freedom, and the assurance that we need fear neither God nor man. Even starving, we must reach that goal. Armies have put up with every kind of want, have lived on roots, have fought off hunger with food too revolting to name — and all of it to win a kingdom, and (more astonishing still) a kingdom that would belong to somebody else. Will any man hesitate to endure poverty, if that's what it takes to free his mind from madness?

So there's no need to pile up riches first. You can make the journey to philosophy without travel money. Is that really the plan? After you've acquired everything else, then you'll want wisdom too — philosophy as life's last requirement, a sort of supplement? No. Be a philosopher now, whether you have anything or not — because if you have anything, how do you know you don't have too much already? — and if you have nothing, then seek understanding first, before you seek anything else. "But I'll lack the necessities of life," you say. In the first place, you can't lack them, because nature demands very little, and the wise man fits his needs to nature. But if the absolute pinch of need arrives, he'll simply take his leave of life and stop being a trouble to himself. And if his means are thin and scanty, he'll make the best of them, without fretting or worrying about anything beyond the bare essentials. He'll give his belly and his shoulders their due, and with a free and happy spirit he'll laugh at the scrambling of rich men and the panic of those chasing wealth, and say: "Why postpone your real life to some far-off date? Are you waiting for interest to come due, for a return on your merchandise, for a place in some rich old man's will — when you can be rich here and now? Wisdom pays out wealth in ready cash, and she pays it to exactly those people she has taught to find wealth unnecessary." But all that applies to other men; you stand nearer the rich class. Move yourself to a different age, and you have too much. And in every age, what is enough remains the same.

I might have closed the letter at this point, if I hadn't gotten you into bad habits. No one greets Parthian royalty without bringing a gift, and I can't say goodbye to you without paying up. So be it — I'll borrow from Epicurus: "For many men, the getting of riches has not been an end of troubles but a change of troubles." I'm not surprised. The fault was never in the wealth; it's in the mind itself. The same thing that made poverty heavy for us makes riches heavy too. It matters little whether you lay a sick man on a wooden bed or a golden one — wherever you move him, he takes his disease along. In just the same way, it matters little whether a diseased mind is set down in riches or in poverty. The sickness follows the man.

Farewell.

Letter 18: On Festivals and Fasting

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

It's December, and the whole city is in a sweat over it. Merrymaking has been given official license. Everything echoes with enormous preparations — as if the Saturnalia were any different from an ordinary business day! The difference has shrunk so close to nothing that I think the man had it right who said: "Once December was a month; now it's a year."

If you were here with me, I'd love to talk it over and hear what you think we should do — keep our daily routine unchanged, or, so as not to seem out of step with everyone around us, dine more festively and shed the toga. As things stand, we Romans now change our dress for pleasure and holiday-making, though in the old days that happened only when the state was in trouble and the times were dark. If I know you, you'd have played the umpire and ruled that we should be neither exactly like the holiday crowd in their liberty-caps nor entirely unlike them — unless, that is, this is the very season when the soul should be given its orders: told to stand alone in abstaining from pleasure at the precise moment the whole mob has thrown itself into pleasure. Because that is the surest proof a man can get of his own steadiness — neither seeking out the things that seduce him and lure him toward indulgence, nor being dragged into them. It takes real courage to stay dry and sober when the mob is drunk and vomiting; but it takes greater self-control not to withdraw at all — to do what the crowd does, only differently: neither making yourself conspicuous nor melting into the crowd. You can keep a holiday without going on a binge.

But I'm so determined to test the steadiness of your mind that I'm going to give you a lesson drawn from the teaching of great men: set aside a certain number of days during which you will be content with the scantiest and cheapest fare, with coarse and rough clothes, saying to yourself the whole time: "Is this the condition that I feared?" It's exactly when the soul is free of care that it should be toughening itself in advance for the times of stress; it's while Fortune is being kind that it should be fortifying itself against her violence. In peacetime the soldier runs maneuvers, throws up earthworks with no enemy in sight, and wears himself out with unnecessary labor so that he'll be equal to the necessary kind. If you don't want a man to flinch in the crisis, train him before it comes. That's the path followed by those men who, in their rehearsal of poverty, have come nearly to the point of actual want every month — so that they would never recoil from what they had practiced so often.

Don't imagine I mean "Timon's dinners," or "paupers' huts," or any of the other games bored millionaires play to kill the tedium of their lives. Let the cot be a real cot, the cloak a genuinely coarse one, the bread hard and grimy. Endure it for three or four days at a stretch, sometimes longer, so that it's a real test of yourself and not a hobby. Then, I promise you, my dear Lucilius, you will leap for joy when you're full on a penny's worth of food — and you'll understand that a man's peace of mind does not depend on Fortune. Even when she's angry, she grants enough for our needs.

Not that you should think you're doing anything great. You'll only be doing what thousands of slaves and thousands of poor men do every day of their lives. But you can credit yourself with this much: you'll be doing it freely, and it will be as easy for you to endure it forever as to try it now and then. Let's practice our strokes on the dummy. Let's get on intimate terms with poverty, so that Fortune can never catch us off guard. We'll be rich with that much less worry once we've learned how far poverty is from being a burden.

Even Epicurus, the teacher of pleasure, kept certain set days on which he fed his hunger grudgingly — he wanted to see whether that left him short of full and complete happiness, and if so, by how much, and whether the difference was worth buying at the price of great effort. At least, that's what he says in the famous letter to Polyaenus, written in the year Charinus held office. He actually brags that he fed himself for less than a penny, while Metrodorus, who hadn't come as far yet, needed a whole one. Do you think a man can be full on fare like that? Yes — and he can have pleasure too. Not that shifty, fleeting pleasure that needs a fresh jolt every so often, but a pleasure steady and sure. Water, barley-meal, and a crust of barley bread are not a cheerful diet, but it is the highest kind of pleasure to be able to take pleasure even in that — to have pared your needs down to the little that no unkindness of Fortune can snatch away. Prison rations are more generous than this; men held for execution aren't fed so meanly by the man who is going to execute them. What a great soul it takes, then, to descend of your own free will to a diet that even men under a death sentence have no reason to fear! That is forestalling Fortune's spear-thrusts.

So begin, my dear Lucilius, to follow these men's custom: set apart certain days on which you withdraw from your business and make yourself at home with the scantiest fare. Open an account with poverty.

  Dare, my friend, to scorn the sight of wealth;
  shape yourself to kinship with your God.

For no one is in kinship with God except the man who has scorned wealth. I'm not forbidding you to own it, of course — I want to bring you to the point where you own it fearlessly. And there's only one way to get there: convince yourself that you can live happily without it as well as with it, and always look at riches as if they were about to fly away.

But now it's time to start folding up this letter. "Settle your debts first," you cry. Very well — here's a draft on Epicurus; he'll pay out the sum: "Ungoverned anger begets madness." You can't help knowing how true that is, since you've had not only slaves but enemies. This passion flares up against every kind of person; it springs from love as much as from hate, and it shows itself in the middle of games and jokes no less than in serious business. What matters isn't how big the provocation is, but what kind of mind it lands in. It's the same with fire: what counts isn't the size of the flame but what it falls on. Solid timber has withstood a raging blaze, while dry, combustible stuff nurses the smallest spark into a conflagration. So it is with anger, my dear Lucilius: the outcome of a mighty anger is madness. Avoid anger, then, not merely to escape excess but to keep a healthy mind.

Farewell.

Letter 19: On Worldliness and Retirement

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I leap for joy whenever a letter arrives from you. Your letters fill me with hope — they're no longer mere promises about you, but guarantees. And I beg and pray you: keep on this course. What better thing could I ask of a friend than something asked for his own sake? If you can, pull yourself out of all the business you describe; if you can't, tear yourself out. We have squandered enough of our time already — let's begin, in old age, to pack our bags. Surely no one can begrudge us that. We have spent our lives on the high seas; let us die in harbor. Not that I'd have you make a name for yourself by your retirement; retirement is nothing to parade, and nothing to hide either. Nothing to hide, I say — I won't push you so far toward condemning all mankind as mad that I'd have you dig yourself a hole and vanish into oblivion. Just manage it so that your retirement is never conspicuous, though it should be plain to see. Second point: men whose choices are still open from the start can deliberate about whether they'd like to pass their lives in obscurity; you no longer have a free choice. Your ability and energy have thrust you into the world's work — that, and the charm of your writing, and your friendships with famous and distinguished men. Renown has already taken you by storm. You could sink into the deepest obscurity and bury yourself completely, and your earlier deeds would still point you out. You cannot keep lurking in the dark; wherever you run, much of the old gleam will follow you.

Peace, though, you can claim without anyone resenting you, without any sense of loss, without a single pang. What will you be leaving behind that you can imagine being sorry to leave? Your clients? None of those men courts you for yourself; they court something from you. Once men hunted friends; now they hunt loot. Let a lonely old man change his will, and by morning his caller has moved to another doorstep. Great things don't come cheap — so do the arithmetic: is it better to walk away from your own true self, or merely from some of your property? If only you'd had the luck to grow old in the modest circumstances you were born to, and fortune had never carried you so high! Your quick rise to prosperity swept you far out of sight of wholesome living — the province, the procuratorship, and everything such things promise. Next you'll take on greater duties, and after those, greater still. And where does it end? Why wait until there's nothing left for you to crave? That moment will never come. We Stoics say there's a chain of causes out of which fate is woven; believe me, there's a chain of desires too — each one begins where the last one ends. You have been dropped into a life that will never, on its own, put an end to your misery and your servitude. Pull your chafed neck out of the yoke; better that it be cut through once than galled forever. If you retreat into private life, everything will be on a smaller scale, but you will be satisfied to the full; as it is, plenty is heaped on you from every side, and none of it satisfies. Which would you rather be — poor and full, or rich and hungry? Prosperity is greedy itself, and it lies exposed to the greed of others. And as long as nothing is enough for you, you will never be enough for anyone else.

"But how can I take my leave?" you ask. Any way you can. Think of all the risks you've run for money, all the labor you've put in for a title — you have to dare something for leisure, too. Otherwise you'll grow old in the churn of procuratorships abroad and civil duties at home, in turmoil and ever-fresh floods of responsibility, which no man has ever escaped by keeping his head down or living quietly. What does your own wish for a secluded life have to do with it? Your position in the world wishes the opposite! And what if you let that position grow even greater now? Everything added to your success will be added to your fears. Here I want to quote you Maecenas, who told the truth from the very summit: "There's thunder even on the loftiest peaks." If you ask what book it's from: the one called Prometheus. He meant simply that the high peaks have their tops wrapped in thunderstorms. But is any power worth a price so high that you — a man like you — would ever adopt a style as debauched as his to get it? Maecenas was a gifted man, and he would have left a great model for Roman oratory to follow if success hadn't gone soft on him — hadn't, in fact, gutted the man in him. That end is waiting for you too, unless you shorten sail right now and, as Maecenas wouldn't do until it was too late, hug the shore.

That saying of Maecenas's might have squared my account with you — but knowing you, you'll file an injunction against me and refuse to accept payment of my debt in such crude, debased coin. However that stands, I'll draw on the account of Epicurus. He says: "Before you consider what you're going to eat and drink, consider whom you're going to eat and drink with — for a feast of meat without a friend is the life of a lion or a wolf." You won't have that privilege unless you withdraw from the world; until then, your dinner guests will be whoever your secretary sorts out of the crowd of morning callers. But it's a mistake to pick out a friend in the reception hall, or to test him at the dinner table: a friend at your table is better made than found there. The worst misfortune of a busy man buried in his possessions is this — he believes men are his friends when he is no friend to them, and he assumes his favors are winning people over, when in fact, with certain men, the more they owe, the more they hate. A small loan makes a man your debtor; a large one makes him your enemy. "What," you say, "don't kindnesses create friendships?" They do — if you've had the freedom to choose who receives them, and if they're placed with judgment instead of scattered broadcast.

So while you're beginning to call your mind your own, apply in the meantime this maxim of the wise: what matters is not what a man receives, but who it is that receives it.

Farewell.

Letters 20–22

Letter 20: On Practising What You Preach

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

If you're healthy — and if you think yourself worthy of finally becoming your own master — I'm glad. The credit will be mine, if I can pull you out of the flood you've been thrashing in with no hope of reaching shore. But here is what I ask of you, my dear Lucilius — what I beg of you: let wisdom sink all the way into your soul, and measure your progress not by what you say or write, but by the steadiness of your heart and the shrinking of your desires. Prove your words by your deeds.

The speechmakers chasing the approval of a packed hall have one aim; the men who charm the ears of the young and the idle with clever, fluent argument have another. Philosophy teaches us to act, not to speak. It demands that every man live by his own standard — that his life not clash with his words, and that his inner life be all one color, in harmony with everything he does. This is the highest duty of wisdom, and its highest proof: that deed and word agree, that a man be equal to himself in every situation — always the one same self.

"But who can hold that standard?" you ask. Very few, granted; but some can. I won't pretend it's easy, and I don't say the philosopher can always keep the same pace — only that he can always keep the same road. So observe yourself. See whether your clothes and your house disagree with each other; whether you're lavish with yourself and stingy with your household; whether you eat frugal dinners and build luxurious houses. Settle, once and for all, on a single rule to live by, and square your whole life to it. Some people pinch pennies at home and strut in public; that kind of discord is a flaw — the mark of a wobbling mind that can't yet hold its balance. And I can tell you where the wobble comes from, this mismatch between actions and intentions: no one decides what he actually wants, and even if he does decide, he doesn't stay decided — he jumps the track. And he doesn't just change; he circles back and slides into the very conduct he'd renounced and sworn off. So let me skip the ancient definitions of wisdom and sum up the whole business of human life in one: What is wisdom? Always desiring the same things, and always refusing the same things. You can even skip the fine print — that what you desire should be right — because nothing but the right thing can satisfy a person the same way every day.

That's why people don't know what they want except in the moment of wanting it. No one ever decides, once and for all, what to desire and what to refuse. Judgment shifts from day to day, flips to its opposite, and turns many a man's life into a kind of game. So press on as you've begun. Perhaps you'll be led all the way to perfection — or at least to a point that you alone will recognize as still short of it.

"But what becomes of my crowded household," you say, "without a household income?" Once you stop feeding that crowd, it will feed itself. Or else poverty will teach you, as a favor, what your own favors never could. Poverty keeps your true and tested friends for you; it clears out everyone who came not for you but for what you have. Shouldn't you love poverty for this one service alone — that it shows you who really loves you? When will the day come when no one lies to you by way of compliment? So point your thoughts, your efforts, your desires at this: being content with yourself and with the goods that grow out of yourself. Every other prayer, leave in God's keeping. What happiness could be nearer at hand? Bring yourself down to a modest level, one you cannot be thrown down from — and to help you do it more willingly, this letter's little wage will speak to exactly that subject. I'll pay it right now.

Glare at me if you like, but Epicurus will once again be glad to settle my debt: "Believe me, your words will carry more weight if you sleep on a cot and wear rags. Then you won't just be saying them; you'll be proving them." I certainly listen in a different spirit to our friend Demetrius, now that I've seen him sleeping without so much as a cloak over him — without even a mat beneath him. He is not just a teacher of the truth; he is a witness to it. "But can't a man despise wealth while it's sitting right in his pocket?" Of course he can. He too is a great soul, the man who sees riches heaped around him, wonders long and deeply that they've come to him, and smiles — and hears rather than feels that they're his. It's no small thing not to be spoiled by living with wealth; the truly great man is the one who is poor in the midst of riches. "Yes, but I don't know," you say, "how that man will endure poverty if he falls into it suddenly." Nor do I know, Epicurus, whether your poor man will despise riches if he suddenly falls into those. So in both cases it's the mind that must be appraised: we have to ask whether your man is pleased with his poverty, and whether my man is displeased with his riches. Otherwise the cot and the rags prove very little about his good intentions — unless it's clear he endures them by preference, not necessity.

The mark of a noble spirit, though, is not to fling yourself into such things because they're better, but to rehearse them because they're easy to endure. And they are easy, Lucilius; come to them after long rehearsal, and they're even pleasant, because they carry a sense of freedom from worry — and without that, nothing is pleasant. So I hold it essential to do what I told you in an earlier letter great men have often done: set aside a few days to prepare for real poverty by practicing pretend poverty. We need this all the more because we've soaked so long in luxury that every duty looks hard and heavy. Better to shake the soul out of its sleep, prod it, and remind it how little nature has actually prescribed for us. No man is born rich. Every one of us, on first seeing daylight, is ordered to be content with milk and a scrap of cloth. That is how we start out — and then whole kingdoms are too small for us.

Farewell.

Letter 21: On the Renown Which My Writings Will Bring You

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Do you really conclude that your trouble is with those men you wrote to me about? Your greatest trouble is with yourself: you are your own stumbling block. You don't know what you want. You're better at approving the right course than at following it. You see where true happiness lies, but you don't have the courage to go get it. Since you can't seem to see what's holding you back, let me tell you.

You think the life you're supposed to leave behind is an important one; and just as you've resolved on that calm state you hope to pass into, the glitter of your present life holds you back — as if leaving it meant stepping down into filth and darkness. That's a mistake, Lucilius. Going from your life into the other one is a promotion. The difference between the two is the difference between shine and light. Light has a definite source of its own inside it; shine is borrowed. Shine gleams by someone else's illumination — and anyone who steps between the source and the object throws the object into dense shadow. Light glows from within.

It's your own studies that will make you shine — that will make you eminent. Let me give you Epicurus's example. He was writing to Idomeneus, trying to call him back from a showy career to a sure and steadfast kind of fame. Idomeneus at the time was a minister of state, a man wielding real authority with important affairs in hand. "If it's fame that attracts you," Epicurus told him, "my letters will make you more renowned than all the things you cherish — all the things that make you cherished." Was he wrong? Who would ever have heard of Idomeneus if the philosopher hadn't engraved his name into those letters? All the grandees and satraps — even the king Idomeneus petitioned for his title — are sunk in deep oblivion. Cicero's letters are what keep the name of Atticus from perishing. It would have done Atticus no good to have Agrippa for a son-in-law, Tiberius for his granddaughter's husband, Drusus Caesar for a great-grandson. Among names that mighty, his would never be spoken at all if Cicero hadn't bound Atticus to himself. The deep flood of time will roll over us. A few great men will lift their heads above it, and though they too must sink at last into the same silence, they will battle oblivion and hold their ground a long while.

What Epicurus could promise his friend, I promise you, Lucilius. I will find favor with later generations; I can take names with me that will endure as long as my own. Our poet Vergil promised two heroes an everlasting name, and he is keeping his promise:

  Fortunate pair! If my songs have any power,
  no day will ever erase you from the memory of time —
  not while Aeneas' line still keeps the Capitol,
  that rock immovable, and a Roman father holds the empire.

Whenever men are pushed forward by fortune — whenever they've become part and parcel of someone else's influence — they enjoy abundant favor and a crowded doorstep exactly as long as they keep their position. The moment they leave it, they slip out of everyone's memory. But the respect paid to native genius keeps growing; and the honor doesn't stop with the man himself — it attaches to whatever his memory touches, and gets passed along from hand to hand.

Now, Idomeneus shouldn't get into my letter free of charge, so he can pay the debt from his own account. It was to him that Epicurus addressed the famous saying urging him to make Pythocles rich — but not rich in the vulgar, double-edged way. "If you wish to make Pythocles rich," he said, "do not add to his money; subtract from his desires." The idea is too clear to need explanation and too well put to need reinforcement. I'll add only one warning: don't assume it applies to riches alone. Apply it anywhere you like; it holds the same value. "If you wish to make Pythocles honorable, do not add to his honors; subtract from his desires." "If you wish Pythocles to have pleasure that lasts, do not add to his pleasures; subtract from his desires." "If you wish to make Pythocles an old man whose life is filled to the full, do not add to his years; subtract from his desires." There's no reason to treat these words as Epicurus's private property; they are public property. I think we should do in philosophy what they do in the Senate: when someone makes a motion I partly approve of, I ask him to divide the motion, and I vote for the part I like. So I'm all the more glad to repeat Epicurus's distinguished words — because I want to prove something to the people who run to him with bad motives, thinking they'll find in him a screen for their own vices: whatever school you follow, you must live honorably.

Go to his Garden and read the motto carved there: "Stranger, here you will do well to linger; here our highest good is pleasure." The caretaker of that place, a kindly host, will be ready for you. He'll welcome you with barley meal and pour you water in abundance, and ask: "Haven't you been well entertained?" "This garden," he says, "does not whet your appetite; it quenches it. It doesn't make you thirstier with every drink; it slakes thirst with a natural cure — a cure that charges no fee. This is the 'pleasure' I have grown old in."

With you, though, I'm speaking of the desires that refuse all soothing — the ones that must be bribed to stop. Because as for the exceptional desires — the kind that can be postponed, disciplined, checked — I have just one thought to share: pleasure of that sort is in line with our nature, but not with our needs. You owe it nothing; whatever you spend on it is a free gift. The belly won't listen to advice: it demands, it pesters. Yet it isn't an expensive creditor. You can send it away at small cost — as long as you give it what you owe, not everything you're able to give.

Farewell.

Letter 22: On the Futility of Half-way Measures

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

By now you understand that you must pull yourself out of these showy and corrupting pursuits; what you still want to know is how. Some things can only be pointed out by someone on the spot. A doctor can't prescribe by letter the right hour for eating or bathing; he has to feel the pulse. There's an old saying about gladiators: they plan the fight in the ring. Something in the opponent's glance, some movement of his hand, even a slight lean of his body — the watchful eye reads the warning there. What is usually done, or ought to be done — that we can put into general rules and write down; advice like that can be given not only to absent friends but to future generations. But the second question — when to make your move, and how — no one can settle at long range. You must take counsel with the situation itself, on the spot. And you must be not only present in body but alert in mind if you're going to catch the opportunity as it flies. So watch for it; and when you see it, seize it, and throw everything — all your energy, all your strength — into this one task: getting yourself free of those business duties.

Now listen carefully to the opinion I'm about to give you. It is this: withdraw from that kind of existence, or withdraw from existence altogether. But I also say: take the gentle path. Loosen the knot you've tied so badly rather than cut it — with this proviso, that if there turns out to be no other way to loosen it, you cut it after all. No man is such a coward that he'd rather hang in suspense forever than fall once and be done with it. In the meantime — and this comes first — don't tie yourself up any further. Be content with the business you've already lowered yourself into — or, as you'd rather have people think, tumbled into. Don't keep struggling on toward something bigger; if you do, you'll lose your whole excuse, and everyone will see it was no tumble. The usual defense men offer is a lie: "I was compelled to do it. Suppose it was against my will — I had to." No one is compelled to chase prosperity at a dead run. Even if you don't fight the current, it counts for something just to call a halt — to stand still instead of pressing eagerly after Fortune's favors.

And will you be put out with me if I don't just come to advise you myself, but call in others — wiser heads than mine, the men I lay every problem before when I'm pondering one? Read Epicurus's letter on this very question; it's addressed to Idomeneus. He begs him to escape as fast as he can, and beat his retreat before some stronger influence steps in and takes away his freedom to withdraw. But he adds: attempt nothing except at the moment when it can be attempted suitably and in season. Then, when the long-sought occasion comes — up and out. He forbids so much as a doze in anyone who is plotting escape; and he holds out hope of a safe release from even the hardest positions, provided we're neither too hasty before the time nor too sluggish when the time arrives.

Now, I suppose, you want the Stoic motto too. Nobody has any business slandering our school to you for recklessness; the truth is, its caution is greater than its courage. Perhaps you're expecting it to say something like this: "It is shameful to flinch under a burden. Wrestle with the duties you have once taken on. No man is brave and earnest if he runs from danger, if his spirit doesn't grow with the very difficulty of his task." Words like those will indeed be said to you — if your perseverance has an object that's worth the price, if you won't have to do or endure anything unworthy of a good man. Besides, a good man doesn't waste himself on shabby, discreditable work, or stay busy merely for the sake of being busy. Nor will he do what you imagine — get so tangled in ambitious schemes that he has to endure their ebb and flow forever. No: when he sees the dangers, the uncertainties, the hazards he used to be tossed around in, he will withdraw — not turning his back to the enemy, but falling back step by step to a safe position. And business, my dear Lucilius, is easy to escape, if only you will despise its rewards. What holds us back, what keeps us from escaping, is thinking like this: "What? Walk away from such great prospects? Leave right at harvest time? No slaves at my side? No retinue for my litter? No crowd in my reception hall?"

So men leave these advantages reluctantly; they love the reward of their hardships and curse the hardships themselves. They complain about their ambitions the way they complain about their mistresses — which is to say, get at their real feelings, and you'll find not hatred but a lovers' quarrel. Examine the minds of those who cry down what they craved, who talk about escaping the things they can't do without, and you'll see it: they are lingering by their own free will in a life they declare hard and wretched to endure. It is so, my dear Lucilius — slavery holds a few men fast, but many more hold fast to their slavery.

But if you intend to be rid of that slavery, if freedom is genuinely pleasing in your eyes, and if you're asking for advice for this one purpose — to accomplish it without perpetual torment — then how could the whole company of Stoics fail to approve? Zeno, Chrysippus, and all their kind will give you counsel that's temperate, honorable, and fitted to your case. But if you keep twisting around to look behind you — how much can I carry away with me? how much money will I need to outfit my life of leisure? — you'll never find the way out. No man can swim ashore and take his baggage with him. Rise to the higher life, and may the gods favor you — but not the way they favor certain men when, with kind and smiling faces, they hand out magnificent evils, excused by the single fact that these things which burn and torture were granted in answer to prayer.

I was already putting the seal on this letter, but it has to be broken again, so it can come to you with its customary contribution and carry some noble saying along with it. And here is one that comes to mind — I honestly can't tell you whether its truth or its fine expression is the greater. "Said by whom?" you ask. By Epicurus — I am still appropriating other men's property. "Everyone goes out of life just as if he had only just entered it." Take anyone off guard — young, old, middle-aged — and you'll find them all equally afraid of death and equally ignorant of life. No one has anything finished, because we keep pushing everything we undertake into the future. Nothing in that saying pleases me more than the way it taunts old men with being infants. "No one," he says, "leaves the world any differently than he came into it." False! We are worse when we die than when we were born. And the fault is ours, not Nature's. Nature has a complaint to file against us: "What is this? I brought you into the world without desires, without fears, free of superstition, treachery, and the rest of the curses. Go out the way you came in!"

A man has caught the message of wisdom if he can die as free from care as he was at birth. But as it is, we all tremble when the dreaded moment approaches: courage fails, cheeks go white, useless tears fall. And what is more shameful than fretting at the very threshold of peace? The reason is that we arrive stripped of all our goods: we have jettisoned life's whole cargo and we're in distress, because none of it was stowed in the hold — all of it was heaved overboard along the way and has drifted off. Men care nothing about living nobly, only about living long — although living nobly is within everyone's reach, and living long is within no one's power.

Farewell.

Letters 23–25

Letter 23: On the True Joy Which Comes from Philosophy

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Do you expect me to write you about how gently winter treated us — a short season, and a mild one — or what a nasty spring we're having, with cold weather out of season, and all the other small talk people fall back on when they can't think of anything to say? No. I'll write you something that might actually help us both. And what could that be, if not a push toward soundness of mind? You ask what the foundation of a sound mind is? This: do not find your joy in worthless things. Foundation, did I say? It's the summit. You have reached the top when you know what it is that makes you glad, and when you have not handed your happiness over to things outside yourself. A man who is tugged forward by hope of anything — even something within reach, even something easy to get, even if his hopes have never once cheated him — is anxious and unsure of himself. Above all, my dear Lucilius, make this your business: learn how to feel joy.

Do you think I'm robbing you of pleasures when I clear away Fortune's gifts, when I advise you to steer wide of hope, the sweetest thing that warms our hearts? Just the opposite: I don't want you ever to be without gladness. I want it born in your own house — and it is born there, if only it is born inside you. Every other kind of cheer fails to fill the heart; it only smooths the forehead, and it never lasts. Unless, of course, you think a man has joy just because he's laughing. The soul itself must be happy and confident, lifted above everything that can happen to it.

Real joy, believe me, is a serious business. Do you imagine anyone can look death in the face with a careless expression — "bright and breezy," as our young dandies like to put it? Or throw the door open to poverty that way, or keep the reins on his pleasures, or rehearse the endurance of pain? The man who turns these things over in his heart is full of joy — but not a giggling joy. That is exactly the joy I want you to own, because once you have found its source, it will never run dry. Shallow mines give up their yield right at the surface; the truly rich ones hide their veins deep, and they pay out more and more to the miner who keeps digging. The trinkets that delight the crowd give only a thin pleasure, a coat of shine — and any joy that is merely plated has nothing underneath. The joy I'm talking about, the joy I'm trying to lead you to, is solid all the way through, and it opens up wider the deeper you go into it. So do the one thing, dearest Lucilius, that can make you truly happy: throw away and trample underfoot everything that merely glitters, everything dangled in front of you by someone else or obtainable from someone else. Look toward the true good, and take joy only in what comes from your own store. And what do I mean by "your own store"? I mean your very self — the best part of you. Even the frail body, though we can do nothing without it, should be treated as necessary rather than important. It drags us into empty pleasures — brief, soon regretted, and, unless they are reined in by strict self-control, ready to flip into their opposites. I mean exactly this: pleasure, unless it is kept within bounds, goes plunging straight over the edge into sorrow.

But keeping within bounds is hard with anything you believe to be good. Only the real good can be craved safely. What is this real good, you ask, and where does it come from? I'll tell you: it comes from a good conscience, from honorable intentions, from right actions, from contempt for Fortune's gifts, from a calm and even way of living that walks a single road. People who leap from one plan to another — or who don't even leap, but get carried across by sheer accident — how can such wavering, drifting people hold on to any good that is fixed and lasting? Only a few steer themselves and their affairs by a guiding purpose. The rest don't move forward at all; they are simply swept along, like debris floating in a river. Some are caught by slack water and carried gently; some are torn along by a stronger current; some are stranded near the bank as the flow gives out; and some are flushed out to sea by the rush of the stream. So: decide what you want, and stand by the decision.

Now it's time to pay my debt. Here is a saying of your friend Epicurus that will clear this letter's account: "It is a nuisance to be always beginning life." Or another, which may catch the meaning better: "They live badly who are always beginning to live." Why? — you're right to ask; the line needs a commentary. Because for such people life is always unfinished. A man cannot stand ready for death if he has only just started to live. We should aim to have already lived enough — and no one thinks he has, if he's still drawing up the blueprints for his life. Don't imagine such people are rare; practically everyone is like this. Some men, in fact, only begin to live when it's time to stop. And if that surprises you, here is something that will surprise you more: some men have stopped living before they ever began.

Farewell.

Letter 24: On Despising Death

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You write that you're anxious about the outcome of a lawsuit an angry opponent is holding over you, and you expect me to advise you to picture a happier ending and rest in the comforts of hope. After all, why summon trouble early — it must be endured soon enough once it arrives — why ruin the present out of fear of the future? It is certainly foolish to be miserable now because you may be miserable later. But I'm going to lead you to peace of mind by a different road: if you want to be rid of all worry, assume that whatever you're afraid might happen is certainly going to happen. Whatever the trouble is, take its measure in your own mind and size up your fear. You will soon see that what you dread is either small or short. And you won't need long to gather examples to steady you; every age has produced them. Turn your thoughts to any period of Roman or foreign history, and cases of great achievement and great endurance will come crowding in on you.

If you lose this case, can anything worse happen to you than exile or prison? Is there any fate a man can dread beyond being burned or being killed? Name those penalties one at a time, and then name the men who shrugged them off — you won't have to hunt for them; the only problem is choosing. Rutilius bore his conviction as if the one thing that bothered him was the injustice of the verdict. Metellus endured exile with courage; Rutilius endured it gladly — the first agreed to come back only because his country called him; the second refused to return when Sulla summoned him. And nobody, in those days, said no to Sulla. Socrates went on holding his conversations in prison, and when certain people offered him the chance to escape, he declined; he stayed, in order to free mankind from its two heaviest fears — death and imprisonment. Mucius put his own hand into the fire. Being burned is painful; how much more painful when you are the one doing it to yourself! Here was a man with no learning, not braced for death and pain by any words of wisdom, equipped with nothing but a soldier's courage, punishing himself for the failure of his daring. He stood and watched his own right hand coming apart on the enemy's brazier, and he did not pull back the dissolving limb, its bones laid bare, until his enemy took the fire away. He could have done something more successful in that camp; he could never have done anything braver. See how much keener a brave man is to seize danger than a cruel man is to inflict it: Porsenna was readier to pardon Mucius for trying to kill him than Mucius was to pardon himself for failing.

"Oh," you say, "those stories have been recited to death in every school. The minute you reach the topic 'On Despising Death,' you'll be telling me about Cato." And why shouldn't I tell you about Cato — how on that last glorious night he read Plato's book with a sword laid at his pillow? He had arranged for the two things he needed at the end: the first, the will to die; the second, the means. So he put his affairs in order — as far as anything ruined and near its end can be put in order — and decided he had to make sure that no one would have the power to kill Cato, and no one the luck to save him. Drawing the sword — which he had kept clean of blood until that final day — he cried: "Fortune, you have gained nothing by blocking everything I attempted. Until now I have fought for my country's freedom, not my own. I did not labor so stubbornly to be free myself, but to live among free men. Now, since the cause of mankind is past hope, let Cato be moved to safety." Then he dealt his body the fatal wound. The doctors bound it up; Cato had less blood and less strength, but no less courage. Angry now not only at Caesar but at himself, he set his bare hands against the wound — and that noble soul, which had defied every power on earth, he did not so much release as expel.

I am not piling up these stories to exercise my wit; I'm doing it to brace you against the thing that is thought most terrible. And the job will be all the easier when I show you that it isn't only heroes who have despised the moment when the soul breathes its last; some men who were cowards in everything else have matched the bravest here. Take Scipio, the father-in-law of Gnaeus Pompeius: driven back onto the African coast by a headwind, he saw his ship in enemy hands. He ran himself through with his sword, and when they asked where the commander was, he answered: "All is well with the commander." Those words lifted him to the level of his ancestors, and kept unbroken the glory that fate had granted the Scipios in Africa. It was a great thing to conquer Carthage; a greater thing to conquer death. "All is well with the commander!" Should a general die any other way — above all, one of Cato's generals? I won't send you back through history, collecting all the men across the ages who have despised death; there are too many. Look at our own times — the softness and over-refinement we're always complaining about — and you will still find people of every rank, every station, every age, who cut their misfortunes short by death.

Believe me, Lucilius: death is so far from being fearful that, thanks to it, nothing is fearful. So when your enemy threatens, listen calmly. Your conscience gives you confidence — but since many things outside the merits of a case carry weight, hope for perfect justice, and prepare yourself for perfect injustice. Above all, remember to strip things of the noise and confusion that surround them, and look at what each thing is at bottom. You will find that there is nothing frightening in them except the fear itself. What you see happening with little boys happens to us too — we are only slightly bigger boys. When the people they love, the people they live with and play with, put on masks, the boys are scared out of their wits. It's the mask we need to strip off — not just from people, but from things — and give each thing back its own face.

"Why do you hold up before my eyes swords and fires and a mob of executioners roaring around you? Drop the parade you hide behind to frighten fools! You are nothing but Death — the same Death that a manservant of mine and a maidservant despised only yesterday. Why do you spread out the whips and the rack again in all that grand display? Why the engines built to torture each joint of the body one by one, and the thousand other machines for taking a man apart piecemeal? Put away the stage props that numb us with terror. And you — silence the groans, the cries, the bitter shrieks torn out of a man as he's stretched on the rack! You are nothing but Pain — the pain that gout-ridden man over there scorns, that the invalid endures in the middle of his dinner parties, that a girl bears in childbirth. If I can bear you, you are slight; if I cannot bear you, you are short."

Turn these words over — you have heard them often and said them often. But now prove by the outcome that what you have heard and said is true. For there is an ugly charge that keeps getting leveled at our school: that we deal in the words of philosophy, not its deeds.

What — have you only just now learned that death hangs over your head, that exile does, that grief does? You were born to these risks. Let us treat everything that can happen as something that will happen. I know you have already done what I'm advising; now I add a warning: don't drown your soul in these petty anxieties of yours. If you do, it will go dull, and it will have too little strength left when the moment comes for it to rise. Turn your mind from your own case to the case of men in general. Tell yourself that our little bodies are mortal and fragile; pain can reach them from other directions than an enemy's wrong or a stronger man's power. Our pleasures themselves turn into torments: banquets bring indigestion; drinking bouts bring numbness and trembling; the habits of lust cripple the feet, the hands, every joint of the body.

I may become poor: then I'll be one among many. I may be exiled: then I'll count myself a native of wherever they send me. They may put me in chains. So what? Am I unchained now? Look at this dragging weight of a body that nature has shackled me to. "I shall die," you say. What you mean is: I shall stop being able to fall sick; I shall stop being able to be imprisoned; I shall stop being able to die. I'm not foolish enough to run through the old Epicurean refrain at this point — that the terrors of the underworld are empty, that Ixion is not spinning on his wheel, that Sisyphus is not shouldering his boulder uphill, that no man's entrails can grow back every day to be devoured again. Nobody is childish enough to fear Cerberus, or the shadows, or the ghost-costume of the dead, held together by nothing but bare bones. Death either annihilates us or strips us bare. If we are released, the better part of us remains, its burden lifted; if we are annihilated, nothing remains — good and bad alike are carried off.

Allow me to quote a verse of your own here — first reminding you that when you wrote it, you wrote it for yourself as much as for anyone else. It is shameful to say one thing and mean another; how much more shameful to write one thing and mean another! I remember the day you were handling that familiar theme: that we don't suddenly fall into death, but move toward it by inches. We die every day. For every day a little of our life is taken from us; even while we are growing, our life is shrinking. We lose infancy, then childhood, then youth. Count yesterday too: all the time that has passed is time lost; this very day we're spending, we split with death. It is not the last drop that empties the water-clock, but all the water that flowed out before. In the same way, the final hour, when we cease to exist, does not produce death all by itself; it only completes it. That is the moment we arrive — but we have been on the road a long time. When you had laid all this out, you said, in your usual style (you're always impressive, but never sharper than when you fit the truth with the right words):

  The death that comes for us is not one death:
  the death that takes us off is just the last.

I'd rather you read your own lines than my letter; they will make it plain to you that this death we fear is the last death, not the only one.

I can see what you're looking for: you're asking what I've packed into this letter — what bracing saying from some master mind, what useful rule. Since the subject at hand is the one we've been discussing, I'll send you something on that very theme. Epicurus scolds those who crave death as much as those who dread it: "It is absurd," he says, "to run toward death because you are tired of life, when it is your way of life that has made you run toward death." And in another passage: "What is so absurd as to seek death, when it is fear of death that has robbed your life of peace?" And you may add a third of the same stamp: "So great is men's thoughtlessness — their madness — that some are driven to death by the very fear of it."

Ponder any of these, and you will strengthen your mind to endure both death and life. For we need warning and toughening on both fronts — against loving life too much, and against hating it too much. Even when reason advises ending it, the impulse should not be obeyed rashly or at a run. A brave and wise man should not flee from life; he should make a proper exit. And above all he should avoid the weakness that has taken hold of so many: a lust for dying. For the mind has its unreasoning drifts, my dear Lucilius, and one of them tilts toward death. It seizes the noblest and most spirited men, and it seizes the abject and cowardly too: the first kind despise life; the second find it a burden.

Others slide into it out of sheer over-fullness — from doing and seeing the same things until it isn't that they hate life; they are simply glutted with it. We slip toward this mood with philosophy itself nudging us along, and we say: "How long the same things? I will wake and sleep, get hungry and get full, shiver and sweat. Nothing ends; everything is linked in a kind of circle — things flee and are chased. Night presses at the heels of day, day at the heels of night; summer ends in autumn, winter crowds hard behind autumn, winter softens into spring. Nothing in nature passes except to come back. I do nothing new; I see nothing new. Sooner or later a man gets sick even of this." There are many who feel that living is not painful — merely pointless.

Farewell.

Letter 25: On Reformation

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

As for our two friends, we'll have to proceed along different lines: the faults of the one are to be corrected; the faults of the other, stamped out. I intend to take every liberty — I don't love a man if I'm unwilling to hurt his feelings. "What," you say, "do you expect to keep a forty-year-old under your tutelage? Consider his age — hardened by now, past kneading. You can't reshape a man like that; only young minds take the mold." I don't know whether I'll make progress; but I would rather fall short of success than fall short of faith. Don't give up on curing the sick even when the disease is chronic — provided you stand firm against their excesses and make them do, and put up with, plenty of things against their will. As for our other friend, I'm not especially confident either — except for one thing: he still has enough shame left to blush at his sins. That modesty should be nursed along; as long as it lives in his soul, there is room for hope. But this veteran of yours, I think, wants more careful handling, so that he doesn't give up on himself altogether. And there is no better moment to approach him than now, while he's in a quiet stretch and looks like a man who has mended his ways. That lull of virtue has fooled others; it doesn't fool me. I fully expect his faults to come back — with compound interest. For the moment, I'm certain, they are suspended, not gone. I'll give the matter some time, and see whether or not something can be done.

But you — show me, as in fact you are doing, that you have a stout heart. Lighten your baggage for the march. Nothing we own is essential. Let us return to nature's law, and riches lie ready for us there. The things we actually need are free for everyone, or cheap: nature craves only bread and water. Nobody is poor by that standard — and the man who has fenced his desires within those limits can go head-to-head with Jupiter himself for happiness, as Epicurus says. And I must fold one or two more of his sayings into this letter: "Do everything as if Epicurus were watching you." There's no question that it does you good to have posted a guardian over yourself — to have someone you can look up to, someone you count as a witness to your very thoughts. It is nobler still, of course, to live as if some good man were always at your side, watching everything you do; but I'll settle for this much: whatever you do, act as if anyone at all were looking on. Solitude is what talks us into every kind of wrong. When you have come far enough to have some respect for yourself as well, you may dismiss your attendant. Until then, set some man's authority as a guard over you — the great Cato, or Scipio, or Laelius — anyone whose presence would make even hopeless wrecks swallow their bad impulses — while you go on making yourself into the kind of man in whose company you wouldn't dare to sin. When that is accomplished, and you begin to hold yourself in some esteem, I'll gradually allow you what Epicurus suggests in another passage: "The time to withdraw into yourself is exactly when you are forced into a crowd."

You need to make yourself different from the multitude. But until it is safe for you to be alone, pick out particular individuals; everyone does better in somebody's company — anybody's — than alone in his own. "The time to withdraw into yourself is exactly when you are forced into a crowd." Yes — provided you are a good man, a calm man, a self-restrained one. Otherwise, you had better withdraw into a crowd to get away from yourself. Alone, you are keeping bad company.

Farewell.

Letters 26–29

Letter 26: On Old Age and Death

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Just recently I told you I was within sight of old age. Now I'm afraid I've left old age behind me. Some other word belongs to my years now — or at least to my body — because "old age" means a time of life that is tired, not broken down. Count me among the worn out, the ones nearing the end.

Even so, I give myself thanks, with you as my witness. Age has done no damage to my mind, though I feel it in my body. Only my vices have grown senile, along with the organs that served them. My mind is strong, and delighted to have so little business with the body anymore. It has set down most of its load. It is alert. It even picks an argument with me about old age — insists that old age is its season of bloom. Let's take it at its word and let it make the most of what it has. It tells me to do some thinking: to sort out how much of this peace and moderation I owe to wisdom and how much simply to my age; to examine carefully what I can no longer do and what I no longer want to do. And if a power fails that was bound to end anyway — why complain? Why call that a loss?

"But," you say, "it is the worst loss there is — to wear out, to die off, or to put it bluntly, to melt away. We aren't cut down with a single blow; we're worn down, and every day shaves off a little more of our strength."

But is there any better ending than to glide into your own harbor while nature slips the cable? Not that there's anything painful about a sudden blow and an abrupt exit from existence — only that this other way out is gentle: a gradual withdrawal. As for me, I act as if the test were at hand — as if the day had come that will pronounce its verdict on all my years — and I watch over myself and talk to myself like this: "Everything we've shown so far, in word or deed, counts for nothing. It's all a small, deceptive down payment on our spirit, wrapped in a great deal of showmanship. I'll let death decide what progress I've really made. So I am preparing myself for that day, and not faintheartedly — the day when the stage tricks and the actor's makeup come off and I pass judgment on myself: am I merely reciting brave sentiments, or do I actually feel them? Were all those bold threats I hurled at Fortune a pretense and a performance? Set aside the world's opinion; it wavers forever and always votes both ways. Set aside the studies you've pursued all your life; death will deliver the final verdict in your case. I mean this: your debates and learned discussions, your maxims collected from the sayings of the wise, your cultured conversation — none of it proves the real strength of your soul. The most timid man alive can deliver a bold speech. What you have really done will show only when you draw your last breath. I accept the terms. I do not shrink from the verdict." That's what I say to myself — but consider it said to you as well. You are younger. What of it? Death doesn't count our years. You don't know where death is waiting for you, so be ready for it everywhere.

I was about to stop — my hand was already shaping the closing line — but the rites must still be performed and this letter's traveling money paid out. Assume I'm not telling you where I'll borrow the sum; you know whose account I draw on. Wait just a moment and I'll pay you out of my own purse; in the meantime, Epicurus will oblige me with this: "Rehearse death" — or, if you prefer the phrase, rehearse the "migration to heaven." The meaning is plain: it is a wonderful thing to learn thoroughly how to die. Maybe you think it pointless to learn a lesson you can use only once. But that is exactly why we ought to rehearse it. When we can never test whether we truly know a thing, we have to keep learning it. "Rehearse death." Whoever says that is telling us to rehearse freedom. The man who has learned how to die has unlearned how to be a slave. He stands above every outside power — or at least beyond it. What are prisons and chains and bars to him? His way out is always open. There is only one chain that keeps us shackled to life, and that is the love of life. We don't have to throw that chain off; we can wear it thin, so that when the moment demands it, nothing holds us back or hinders us from doing at once what someday we must do anyway.

Farewell.

Letter 27: On the Good Which Abides

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

"What's this," you say, "you're giving me advice? Have you already advised yourself? Already fixed your own faults? Is that why you have the spare time to reform everyone else?" No — I'm not so shameless as to set up as a doctor while I'm sick myself. What I'm doing is talking through the troubles we share and passing the medicine across, as if we were lying in the same hospital ward. So listen to me as you would if I were talking to myself. I'm letting you into my private thoughts; I'm having it out with myself, and merely using you as my excuse.

I keep crying out to myself: "Count your years, and you'll be ashamed to want and chase the same things you wanted as a boy. Make sure of this one thing before your dying day: let your faults die before you do. Away with those disordered pleasures that must be paid for so dearly — it isn't only the ones still ahead that hurt me, but the ones already come and gone. Crimes, even when they go undetected, don't let the anxiety end when the act does; guilty pleasures are the same — the regret outlasts them. They have no substance, no reliability; even when they do no harm, they vanish. Look around instead for some good that will last. And there is none — except what the soul discovers for itself, within itself. Virtue alone gives a joy that is permanent and secure. Even if something gets in its way, it's only a passing cloud, drifting beneath the sun but never winning against it."

When will it be your luck to reach that joy? You haven't been slow so far, but you need to quicken your pace. A great deal of work remains, and if you want it done, you must spend your own waking hours and your own effort on it. This is one job that can't be delegated. Other kinds of book-work accept outside help. Within our own lifetime there was a rich man named Calvisius Sabinus, who had the bank account of a millionaire and the brains of one. I never saw a man whose good luck was a greater offense against decency. His memory was so bad he would forget the name of Ulysses, or Achilles, or Priam — names we know as well as we know our own servants'. No senile old butler, the kind who can't get anyone's name right and has to invent them, ever botched the roll call of his master's tribesmen the way Sabinus botched the Trojan and Greek heroes. And still he wanted to seem learned. So he devised a shortcut to scholarship: he paid staggering prices for slaves — one to know Homer by heart, another to know Hesiod, and one apiece for the nine lyric poets. That he paid top price shouldn't surprise you; when he couldn't find them ready-made, he had them made to order. Once he'd assembled this retinue, he began making life miserable for his dinner guests. He kept the fellows stationed at the foot of his couch and asked them, again and again, for verses he could repeat — and then broke down in the middle of a word anyway.

Satellius Quadratus — a sponger on rich fools, therefore a flatterer of them, and therefore (the third quality follows the other two) a mocker of them — suggested that Sabinus hire scholars to pick up his dropped lines. When Sabinus mentioned that each slave had cost him a hundred thousand sesterces, Satellius replied: "You could have bought that many bookcases for less." But Sabinus held firmly to the opinion that whatever anyone in his household knew, he himself knew too. This same Satellius then urged him to take up wrestling — Sabinus, who was sickly, pale, and thin. "How can I?" said Sabinus. "I can barely stay alive as it is." "Don't say that, I beg you," said Satellius. "Look how many perfectly healthy slaves you have!"

A sound mind cannot be borrowed or bought. In fact, I suspect that even if sound minds were up for sale, they'd find no buyers. Depraved ones, though, are bought and sold every day.

But let me pay off my debt and say goodbye: "Poverty brought into line with the law of nature is great wealth." Epicurus says this over and over, in one form or another — but a thing that can never be learned too well can never be said too often. For some patients you need only write the prescription; for others you must force the dose down their throats.

Farewell.

Letter 28: On Travel as a Cure for Discontent

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Do you think this has happened to you alone? Are you actually surprised — as if it were news — that all that travel, all those changes of scenery, haven't shaken off the gloom and the weight on your mind? You must change the mind, not the sky. You can cross ocean after ocean; as our Virgil says,

  Lands and cities drop astern —

but your faults will travel with you wherever you go. Socrates said the same thing to a man with the same complaint: "Why are you surprised that globe-trotting doesn't help you, when you take yourself with you? The thing that drove you out the door is right at your heels." What pleasure is there in seeing new countries? What good is touring cities and famous sights? All that rushing around is wasted. You ask why running away doesn't help? Because you run away in your own company. You must set down the burdens of the mind; until you do, no place on earth will please you.

Consider that your present condition is like the prophetess Virgil describes — worked up, goaded into frenzy, filled with a spirit not her own:

  The priestess raves, and strains to shake
  the mighty god out of her breast.

You hurry from place to place to shake off a burden that only chafes more with the motion — the way a ship's cargo causes no trouble while it lies stowed, but the moment it shifts, it heels the vessel toward whichever side it has slid to. Everything you do counts against you; you hurt yourself by the very restlessness. You're jostling a sick man.

But remove that trouble, and every change of scene becomes a pleasure. Drive you to the ends of the earth, set you down in any corner of a savage country you like — and that place, however forbidding, will be a hospitable home to you. Who you are when you arrive matters more than where you arrive; that is why we should never chain the mind to any one place. Live with this conviction: "I was not born for one corner of the world; this whole universe is my country." If you saw that clearly, you would stop being surprised that moving to fresh scenery, after growing weary of the old, does you no good. The first place would have pleased you too, if you had believed every place was yours. As it is, you aren't traveling; you're drifting — driven along, trading one place for the next, when the thing you're looking for — living well — is available everywhere.

Can anywhere be as chaotic as the Forum? Even there you can live in peace, if you must. Though frankly, given a free choice, I would flee far from the very sight and neighborhood of the place. Just as pestilential districts wear down even the strongest constitution, so some places are unhealthy for a mind that is healthy but not yet quite sound — still convalescing. I disagree with the people who wade straight into the breakers, who welcome a stormy life and wrestle daily, gamely, with hardship. The wise man will endure all that, but he won't choose it; he would rather be at peace than at war. It does little good to have thrown out your own faults if you must now brawl with other people's. "But there were thirty tyrants standing over Socrates," someone says, "and they couldn't break his spirit." What does it matter how many masters a man has? Slavery has no plural. Whoever has scorned it is free — no matter how thick the mob of overlords around him.

Time to stop — but not before I've paid the toll. "To know your sin is the beginning of salvation." That strikes me as a noble saying of Epicurus. A man who doesn't know he is doing wrong has no wish to be corrected; you must catch yourself in the act before you can reform. Some people brag about their vices — and do you think anyone who counts his faults as accomplishments gives a single thought to curing them? So, as far as you can, put yourself on trial. Investigate yourself; gather the evidence against yourself. Play the accuser first, then the judge, and only last the advocate pleading for mercy. And now and then, be harsh with yourself.

Farewell.

Letter 29: On the Critical Condition of Marcellinus

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You've been asking about our friend Marcellinus — you want to know how he's getting along. He rarely visits me, for exactly one reason: he's afraid to hear the truth. For the moment he's in no danger of that, because you can't talk to a man unless he's willing to listen. That's why people have long debated whether Diogenes and the other Cynics — who handed out blunt honesty indiscriminately and gave advice to anyone who crossed their path — were right to work that way. What's the sense of scolding the deaf, or people mute from birth or illness?

"Why should I be stingy with words?" you answer. "They cost nothing. I can't know whether I'll help the particular man I advise, but I know I'll help someone if I advise many. I have to scatter this advice by the handful. Try often enough, and sooner or later you succeed."

That, my dear Lucilius, is exactly what I believe a great soul should not do. His influence gets watered down; it loses its force with the very people it might have straightened out, if it hadn't gone stale. An archer shouldn't hit the mark only sometimes; he should miss it only sometimes. Whatever works by chance is not an art. And wisdom is an art. It should have a definite aim: choose the students who will make progress, and withdraw from the ones it has come to see as hopeless — though not giving up on them too soon, and trying the drastic remedies at exactly the point where the case turns desperate.

As for our friend Marcellinus, I haven't lost hope yet. He can still be saved — but the helping hand must be offered soon. There is a real risk he'll pull his rescuer down with him: there's a native vigor in the man, though it's already bending toward vice. Still, I'll brave the danger and dare to show him his faults. He'll do what he always does — reach for his wit, the wit that can get a smile out of mourners. He'll turn the joke first on himself, then on me. He'll say everything I'm about to say before I can say it. He'll ridicule our philosophic systems; he'll accuse philosophers of taking handouts, keeping mistresses, and indulging their appetites. He'll point out one philosopher caught in adultery, another who haunts the taverns, another who hangs around court. He'll bring up Aristo, Marcus Lepidus's philosopher, who used to hold his discussions in his carriage — that being the time slot he'd set aside for producing his researches — so that Scaurus, asked what school Aristo belonged to, said: "Well, one thing's certain — he's not one of the Walking Philosophers." And Julius Graecinus, a distinguished man, asked his opinion on the same question, replied: "I can't tell you — I don't know what he does on foot," as though he'd been asked about a chariot fighter. Charlatans of that sort — men who would have done philosophy more credit by leaving her alone than by peddling her — are what Marcellinus will throw in my face.

But I've decided to put up with the taunts. He may stir my laughter; I may yet stir him to tears. And if he insists on joking, then I'll rejoice, so to speak, in the midst of sorrow, that he's been blessed with such a merry strain of madness. But that kind of merriment doesn't last. Watch such men and you'll see: within a short span they laugh too hard and they rage too hard. My plan is to approach him and show him how much more he was worth back when so many thought him worth less. Even if I don't root out his faults, I'll put a check on them. They won't stop, but they'll pause — and perhaps they'll even stop, if pausing becomes a habit. That is nothing to sneer at: for the gravely ill, a good remission stands in for health.

So while I get myself ready for Marcellinus, you — who can act, who understand where you started and how far you've come, and can therefore guess how far there is still to go — put your character in order. Rouse your courage. Stand firm against the things that have frightened you. Don't count the people who scare you. You'd call a man a fool for fearing a crowd at a spot where only one person can pass at a time, wouldn't you? Just so: not many can actually reach you to kill you, however many threaten it. Nature has arranged it this way — just as only one person gave you life, only one will take it away.

If you had any shame, you'd have let me off this last installment. But no — I won't be stingy at the finish either; I'll clear my debts to the last penny and force on you what I still owe: "I have never wished to please the crowd; for what I know, they do not approve, and what they approve, I do not know." "Who said that?" you ask — as if you didn't know whose payroll I'm on. Epicurus. But every school will shout the same watchword at you — Peripatetic, Academic, Stoic, Cynic. If virtue delights you, how can you delight the crowd? Popular approval has to be won by trickery: you must make yourself just like them, and they will withhold their applause unless they recognize you as one of their own. And what you think of yourself matters far more than what others think of you. The favor of low men can be bought only by low means. So what will that celebrated philosophy do for you — the one we praise, the one we're told outranks every art and every possession? This: it will make you prefer pleasing yourself to pleasing the public. It will make you weigh men's judgments, not merely count them. It will make you live without fear of gods or men. It will make you either conquer your troubles or end them. Otherwise — if I see you carried along on popular applause, if your entrance is met with a roar of cheering and clapping, honors fit for an actor, if the whole city, women and children included, sings your praises — how could I help pitying you? I know what road leads to that kind of popularity.

Farewell.

Letters 30–33

Letter 30: On Conquering the Conqueror

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I have just seen Aufidius Bassus — that good and noble man — broken in health and wrestling with his years. But the years now press on him too heavily for him to get back up; old age has settled onto him with its great weight — no, with its entire weight. You know his body was always frail and dry. For a long time he kept it in hand — kept it together, to put it more accurately — and then all at once it gave way. When a ship springs a leak, you can always plug the first crack, and the second; but when many seams begin to open at once and let in the sea, there is no saving the gaping hull. It is the same with an old man's body: up to a certain point you can prop its weakness and hold it up. But when it starts to resemble a condemned building — when every joint is spreading, and while you repair one, another fails — then it is time for a man to look around and consider how to make his exit.

But the mind of our friend Bassus is fully awake. That is the gift philosophy gives us: joy in the very sight of death; strength and courage no matter what state the body is in; cheerfulness that does not fail even when the body is failing. A great pilot can sail with his canvas in shreds; if his ship is stripped to the hull, he rigs what is left of her and holds her on course. That is exactly what our friend Bassus is doing. He contemplates his own end with a courage and a calm face that you would call excessive indifference in a man contemplating someone else's.

This is a great accomplishment, Lucilius, and one that takes long practice to learn: to depart calmly when the unavoidable hour arrives. Other kinds of death carry an ingredient of hope. A disease runs its course; a fire gets put out; houses that seemed certain to crush the people inside have set them down unhurt; the sea has thrown swimmers back on shore alive by the same force that dragged them under; the soldier has drawn back his sword from the very neck of the doomed man. But those whom old age is leading off to death have nothing left to hope for. Old age alone grants no reprieve. No ending is more painless, true; but none takes longer.
