Bassus seemed to me to be attending his own funeral — laying out his own body for burial, living almost like a man who had outlived his own death, bearing his grief at his own departure the way a wise man should. Because he talks freely about death, and works hard to persuade us that if this process contains any discomfort or fear, the fault belongs to the dying person and not to death itself — and that there is no more trouble in the actual moment than there is after it is over. "And it is just as insane," he adds, "to fear what will not happen to you as to fear what you will not feel if it does happen." Can anyone really believe that the very thing that removes all feeling can itself be felt? "Therefore," says Bassus, "death stands so far beyond every evil that it is beyond every fear of evil."

I know all this has been said often and needs to be said often. But such precepts never worked on me the same way when I read them in books, or heard them from men declaring — from a safe distance — that the thing was nothing to fear. This old man carried the greatest weight with me because he discussed death with death standing near. I will tell you what I actually think: I believe a man is braver at the very moment of death than on the approach to it. When death is right in front of us, it gives even the inexperienced the courage to stop dodging what cannot be dodged. That is how a gladiator who was faint-hearted through the entire fight ends it by offering his throat to his opponent and guiding the wavering blade to the spot himself. But an end that is close at hand and certain to come, and slow in coming — that demands a tough, tenacious courage of soul. It is a rarer thing, and only the wise man can supply it.

So I listened to Bassus with the deepest pleasure. He was casting his vote on death — pointing out what sort of thing it is when observed, so to speak, at close range. I suppose a man would command more of your confidence, and carry more weight, if he had come back to life and could report from experience that there is no evil in death. Short of that, the people best qualified to tell you what disquiet the approach of death brings are the ones who have stood in its path, watched it coming, and welcomed it. Count Bassus among them — and he had no wish to deceive us. He says it is as foolish to fear death as to fear old age; for death follows old age exactly as old age follows youth. Whoever does not want to die never really wanted to live. Life is given to us with death as the condition; death is where the whole road leads. How foolish to fear it, then — a man simply awaits what is certain; what he fears is the uncertain. Death has its fixed rule, fair and unavoidable. Who can complain about being governed by terms that include everyone? The chief part of fairness, after all, is that it falls on all alike.

But there is no need to plead Nature's case at the moment. She wants our law to be identical with her own: she simply takes apart what she has put together, and puts together again what she has taken apart. And if any man has the luck to be set gently adrift by old age — not torn suddenly out of life but withdrawn from it bit by bit — he ought to thank the gods, every one of them, because after he has had his fill he is carried off to the rest that is appointed for mankind, the rest a tired man welcomes. You can find certain people who crave death more eagerly than others beg for life. And I honestly do not know which ones give us more courage — those who call for death, or those who meet it calm and cheerful — because the first attitude sometimes springs from madness or a burst of rage, while the second is the calm that comes from settled judgment. Men have gone charging to meet death in a fit of anger before now. But when death comes to meet him, no one welcomes it cheerfully except the man who composed himself for it long ago.

So I admit it: I visited this dear friend of mine again and again on various pretexts, but really to learn whether I would find him the same each time — whether his strength of mind was fading along with his strength of body. It was growing, the way a charioteer's joy shows more and more clearly as he rounds the seventh lap and closes on the prize. In fact, following the counsels of Epicurus, he often said: "First of all, I hope there is no pain at the moment a man breathes his last. But if there is, its very shortness carries a certain comfort — no great pain lasts long. And in the worst case, even if soul and body are being torn apart with excruciating pain, a man finds relief at that very moment in the thought that after this pain he can never feel pain again. As for me, I am sure an old man's soul is sitting on his very lips; it takes only a little force to detach it from the body. A fire that has caught hold of solid fuel needs water to put it out — sometimes the collapse of the whole building. But a fire with nothing left to feed on dies down by itself."

I am glad to hear such words, my dear Lucilius — not because they are new to me, but because they lead me into the presence of the actual fact. And what then? Have I not watched many men break the thread of life? I have indeed. But the ones who carry weight with me are those who come to death without any loathing for life — who let death in, so to speak, instead of pulling it toward them. Bassus kept saying: "This torture is our own fault, because we only shrink from dying when we believe the end is close at hand." But who is not close to it? Death is ready for us in every place and at every hour. "Consider," he went on, "whenever some cause of death seems imminent, how much nearer other kinds of dying stand — kinds we do not fear at all." A man is threatened with death by an enemy; a bout of indigestion beats the enemy to it. If we are willing to examine our fears honestly, we will find that some are real and others only seem to be. We do not fear death; we fear the thought of death. From death itself we are always the same distance. So if death is to be feared at all, it is to be feared always — for what season of life is exempt from it?

But what I really ought to fear is that you will hate a letter this long worse than death itself. So I will stop. As for you: think on death always, so that you never fear it.

Farewell.

Letter 31: On Siren Songs

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Now I recognize my Lucilius! He is beginning to show the character he promised. Follow up that impulse that sent you striding after everything that is best, trampling underfoot whatever the crowd approves. I would not have you greater or better than you planned — in your case the foundations alone already cover a great deal of ground; just finish everything you laid out, and take in hand the plans you have been carrying in your head. In short: you will be a wise man if you stop up your ears. And wax is not enough — you need a denser plug than the one they say Ulysses used on his crew. The song he feared was seductive, but it came from only one direction; the song you have to fear echoes around you not from a single headland but from every quarter of the world. So do not sail past one stretch of coast you distrust for its treacherous pleasures — sail past every city. Be deaf even to the people who love you most: they pray for bad things with good intentions. And if you want to be happy, beg the gods to grant none of their fond wishes for you. The things they want heaped on you are not really good things. There is only one good — the cause and support of a happy life: trust in yourself. And you cannot reach it until you have learned to despise hard work, and to count it among the things that are neither good nor bad. Because a single thing cannot be bad at one time and good at another — light and bearable now, a cause of dread later. Work is not a good. Then what is a good? Contempt for work. So I fault the men who toil for nothing. But when a man is straining toward honorable things, then the harder he pushes, the less he lets himself be beaten or brought to a halt, the louder I will applaud him and shout my encouragement: "So much the better! Up — take a fresh breath — take that hill in a single push if you can!"

Work is the food of noble minds. So there is no reason why, following that old vow of your parents, you should pick and choose which fortune to want or which to pray for — and anyway it is beneath a man who has already made the full circuit of the highest honors to keep pestering the gods. Why pray at all? Make yourself happy through your own effort. You can do it, once you understand this: whatever is blended with virtue is good; whatever is joined to vice is bad. Just as nothing gleams without light mixed into it, and nothing is black unless it contains darkness or draws some dimness into itself, and nothing is hot without the help of fire or cold without air — in the same way it is the presence of virtue or vice that makes things honorable or shameful.

Then what is good? Knowledge. What is evil? Ignorance. Your wise man — who is also a craftsman — will reject or select in each case as the occasion demands; but he does not fear what he rejects or admire what he selects, so long as his soul is stout and unconquerable. I forbid you to be discouraged or cast down. It is not enough not to shrink from work: ask for it. "But," you say, "isn't trivial, pointless work — work sprung from shabby motives — a bad kind of work?" No more than the work spent on noble efforts. Because the very quality that endures toil and drives itself up the hard uphill road belongs to the spirit, which says: "Why are you slacking? It is not a man's part to fear sweat." And beyond this, for virtue to be complete there must be an even temperament and a scheme of life consistent with itself all the way through — and that result cannot be reached without knowledge, and without the art that lets us understand things human and things divine. That is the greatest good. Seize it, and you begin to be the associate of the gods, not their beggar.

"But how," you ask, "do you get there?" Not by crossing the Pennine or Graian passes, or trekking the wastes of Candavia. You do not have to face the Syrtes, or Scylla, or Charybdis — though you traveled through every one of those places for the bribe of a petty governorship. The journey nature has equipped you for is safe and pleasant. She has given you gifts that will lift you level with God — if you do not desert them. Your money will not put you on a level with God: God owns no property. Your bordered robe will not do it: God wears no clothes. Your reputation will not do it, nor showing yourself off, nor having your name known across the world: no one has knowledge of God, and many think poorly of him without paying any price for it. The mob of slaves carrying your litter through the city streets and down foreign roads will not help you: this God I am speaking of, though the highest and most powerful of beings, carries everything on his own shoulders. Even beauty and strength cannot make you blessed; neither of them survives old age.

What we have to look for, then, is something that does not pass, day after day, further under the control of a power that cannot be resisted. And what is that? The soul — but the soul that is upright, good, and great. What else could you call such a soul but a god dwelling as a guest in a human body? A soul like that can descend into a Roman knight just as easily as into a freedman's son, or a slave. For what is a Roman knight, or a freedman's son, or a slave? Titles — born of ambition or of injustice. You can leap to heaven from the very slums. Only rise

  and shape yourself into kinship with your God.

You will not do that shaping in gold or silver; an image in the likeness of God cannot be cast from materials like those. Remember that back when the gods were kind to men, they were shaped from clay.

Farewell.

Letter 32: On Progress

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I have been asking about you — questioning everyone who comes from your part of the country: what you are doing, where you are spending your time, and with whom. You cannot deceive me; I am with you. Live exactly as if I were certain to get news of everything you do — no, as if I were certain to see it. And if you wonder what pleases me most in what I hear about you, it is that I hear nothing: most of the people I ask do not know what you are doing.

That is sound practice — keeping clear of people of a different stamp with different aims. And I am confident enough that you cannot be bent, that you will hold to your purpose even if the crowd surrounds you and tries to pull you off it. Then what is on my mind? I am not afraid they will change you; I am afraid they will slow you down. Even a man who merely holds you back does real damage — especially since life is so short, and we make it shorter still with our unsteadiness, forever making fresh starts at living, first one beginning and then immediately another. We break life into little pieces, and fritter it away. So hurry ahead, dearest Lucilius, and think how you would quicken your pace if you had an enemy at your back — if you suspected cavalry closing in, pressing hard on your tracks as you fled. It is true: the enemy is pressing. So pick up speed, get away, reach safe ground — and keep remembering what a noble thing it is to round off your life before death comes, and then wait in peace for whatever portion of time remains, claiming nothing more for yourself, because you are already in possession of the happy life — and a happy life is not made happier by being longer. Oh, when will you see the day when you know that time means nothing to you — when you are peaceful and calm, careless of tomorrow, because you are enjoying your life to the full?

Do you want to know what makes men greedy for the future? No one has yet found himself. Your parents, to be sure, prayed for other blessings for you; but I pray instead that you will despise all the things they wished for you in abundance. Their prayers plunder plenty of other people just so that you can be enriched; whatever is handed over to you has to be taken from someone else. What I pray is this: that you gain such command over yourself that your mind, shaken now by wandering thoughts, finally comes to rest and holds steady; that it becomes content with itself; and that once it understands what things are truly good — and they are ours the moment we understand them — it has no need of added years. He has finally passed beyond every necessity — he has won his honorable discharge and is free — who is still alive after his life is complete.

Farewell.

Letter 33: On the Futility of Learning Maxims

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You want me to close these letters, as I closed the earlier ones, with sayings drawn from the masters of our school. But they never dealt in choice extracts; the whole weave of their work is strong. There is unevenness, you know, when some objects stand out above the rest. A single tree is nothing remarkable when the whole forest rises to the same height. Poetry is crammed with sayings of this kind, and so is history. So do not think of those lines as belonging to Epicurus: they are common property — really, they are ours most of all. They simply get noticed more in Epicurus, because they come at rare intervals, when you are not expecting them, and because it is surprising to hear brave words from a man who made a practice of going soft. That, at least, is what most people say of him. In my own opinion Epicurus is genuinely brave, long sleeves and all. Courage, energy, and readiness for battle can be found among the Persians just as surely as among peoples who dress girded for a fight.

So stop calling on me for extracts and quotations. The kind of thought you pick out here and there from other philosophers runs unbroken through the whole body of our writings. We keep no window display; we do not trick the customer into stepping inside the shop only to find nothing there but what was hanging in the window. We let the customers take their samples from wherever they please. Suppose we did want to sort individual mottoes out of the common stock — to whom would we credit them? Zeno, Cleanthes, Chrysippus, Panaetius, or Posidonius? We Stoics are not subjects of a king; each of us claims his own freedom. Among the others, whatever Hermarchus says, whatever Metrodorus says, gets credited to one source; everything anyone in that brotherhood utters is spoken under the leadership and commanding authority of one man alone. But we, I insist, cannot pick out any single thing — try as we may — from so vast a supply of things equally good.

  Only the poor man counts his flock.

So give up hoping that you can skim the wisdom of great men out of summaries. You have to look at their wisdom whole; study it whole. They are working out a design, weaving a masterpiece line upon line, from which nothing can be removed without injury to the whole. Examine the separate parts if you like — as long as you examine them as parts of the man himself. A woman is not beautiful because her ankle or her arm draws compliments; she is beautiful when the whole of her makes you forget to admire the parts.

Still, if you insist, I will not be stingy with you — I will be lavish. There is a huge crowd of these passages, scattered about everywhere in profusion; they do not need to be gathered, only picked up. They do not drip out a drop at a time; they flow continuously, unbroken, all of a piece. And no doubt they do real good for beginners — the ones still worshipping outside the shrine. Single maxims sink in more easily when they are framed and fenced off like a line of verse. That is why we give children a proverb to learn by heart — what the Greeks call a chria — because a young mind can hold that much and no more. But for a man whose progress is real to go chasing choice extracts, propping his weakness on the best-known and briefest sayings, leaning on his memory — that is disgraceful. It is time for him to lean on himself. He should be making such maxims, not memorizing them. It is shameful for an old man, or a man in sight of old age, to have nothing but notebook wisdom. "This is what Zeno said." And what have you said? "This is the opinion of Cleanthes." And what is your opinion? How long will you march under another man's orders? Take command. Say something posterity will remember. Put out something from your own stock. This is why I hold that there is nothing distinguished in any of these men who never create anything themselves but lurk forever in someone else's shadow — playing the interpreter, never once daring to put into practice what they have spent so long learning. They have exercised their memories on other men's material. But remembering is one thing; knowing is another. Remembering is guarding something left in your memory's keeping; knowing means making everything your own — not depending on the copy, not forever glancing back at the master. "Thus said Zeno, thus said Cleanthes," indeed! Let there be some daylight between you and your book! How long will you be a student? From now on, be a teacher as well. "But why," someone asks, "should I keep going to lectures on things I can read?" "The living voice," comes the answer, "is a great help." Perhaps — but not the voice that only mouths another man's words and does the work of a stenographer.

Consider this fact, too. Those who never reach independence of mind begin by following their leader in cases where everyone else has abandoned him; then they follow him into questions where the truth is still being worked out. And the truth will never be discovered if we sit content with the discoveries already made. Besides, a man who follows another discovers nothing — he is not even searching. What then? Shall I not walk in the footsteps of the men who went before me? I will indeed use the old road; but if I find a shorter cut that is smoother to travel, I will open the new road. The men who made these discoveries before us are not our masters; they are our guides. Truth lies open to everyone. No one has a monopoly on it yet — and there is plenty of it left for those still to come.

Farewell.

Letters 34–40

Letter 34: On a Promising Pupil

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I swell with pride, I jump for joy, I shake off my years and my blood runs warm again, every time your actions and your letters show me how far you have outdone yourself — the ordinary man you left behind long ago. If a farmer is delighted when his tree finally bears fruit, if a shepherd takes pleasure in the new lambs of his flock, if every teacher looks at his student and sees his own youth again — then how do you think it feels to have trained a mind, to have shaped it while it was still young, and suddenly to see it grown up?

I claim you as my own. You are my handiwork. When I first saw what you were capable of, I laid my hand on you. I urged you, I used the goad, I never let you slow to a stroll — I kept prodding you awake. I am still doing it; only now I am cheering on a runner who is already in the race, and who cheers me on in return.

"What more do you want from me, then?" you ask. "The will is still my own." Well, the will in this case is nearly everything — not merely half, as in the proverb "Well begun is half done." It is more than half, because this whole business is decided in the soul. So the greater part of goodness is the will to become good. Do you know what I mean by a good man? One who is complete, finished — a man no pressure and no need can make bad. I see that man in you, if you keep on steadily, bend to the work, and see to it that everything you do and everything you say match each other, answer to each other, and come stamped from the same mold. If a man's acts are out of harmony, his soul is crooked. Farewell.

Letter 35: On the Friendship of Kindred Minds

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

When I push you so hard toward your studies, I am serving my own interest: I want your friendship, and I cannot have it unless you keep developing yourself the way you have begun. Right now you love me, but you are not yet my friend. "Wait," you say, "are those two different things?" Different? They are not even alike. A friend loves you, of course; but someone who loves you is not automatically your friend. Friendship always helps; love sometimes does harm. If for no other reason, then, perfect yourself for this one: so that you may learn how to love.

Hurry, then — so that while you are perfecting yourself for my benefit, you don't end up having learned perfection for somebody else's. True, I am already collecting some of the profit just by imagining that we two will be of one mind, and that whatever strength age has drained from me will flow back to me out of yours — though there really isn't so much difference in our ages. But I want to enjoy the finished fact. We feel a certain joy in the people we love even when they are far away, but that kind of joy is thin and fleeting. Seeing a man, being with him, talking with him — that is living pleasure; above all when you not only see the man you want, but the kind of man you want. So give yourself to me as a gift of great price. And to spur yourself on, remember that you are mortal and that I am old. Hurry to me — but hurry to yourself first. Make progress, and above all work at being consistent with yourself. Whenever you want to test whether you have accomplished anything, check whether you want the same things today that you wanted yesterday. A change of wishes shows a mind at sea, blown here and there, wherever the wind sets. What is settled and solid does not wander from its place. That is the blessed condition of the completely wise man — and, up to a point, of the man who is making progress too. What is the difference between them? The one moves but never changes position: he bobs at anchor where he is. The other does not move at all. Farewell.

Letter 36: On the Value of Retirement

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Encourage your friend to look down, with a stout heart, on the people who criticize him for choosing the shade of retirement — for walking away from a career of public honors and preferring peace to everything he might still have won. Let him show these critics, every day, how wisely he has looked out for his own interests. The men everyone envies will keep marching past him; some will be shoved out of the ranks, and others will fall. Prosperity is a turbulent thing; it torments itself. It rattles the brain in more ways than one, goading men toward this aim and that — power for some, high living for others. Some it puffs up; others it slackens until they go completely soft.

"But," comes the retort, "so-and-so carries his prosperity well." Yes — the way he carries his liquor. So don't let that crowd persuade you that a man besieged by admirers is happy. They run to him the way crowds rush a pool of water, muddying it as they drain it. "But," you say, "men call our friend an idler and a slacker." There are people, you know, whose speech comes out backwards — they use words to mean their opposites. They used to call him happy; so what? Was he? It doesn't even bother me that some find him rough and gloomy. Aristo used to say he preferred a stern young man to a cheerful one who pleased the crowd. "Wine that tastes harsh and sour when it is new," he said, "ages into good wine; the wine that tastes fine at the vintage can't survive the years." So let them call him stern, an enemy of his own advancement. That very sternness will age well — provided he keeps cherishing virtue and keeps soaking in the studies that genuinely cultivate a man: not the kind you sprinkle over yourself, but the kind the mind must be steeped in. Now is his time to learn. "What? Is there any time when a man should not learn?" None at all. But while studying is creditable at every age, being schooled is not. An old man learning his ABC's is a disgraceful and ridiculous sight; the young man must store up, the old man must draw out and use. So you will be doing yourself an enormous favor by making this friend of yours as good a man as possible. The kindnesses worth seeking and worth giving, they tell us, are the ones that benefit the giver as much as the receiver — and those are unquestionably the best kind.

Besides, he no longer has any choice in the matter: he has given his word. And it is less disgraceful to settle short with a creditor than to settle short with a promising future. To pay his debt of money, the merchant needs a lucky voyage, the farmer needs fertile fields and kindly weather; the debt your friend owes can be paid in full with nothing but good will. Fortune has no jurisdiction over character. Let him govern his character so that, in perfect peace, he can bring to perfection the spirit within him that feels neither loss nor gain, but stays in the same attitude no matter how things fall out. Heap worldly goods on a spirit like that, and it rises above its wealth; let chance strip away part of that wealth, or all of it, and the spirit is not diminished.

If your friend had been born in Parthia, he would have begun bending the bow as a child; in Germany, he would have been hurling his slender spear from boyhood; in our great-grandfathers' day, he would have learned to ride a horse and strike an enemy hand to hand. That is what each nation's way of life recommends to a man — no, prescribes for him. So what should this friend of yours train at? The one skill that works against every weapon and every kind of enemy: contempt of death. Nobody doubts that death carries something terrifying in it; it shocks even our souls, which nature has shaped to love their own existence. Otherwise there would be no need to prepare ourselves and whet our courage for something we would walk toward by a kind of instinct, the way every creature moves to preserve itself. No man trains so that he can lie down calmly on a bed of roses if the need ever arises. He hardens his courage for this: so that torture cannot make him break his sworn word, and so that, if it comes to it, he can stand his watch in the trenches, wounded, without even leaning on his spear — because sleep steals over a man who props himself up on anything at all.

In death there is nothing harmful — for there would have to be someone left for it to harm. And if you are gripped by such a craving for a longer life, consider this: nothing that vanishes from our sight is annihilated. It is reabsorbed into the world of things it came from — and will soon come from again. Things end their course; they do not perish. And death, which we fear and flinch from, interrupts life; it does not steal it. The day will come when we are returned to the light — though many men would object to that, if they were not brought back with their memories wiped clean.

Later I mean to show you, more carefully, that everything which seems to perish is merely changed. Since you are destined to return, you ought to leave with a calm mind. Watch how the universe circles back on itself: you will see that no star in our sky is snuffed out; they all set and rise in their turns. Summer has gone, but another year will bring it back; winter lies low, but its own proper months will restore it; night has swallowed the sun, but day will soon chase night away again. The wandering stars retrace their old paths; one part of the sky is forever rising, another forever sinking. One thing more, and then I'll stop: infants, and small boys, and the insane have no fear of death — and it is deeply shameful if reason cannot give us the peace of mind their foolishness has given them. Farewell.

Letter 37: On Allegiance to Virtue

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You have promised to be a good man; you have enlisted under oath — and that oath is the strongest chain that can bind you to a sound mind. Anyone who tells you this is a soft, easy kind of soldiering is mocking you, and I won't have you deceived. The words of this most honorable contract are the same as the words of the most disgraceful one — the gladiator's: "to be burned, to be bound, to be killed by the sword." From the men who rent their strength to the arena, who eat and drink what they must pay for in blood, a bond is taken that they will endure all this even against their will. From you the bond is that you will endure it willingly, even gladly. The gladiator may lower his weapon and appeal to the crowd for mercy; you will neither lower your weapon nor beg for your life. You must die on your feet, unbeaten. And anyway, what would you gain — a few days, a few years? From the moment we are born, there is no discharge.

"Then how do I get free?" you ask. You cannot escape necessity, but you can conquer it.

  Force makes a way.

And philosophy will give you that force. Turn to philosophy, then, if you want to be safe, untroubled, happy — in short, and this matters most, free. There is no other road to it. Folly is low, abject, mean, slavish — thrown open to a whole pack of the cruelest passions. And those passions, savage taskmasters that rule you sometimes by turns and sometimes all at once, can be driven out by wisdom, which is the only real freedom. There is just one path there, and it runs straight; you will not lose your way. Walk with a steady step. If you want everything under your control, put yourself under the control of reason; if reason rules you, you will rule much else. From reason you will learn what to attempt and how to go about it; you will stop blundering into things. Show me one man who can tell you how he began to crave what he craves. Nobody arrived there by planning; he was shoved there by impulse. Fortune attacks us as often as we attack Fortune. It is disgraceful to be carried along instead of walking — to find yourself suddenly in the middle of a whirlpool of events, asking, dazed: "How did I get here?" Farewell.

Letter 38: On Quiet Conversation

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You are right to insist that we step up our exchange of letters. But the greatest benefit comes from conversation, because it creeps into the soul a little at a time. Prepared lectures, declaimed in front of a crowd, make more noise and less intimacy. Philosophy is good advice, and no one gives advice at the top of his lungs. Sometimes, granted, we need those harangues — if I may call them that — when a doubter needs a shove. But when the aim is not to make a man want to learn but actually to make him learn, we must come down to the low tones of conversation. Quiet words enter more easily and stay in the memory. We do not need many words; we need effective ones.

Words should be scattered like seed. However small a seed may be, once it finds favorable ground it unfolds its strength, and from almost nothing it spreads to its fullest growth. Reason works the same way: it does not look large from the outside, but it grows as it works. Few words are spoken; but if the mind truly catches them, they gather strength and shoot up. Yes, precepts are like seeds: they produce much, and they are slight things. Only — as I said — a favorable mind must receive them and take them in. Then the mind will produce abundantly in its turn, giving back more than it received. Farewell.

Letter 39: On Noble Aspirations

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I will indeed put together the notes you ask for, carefully ordered and tightly packed. But consider whether the usual course might not help you more than what people now call a "breviary" — in the good old days, when we still spoke real Latin, it was called a "summary." The full course is what a learner needs; the digest is for the man who already knows. One teaches; the other jogs the memory. But I'll give you plenty of both. And a man like you should not be asking me for this authority or that; the man who has to produce a voucher for every statement admits he is a nobody. So I will write exactly what you want, but in my own way; until then, you have plenty of authors whose works will keep your thinking in order well enough. Pick up the list of the philosophers. That act alone will wake you up, once you see how many men have been working for your benefit. You will burn to be one of them yourself. For this is the finest quality the noble soul carries within it: it can be roused toward honorable things.

No man of high gifts is content with what is low and mean; the vision of great achievement calls him and lifts him up. Just as flame leaps straight into the air and can no more be boxed in or held down than it can hold still, so our soul is always in motion — and the more fire it has, the livelier its motion. But happy is the man who has aimed that momentum at better things! He puts himself beyond the jurisdiction of chance. He will handle prosperity wisely, shrink adversity, and look down on what other men gawk at. It is the mark of a great soul to scorn great things and prefer the moderate to the excessive; the moderate is useful and life-giving, while the excessive does harm precisely by being too much. Too rich a soil lays the grain flat; branches snap under too heavy a load; an over-bearing tree never ripens its fruit. The same happens to souls: unchecked prosperity wrecks them, and they use it to injure not only others but themselves. What enemy was ever as insolent to a foe as some men's pleasures are to them? The only excuse we can allow for their lack of self-control, their mad lusts, is that they suffer the very evils they inflicted on others. And the madness torments them justly, because desire that oversteps nature's mean must run off into limitless space. Nature's mean has boundaries; waywardness, and the acts born of willful lust, have none. Utility measures what you need — but by what yardstick do you measure the superfluous? So men sink into their pleasures, and once accustomed to them they cannot do without them; and they are most wretched of all for this reason: they have reached the point where what was once superfluous has become indispensable. They are the slaves of their pleasures, not the enjoyers of them. They even love their own ills — and that is the worst ill of all. Unhappiness hits bottom when shameful things not only attract a man but delight him, and there is no room left for a cure, because what used to be vices have become habits. Farewell.

Letter 40: On the Proper Style for a Philosopher's Discourse

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Thank you for writing to me so often; you are showing me your real self in the only way you can. I never receive a letter from you without instantly being in your company. If portraits of absent friends give us pleasure — though they only refresh the memory and offer a false, hollow comfort for our missing them — how much more pleasant is a letter, which carries real traces, real marks, of an absent friend! The sweetest thing about meeting face to face is there in the print of a friend's hand on his letter: recognition.

You write that you heard the philosopher Serapio lecture when his ship put in at your town. "He likes to haul his words up in one great rush," you say. "He doesn't let them flow one at a time; he drives them on so they crowd and trample each other. More words come than any single voice can carry." I do not approve of this in a philosopher. A philosopher's speech, like his life, should be composed — and nothing that rushes headlong in a hurry is well ordered. That is why in Homer the rapid style, sweeping down without a break like a snow-squall, is given to the younger speaker, while from the old man eloquence flows gently, sweeter than honey.

So mark my words: that torrential manner of speaking, fast and copious, suits a sidewalk showman better than a man discussing and teaching a serious, important subject. But I object just as strongly to dripping the words out as to racing them: a speaker should neither keep the ear straining nor bury it. The starved, thin-spun style also loses the audience, worn out by its stammering slowness — though it is true that a word we have waited for sinks in more easily than a word that flits past on the wing. Besides, people speak of "handing down" precepts to a pupil; but you are not handing down what will not stay in the hand. And there is this: speech that serves the truth should be plain and unadorned. This crowd-pleasing style has nothing to do with truth. It wants to stampede the mob, to sweep away careless ears by sheer momentum; it doesn't offer itself for examination — it snatches itself out of reach. How can speech govern others when it cannot govern itself? And may I add that speech meant to heal our minds has to sink down into us? Remedies do no good unless they stay in the system.

Anyway, this kind of speech is mostly hollow: more sound than power. My terrors need to be calmed, my irritations soothed, my illusions shaken off, my indulgences reined in, my greed rebuked. Which of those cures can be done in a hurry? What doctor heals his patient on a flying visit? Can I even say that this roaring jumble of ill-chosen words gives pleasure? No. Just as with tricks you would have sworn were impossible — finding out once how they're done is enough — so with these word-acrobats: to have heard them once is plenty. What would anyone want to learn or imitate there? What should you conclude about a man's soul when his speech charges out in total disorder and cannot be reined in? When you run downhill, you cannot stop where you meant to stop; your body's momentum carries your steps past the place you chose. It is the same here: this speed of speech has no control over itself, and it is unworthy of philosophy — for philosophy should place her words carefully, not fling them, and should move one step at a time.

"What then?" you say. "Shouldn't philosophy sometimes raise her voice?" Of course she should — but let her keep her dignity of character, and that is exactly what violent, excessive force strips away. Let philosophy have great strength, but strength held well under control; let her be a stream that never stops flowing — never a torrent. I would hardly allow even a courtroom orator a speed like this, which cannot be called back and plows ahead lawlessly. How could the jurors follow it — men who are often inexperienced and untrained? Even when an orator is carried away by the urge to show off, or by real emotion, even then he should not quicken his pace and pile up words beyond what the ear can bear.

You will be acting rightly, then, if you pay no attention to the men who care how much they can say rather than how well — and if, forced to choose, you choose to speak like Publius Vinicius, the stammerer. When Asellius was asked how Vinicius spoke, he answered: "Gradually." (Geminus Varius put it this way: "I don't see how you can call that man eloquent — he can't get three words out in a row.") Well, why shouldn't you choose to speak the way Vinicius does? Some joker may cross your path, of course, like the man who — while Vinicius was hauling his words out one at a time, as if he were dictating rather than speaking — called out: "Say, haven't you got anything to say?" And yet that is the better fault to have; for the rush of Quintus Haterius, the most famous orator of his age, is exactly what a sensible man should avoid. Haterius never hesitated, never paused; he started once, and he stopped once.

I suppose, too, that some styles suit some nations more, some less. In a Greek you can put up with the unbridled style; we Romans have gotten into the habit of spacing out our words even when we write. And our own Cicero, the man with whom Roman oratory sprang into prominence, was a slow pacer too. The Roman tongue is more inclined to take stock of itself — to weigh, and to offer something worth weighing. Fabianus, a man notable for his life, for his learning, and — third and least — for his eloquence, used to discuss a subject with dispatch rather than haste; you would call it ease rather than speed. That quality I approve in the wise man, though I do not demand it: let his speech simply come out unhampered — though I would rather it were delivered deliberately than spouted.

But here is one more reason to frighten you away from the disease of speed: you could only master that style by losing your sense of shame. You would have to rub all the modesty off your face and refuse to hear yourself speak. That heedless flood will carry along plenty of expressions you would wish you could call back; and, I repeat, you cannot reach that pace and keep your self-respect. Besides, you would need to practice every day and shift your attention from matter to words. And even if the words came readily and flowed without any effort of yours, they would still have to be kept on the leash. A modest walk suits a philosopher; so does restrained speech, far removed from boldness. So when everything is boiled down, the kernel of my advice is this: I bid you be slow of speech. Farewell.

Letters 41–45

Letter 41: On the God Within Us

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You are doing something excellent — something that will do you real good — if, as your letter says, you are pressing on toward sound understanding. It is foolish to pray for what you can get from yourself. We don't need to lift our hands toward heaven, or beg some temple keeper to let us up close to the idol's ear, as if a prayer whispered there carried better. God is near you, he is with you, he is within you. I mean that, Lucilius: a holy spirit lives inside us, keeping watch over our good deeds and our bad ones — our guardian. As we treat this spirit, so it treats us. In fact, no one is good without God's help. Can anyone rise above Fortune unless God helps him rise? He is the one who supplies noble and upright counsel. In every good man,

  A god dwells — which god, we do not know.

If you have ever come into a grove of ancient trees grown far past their usual height, their crossed and woven branches curtaining off the sky, then the sheer height of the forest, the seclusion of the spot, and your wonder at that thick unbroken shade in the middle of open country will convince you a divine presence is there. Or if a cave, eaten deep into crumbling rock, holds up a mountain on its arch — a space no hands built, hollowed out to that vastness by nature alone — your soul will be shaken by a certain intimation that God exists. We worship the sources of mighty rivers; we set up altars where a great stream bursts suddenly out of hiding; we honor hot springs as divine; we consecrate certain pools for their dark water or their bottomless depth. So when you see a man unterrified in the middle of dangers, untouched by cravings, happy in adversity, calm in the storm — a man who looks at other men from a higher level and at the gods as equals — won't a feeling of reverence for him steal over you? Won't you say, "This thing is too great and too lofty to be of the same stuff as the little body it lives in. A divine power has come down into that man"? When a soul rises above other souls — steady, under control, passing through every experience as if it were a small matter, smiling at the things we fear and the things we pray for — a force from heaven is moving it. A thing like that cannot stand upright unless the divine is propping it up. So the greater part of it stays in the place it came down from. Just as the sun's rays touch the earth but remain rooted in the source they are sent from, so the great and holy soul — sent down so that we could know the divine at closer range — keeps company with us but stays fastened to its origin. It hangs from that source; it turns its gaze there and strains to return; and it takes part in our affairs the way a superior does, not an equal.

What, then, is such a soul? One that shines with no borrowed good — only its own. What is more foolish than to praise a man for things that aren't his? What is more insane than to marvel at things that can pass to somebody else in the next instant? A golden bit does not make a better horse. The lion with the gilded mane — handled, tamed, worn down until he puts up with the decoration — goes into the arena a very different creature from the wild lion whose spirit is unbroken. The wild one, bold in his attack, exactly as nature meant him to be, magnificent in his shaggy fierceness — his glory is that no one can look at him without fear — stands above that other one, the listless gilded brute.

No one should glory in anything but his own. We praise a vine if it loads its shoots with grapes, if its weight bends the very poles that hold its fruit down to the ground. Would anyone prefer to that vine one with golden grapes and golden leaves hanging on it? Fertility is the virtue that belongs to a vine. In a man, too, praise what belongs to the man. He has a household of handsome slaves, a beautiful house; he plants wide acres and collects a wide income? None of that is in the man himself; it is all around him. Praise in him what can neither be given nor snatched away — what is peculiarly a man's own. You ask what that is? The soul, and reason brought to perfection in the soul. For man is a reasoning animal. So man's highest good is reached when he has fulfilled the purpose nature designed him for at birth. And what does this reason demand of him? The easiest thing in the world — to live according to his own nature. It is the general madness of mankind that makes this hard: we keep shoving one another into vice. And how can a man be called back to safety when no one is holding him back and the whole crowd is pushing him on?

Farewell.

Letter 42: On Values

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

So that friend of yours has already convinced you he is a good man? And yet no one can become good — or be known to be good — in so short a time. Do you know whom I mean now when I say "a good man"? One of the second grade, like your friend. The first-class kind is born perhaps once every five hundred years, like the phoenix. And no wonder greatness comes only at long intervals. Fortune turns out ordinary talents by the batch, made to please the mob; but what she wants us to admire, she advertises by the very fact that she makes it rare.

This man you mention, though, is still a long way from the state he claims to have reached. If he knew what "a good man" meant, he wouldn't believe himself one yet; he might even despair of ever becoming one. "But he thinks badly of bad men," you say. So do bad men themselves; there is no heavier penalty for vice than that it disgusts itself and everything like it. "But he hates men who grab great power suddenly and use it without restraint." And he will do exactly the same the moment he gets the same power. With many men, the vices go unnoticed only because they are powerless; the instant these men feel sure of their own strength, their vices will be every bit as daring as the ones prosperity has already put on display. All they lack is the means to unfold their wickedness. You can handle even a poisonous snake while it is stiff with cold; the venom isn't missing — it is only numbed. With many men, the cruelty, the ambition, the self-indulgence want nothing but Fortune's favor to dare crimes that would match the worst on record. That the wish is there you can discover in a moment: give them power equal to their wishes.

Do you remember? You once declared a certain man was under your influence, and I called him fickle, a bird of passage, and said you were holding him not by the foot but by a wing. Was I wrong? You had him by a single feather; he left it in your hand and escaped. You know what a spectacle he made of himself in front of you afterward, and how many of his schemes were poised to come down on his own head. He did not see that in endangering others he was tottering toward his own fall. He did not stop to think how heavy the things he was chasing were — even the ones that weren't superfluous.

So here is the truth to note about the things we pursue, the things we sweat and strain for: either there is nothing desirable in them, or the undesirable outweighs the rest. Some are superfluous; others are not worth their price. But we don't see this clearly. We treat things as free gifts when in fact they cost us dearly. Here is the proof of our stupidity: we think "buying" applies only to the things we pay cash for, and we call free the things we pay for with our very selves. Things we would refuse to buy if the price were our house or some pleasant, profitable estate — those we are eager to get at the cost of anxiety, of danger, of lost honor, lost freedom, lost time. So true it is that every man holds nothing cheaper than himself.

In all our plans and dealings, then, let's act the way we act at a huckster's stall: let's see what the thing we crave is going to cost. Very often what costs nothing costs us the most. I can show you plenty of prizes whose pursuit and capture have wrenched our freedom right out of our hands. We would belong to ourselves, if only these things did not belong to us.

I would have you run the same calculation on losses, too, not just gains. "This thing is bound to perish." It was an extra; you will live without it as easily as you lived before it. If you had it a long time, you lose it after you've had your fill; if only a short time, you lose it before you've grown attached. "You will have less money." Yes — and less trouble. "Less influence." Yes — and less envy. Look around at the things that drive us mad, the things we lose with floods of tears, and you will see: it is not the loss that hurts us, it is the notion of loss. No one feels that these things are gone; he only thinks they are. The man who owns himself has lost nothing. But how few men are blessed with ownership of themselves!

Farewell.

Letter 43: On the Relativity of Fame

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You want to know how the news reached me — who told me you were entertaining this idea you hadn't breathed to a single soul? The best-informed character there is: gossip. "What," you say, "am I such a great personage that I can set gossip going?" There is no reason to measure yourself against this part of the world; measure yourself against the place where you live. Whatever rises above its surroundings is great — right at the spot where it rises. Greatness is not absolute; comparison swells it or shrinks it. A ship that looms large on a river looks tiny on the ocean. A rudder that is big for one vessel is small for another.

So in your province you really do matter, however little you think of yourself. People are asking what you do, how you dine, how you sleep — and they are finding out, too. All the more reason to live carefully. But do not count yourself truly happy until you can live in full view — until your walls protect you instead of hiding you. We like to believe we build walls around ourselves for safety; the truth is, we build them so we can sin in more privacy. Here is a fact you can weigh a man's character by: you will hardly find anyone who can live with his door wide open. It was conscience, not pride, that stationed doorkeepers at our doors; we live in such a way that being suddenly seen is the same as being caught in the act. But what good does it do to hide, to dodge every eye and ear? A good conscience welcomes the crowd; a bad one is anxious and troubled even when it is alone. If your deeds are honorable, let everyone know them. If they are shameful, what does it matter that no one else knows, so long as you know? How wretched you are, if you hold that witness cheap!

Farewell.

Letter 44: On Philosophy and Pedigrees

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You are at it again — telling me you are a nobody, that nature treated you shabbily first and Fortune second — when all the while you have it in your power to step out of the crowd and rise to the highest human happiness. If there is one good thing about philosophy, it is this: it never looks into pedigrees. Trace anyone back to his first source and everyone descends from the gods. You are a Roman knight, and your own steady work carried you into that class; but plenty of men find the fourteen rows barred to them; the senate house does not open to everyone; even the army is choosy about whom it admits to its toil and danger. A noble mind, though, is open to all men. By that test we can all achieve distinction. Philosophy neither rejects nor selects anyone; its light shines for all. Socrates was no aristocrat. Cleanthes hauled water and hired himself out to keep a garden. Philosophy did not find Plato a nobleman; it made him one. Why despair of ranking with men like these? They are all your ancestors — if you conduct yourself in a way that is worthy of them. And you will, if you convince yourself right now that no man outranks you in real nobility. We have all had the same number of forefathers; there is no man whose beginnings do not lie beyond memory. Plato says: "Every king springs from a race of slaves, and every slave has had kings among his ancestors." The long flight of time, with all its turns, has shuffled these things together, and Fortune has turned them upside down. Then who is well-born? The man whose nature is well fitted for virtue. That is the only point worth looking at. Otherwise, if you go hunting through antiquity, everyone traces back to a date before which there is nothing. From the first beginnings of the universe down to today, we have come along a line that runs bright, then shabby, then bright again. A hall packed with smoke-blackened busts does not make the nobleman. No one in the past lived his life to lend us glory; what came before us is not ours. It is the soul alone that makes us noble — and the soul may rise above Fortune from any condition at all, no matter what that condition used to be.

Suppose, then, you were not a Roman knight but a freedman. Even so, you could by your own effort come to be the one free man in a crowd of gentlemen. "How?" you ask. By marking off good things from bad without taking the crowd's word for it. Look not at where things come from but at where they lead. If anything can make life happy, it is good on its own merits — because it cannot be twisted into evil. Then where do we all go wrong, since everyone craves the happy life? We mistake the equipment of happiness for happiness itself, and the harder we chase it, the farther we run from it. The sum and substance of the happy life is unshakable peace of mind, and the secret of that peace is unshaken confidence; yet men keep gathering up the causes of worry, and while they haul their baggage down life's treacherous road, they don't just carry burdens — they pick up more along the way. So they fall farther and farther from what they are seeking, and the more effort they spend, the more they get in their own way and are driven back. That is what happens when you hurry through a maze: the faster you go, the worse you are tangled.

Farewell.

Letter 45: On Sophistical Argumentation

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You complain that books are scarce where you are. But it is quality, not quantity, that matters. Reading from a short list does you good; reading from a big assortment only entertains you. A man who wants to reach his destination follows one road; he does not wander down many. What you're describing is not traveling; it is drifting.

"But," you say, "I would rather have your advice than your books." Even so, I am ready to send you every book I own — to ransack the whole storehouse. If I could, I would come myself; and if I weren't hoping you will finish your term of office soon, I would have imposed this old man's journey on myself. No Scylla, no Charybdis, none of their storied straits could have scared me off. I would not just have crossed those waters — I would have swum them, for the chance to greet you and judge with my own eyes how much you have grown in spirit.

As for your asking me to send my own writings — that does not make me think myself learned, any more than a request for my portrait would flatter my looks. I know it comes from your affection rather than your judgment; and if judgment is in it anywhere, affection forced the judgment on you. But whatever my books are worth, read them as the work of a man still searching for the truth — not in possession of it, and searching stubbornly, too. I have sold myself to no man; I carry no master's name. I give great weight to the judgment of great men, but I claim something for my own as well. Those men left us not finished discoveries but open problems still waiting to be solved. They might have discovered the essentials, if they hadn't gone hunting the superfluous too. They lost a great deal of time quibbling over words and building tricky arguments — the sort of thing that exercises the wit to no purpose. We tie knots in words, wrap them in double meanings, and then try to untie them.

Do we have that much spare time? Do we already know how to live — how to die? Our whole soul should be pressing toward the point where the real duty lies: seeing to it that things do not deceive us, not just words. Why draw fine distinctions between look-alike words, when no one is ever fooled by them except in the middle of the debate? It is things that lead us astray; it is between things that you must learn to distinguish. We embrace evil instead of good; we pray for the opposite of what we prayed for yesterday. Our prayers clash with our prayers, our plans with our plans. How closely flattery resembles friendship! It doesn't just imitate friendship — it outdoes it, passes it in the race. It finds ears wide open and indulgent, sinks straight to the bottom of the heart, and pleases precisely where it does harm. Show me how to see through that resemblance! An enemy comes to me full of compliments, wearing a friend's face. Vices creep into us under the names of virtues: recklessness hides under the label of courage, moderation gets called laziness, the coward passes for prudent. And here the danger of going astray is real. So stamp these things with clear labels.

Then again, the man who is asked whether he has horns on his head is not such a fool that he feels his forehead to check — and not so silly or slow, either, that any argument, however subtle, can persuade him he doesn't know the facts. Quibbles like that deceive as harmlessly as the juggler's cups and dice, where the trickery itself is the pleasure. Show me how the trick is done, and I lose all interest. I say the same about these word games — for what else should we call such sophistries? Not knowing them does no harm; mastering them does no good. If you really want to sort out slippery meanings, then teach us this instead: that the happy man is not the one the crowd calls happy — the man whose coffers the money pours into — but the man whose goods are all in his soul. A man who stands upright and exalted, who spurns inconstancy; who sees no one he would trade places with; who values men only at their worth as men; who takes Nature for his teacher, conforms to her laws, and lives as she commands; whom no violence can strip of what he owns; who turns evil into good; who is sure in judgment, unshaken, unafraid — a man whom force may move but never bewilder. When Fortune hurls her deadliest missile at him with everything she has, she may graze him — rarely — but never wound him. Her other missiles, the ones she flattens the rest of mankind with, bounce off him like hail rattling on a roof: it makes noise, harms no one inside, and melts away.

Why do you bore me with the famous "liar" fallacy, the one so many books have been written about? Look — suppose my whole life is a lie. Refute that. If you are sharp enough, bring my life back to the truth. As it stands, my life counts things essential that are mostly superfluous; and even the part that is not superfluous has no power to make a man fortunate and blessed. Just because a thing is necessary, it does not follow that it is good. Otherwise we degrade the word "good" by handing it to bread and barley porridge and the other bare necessities of staying alive. The good is always necessary; the necessary is not always good, since some downright paltry things are necessary too. No one so misses the noble meaning of the word "good" as to sink it to the level of these humdrum utilities.

Well then — wouldn't your effort be better spent proving to everyone that the hunt for the superfluous costs a great deal of time, and that many people go through life merely collecting the tools of living? Examine individuals, survey mankind in general: there is no one whose life does not face toward tomorrow. "What's the harm in that?" you ask. Infinite harm. Such people are not living; they are getting ready to live. They postpone everything. Even if we paid strict attention, life would outrun us; as it is, life finds us dawdling and passes us by as if it belonged to someone else — and though it ends on the final day, it perishes every day.

But I must not run past the bounds of a letter, which ought not to overfill the reader's left hand. So I will put off to another day our case against the hair-splitters — those over-subtle fellows who make argument the master when it should be the servant.

Farewell.

Letters 46–49

Letter 46: On a New Book by Lucilius

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Your book arrived — the one you promised me. I opened it intending only to sample it; the plan was to skim now and read properly later. But the book itself coaxed me in deeper. It charmed me into going on. You can judge how eloquent it is from this: it felt light in the hand, though at first glance you'd never guess it was your work or mine — it reads like something by Titus Livius, the historian, or even Epicurus. And the charm gripped me and carried me along so completely that I finished it without setting it down once. The sunshine was calling me outside; hunger was nagging; rain clouds were piling up. And still I swallowed the book whole, from the first page to the last.

I wasn't just pleased with it. I was delighted. What wit it has, what spirit! I would have said what force, if the book had had quiet stretches in it — if it had gathered itself and surged only now and then. But there was no surging; instead there was a steady, even flow, a style muscular and clean. Here and there, though, I caught your sweetness breaking through, and in places that gentleness of yours. The style is tall and noble. That's the manner I want you to keep — that direction, that stride. The subject helped too, which is why you should choose rich, productive topics: subjects that grab hold of the mind and wake it up.

I'll say more about the book after a second reading. For now my judgment is still unsettled — it's as if I'd heard the book read aloud rather than read it myself. Let me really examine it. And don't worry: you'll get the truth from me. What a lucky man you are, to give a friend no opening to lie to you at such long range! Though I suppose that even now, with every excuse for lying stripped away, we still have one left: habit — we lie to each other because we always have.

Farewell.

Letter 47: On Master and Slave

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I was glad to hear, from the people who've been visiting you, that you live on friendly terms with your slaves. That's exactly what I'd expect from a man of your good sense and education. "They are slaves," people say. No — they are men. "Slaves!" No — comrades. "Slaves!" No — unpretentious friends. "Slaves!" No — fellow-slaves, if you stop to consider that Fortune holds exactly the same power over them and over us.

That's why I laugh at the people who think it's degrading to dine with your slave. Degrading — why? Only because of the snobbish custom that surrounds a rich man at his dinner with a crowd of slaves who must stand while he eats. And how he eats! More than he can hold, with monstrous greed, loading a belly stretched so far it forgets how to do a belly's work — so that he labors harder to get all that food back out of himself than he did to cram it in. And the whole time, the poor slaves may not so much as move their lips. Not to speak — not for anything. The rod silences every murmur. Even accidents — a cough, a sneeze, a hiccup — are paid for with the lash. Any sound that breaks the silence is punished savagely. All night long they stand there, hungry and mute.

The result? Slaves who may not talk in front of the master talk about the master. But the slaves of the old days — the ones allowed to talk not just in the master's presence but with him, the ones whose mouths were not stitched shut — those slaves were ready to bare their own necks for their master, to pull any danger hanging over his head down onto their own. They talked at dinner and kept silent under torture. It was this same arrogant treatment that gave rise to the proverb: "You have as many enemies as you have slaves." We do not acquire them as enemies. We make them enemies.

I'll pass over the other cruel and inhuman ways we treat them — how we abuse them not as if they were men, but as if they were pack animals. While we sprawl at the dinner table, one slave wipes up the spat-out food, another crouches under the table collecting what the drunken guests let fall. Another carves the expensive birds, guiding his practiced hand through breast and thigh in sure, exact strokes — a miserable man, if he exists only to carve fat poultry correctly. Though the man who teaches this art for the sake of pleasure may be more miserable still than the man who learns it because he has no choice. Another slave, the wine-server, is dressed up like a woman and forced to fight against his own advancing years: he cannot escape his boyhood — he's dragged back into it. His body is a soldier's already, but he's kept smooth-cheeked, his hair rubbed away or plucked out at the roots, and he stays awake all night, dividing it between his master's drunkenness and his master's lust — a man in the bedroom, a boy at the table. Another has the job of appraising the guests: he must stand there, poor man, and watch to see whose flattery, whose shamelessness of appetite or of tongue, will win an invitation for tomorrow. And think of the caterers, who study the master's palate with delicate expertise — which flavors will sharpen his appetite, which dishes will please his eye, what new combination might rouse a stomach sick with excess, what he'll refuse today out of sheer glut, and what he'll be hungry for. These are the slaves the master cannot bear to eat with. He'd think it beneath his dignity to share a table with his own slave. God forbid!

And yet how many masters he is creating among those very men. I have seen Callistus's former master standing in line at Callistus's door — I have seen him shut out while others were shown in: the same master who once pinned the "For Sale" ticket on Callistus and put him up in the market with the worthless lots. That slave has paid him back — the slave who was shuffled into the first batch, the ones the auctioneer warms up his voice on. Now it is the slave's turn to strike a name off his list, his turn to judge a man unfit to enter his house. The master sold Callistus. But how dearly Callistus has made the master pay.

Please remember this: the man you call your slave sprang from the same stock as you, is smiled on by the same skies, and on precisely equal terms breathes, lives, and dies. You could as easily see the free man in him as he could see the slave in you. When the massacres of Marius's day were over, plenty of men of distinguished birth — men doing their army service as the first step toward the Senate — were flattened by fortune: one ended up a shepherd, another the caretaker of a country cottage. Go ahead and despise a man in whose place you may find yourself at any moment — despise him if you dare.

I don't want to launch into the whole enormous question of how we treat slaves — and we Romans are excessively arrogant, cruel, and insulting toward them. But here is the kernel of my advice: live with your inferior as you would have your superior live with you. Every time you remember how much power you hold over your slave, remember that your own master holds exactly that much power over you. "But I have no master," you say. You're still young; you may have one yet. Have you forgotten how old Hecuba was when she was enslaved? Or Croesus? Or the mother of Darius, or Plato, or Diogenes?

Live with your slave on kind terms — friendly terms, even. Let him talk with you, plan with you, share your life. I know the whole club of exquisites will howl at me for this: "Nothing could be lower," they'll say, "nothing more shameful." These are the same men I catch kissing the hands of other people's slaves. And don't you see how our ancestors stripped away everything hateful from the master's position and everything humiliating from the slave's? They called the master "father of the household" and the slaves "members of the household" — a usage that survives in the theater to this day. They established a holiday on which masters and slaves ate together — not the only day it was allowed, but a day when it was required. They let slaves hold offices within the household and pronounce judgments there; they considered the household a miniature commonwealth.

"So you're telling me," comes the retort, "that I have to seat every one of my slaves at my table?" No — no more than you have to invite every free man to it. But you're wrong if you think I'd turn away certain slaves because their work is dirty — that muleteer, say, or that herdsman. I propose to value men by their character, not by their jobs. A man's character is his own doing; his job is assigned by accident. Invite some to your table because they deserve the honor, and others so that they may come to deserve it — for if there's anything servile in them from the low company they've kept, eating with better men will shake it out. You don't have to hunt for friends only in the forum or the Senate-house, my dear Lucilius. If you pay attention, you'll find them in your own house. Good material often stands idle for want of a craftsman — try it and see for yourself. A man who goes to buy a horse and inspects not the animal but its saddle and bridle is a fool; the man who appraises another man by his clothes and his rank is a fool twice over — for rank is only one more robe we wear.

"He is a slave." But perhaps a free man in soul. "He is a slave." And will that count against him? Show me a man who isn't one. One man is a slave to lust, another to greed, another to ambition — and every one of us is a slave to fear. I can show you an ex-consul who is slave to a little old woman, a millionaire who is slave to a serving-girl; I can show you young men of the noblest families in bondage to pantomime dancers. No slavery is more shameful than the slavery we choose ourselves.

So don't let those fastidious people scare you out of showing your slaves a friendly face instead of a superior one. Let them respect you rather than fear you. Someone will say I'm calling for the liberty-cap for slaves everywhere, that I'm toppling masters from their thrones, because I said slaves should respect a master rather than fear him — "That's his meaning exactly: slaves paying their respects like clients, like morning callers!" Whoever says this forgets that what satisfies a god cannot be too little for a master. To be respected is to be loved — and love cannot be mixed with fear. So I think you are entirely right in not wanting your slaves to fear you, and in doing your correcting with words. The whip is for animals that can't understand them.

Not everything that annoys us actually harms us. But soft living drives us into fits of rage, so that whatever fails to answer our whim sets off our anger. We put on the temper of kings — for kings, too, forgetting their own strength and other men's weakness, go white-hot with fury as though they'd been injured, when their high station keeps them perfectly safe from any such injury. And they know it perfectly well. But by complaining they seize their chances to do harm; they insist they have received injuries in order to inflict them.

I won't keep you any longer — you don't need the sermon. Good character has this mark, among others: it makes its own judgments and sticks to them. Badness is fickle; it changes constantly — never into something better, only into something different.

Farewell.

Letter 48: On Quibbling as Unworthy of the Philosopher

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

That letter you wrote me on the road — as long as the road itself — I'll answer later. I need to go off by myself and work out what advice to give you. You, after all, thought long and hard before deciding to consult me; shouldn't I think longer and harder before answering? It takes more deliberation to settle a question than to raise one — especially when one answer suits you and a different one suits me.

Am I talking like an Epicurean again? The truth is that what's good for you is good for me. I'm no friend of yours unless everything that concerns you concerns me too. Friendship makes us partners in everything — there is no such thing as good luck or bad luck for one of us alone; we live in common. And no one can live happily who looks only at himself, who converts everything into a question of his own profit. You must live for others if you want to live for yourself. This fellowship — kept carefully and reverently, the fellowship that binds us to all human beings and holds that the human race has certain rights in common — does great service to that closer fellowship I was speaking of, friendship. A man who shares much with his fellow man will share everything with his friend.

And this, my excellent Lucilius, is what I'd rather have your subtle logicians teach me: how to help a friend, how to help a fellow human being — not how many senses the word "friend" can carry, or how many things "man" can mean. Look: Wisdom and Folly are lining up on opposite sides. Which do I join? Which side do you want me on? For one man, "friend" and "man" mean the same thing; for another, "friend" and "man" are worlds apart — the first wants a friend for his own advantage, the second wants to make himself an advantage to his friend. And what do you offer me? Word-twisting and syllable-splitting. Apparently, unless I can rig up some cunning premises and stitch a fallacy onto them by false deduction — some lie grown from a seed of truth — I'll never manage to tell what to pursue from what to avoid! I'm ashamed of us. Old men, facing a matter this serious, and we're playing games.

"'Mouse' is a syllable. A mouse eats cheese. Therefore, a syllable eats cheese." Suppose I can't untangle that one — what terrible danger does my ignorance put me in? What a fix I'll be in! No doubt I'd better watch out, or one of these days I'll be catching syllables in a mousetrap — or, if I get careless, a book will eat my cheese. Or wait — here's a shrewder syllogism still: "'Mouse' is a syllable. A syllable does not eat cheese. Therefore a mouse does not eat cheese." What childish nonsense. Is this what we knit our brows over? Is this why we let our beards grow long? Is this what we teach with faces sour and pale?

Do you want to know what philosophy actually offers the human race? Advice. Death is calling one man away; poverty is grinding another; a third is tormented by his neighbor's wealth — or by his own. This man dreads bad luck; that one wants to escape his good luck. Some are mistreated by men, some by the gods. And you're setting up parlor games for me? This is no time for jokes. You have been retained as counsel for suffering humanity. You have promised to help the shipwrecked, the captive, the sick, the poor, the man whose neck waits under the lifted axe. Where are you wandering off to? What are you doing?

The man you're playing word games with is afraid. Help him — take the noose from around his neck. From every side, hands are stretched out to you, begging; ruined lives, lives about to be ruined, plead for some kind of aid; men's hopes and men's resources hang on you. They ask you to pull them out of their restlessness; they ask you to show them, scattered and wandering as they are, the clear light of truth. Tell them what nature has made necessary and what superfluous. Tell them how simple her laws are — how pleasant and unobstructed life is for those who follow them, how bitter and tangled for those who trust opinion instead of nature.

I might grant your logic-games some value in easing men's burdens if you could first show me which part of the burden they will ease. Which of these games drives out lust? Which one even restrains it? I only wish they were merely useless. They are positively harmful. Whenever you like, I can show you plainly that a noble spirit gets impaired and weakened when it's tangled up in such subtleties. I'm ashamed to say what weapons these men hand to soldiers marching out to war against Fortune, and how wretchedly they equip them. Is this the road to the greatest good? Does philosophy get there by if-and-provided-that — by quibbles that would disgrace even a lawyer drafting contracts? For when you deliberately trap the man you're questioning, what are you doing but making him lose his case on a technicality?
