And yet — just as a judge can reinstate a litigant tricked out of his suit, so philosophy has reinstated these victims of quibbling to their former condition. Why do you men walk away from your own mighty promises? You swore to me, in the grandest language, that gold's glitter would dazzle my eyes no more than a sword's gleam; that I would trample, with magnificent steadiness, on everything all men crave and everything all men fear — and now you descend to the schoolteacher's ABC's? What is your answer?

  Is this the path to heaven?

Because that is exactly what philosophy promises me: that I will be made equal to God. That is the invitation I received; that is what I came for. Philosophy — keep your promise!

So, my dear Lucilius, pull back as far as you can from these exceptions and objections of the so-called philosophers. Frankness and simplicity are the right dress for true goodness. Even if you had many years left, you'd have to spend them thriftily to cover life's necessities. As it is, your time is scarce — and what madness it is to spend scarce time learning superfluous things!

Farewell.

Letter 49: On the Shortness of Life

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

A man is truly lazy and careless, my dear Lucilius, if he remembers a friend only when a landscape jogs the memory. And yet there are times when the old familiar places wake a sense of loss that had been stored away in the soul — not restoring dead memories, but rousing sleeping ones, the way the sight of a lost friend's favorite slave, or his cloak, or his house, sharpens the mourner's grief even after time has softened it.

And now — look — Campania, and above all Naples and your beloved Pompeii, have struck me with an astonishingly fresh longing for you. You stand in full view before my eyes. I am at the moment of parting from you all over again. I see you choking down your tears, fighting — and failing — against the feelings that surge up at the very instant you try to stop them. It's as if I lost you only a moment ago.

But what isn't "only a moment ago," once you start using your memory? Only a moment ago I was a boy sitting in the school of the philosopher Sotion; only a moment ago I began pleading cases in the courts; only a moment ago I lost the desire to plead them; only a moment ago I lost the ability. The flight of time is infinitely fast — and no one sees it more clearly than the man looking backward. While we're fixed on the present, we miss it: the rush is so smooth, so headlong, that it feels gentle. Do you want to know why? All past time is in the same place. It wears one face; it lies in one heap. Everything drops into the same abyss. Besides, a thing that is short in its entirety can't contain long stretches. The time we spend living is a point — no, less than a point. And nature has played a trick even on this infinitesimal point, dressing it up to look longer: she made one piece of it infancy, another childhood, another youth, another the long downslope, so to speak, from youth to old age, and old age itself another still. So many steps — for so short a climb!

It was only a moment ago that I saw you off on your journey. And yet that "moment ago" is a healthy share of a lifetime — a lifetime so brief, we should remind ourselves, that it will soon run out altogether. Time never used to seem this fast to me. Now its speed is beyond belief — maybe because I feel the finish-line moving toward me, or maybe because I've finally started paying attention and adding up my losses.

That's why it makes me all the angrier that some men spend the greater part of this time on superfluous things — time which, guard it as carefully as you like, isn't enough even for the necessary ones. Cicero declared that if his days were doubled, he still wouldn't have time to read the lyric poets. Put the logicians in the same bin — only their foolishness is more depressing. The lyric poets are at least openly frivolous; the logicians actually believe they're doing serious work. I don't deny that one should give logic a glance — but only a glance, a nod from the doorway, just enough to make sure you're not being tricked into thinking there's buried treasure in it.

Why torture yourself, why lose weight over some puzzle it's cleverer to scorn than to solve? A soldier at his ease, marching in peacetime, can hunt for trinkets along the road. But when the enemy is closing in from behind and the order comes to quicken the pace, necessity makes him throw away everything he picked up in the easy hours. I have no time to chase disputed inflections of words or to try my cleverness on them.

  Look at the clans assembling, the gates slammed shut,
  the weapons being whetted for the war.

I need a stout heart to hear this din of battle around me without flinching. And everyone would rightly call me mad if — while the old men and the women were hauling rocks for the fortifications, while armed young men inside the gates were waiting for the order to charge out, or demanding it, while enemy spears quivered in our gates and the very ground shuddered with tunnels and mines — if in the middle of all that I sat idle, posing dainty little puzzles like this one: "What you have not lost, you have. You have not lost horns. Therefore, you have horns" — or some other trick built on the same model of pure silliness. And yet you'd be just as right to call me mad if I spent my energy on that stuff now — because I am under siege right now. In the other case the danger would only be outside me, with a wall between me and the enemy. As it is, the deadly threats are in the room with me.

I have no time for such nonsense. There's an enormous piece of work in my hands. What am I to do? Death is on my trail, and life is running away from me. Teach me something that meets that. Bring it about that I stop fleeing from death, and that life stops fleeing from me. Give me courage against hardship; give me calm in the face of the unavoidable. Widen the narrow limits of the time I've been allotted. Teach me that the good in life doesn't hang on life's length but on the use we make of it — and that it is possible, in fact usual, for a man who has lived long to have lived too little. Say to me when I lie down to sleep: "You may not wake again." And when I wake: "You may never sleep again." Say to me when I walk out my door: "You may not come back." And when I come back: "You may never leave again." You are wrong if you think it's only at sea that the gap between life and death is paper-thin. The gap is exactly that narrow everywhere. Death doesn't show himself this close everywhere — but everywhere he is this close.

  The language of truth is simple.

So let's not tangle that language up. Nothing suits a soul aiming at great things less than this sly cleverness.

Farewell.

Letters 50–53

Letter 50: On Our Blindness and Its Cure

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Your letter reached me many months after you sent it, so I saw no point in asking the courier what you were up to. He'd need a remarkable memory to recall that far back! But I hope by now you're living in such a way that I know what you're up to no matter where you are. And what else would you be up to except improving yourself a little every day, dropping some error, and learning that the faults you blame on your circumstances are actually in you? We love to pin certain vices on the place or the time; but those vices will follow us wherever we move.

You know Harpaste, my wife's clown — she's still in my house, a burden that came with a legacy. Personally I have no taste for these sideshow characters; if I ever want to laugh at a fool, I don't have to look far. I can laugh at myself. Well, this clown suddenly went blind. I know the story sounds unbelievable, but I promise you it's true: she doesn't know she's blind. She keeps asking her attendant to move her to different rooms; she says her apartment is too dark.

You can see at once that what makes us smile at Harpaste happens to every one of us. No one understands that he himself is greedy, or that he is grasping. The blind at least ask for a guide; we wander without one, saying: "I'm not ambitious — but nobody can live at Rome any other way. I'm not extravagant — but city life simply demands a big budget. It isn't my fault that I have a temper, or that I haven't settled on any definite plan of life; blame my youth for that." Why do we lie to ourselves? Our trouble isn't outside us. It's inside us, lodged deep in our vitals — and that is exactly why health is so hard to reach: we don't know we're sick.

Suppose we do begin the cure — when will we shake off all these diseases in full bloom? Right now we won't even call in the doctor, whose job would be far easier if he were brought in while the illness was young. A tender, inexperienced mind would follow his advice if he pointed out the right way. No one finds it hard to return to nature except the person who has deserted her. We blush to be taught good sense; but honestly, if it's beneath us to look for a teacher of this art, we should also give up hoping that so great a good will drop into us by accident.

No — we have to work. And to tell the truth, even the work isn't great, provided we begin to shape and rebuild our souls before the crookedness hardens in. But I don't despair even of the hardened case. There is nothing that won't surrender to persistent treatment, to focused and careful attention. However bent the timber, you can straighten it again; heat unbends curved beams, and wood that grew naturally into one shape gets worked into whatever shape we need. How much more easily is the soul reshaped — pliable as it is, more yielding than any liquid! For what is the soul but air in a certain condition? And air, you notice, is more adaptable than anything else precisely because it's thinner than anything else.

So there is nothing, Lucilius, to keep you from having high hopes for us, just because evil has us in its grip right now, or because it has held us a long while. No one gets a good mind before a bad one; the bad mind takes first possession of us all. Learning virtue means unlearning vice. And we should attack the job of freeing ourselves from our faults with all the more courage because, once the good is handed over to us, it's ours forever. Virtue is not unlearned. Opposites sit uneasily where they don't belong, which is why they can be driven out and hustled away; but what arrives in its rightful place stays faithfully put. Virtue is according to nature; vice is her opponent and her enemy. But though virtues, once admitted, cannot leave and are easy to guard, the first steps toward them are hard going, because a weak and sickly mind is afraid of anything unfamiliar. So the mind has to be forced to make a beginning. After that, the medicine isn't bitter; it starts to please us the moment it starts to cure us. With other treatments the pleasure comes only after the recovery; a draught of philosophy is wholesome and sweet in the same swallow.

Farewell.

Letter 51: On Baiae and Morals

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Every man does the best he can, my dear Lucilius! You over there have Etna, Sicily's towering and most celebrated mountain — though I can't work out why Messala (or was it Valgius? I've been reading both) called it "unique," since plenty of regions belch fire, and not just the high ones, where it happens more often — presumably because fire climbs as high as it can — but low ground too. As for me, I do the best I can: I've had to settle for Baiae. And I left it the day after I arrived. Baiae is a place to avoid, because for all its natural advantages, luxury has claimed it as her own private resort.

"What," you say, "should any place be singled out as an enemy?" Not at all. But just as one style of clothing suits the wise and upright man better than another — not because he hates any particular color, but because he thinks some colors don't fit a person who has chosen the simple life — so there are also places that the wise man, or the man on his way to wisdom, will avoid as bad company for good character. If he's planning his retreat from the world, he won't pick Canopus (though Canopus doesn't forbid anybody to live simply), and he won't pick Baiae either: both have become headquarters of vice. At Canopus luxury pampers itself to the limit; at Baiae it's looser still, as if the place itself licensed a certain amount of misbehavior.

We ought to choose surroundings that are healthy not only for the body but for the character. I wouldn't want to live in a torture chamber; I don't want to live in a nightclub either. Watching drunks stagger along the beach, the rowdy carousing of the boat parties, the lakes ringing with singing troupes, and all the other ways luxury — off the leash of the law, so to speak — not only sins but advertises its sins: why should I have to watch all that? We should put the greatest possible distance between ourselves and whatever provokes vice. We should toughen our minds and drag them far away from the seductions of pleasure. One winter unstrung Hannibal: the soft living of Campania took the fight out of the hero whom Alpine snows could not break. He conquered with his weapons and was conquered by his vices.

We too have a war to fight — a kind of war that allows no rest and no leave. The first enemy to be beaten is pleasure, which, as you see, has carried off even the sternest characters. Anyone who has grasped how big a task he's taken on will know there's no room for daintiness or soft handling. What do I want with those hot baths, or the sweating-rooms where they trap the dry steam that's meant to drain your strength? Sweat should be earned by work.

Suppose we did what Hannibal did — halted mid-campaign, gave up the war, and handed our bodies over to be coddled. Everyone would rightly blame us for quitting early — a dangerous move even for a victor, let alone for a man still fighting his way toward victory. And we have even less right to it than the soldiers under Carthage's flag: we risk more than they did if we slacken, and our labor is greater than theirs even if we press on. Fortune is at war with me, and I will not carry out her orders. I refuse the yoke — no, more than that, I shake off the yoke already on me, which takes even greater courage. The soul must not be pampered. Surrender to pleasure and you have also surrendered to pain, to toil, to poverty. Ambition and anger will demand the same rights over me that pleasure got, and I'll be torn apart — pulled to pieces, rather — among all these competing passions. I have set freedom before my eyes, and that is the prize I'm working for. And what is freedom, you ask? Being a slave to nothing — no circumstance, no compulsion, no accident — and forcing Fortune to meet me on level ground. The day I know I have the upper hand, her power is nothing. Shall I take orders from her when death is in my own hands?

A man occupied with thoughts like these should choose an austere and clean place to live. Surroundings that are too pleasant weaken the spirit, and there's no doubt that where you live can sap your vigor. Animals whose hooves have hardened on rough ground can travel any road; fatten them on soft, marshy meadow and their hooves soon wear through. The bravest soldiers come from stony country; the town-bred and the house-raised are slow off the mark. The hand that goes straight from plough to sword never complains of the work, but your sleek, well-dressed dandy folds at the first cloud of dust. Training in rugged country makes a character strong and fits it for great undertakings. It was more honorable for Scipio to spend his exile at Liternum than at Baiae; a fall like his didn't need so soft a setting. Even the men into whose hands Rome's rising fortunes first poured the wealth of the state — Gaius Marius, Gnaeus Pompey, Caesar — did build villas near Baiae, but they set them on the very tops of the mountains. It seemed more like a soldier's way, to look down from a height on the lands spread wide below. Look at the sites they chose, the positions, the style of building, and you'll see they weren't country houses — they were camps. Do you imagine Cato would ever have lived in a pleasure palace so he could count the loose women sailing past, the barges painted every color there is, the roses drifting across the lake — or so he could listen to serenaders brawling in the night? Wouldn't he rather have stayed behind a trench he'd dug with his own hands to hold for a single night? Wouldn't any real man rather have his sleep broken by a war-trumpet than by a chorus of serenaders?

But I've ranted against Baiae long enough — though against vice, never long enough. Vice, Lucilius, is what I want you to hound, without limit and without end, for it has neither limit nor end. If any vice is tearing at your heart, throw it away from you; and if it can't be pried loose any other way, tear out your heart along with it. Above all, drive pleasures out of your sight. Hate them beyond everything else. They are like those bandits the Egyptians call "lovers" — they embrace us only to strangle us.

Farewell.

Letter 52: On Choosing Our Teachers

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

What is this force, Lucilius, that drags us one way when we're aiming another — that pushes us toward the very place we're longing to leave? What is it that wrestles with our spirit and never lets us settle a desire once and for all? We lurch from plan to plan. Not one of our wishes is free, not one is unqualified, not one lasts. "But that's the fool's problem," you say. "Nothing suits him for long." Yes — but how and when do we tear ourselves loose from that folly? No one has strength enough to climb out on his own. Someone has to reach down a hand; someone has to pull us clear.

Epicurus says that certain men have worked their way to the truth without anyone's help, cutting their own path — and he saves his special praise for these, whose drive came from within, who pushed to the front on their own. Others, he says, need outside help: they won't move unless someone leads the way, but they'll follow faithfully. Metrodorus, he says, was one of these. That kind of man is excellent too, but second class. And we're not first class ourselves; we'll be well treated if we're admitted to the second. Don't sneer at a man who can only be saved with someone else's help, either — the will to be saved counts for a great deal too.

You'll find yet a third class of man, and not one to despise: men who have to be forced and driven into doing right, who need not so much a guide as someone to encourage them — practically to shove them along. If you want a specimen of this pattern too, Epicurus tells us Hermarchus was one. And of the last two classes, he's readier to congratulate the one, but he has more respect for the other: both reached the same goal, and it's a greater credit to have gotten the same result out of tougher material.

Imagine two buildings, equal in height and grandeur but nothing alike underneath. One went up on flawless ground, and construction ran straight ahead. The other swallowed its materials into the foundations, sunk into soft and shifting soil, with enormous labor wasted just reaching solid rock. Looking at the two, you see plainly everything the first has achieved, while the larger and harder part of the second is hidden. It's the same with human dispositions. Some are pliable and easy to manage; others have to be hammered out by hand, so to speak, and spend all their effort laying their own foundations. So I'd call the man who has never had trouble with himself the luckier one — but the other man, I feel, has deserved better of himself: he won a victory over the meanness of his own nature, and he didn't stroll to wisdom. He wrestled his way there.

You can be sure this stubborn nature, the kind that demands heavy labor, is the one planted in us. There are obstacles in the road — so let's fight, and call in some reinforcements. "Whom shall I call?" you say. "This man, or that one?" There's another option open to you: go to the ancients. They have the time to help you. Help can come not only from the living but from the dead. Among the living, though, let's choose — not the men who pour out words with the greatest fluency, grinding out platitudes and putting on their own little private exhibitions — not those, I say, but men who teach us by their lives: men who tell us what to do and then prove it by doing it, who show us what to avoid and are never caught doing what they've ordered us to avoid.

Choose as your guide a man you admire more when you see him act than when you hear him speak. I wouldn't stop you from listening to the philosophers who hold public lectures and debates, provided they come before the crowd for the purpose of improving themselves and their hearers, and not to advance their own careers. For what is uglier than philosophy fishing for applause? Does the patient praise the surgeon in the middle of the operation? Be silent, be reverent, and submit to the cure. Even if you shout your approval, I'll hear it as the groan of a man whose sores are being probed. You want to show that you're paying attention, that the grandeur of the subject stirs you? You may — at the proper time. I'll certainly allow you to judge and cast your vote for the better course. Pythagoras made his pupils keep silent for five years; do you think that entitled them to burst into instant applause?

How mad is the man who walks out of a lecture hall happy — because the ignorant clapped! Why does it please you to be praised by men you couldn't praise yourself? Fabianus used to speak to popular audiences, but his listeners kept their self-control. Now and then a great shout of praise would break out, but it was set off by the greatness of his subject, not by the sound of speech sliding out smooth and soft. Let there be a difference between the applause of the theater and the applause of the school; there is a decent way even to give praise. Watch closely, and every act is significant; you can read character in the smallest signs. The lecher is betrayed by his walk, by a movement of the hand, sometimes by a single answer, by a finger touching his head, by a shifting eye. The scoundrel is exposed by his laugh; the madman by his face and bearing. These things show themselves by their marks — and you can tell the character of any man by watching how he gives and receives praise. The philosopher's audience stretch out admiring hands from every corner, and sometimes the adoring crowd practically hangs over the speaker's head. But if you really understand what you're seeing, that isn't praise; it's just applause. Leave the shouting to the arts whose business is pleasing the crowd; let philosophy be worshipped in silence. Young men, true, must sometimes be given free rein to follow an impulse — but only at moments when it really is impulse, when they can't force themselves to stay silent. Praise like that encourages the listeners themselves and spurs the young mind. But let them be stirred by the matter, not the style; otherwise eloquence does them harm, making them fall in love with itself and not with the subject.

I'll set this topic aside for now; it deserves a long study of its own — how the public should be addressed, what liberties a speaker may take with a crowd, and what a crowd may take with a speaker. There's no doubt philosophy has lost something now that she has put her charms up for sale. But she can still be seen in her sanctuary — if the man presenting her is a priest and not a peddler.

Farewell.

Letter 53: On the Faults of the Spirit

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You can talk me into almost anything now: I was recently talked into traveling by water. We cast off on a lazily smooth sea. The sky, I admit, was loaded with dirty clouds, the kind that usually break into rain or squalls; but I figured the few miles from Puteoli to your dear Parthenope could be dashed off quickly, doubtful, lowering sky or not. So to get away faster I struck straight out to sea toward Nesis, meaning to cut across all the inlets. When we were far enough out that it hardly mattered whether I turned back or kept going, the smooth water that had lured me out vanished. It wasn't a storm yet, but the ground-swell was running, and the waves kept coming faster. I began asking the pilot to put me ashore somewhere. He said the coast was rough, with nowhere to land, and that in bad weather nothing frightened him like a lee shore. But I was suffering too badly to think about danger: I was in the grip of that sluggish seasickness that brings no relief — the kind that churns the bile without clearing it. So I laid down the law to my pilot and forced him, like it or not, to make for shore. As we drew near, I didn't wait for things to be done by Virgil's book, until

  Prow faced seawards

or

  Anchor plunged from bow;

I remembered my credentials as a veteran devotee of cold water, and, cloak and all, I let myself down into the sea, just as a cold-water bather should. What do you think I went through, scrambling over the rocks, hunting out a path or making one? I understood then that sailors have good reason to fear the land. It's hard to believe what I endured when I could not endure myself. Take my word for it: the reason Ulysses got shipwrecked at every possible opportunity wasn't so much that the sea-god had a grudge against him from birth — he was simply prone to seasickness. And I too, whenever I have to go anywhere by sea, will reach my destination in the twentieth year.

Once I'd finally settled my stomach (you know one doesn't escape seasickness just by escaping the sea) and refreshed my body with a rubdown, I began to reflect on how completely we forget or ignore our failings — even the bodily ones, which keep reminding us that they exist, to say nothing of the failings that hide deeper as they grow more serious. A slight chill fools us; but when it builds and a genuine fever catches fire, it forces a confession even out of a hardy man who can take a great deal of suffering: I am ill. There's a pain in the foot, a prickling in the joints; we still cover the complaint up and announce we've twisted an ankle or overdone the exercise. While the ailment is uncertain and just beginning, it hunts for a name; but once it starts swelling the ankles too, and has turned both our feet into "right" feet, there's no way around it — we have to confess it's gout.

With diseases of the soul, the opposite holds: the worse a man has one, the less he feels it. Don't be surprised at that, my dear Lucilius. A light sleeper follows visions in his sleep, and sometimes even knows, while sleeping, that he's asleep; but heavy slumber wipes out the very dreams and sinks the spirit so deep it has no sense of itself at all. Why will no man confess his faults? Because he's still inside them. Only the man who has woken up can tell his dream; confessing your faults is proof of a sound mind.

So let us rouse ourselves, so that we can catch our own mistakes and correct them. But philosophy is the only power that can wake us, the only power that can shake us out of this deep sleep. Devote yourself to her entirely. You are worthy of her; she is worthy of you — fall into each other's arms. Say goodbye to every other interest, boldly and openly. There is no studying philosophy in your spare time.

If you were ill, you'd drop your personal affairs and forget your business; you wouldn't think enough of any client to go plead his case during a mild afternoon between attacks. You'd try your hardest to be rid of the illness as fast as possible. Well then — won't you do the same thing now? Push every obstacle aside and clear your time for getting a sound mind; no one reaches it while busy with other things. Philosophy wields her own authority; she appoints her own hours and doesn't accept whatever slot you assign her. She is not a hobby for odd moments but a daily practice; she is mistress, and she commands our attendance. Alexander, when some city promised him part of its territory and half its property, answered: "I didn't invade Asia to accept whatever you might give — you'll keep whatever I might leave." Philosophy says the same to every other occupation: "I do not intend to take the time you have left over. You will keep the time I leave."

Turn to her, then, with your whole soul; sit at her feet; cherish her — and a great distance will open between you and other men. You'll be far ahead of all mortals, and the gods will not be far ahead of you. What will be the difference between you and the gods, you ask? They will live longer. But truly, it takes a great artist to fit a full likeness into a miniature. The wise man's life spreads as wide for him as all eternity does for a god. And on one point the sage actually beats the god: the god is free from fear by the gift of nature; the wise man, by his own gift. What a thing that is — to have the weaknesses of a man and the serenity of a god! The power of philosophy to blunt every blow of chance is beyond belief. No missile can lodge in her body; she is well guarded and impenetrable. Some spears she simply robs of their force, shrugging them off with the loose folds of her gown as if they had no power to harm; others she knocks aside and hurls back so hard they rebound on the sender.

Farewell.

Letters 54–57

Letter 54: On Asthma and Death

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

My bad health had granted me a long furlough — and then, without warning, it attacked again. "What kind of bad health?" you ask. A fair question, since there's no kind I haven't met. But one particular ailment has been assigned to me, so to speak, as my very own. I don't see why I should call it by its Greek name; "shortness of breath" describes it well enough. The attack is very brief, like a squall at sea; it's usually over within an hour. And no wonder — who can spend a long time breathing his last? Every misery and hazard the body has to offer has passed through me, and none seems worse than this one. Naturally so. Anything else is merely being sick; this is gasping out your life. That's why the doctors call it "rehearsing death." One of these days the breath will finally do what it has practiced so often.

Do you think I'm writing this letter in high spirits just because I escaped? Celebrating this so-called recovery would be as absurd as a defendant thinking he's won his case because he's managed to postpone the trial. But even while I was fighting for air, I never stopped resting easy in cheerful, brave thoughts.

"What's this?" I said to myself. "Does death keep testing me? Let it. I tested death a long time ago." "When?" you ask. Before I was born. Death is simply not existing, and I already know what that's like. What was before me will be after me. If there's any suffering in that state, there must have been suffering back then too, before we came into the light of day — and we felt no discomfort then. I ask you: wouldn't you call a man an utter fool for believing that a lamp is worse off after it's put out than before it was lit? We too are lit and put out. In between comes a stretch of suffering; on either side lies deep peace. Unless I'm badly mistaken, my dear Lucilius, this is where we go wrong: we think death lies only ahead of us, when in fact it lies both behind us and ahead. Whatever was before our birth was death. And what difference does it make whether you never begin or you leave off, when the result of either is the same — you do not exist?

I kept up these encouragements to myself — silently, of course, since I didn't have breath to spare for talking. Then little by little the shortness of breath, already dwindled to a sort of panting, came at longer intervals, then slowed, and finally leveled off. Even now, though the gasping has stopped, my breath doesn't come and go naturally; I feel a catch in it, a hesitation. Let it do as it pleases, so long as the sighing doesn't come from my soul. Take this assurance from me: I will not tremble at the final hour. I'm already prepared; I make no plans for a whole day ahead. But the man you should praise — and imitate — is the one who enjoys living and yet doesn't object to dying. Where's the virtue in leaving when you're being thrown out? And yet there's a virtue here too: I am being thrown out, yes, but as if I were choosing to leave. That's why the wise man can never be thrown out — being thrown out means being removed from a place you're unwilling to leave, and the wise man does nothing unwillingly. He escapes necessity because he wills what necessity is going to force on him anyway.

Farewell.

Letter 55: On Vatia’s Villa

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I've just come back from a ride in my litter, and I'm as worn out as if I'd walked the whole distance instead of being carried sitting down. Even being carried is hard work if it goes on long enough — maybe all the more so because it's an unnatural kind of exercise. Nature gave us legs to do our own walking and eyes to do our own seeing. Our luxuries have sentenced us to weakness: what we long refused to do, we've finally lost the power to do. Still, I needed to give my body a shaking — either to jolt loose the bile that had gathered in my throat, if that was my trouble, or, if the very breath in me had somehow thickened, to let the jolting thin it out; I could feel it was doing me good. So I insisted on riding longer than usual, along an attractive stretch of beach that curves between Cumae and Servilius Vatia's country house, pinned like a narrow path between the sea on one side and the lake on the other. A recent storm had packed the sand firm underfoot. As you know, when the waves beat down hard and fast they level a beach, while a long spell of fair weather loosens it, because the sand loses the moisture that binds it together.

As my habit is, I began looking around for something I could use, and my eyes landed on the villa that had once belonged to Vatia. So this was where that famous praetorian millionaire spent his old age! He was famous for nothing but his leisure, and for that alone people counted him lucky. Whenever men were ruined by their friendship with Asinius Gallus, or ruined by hating Sejanus — and later by being close to him, since offending him was no more dangerous than loving him — people would cry out: "O Vatia, you alone know how to live!" But what Vatia knew was how to hide, not how to live. And there is a world of difference between a life of leisure and a life of idleness. While Vatia was alive, I never drove past that place without saying to myself: "Here lies Vatia."

But philosophy, my dear Lucilius, is such a holy thing — so deserving of reverence — that even its counterfeit pleases people. The crowd assumes that anyone who has withdrawn from society is a man of leisure: carefree, self-sufficient, living for himself. In fact those rewards belong only to the wise man. Does a man gnawed by anxiety know how to live for himself? Does he even know — and this comes first — how to live at all? The man who has run away from affairs and from other people, who has been driven into seclusion by the wreckage his own desires made, who can't bear to see his neighbor happier than he is, who has gone to ground in fear like some timid, sluggish animal — that man is not living for himself. He is living for his belly, his sleep, and his lust, and that is the most shameful thing of all. Living for no one doesn't automatically mean living for yourself. Still, steadiness and sticking to a purpose count for so much that even sheer laziness, stubbornly kept up, manages to command a certain respect.

I can't describe the villa itself with any accuracy. I know only the front of the house and the parts on public display, the parts any passerby can see. There are two grottoes, cut by hand at enormous labor, each as big as the most spacious hall: one never admits the sun; the other holds it until sunset. A stream runs through a grove of plane-trees, drawing its supply from both the sea and Lake Acheron; it cuts through the grove like a race-course, and it's big enough to stock fish, though its water is constantly being drawn off. When the sea is calm they leave it alone; only when storms give the fishermen a forced holiday do they dip into that well-stocked reserve. But the most convenient thing about the villa is this: Baiae is right next door. The villa is spared all the annoyances of that resort and enjoys all its pleasures. I appreciate these attractions myself, and I believe it's a villa for every season of the year. It faces the west wind — intercepts it, in fact, so completely that Baiae never gets it. Vatia was no fool, clearly, when he picked this spot to park his leisure in, though by then his leisure was already barren and decrepit.

But the place where you live contributes very little to your peace of mind. It's the mind that must make everything agreeable to itself. I have seen men gloomy in a cheerful, lovely villa, and I have seen men in the middle of total solitude who looked for all the world like men buried in business. So don't conclude that your life is badly placed just because you aren't here in Campania. Anyway, why aren't you? Just send your thoughts this way. You can converse with absent friends as often as you like, and for as long as you like. In fact we enjoy this pleasure — the greatest pleasure there is — all the more when we're apart. Presence makes us spoiled: because we can talk or sit together whenever we please, once we've parted we give no thought at all to the people we just saw. And we should bear absence cheerfully for another reason: everyone is constantly absent from his friends, even when they're present. Count it up — first the nights spent apart, then each friend's separate engagements, then each one's private studies and trips to the country — and you'll see that foreign travel doesn't steal much from us. A friend should be held in the spirit, and the spirit never loses him; the spirit can see whomever it wants to see, every single day.

So share your studies with me, and your meals, and your walks. Life would be lived within cramped walls indeed if anything were closed off to our thoughts. I see you, my dear Lucilius; I hear you at this very moment. I am so completely with you that I wonder whether I should stop writing you letters and start sending you notes.

Farewell.

Letter 56: On Quiet and Study

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

May I die if silence isn't the first requirement for a man who has shut himself away to study! And now imagine the assortment of noises booming around my ears. I have lodgings directly over a public bathhouse. Picture every variety of sound that can make a man hate his own ears. When the strongmen are working out, swinging their lead weights around, straining hard — or pretending to strain hard — I hear the grunting; and every time they let out the breath they've been holding, I hear the whistling, high-pitched panting. When some lazy customer settles for a cheap rubdown, I hear the smack of the masseur's hand on his shoulders — a different note depending on whether the palm lands flat or cupped. Then a professional ball-player shows up and starts shouting the score: that's the finishing touch. Add the occasional rowdy being hauled off, or a pickpocket caught in the act; add the man who loves the sound of his own voice in the bath, and the enthusiasts who hit the swimming pool with a tremendous smack and splash. Besides all these — people whose voices, if nothing else, are at least normal — imagine the hair-plucker, forcing out his thin, shrill cry over and over to advertise himself, never quiet except when he's plucking someone's armpits and making the customer shriek instead of him. Then the cake-seller with his whole repertoire of cries, and the sausage-man, and the sweets-vendor, and every hawker of food crying his wares, each with his own distinctive tune.

"You must have nerves of iron," you say, "or be deaf as a post, if your mind holds together in all that assorted, clashing racket — when our friend Chrysippus is driven half to death by the steady stream of people telling him good morning!" But I swear to you this uproar means no more to me than waves or falling water — even though I'm told a certain tribe once moved their city for the sole reason that they couldn't stand the thunder of a Nile cataract. Words distract me more than noise does. Words demand attention; noise just fills the ears and beats on them. In the category of sounds that surround me without distracting me, I count passing carriages, the machinist on my block, a saw-sharpener nearby, and the fellow demonstrating little pipes and flutes at the Trickling Fountain — who doesn't so much sing as shout.

An intermittent noise, I'll admit, bothers me more than a steady one. But by now I've toughened myself against the whole lot; I could endure even a boatswain screeching out the stroke to his rowers. I force my mind to pay attention to itself and not be lured outside. Let all outdoors be bedlam, so long as there's no riot within — so long as fear and desire aren't brawling in my chest, so long as stinginess and extravagance aren't at each other's throats. What good is a quiet neighborhood if your emotions are in an uproar?

  It was night, and all the world lay lulled to rest.

Not true. There is no real rest unless reason has done the lulling. Night doesn't banish our troubles; it brings them out where we can see them. It merely trades one set of worries for another. Even in bed, the sleepless hours are as harassing as the day was. Real tranquility is what an uncorrupted mind enjoys when it relaxes. Look at the poor wretch courting sleep in his hushed mansion: so that no sound may disturb his ear, his whole troop of slaves is ordered silent, and whoever approaches him must come on tiptoe — and there he tosses from side to side, snatching at scraps of sleep between complaints, insisting he heard sounds he never heard at all. And why? His soul is making the noise. The soul is what must be quieted; that's where the mutiny needs putting down. Don't assume a soul is at peace just because the body lies still. Sometimes quiet means disquiet.

So whenever that ungovernable sluggishness gets a grip on us, we should rouse ourselves to action and keep busy with good pursuits. Great generals, when they see their troops turning mutinous, put them to work or keep them occupied with small raids. A busy man has no time for mischief, and nothing is more certain than this: the vices of leisure are worked off by work. People have often supposed I retired from public life out of disgust with politics, out of regret at my luckless, thankless position. Yet here in this retreat — where fear and weariness drove me — my ambition sometimes sprouts up fresh. It never quieted down because it was rooted out; it quieted down because it got tired, or maybe just annoyed at how its plans were going. It's the same with luxury. It seems to have left for good; then, once we've made our public profession of frugality, it starts nagging at us, and in the middle of our economies it goes hunting for the pleasures we merely walked away from but never condemned. And the more stealthily it comes back, the harder it hits. Vices out in the open are milder; even a disease is further along the road to a cure once it breaks out of hiding and shows its strength. Greed, ambition, and the other diseases of the mind — you can be sure they do their worst harm when they hide behind a show of health.

We look retired. We are not. If we had retired sincerely — if we had truly sounded the signal for retreat and turned our backs on the world's glitter — then, as I said above, nothing outside us could distract us. No chorus of men, no chorus of birds, could break in on good thoughts once those thoughts had grown steadfast and sure. A mind that startles at voices and chance sounds is unstable; it hasn't yet withdrawn into itself. It carries a residue of anxiety inside, a rooted fear that makes it prey to worry — as our Vergil says:

  I, whom no hurled spear could once make flee,
  nor Greeks advancing in their massed array,
  now startle at each sound and fear each breeze,
  afraid both for my child and for the load I bear.

The first man is the wise man: he doesn't flinch at the whistle of spears, or the clashing armor of the massed enemy, or the roar of a city falling around him. The second man knows nothing; he fears for his belongings, pales at every sound; any cry at all knocks him flat, because he takes it for the battle-shout; the slightest stir leaves him breathless with fright. His load is what makes him afraid. Pick anyone you please from among Fortune's favorites, dragging their many responsibilities, hauling their many burdens, and you'll see Vergil's man — "afraid both for his child and for the load he bears."

You may be sure you're at peace with yourself, then, when no noise reaches you, when no voice can shake you out of yourself — not a flattering voice, not a threatening one, not just an empty racket buzzing meaninglessly around you. "Fine," you say, "but isn't it sometimes simpler just to get away from the uproar?" Granted. And I'm going to do exactly that: I'll move out of this place. I only wanted to test myself and get some practice. Why should I go on being tormented, when Ulysses found so simple a remedy for his crew against even the Sirens?

Farewell.

Letter 57: On the Trials of Travel

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

When the time came to return to Naples from Baiae, I had no trouble convincing myself that a storm was brewing — anything to avoid another trip by sea. But the road was so deep in mud the whole way that you might say I made a voyage after all. That day I went through the athlete's complete routine: first the anointing — with mud, in our case — and then the dusting with sand, courtesy of the Naples tunnel. No place could feel longer than that prison. Nothing could be dimmer than those torches, which didn't help us see through the darkness so much as see the darkness itself. And even if the place had any light, the dust would smother it — dust that is oppressive and unpleasant even in the open air. Imagine it in there, where it rolls back on itself and, penned in with no ventilation, blows back into the faces of the very people who stirred it up. So we suffered two opposite plagues at once: on the same road, on the same day, we fought both mud and dust.

Still, the gloom gave me something to think about. I felt a certain jolt in my mind — a change, though not fear — caused by the novelty and the nastiness of an unusual situation. I'm not talking about myself here, of course; I'm nowhere near being a perfect man, or even a middling one. I mean the man over whom Fortune has lost her power. Even his mind will feel the jolt; even his color will change. There are certain feelings, my dear Lucilius, that no courage can escape; nature keeps reminding courage what perishable stuff it is. So even the brave man will knit his brow at grim news, and shudder at a sudden apparition, and go dizzy standing at the rim of a cliff and looking down. That isn't fear. It's a natural reaction that reason cannot argue away. That's why some brave men, perfectly willing to shed their own blood, can't bear to watch someone else's being shed. Some people go weak and faint at the sight of a fresh wound; others react the same way to handling — or even looking at — an old, festering one. And some men will take a sword-stroke more readily than they can watch one being struck.

What I felt, then, as I said, was a change, not a real disturbance. At the first glimpse of daylight my good spirits came back — unbidden, uncommanded. And I began musing to myself on what fools we are to fear some things more than others, when every one of them ends the same way. What difference does it make whether a watchtower falls on you or a mountain? None — you'll find none at all. Yet some men fear the mountain more, though both are equally deadly. That's how blind fear is: it looks not at the outcome but at the machinery of the outcome.

Now perhaps you think I'm talking about the Stoics, who hold that the soul of a man crushed under a great weight can't survive, but is scattered on the spot because it had no free way out. I am not — and the people who say that are, in my opinion, wrong. Just as a flame cannot be crushed out, because it slips around the edges of whatever presses down on it; just as air cannot be damaged by whips and blows, or even sliced apart, but flows back around whatever it yields to — in the same way the soul, which is made of the finest particles there are, cannot be trapped or destroyed inside the body. Because of its very fineness, it slips through the very thing that is crushing it. As a lightning-bolt, however widely its strike flashes, makes its return through a narrow crack, so the soul, subtler even than fire, has a way of escape through any part of the body. So the only real question is this: can the soul be immortal? But of one thing be sure: if the soul does survive the body's crushing, then the soul cannot possibly be crushed out — precisely because it does not perish. The rule of immortality admits no exceptions, and nothing can harm what is everlasting.

Farewell.

Letter 58

Letter 58: On Being

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Until today I never fully understood how scant of words our language is — how downright poverty-stricken. We happened to be talking about Plato, and a thousand subjects came up that needed names and had none; and there were others that once had names but have lost them, because we became too fussy about using them. But who can afford to be fussy in the middle of poverty? There is an insect the Greeks call oestrus — a gadfly that drives cattle mad and scatters them all over their pasture. Our old word for it was asilus. Don't take my word for it; take Virgil's:

  Near Silarus' groves and the green oaks
  of Alburnus there swarms an insect that the Romans
  call asilus — in Greek the name is oestrus.
  Fierce, with a harsh and strident buzz, it drives
  the panicked herds stampeding through the woods.

From which I gather the word has died out. And, not to keep you waiting too long: there used to be plain, uncompounded words in circulation. Men once said cernere ferro — "to decide it with the sword" — as Virgil again will prove:

  Great heroes, born in many lands, had come
  to settle matters, man to man, with steel.

This "settling of matters" we now express only with the compound decernere; the plain verb has gone obsolete. And the ancients used to say iusso, instead of iussero, for "I shall have ordered" in a conditional clause. Again, don't take it from me; take it from Virgil:

  The rest of the soldiers shall conduct the fight
  with me, where I shall bid.

My purpose in piling up these examples is not to show off how much time I've wasted on the study of language. I only want you to see how many words that were everyday currency in Ennius and Accius have grown moldy with age — and how even Virgil, whose pages are thumbed daily, has had some of his words filched out from under us.

"And what," I suppose you'll say, "is all this preamble for?" I won't keep you in the dark. I want, if possible, to say the word essentia — "essence" — in your hearing and get a favorable reception. If I can't manage that, I'll risk the word anyway and put you out of humor. I have Cicero as my authority for it, and I count him a powerful authority. If you want a more recent witness, I'll cite Fabianus — careful of speech, cultivated, so polished in style that he'll satisfy even our fussy modern taste. For what else can we do, my dear Lucilius? How else are we to find a word for what the Greeks call ousia — the indispensable thing, the natural underpinning of everything? So I beg you: allow me to use essentia. I promise to exercise the privilege you grant me as sparingly as possible; maybe I'll be content merely to hold the right without using it.

Yet what good will your indulgence do me when — look at this — I still can't express in Latin the very idea that gave me my excuse to rail at our language's poverty? And you'll condemn our narrow Roman limits even more when you learn that the word I can't translate is one syllable long. "What word?" you ask. The Greek on: "being." You think I'm slow; you think the translation is lying right there in the open — just say "that which is." But I notice a great difference: you're forcing me to render a noun with a verb. Still, if it must be done, "that which is" it shall be.

A friend of ours, a man of great learning, was saying today that Plato uses this idea in six different senses. I'll explain all six to you — but first I have to point out that there is such a thing as a genus and such a thing as a species.

For the moment we're hunting the primary genus — the one on which all the different species depend, the source of every classification, the term that gathers up all universal ideas. We'll find it if we start from particulars and reckon backward, for the particulars will lead us up to the first notion. "Man" is a species, as Aristotle says; so is "horse"; so is "dog." So we need some common bond that embraces all three and holds them subordinate to itself. What is it? "Animal." Now we have the beginnings of a genus, "animal," containing man, horse, and dog. But some things have life — anima — and yet are not animals: we all agree that plants and trees are alive, which is why we speak of them living and dying. So "living things" takes a still higher place, since animals and plants both fit inside it. Some things, however, lack life — rocks, for instance. So yet another term outranks "living things," and that term is "substance." I'll classify substance by saying that all substances are either animate or inanimate. But there is still something above substance, because we say some things possess substance and other things lack it. What, then, is the term from which everything else derives? The one we gave that awkward name a moment ago: "that which is." For that term divides into species, so that we can say: that which is either possesses substance or lacks it.

This, then, is what genus means — the primary, the original, the "general," to play on the word. There are other genera, of course, but they are special genera. "Man," for example, is a genus: it contains species by nation — Greek, Roman, Parthian; by color — white, black, yellow; and individuals too — Cato, Cicero, Lucretius. Insofar as "man" includes many kinds, it counts as a genus; insofar as it sits beneath a higher term, it counts as a species. But the genus "that which is" is general absolutely: no term stands above it. It is the first word in the classification of things, and everything falls under it.

The Stoics would set yet another genus above this one, more primary still; I'll come to it in a moment, once I've shown that the genus discussed above deserves its first place, capable as it is of including everything. So: I divide "that which is" into two species — things with substance and things without. There is no third class. How do I divide substance? By saying it is either animate or inanimate. How do I divide the animate? "Some things have mind, while others have only life." Or put it this way: "Some things have the power of movement, of walking, of changing place, while others are rooted in the ground and are fed and grow only through their roots." And into what species do I divide animals? They are either perishable or imperishable.

Now, certain Stoics take the primary genus to be "the something," and here is their reasoning. They say: "In the order of nature, some things exist and some things do not — and even the things that do not exist are part of the order of nature. What these are will occur to you at once: centaurs, giants, and every other figment of faulty reasoning, things that have taken on a definite shape even though they have no bodily reality."

But now I return to what I promised: how Plato sorts everything that is into six different classes.

The first class of "that which is" cannot be caught by sight or touch or any of the senses; it can be grasped only by thought. The generic concept — "man" in general — never comes within range of the eyes; a particular man does: Cicero, Cato. Nobody has ever seen "animal"; we think it, we don't see it. What we see is some particular animal — a horse, a dog.

The second class, according to Plato, is that which is prominent and stands out above everything else; this, he says, exists pre-eminently. The word "poet" gets used loosely of anyone who writes verse; but among the Greeks it has come to be the badge of one man. Say "the poet," and everyone knows you mean Homer. So what is the pre-eminent Being? God, surely — greater and more powerful than anything else.

The third class is made up of the things that exist in the proper sense of the term. They are countless, but they lie beyond our sight. "What things?" you ask. Plato's own private furniture, so to speak: he calls them "ideas," and from them all visible things are created, and on their pattern everything is shaped. They are immortal, unchangeable, inviolable. Listen to what an "idea" is — or rather, what Plato takes it to be: "The idea is the everlasting pattern of the things that nature creates." Let me unpack that definition to set the matter in a clearer light. Suppose I want to paint your portrait. You yourself are the pattern of the picture: from your face my mind receives a certain outline, which it then embodies in its own handiwork. The look that teaches and guides me, the pattern I set out to imitate — that is the "idea." Nature possesses an infinite number of such patterns — of men, of fish, of trees — and everything nature has to make is worked out on their model.

In fourth place we'll put "form." To understand what form means you'll have to pay close attention — and hold Plato, not me, to account for the difficulty. But then, there are no fine distinctions without difficulty. A moment ago I used the painter as an illustration. When that artist wanted to paint Virgil in colors, he gazed at Virgil himself. Virgil's outward appearance was the "idea" — the pattern of the intended work. What the artist draws from that pattern and embodies in his own work is the "form." Where's the difference, you ask? The one is the pattern; the other is the shape taken from the pattern and put into the work. The artist follows the one; the other he creates. A statue has a certain look: that look of the statue is the "form." And the pattern itself has a certain look, which the sculptor studied as he carved: that is the "idea." One more distinction, if you want it: the form is in the artist's work; the idea is outside the work — and not just outside it, but prior to it.

The fifth class is the things that exist in the ordinary sense of the term. These are the first things that actually concern us; here belongs everything around us — men, cattle, objects. And into the sixth class goes whatever has a kind of make-believe existence, like void, or time.

Whatever is solid to the sight or the touch, Plato refuses to count among the things that exist in the strict sense. For such things are in flux, endlessly shrinking or growing. None of us is the same man in old age that he was in youth; none of us is the same tomorrow as he was yesterday. Our bodies are swept along like running water; everything we see races past along with time; nothing we look at stands still. I myself, even as I remark on all this change, have changed. This is just what Heraclitus says: "We go down twice into the same river, and yet into a different river." The stream keeps its name, but the water has already flowed past. This is more obvious in a river than in a man, of course; but we mortals are carried past no less swiftly ourselves — which makes me marvel at our madness: we cling with such affection to the body, the most fleeting thing of all, and we fear that some day we may die, when every instant is the death of our previous condition. Won't you stop fearing that what happens every day may happen once?

So much for man — a substance that flows away and slumps, exposed to every blow. But the universe, too, immortal and enduring as it is, changes and never stays the same. It still has within it everything it ever had, but held differently than before: it keeps rearranging itself.

"Very well," you say, "and what good will I get from all this fine reasoning?" None, if you insist on an answer. But just as an engraver rests his eyes when they've been strained too long, calls them away from the work and "feasts" them, as the saying goes, so we ought at times to unbend the mind and refresh it with a little entertainment. Only, let even your entertainment be work; even from amusements, if you stay alert, you'll pick out something worth keeping. That's my habit, Lucilius: from every field of thought, however far from philosophy, I try to extract something and put it to use. And what could be less likely to reform character than the subjects we've just been sifting? How can Plato's "ideas" make me a better man? What can I draw from them to rein in my appetites? Maybe just this: all the things that pander to our senses, that inflame and excite us, Plato denies a place among the things that really exist. They are imaginary; they wear a certain appearance for the moment, but none of them is lasting or solid — and yet we crave them as if they would exist forever, as if we would possess them forever.

We are weak, watery creatures standing in the middle of unrealities. So let us turn our minds toward the things that last. Let us look up at the ideal shapes of all things, hovering on high, and at God moving among them, planning how to defend from death what he could not make deathless — because its material forbade it — and so to overcome by reason the defects of the body. For all things endure, not because they are everlasting, but because they are protected by the care of their governor; what was imperishable would need no guardian. The Master Builder keeps them safe, overriding the weakness of the material by his own power. Let us despise everything so worthless that we're left wondering whether it exists at all.

And let us reflect on this at the same time: if Providence rescues from its perils the world itself, which is no less mortal than we are, then to some extent our own little bodies can be made to linger longer on earth by our own providence — if we learn to control and check the pleasures that destroy the greater part of mankind. Plato himself, by taking pains, made it to old age. He had the luck, true, of a strong and sound body — his very name came from his broad chest — but sea voyages and desperate adventures had drained much of his strength. Even so, by frugal living, by setting a limit on everything that whets the appetite, and by painstaking attention to himself, he reached old age despite the many obstacles. You know, I'm sure, that thanks to his careful living Plato had the good fortune to die on his own birthday, having completed exactly his eighty-first year. Wise men of the East who happened to be in Athens at the time sacrificed to him after his death, convinced that a lifespan so full was too much for a mortal man, since he had rounded out the perfect number of nine times nine. I have no doubt he'd have gladly given back a few of those days — and the sacrifice with them.

Frugal living can carry you to old age; and old age, to my mind, is no more to be refused than it is to be craved. There is a pleasure in being in your own company as long as possible — once you've made yourself worth enjoying. So here is the question on which we must record a judgment: should one shrink from extreme old age and hasten the end by one's own act, instead of waiting for it to come? The man who sits sluggishly waiting for his fate is next door to a coward — just as a man is immoderately fond of wine if he drains the jar dry and sucks up even the dregs. But we'll ask this question too: is the last stretch of life the dregs, or is it the clearest and purest part of all — provided the mind is unimpaired, the senses are still sound and give the spirit their support, and the body is not worn out and dead before its time? For it makes a great deal of difference whether a man is lengthening his life or lengthening his death.

But if the body can no longer perform its service, why shouldn't one free the struggling soul? Perhaps one ought to do it a little before the debt falls due, so that when it does fall due, he isn't unable to pay. And since the danger of living wretchedly is greater than the danger of dying soon, a man is a fool who won't stake a little time for the chance of a great gain. Few men have lasted through extreme old age to death unimpaired; many have lain inert, making no use of themselves. After that, how much crueler do you think it is to have lost a piece of your life than to have lost the right to end it?

Don't hear me out reluctantly, as if this verdict pointed straight at you; just weigh what I say. And it is this: I will not abandon old age, if old age keeps me whole for myself — whole, that is, in my better part. But if it begins to shatter my mind and pull its faculties down piece by piece, if it leaves me not life but mere breath, I shall rush out of a house that is crumbling and tottering. I will not escape illness by dying, so long as the illness is curable and no hindrance to my soul. I will not lay hands on myself because of pain; to die that way is to be defeated. But if I learn that the pain must be endured forever, I shall depart — not because of the pain itself, but because it will block everything I live for. The man who dies just because he is in pain is a weakling and a coward; the man who lives merely to brave out his pain is a fool.

But I'm running on too long — and besides, there's matter here to fill a whole day. And how will a man be able to end his life if he can't end a letter? So farewell — a word you'll read with more pleasure than all my deadly talk about death. Farewell.

Letters 59–63

Letter 59: On Pleasure and Joy

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Your letter gave me great pleasure — and kindly allow me to use that word in its everyday sense, without insisting on its strict Stoic meaning. For we Stoics hold that pleasure is a vice. Very likely it is; but it's the word we all reach for when we want to name a happy state of mind. I'm well aware that if we test words against our formula, even pleasure has a bad reputation, and joy is something only the wise man attains. For "joy" is an elevation of the spirit — of a spirit that trusts in the goodness and truth of what it possesses. In common speech, though, we say we take great "joy" in a friend's election to the consulship, or his wedding, or the birth of his child — events which, far from being joys, are more often the openings of sorrows to come. No: the mark of real joy is that it never stops, and never turns into its opposite.

So when our Virgil speaks of

  The evil joys of the mind,

the phrase is eloquent, but not strictly accurate. No joy can be evil. He gave the name "joy" to pleasures, and in doing so he said what he meant: that people take delight in their own harm.

Still, I wasn't wrong to say I received great "pleasure" from your letter. Even when an ignorant man's delight has an honorable cause, his emotion is unruly, ready at any moment to swing off in another direction. So I call it "pleasure": it starts from an opinion about a false good; it escapes control; it runs to excess.

But back to the point — let me tell you what delighted me in your letter. You have your words under control. You aren't swept away by your own language, or carried past the limits you set for yourself. Many writers get lured by the charm of some attractive phrase into topics they never meant to discuss. Not you: everything is compact and fitted to the subject. You say all you want to say, and you mean even more than you say. That is proof of the weight of your material — it shows that your mind, like your words, contains nothing superfluous, nothing inflated.

I do find metaphors — not reckless ones, but the kind that have earned their keep through use. I find similes too; and if anyone forbids similes, insisting they belong to poets alone, then he clearly hasn't read our older prose writers, who had not yet learned to strike poses for applause. Those writers, whose eloquence was plain and aimed only at proving the case, are full of comparisons; and I think comparisons are necessary — not for the reasons poets need them, but as props for our weakness, to bring speaker and listener alike face to face with the subject.

As it happens, I'm reading Sextius at this very moment — a sharp man, a philosopher who writes in Greek but holds to the Roman standard of ethics. One of his similes struck me especially: an army marching in hollow square, through country where the enemy might appear from any quarter, ready for battle. "That," he says, "is exactly what the wise man should do: keep his fighting qualities deployed on every side, so that wherever attack threatens, his defenses are ready at hand and answer the commander's signal without confusion." We see this in armies serving under great generals: every unit understands the general's order at once, because the troops are arranged so that a signal from one man reaches cavalry and infantry at the same moment. And this, he says, is even more necessary for people like us. Soldiers have often feared an enemy for no reason at all, and the stretch of road they thought most dangerous has turned out safest. But folly gets no such rest. Fear haunts it at the front of the column and at the rear; both flanks are in a panic. Peril chases folly and meets it head-on. It flinches at everything; it is never prepared; it takes fright even at its own reinforcements. But the wise man is fortified against every assault. He is alert. Let poverty attack, or sorrow, or disgrace, or pain — he will not retreat. He will walk undaunted straight at them, and straight through the middle of them.

We human beings are chained and weakened by many vices. We've wallowed in them so long that getting clean is hard: we aren't merely stained by them — we are dyed. But rather than hop from one figure to the next, let me raise a question I often turn over in my own heart: why does folly grip us so stubbornly? First, because we don't fight it hard enough, don't struggle toward salvation with everything we have. Second, because we don't put enough trust in what the wise have discovered — we don't drink in their words with open hearts; we come at this enormous problem in far too casual a spirit. And how could anyone learn enough in the fight against his vices, if the time he gives to learning is only the time left over from his vices? None of us goes deep. We skim the surface, and we consider a few stray minutes spent in the search for wisdom plenty — more than plenty — for a busy man.

What hinders us most of all is that we are too easily satisfied with ourselves. If we meet someone who calls us good men, or sensible men, or holy men, we recognize ourselves in the portrait. Not content with moderate praise, we accept whatever shameless flattery heaps on us, as if it were owed to us. We agree with the people who declare us the best and wisest of men, even though we know they lie constantly. We're so pleased with ourselves that we want to be praised for a virtue at the very moment we're indulging its opposite. A man hears himself called "most gentle" while he's inflicting torture, "most generous" while he's looting, "most sober" in the middle of drunkenness and lust. It follows that we don't want to be reformed — precisely because we believe we're already the best of men.

Alexander was ranging as far as India, laying waste to tribes barely known even to their own neighbors. During the siege of one city, while he was riding around the walls hunting for the weak spot in the fortifications, an arrow struck him. He kept up the siege for a long time anyway, determined to finish what he had begun. But as the wound dried and the bleeding stopped, the pain grew; his leg went slowly numb against his horse's flank; and when at last he was forced to withdraw, he said: "Everyone swears I am the son of Jupiter — but this wound cries out that I am a man." Let's do the same. Flattery makes a fool of each of us according to his station. Let's answer the flatterer: "You call me a man of sense. But I know how many of the things I crave are useless, and how many of the things I want will do me harm. I don't even have the knowledge a full stomach teaches the animals — the right measure of my own food and drink. I still don't know how much I can hold."

Now I'll show you how to know that you are not wise. The wise man is joyful, cheerful and calm, unshaken; he lives on the same plane as the gods. Now go and examine yourself. If you are never downcast; if no anxiety harasses your mind with worry about what's coming; if day and night your soul keeps its even, unswerving course, upright and content with itself — then you have reached the greatest good a mortal can possess. But if you go seeking pleasures of every kind, in every direction, then know this: you are as far short of wisdom as you are short of joy. Joy is the destination you're aiming for, but you have lost the road if you expect to arrive in the middle of riches and official titles — in other words, if you go looking for joy surrounded by worries. Those things you chase so eagerly, as if they held happiness and pleasure, are causes of grief.

All people of this kind, I insist, are chasing joy; they simply don't know where a joy that is both great and lasting can be found. One hunts for it in dinner parties and self-indulgence; another in campaigning for office and a crowd of clients around him; another in a mistress; another in the idle display of culture, in literature with no power to heal. All of them are led astray by delights that are deceptive and short-lived — like drunkenness, which pays for a single hour of hilarious madness with days of sickness; like applause and the roar of an enthusiastic crowd, which are won, and paid for, at the cost of enormous disquiet.

So reflect on this: the effect of wisdom is a joy that is unbroken and continuous. The wise man's mind is like the sky above the moon: eternal calm fills that region. Here, then, is your reason for wanting to be wise — the wise man is never without joy. And that joy springs from one source only: the knowledge that you possess the virtues. Only the brave, the just, the self-controlled can rejoice. "What," you ask, "don't the foolish and the wicked rejoice too?" No more than lions rejoice over a kill. When men have worn themselves out with wine and lust, when the night gives out before the debauch does, when the pleasures they've heaped on a body too small to hold them begin to fester — then, in their misery, they groan out Virgil's lines:

  You know how we passed that last of nights
  among false-glittering joys.

Pleasure-lovers spend every night among false-glittering joys, and every night as if it were their last. But the joy that comes to the gods, and to those who imitate the gods, is never interrupted, and never ends. It would end, if it were borrowed from outside. Because it is not in anyone else's power to give, it is not subject to anyone else's whim. What Fortune has not given, Fortune cannot take away.

Farewell.

Letter 60: On Harmful Prayers

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I file a complaint, I bring a lawsuit, I am angry. Do you still want what your nurse, your guardian, your mother prayed you would get? Don't you see yet what evil they were praying for? How hostile to us are the wishes of our own families! And the more completely those wishes come true, the more hostile they are. It no longer surprises me, at my age, that trouble has dogged us from earliest youth: we grew up amid our parents' curses. May the gods hear our own prayer too — one that asks for no favors!

How long shall we go on asking the gods for things, as if we still couldn't support ourselves? How long will we keep filling the marketplaces of our great cities with grain? How long must whole populations gather the harvest for us? How long will fleets of ships, drawing on more than one sea, deliver the makings of a single meal? A bull is filled by grazing a few acres; one forest is enough for a herd of elephants. Man alone forages both the earth and the sea. What then — did nature give us such insatiable bellies, along with such puny bodies, that we should outdo the biggest and hungriest animals in sheer greed? Not at all. How little it takes to satisfy nature! A very little sends her away content. It isn't the natural hunger of our bellies that costs us dear; it's our restless craving. So the people who, as Sallust puts it, "listen to their bellies" should be counted among the animals, not among men — and some of them not even among the animals, but among the dead. He truly lives who is useful to many; he truly lives who makes use of himself. But those who creep into a hole and go torpid are no better off in their houses than they would be in their tombs. You could carve such a man's name right there on the marble over his door — for he has died before he is dead.

Farewell.

Letter 61: On Meeting Death Cheerfully

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Let us stop wanting what we have always wanted. I, at least, am doing it: in my old age I have stopped wanting what I wanted as a boy. My days and my nights go to this one task; this is my work, this is what I think about — putting an end to my old faults. I am trying to live every day as if it were a complete life. I don't snatch at it as if it were my last; I simply regard it as though it could be. This very letter is written in that spirit — as if death might call me away in the act of writing it. I am ready to depart; and I will enjoy life precisely because I am not over-anxious about the date of my departure.

Before I grew old, I tried to live well. Now that I am old, I shall try to die well — and dying well means dying gladly. See to it that you never do anything unwillingly. What is bound to be necessity if you resist it is not necessity if you want it. I mean this: the man who takes his orders gladly escapes the bitterest part of slavery — doing what he does not want to do. The unhappy man is not the one who acts under orders; it's the one who acts against his will. So let us set our minds to want whatever our circumstances demand — and above all, to think about our own end without sadness.

We must prepare for death before we prepare for life. Life is well enough furnished, but we are greedy about the furnishings: something always seems to be missing, and always will. Having lived long enough depends neither on our years nor on our days, but on our minds. I have lived long enough, my dear Lucilius. I have had my fill. I await death.

Farewell.

Letter 62: On Good Company

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

People deceive us when they claim that a crowd of obligations keeps them from their liberal studies.
