They fake their engagements, and multiply them — when their only real engagement is with themselves. As for me, Lucilius, my time is free. It really is free: wherever I am, I am my own master. I don't hand myself over to my affairs; I lend myself to them, and I don't go hunting for excuses to waste time. Wherever I find myself, I carry on my own reflections and turn over some healthy thought in my mind. When I give myself to my friends, I don't withdraw from my own company; and I don't linger with the people some occasion, or some case arising from my official position, has attached to me. Instead, I spend my time with the best men of all: whatever land they lived in, whatever age, I let my thoughts fly to them. Demetrius, the best of men, I take everywhere with me; I leave the wearers of purple and fine linen and talk with him, half-naked as he is, and I hold him in the highest esteem. And why shouldn't I? I have found that he lacks nothing. Anyone at all can despise all things; no one can possess all things. The shortest way to riches is to despise riches. But our friend Demetrius lives not merely as if he had learned to despise everything, but as if he had handed everything over for other people to own.

Farewell.

Letter 63: On Grief for Lost Friends

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I am sorry to hear that your friend Flaccus has died — but I would not have you grieve more than is fitting. That you should not grieve at all, I hardly dare demand; and yet I know it would be the better way. But where is the man blessed with the steadiness of soul to manage it, unless he has already risen far above Fortune's reach? Even such a man would feel the sting of a loss like this — but only a sting. As for the rest of us, our tears can be forgiven, provided they don't run to excess, and provided we have checked them ourselves. When we lose a friend, our eyes should be neither dry nor streaming. Let your tears flow, but let them also cease. We may weep; we must not wail.

Does the rule I'm laying down for you seem harsh? Then consider that the greatest of Greek poets granted the right of weeping for one day only — he tells us that even Niobe remembered to eat. Do you want to know where all the lamentation and extravagant weeping comes from? Through our tears we are hunting for proof that we feel the loss. We aren't surrendering to sorrow — we're parading it. Nobody goes into mourning for an audience of himself alone. Shame on our miserable folly: there is a streak of vanity even in our grief!

"What," you say, "am I to forget my friend?" It's a short-lived memory you're promising him, if it lasts only as long as your grief. Soon enough some passing thing, however trivial, will smooth that brow of yours into a laugh. And I don't put off to any longer date than that the softening of every regret, the quieting of even the bitterest grief. The moment you stop watching yourself, this picture of sorrow you've been contemplating will fade. Right now you are standing guard over your own suffering — but even while you stand guard, it slips away, and the sharper it is, the sooner it ends.

Let's work at making the memory of those we've lost a pleasant one. Nobody willingly returns to a subject he can't think about without pain. It's inevitable that the names of those we loved and lost come back to us with a certain sting — but there is a pleasure even in that sting. As my friend Attalus used to say: "The memory of dead friends is pleasant the way certain fruits are pleasantly tart, or the way the very bitterness of an extremely old wine is what delights us. Give it enough time, and everything that hurt is extinguished, and the pleasure reaches us pure." If we take his word for it, "thinking of friends alive and well is like enjoying cakes and honey; remembering friends who have passed away gives a pleasure that carries a touch of bitterness. Yet who will deny that even bitter, sharp-edged things can whet the appetite?" There, for my part, I disagree with him. To me, the thought of my dead friends is sweet — even comforting. For I had them as if I would one day lose them; I have lost them as if I have them still.

So, Lucilius, act in keeping with your usual serenity of mind, and stop putting the wrong interpretation on Fortune's gifts. Fortune has taken away — but Fortune gave. Let us enjoy our friends greedily, because we don't know how long the privilege will be ours. Think how often we leave them behind when we set out on some long journey; think how often we fail to see them even while we're staying in the same place. We'll soon realize that we lost too much of their time while they were alive. And will you put up with people who neglect their friends utterly, and then mourn them abjectly — who love no one until they have lost him? The reason they grieve so extravagantly at such times is that they're afraid people will doubt whether they ever loved at all; too late, they go looking for proofs of their feeling. If we have other friends, we treat them shabbily and rate them cheaply if they count for so little that they can't console us for the loss of one. If we have no other friends, then we have done ourselves a worse injury than Fortune has done us: Fortune robbed us of one friend, but we robbed ourselves of every friend we failed to make. Besides, a man who couldn't love more than one never had much love even for that one. If a thief made off with a man's only tunic, and the man chose to stand there wailing over his loss rather than look around for some way to escape the cold, for something to throw over his shoulders — wouldn't you call him an utter fool?

You have buried someone you loved; now look for someone to love. It is better to replace a friend than to weep for him. What I'm about to add is, I know, a worn old commonplace, but I won't leave it out just because everyone says it: time ends a man's grief even when he never chose to end it himself. And for a sensible man, the most shameful cure for sorrow is simply growing tired of sorrowing. I would rather you abandon grief than have grief abandon you. Stop grieving as soon as you can — because even if you wanted to keep it up, you couldn't keep it up for long. Our ancestors fixed one year as the limit of mourning for women — not because women needed to mourn that long, but so that they would mourn no longer. For men, no term is set, because for a man no term of mourning is honorable. Even so, show me one woman — out of all the pitiful creatures who had to be dragged from the funeral pyre or pried off the corpse — whose tears lasted a whole month. Nothing turns offensive as quickly as grief. While it's fresh, it finds consolers and draws people to its side; once it goes chronic, it gets laughed at — and rightly, because by then it is either put on or foolish.

And the man writing you all this is the very one who wept so uncontrollably for his dear friend Annaeus Serenus that I must be counted — much against my will — among the examples of men defeated by grief. Today I condemn what I did, and I understand the main reason I mourned so wildly: I had never imagined that his death could come before mine. The only thought that ever occurred to me was that he was the younger — much younger, at that. As if the Fates called us up in order of age!

So let us think constantly about our own mortality, just as much as about the mortality of all those we love. Back then I should have said: "My friend Serenus is younger than I am — but what does that matter? He ought to die after me, but he may die before me." Because I never said it, Fortune caught me unprepared with one sudden blow. Now is the time for you to reflect, not only that all things are mortal, but that their mortality follows no fixed schedule. Whatever can happen at any time can happen today. So let us reflect, my beloved Lucilius, that we shall soon arrive where this friend — to our sorrow — has arrived already. And perhaps, if the tale the wise men tell is true and some place stands ready to welcome us, then the friend we think we have lost has only been sent on ahead.

Farewell.

Letters 64–65

Letter 64: On the Philosopher's Task

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

  And restless among the harmless flocks he prays
  that a foam-flecked boar will cross his path, or a tawny lion
  come stalking down from the hills.

That is the mood exactly. I want something to overcome — something I can test my endurance against. And this is another remarkable thing about Sextius: he shows you the grandeur of the happy life without ever making you despair of reaching it. You come away understanding that it stands high above you, but that it is within reach of anyone with the will to climb.

Virtue herself has the same double effect: she makes you admire her, and she makes you hope to win her. In my own case, at any rate, just contemplating wisdom takes up much of my time. I stare at her in amazement, the way I sometimes stare at the sky itself, which I often look at as if I were seeing it for the first time. And so I revere the discoveries of wisdom and their discoverers. Stepping into the inheritance of so many predecessors is a delight. It was for me that they stored up this treasure; it was for me that they did all that work. But we should act like a responsible head of the household: we should make what we inherited grow. That inheritance will pass from me to my descendants larger than it came. There is still much work left, and much will always be left; a person born a thousand generations from now will not be shut out from the chance of adding something more.

But suppose the old masters really did discover everything. One thing will always be new: the use of those discoveries — studying them, sorting them, applying them. Say we have inherited prescriptions for healing the eyes. I don't need to hunt for new ones; but the ones we have still must be fitted to the particular disease, and to the particular stage of the disease. Use this one to soothe an inflamed eyelid, that one to bring down the swelling; this one to head off a sudden stab of pain or a rush of tears, that one to sharpen the vision. Then combine them, watch for the right moment, and apply the right treatment to each case.

The cures for the spirit were also discovered by the ancients; but learning how and when to apply them — that is our task. The people who went before us accomplished a great deal, but they did not finish the job. Even so, they deserve reverence; we should honor them the way we honor gods. Why shouldn't I keep statues of great men to fire my enthusiasm, and celebrate their birthdays? Why shouldn't I greet them, every time, with respect and honor? The reverence I owe my own teachers I owe in the same measure to those teachers of the human race, the source from which so much good first flowed. If I meet a consul or a praetor, I will pay him all the honor his office is used to receiving: I will dismount, bare my head, and yield the road. Then what of Marcus Cato, the Elder and the Younger? Laelius the Wise? Socrates and Plato, Zeno and Cleanthes? Shall I let them into my soul with anything less than the highest marks of respect? I revere them in earnest, and I always stand up in honor of such noble names.

Farewell.

Letter 65: On the First Cause

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Yesterday I split the day with illness. It claimed the whole morning for itself; in the afternoon it gave way to me. So I began by testing my spirit with some reading. When that went well, I dared to demand more of it — or maybe I should say, to indulge it more. I wrote a little, and with more concentration than usual, since I am wrestling with a difficult subject and refuse to be pinned. In the middle of this, some friends came by, with the declared intention of using force to stop me — as if I were a sick man sneaking pleasures he'd been forbidden. So conversation took the place of writing, and from that conversation I'll pass along to you the question that is still in dispute. We have appointed you the referee. You have more work on your hands than you think, because the case has three sides.

Our Stoics, as you know, say there are two things in the universe from which everything comes: cause and matter. Matter lies there inert, a stuff ready for any use, but certain to stay idle if no one sets it moving. Cause — by which we mean reason — molds matter and turns it wherever it wants, producing all the concrete things we see. So for anything at all, there must first be something it is made from, and then an agent it is made by. The first is its material; the second is its cause.

All art is imitation of nature, so let me carry these general principles over to the things human beings make. A statue involves matter that had to submit to the artist's hands, and an artist who had to give that matter its shape. In the statue's case, then, the material was bronze and the cause was the sculptor. Every object works the same way: it consists of what it is made from and who made it.

The Stoics hold that there is one cause only — the maker. But Aristotle thinks "cause" can be used in three ways. "The first cause," he says, "is the matter itself, without which nothing can be made. The second is the workman. The third is the form that is stamped on every work" — on a statue, say. (His own word for this is the eidos.) "And there is a fourth as well: the purpose of the work as a whole." Let me show you what each of these means. Bronze is the "first cause" of the statue, since the statue could never have been made without something to cast and mold it from. The "second cause" is the artist: without a workman's skilled hands, that bronze could never have taken on the statue's outlines. The "third cause" is the form, because our statue could never be called The Spear-Bearer or The Boy Tying His Hair if that particular shape had not been stamped into it. The "fourth cause" is the purpose of the work — for if there had been no purpose, the statue would not have been made. And what is a purpose? Whatever drew the artist on, whatever he was pursuing when he made it: money, if he made it to sell; fame, if he worked for a reputation; piety, if he made it as a gift for a temple. Surely that too is a cause contributing to the statue's existence — or do you think we should refuse to count, among the causes of a finished thing, the very element without which it would never have been finished?

To these four Plato adds a fifth: the pattern, which he calls the "idea" — for this is what the artist kept gazing at while he created the work he had set out to make. It makes no difference whether that pattern is outside him, where he can turn his eyes to it, or inside him, conceived and set up there by himself. God has these patterns of all things within himself; his mind holds the harmonies and measures of everything that is to be brought into being. He is filled with those shapes Plato calls the "ideas" — imperishable, unchangeable, immune to decay. And that is why, though men die, humanity itself — the idea of man, the pattern man is molded on — lasts; and while men labor and perish, it suffers no change at all.

So by Plato's count there are five causes: the material, the agent, the form, the model, and the end in view. And last comes the result of all of them. Take the statue again, since that's where we started: the material is the bronze; the agent is the artist; the form is the shape fitted onto the material; the model is the pattern the agent imitated; the end in view is the purpose in the maker's mind; and the result of all these is the statue itself. The universe, in Plato's opinion, has all the same elements. The agent is God. The source is matter. The form is the shape and arrangement of the visible world. The pattern, of course, is the model on which God made this vast and supremely beautiful creation. And the purpose is his reason for making it. You ask what God's purpose is? Goodness. At least, that is what Plato says: "What was God's reason for creating the world? God is good, and no good person begrudges anything good. So he made it the best world he could." Hand down your verdict, then, judge: tell us who seems to you to say what is truest — not who says what is absolutely true. That is as far beyond our reach as truth itself.

Now, this crowd of causes assembled by Aristotle and Plato takes in either too much or too little. If they count as "causes" everything without which a thing cannot be made, they have named too few. Time would have to be included — nothing is made without time. And place — if there is nowhere for a thing to be made, it will not be made. And motion — nothing is made or destroyed without motion; there is no art, no change of any kind, without it. But what I am after is the first cause, the general cause. And that must be simple, since matter, too, is simple. What is cause, then? Surely it is Creative Reason — in other words, God. All those items you listed are not a series of separate, independent causes; they all hang on one, the one that actually creates. You say form is a cause? Form is only what the artist stamps on his work — a part of a cause, not the cause. The pattern is not a cause either, but an indispensable tool of the cause. The pattern is as necessary to the artist as his chisel or his file; without them art gets nowhere; but they are neither parts of the art nor causes of it. "Well then," someone will say, "the artist's purpose — the thing that moves him to create — that is the cause." Perhaps it is a cause; but not the efficient cause, only an accessory one. And accessory causes are countless; what we are after is the general cause. In fact, when Plato and Aristotle call the whole universe — the finished, perfected work — a cause, they fall short of their usual sharpness. There is a great difference between a work and the cause of a work.

Now either give your verdict or do what is easier in cases like this: declare that the matter is not clear and send it back for another hearing. But I hear you object: "What pleasure do you get from wasting time on these puzzles? They rid you of no passion, they drive out no desire." For my part, I raise and discuss these questions as things that greatly calm the spirit; and I examine myself first, and then the world around me. And even now I am not wasting time, whatever you think. All these questions — provided they aren't minced and shredded into useless hair-splitting — lift and lighten the soul. The soul is weighed down by a heavy load; it longs to be freed and to return to the elements it once belonged to. For this body of ours is the soul's burden and its punishment. Under that pressing weight the soul is crushed and held in chains — unless philosophy comes to its rescue, tells it to breathe freely again in the contemplation of the universe, and turns it from things earthly to things divine. There the soul has its liberty; there it can roam; meanwhile it slips its guards and is restored in heaven. Think of craftsmen doing close, delicate work that strains their eyes: if their light is scanty or uncertain, they go outside and, in some park set aside for the public's recreation, treat their eyes to the free light of day. Just so the soul, shut up in this gloomy, darkened house, escapes into the open sky whenever it can, and finds its rest in contemplating the universe.

The wise man — and the seeker after wisdom too — is tied tightly to his body, but in his better self he is elsewhere; his thoughts are fixed on higher things. He is like a man bound by an oath of service: he treats his lifespan as his term of enlistment. He has trained himself neither to love life nor to hate it; he endures a mortal lot, knowing a larger one awaits him.

Do you forbid me to contemplate the universe? Do you drag me away from the whole and confine me to a part? May I not ask what the beginnings of all things are — who shaped the universe, who took the confused, sluggish jumble of matter and sorted it into its parts? May I not ask who the Master Builder of this universe is, how so vast a bulk was brought under law and order, who gathered the scattered atoms, who separated the tangled elements and gave outward form to what lay in one great shapeless heap? Or where all this flood of light comes from — whether it is fire, or something brighter still? Am I not to ask these questions? Must I stay ignorant of the heights I came down from? Whether I will see this world once only, or be born many times? Where I go afterward — what home waits for my soul when it is released from the terms of its human slavery? Do you forbid me a share in heaven — which is to say, do you order me to live with my head bowed? No. I am above that. I was born for something greater than to be my body's property, and I regard this body as nothing but a chain clamped on my freedom. So I hold it out as a kind of buffer against Fortune, and I let no blow pass through it to reach my soul. My body is the only part of me that can be injured; inside this vulnerable house, my soul lives free. This flesh will never drive me to fear, or to any pretense unworthy of a good man. I will never tell a lie for this little body's sake. When it seems right, I will dissolve our partnership. And even now, while we are bound together, we are not equal partners: the soul hears every dispute in its own court. To despise the body is sure freedom.

To come back to our subject: this freedom is helped enormously by the contemplation we were just discussing. All things are made of matter and of God. God governs matter, which surrounds him and follows him as its guide and leader. What creates — that is, God — is more powerful and more precious than matter, which merely receives his action. God's place in the universe is the soul's place in a man; what matter is to the world, the body is to us. So let the lower serve the higher. Let us be brave in the face of every hazard. Let us not fear wrongs, or wounds, or chains, or poverty. And what is death? Either an end or a crossing. I do not fear ending — it is the same as never having begun. And I do not shrink from crossing into another state, because nowhere will I be as cramped as I am here.

Farewell.

Letter 66

Letter 66: On Various Aspects of Virtue

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I have just seen Claranus, an old schoolmate of mine, for the first time in many years. You don't need me to add that he is an old man now; but I can tell you that I found him vigorous in spirit and sturdy, even though he is wrestling with a frail and feeble body. Nature was unfair to him — she gave a rare soul a poor house to live in. Or maybe she did it deliberately, to prove to us that an absolutely strong and happy mind can hide under any exterior at all. Either way, Claranus overcomes every one of his handicaps: he began by despising his own body, and from there he has worked his way up to despising everything else that doesn't matter. The poet who sang

  Virtue shows more pleasing in a form that's fair,

was mistaken, in my opinion. Virtue needs nothing to set it off; it is its own great glory, and it makes holy whatever body it lives in. At any rate, I have started to see Claranus in a different light. To me he looks handsome — as straight and well-built in body as he is in mind. A great man can come out of a shack; just so, a great and beautiful soul can come out of an ugly, insignificant body. I think Nature breeds certain men of this stamp on purpose, to prove that virtue can be born absolutely anywhere. If she could have produced souls by themselves, naked, she would have done it. As it is, she does something even greater: she produces men whose bodies are obstacles — and who break through the obstruction anyway. Claranus, I believe, was produced as a demonstration, so that we could understand that a soul is not disfigured by an ugly body; rather, it is the body that is beautified by the loveliness of the soul.

Now, though Claranus and I spent only a few days together, we had many conversations, and I'll pour them out to you here just as they came. The first day we dug into this problem: how can all goods be equal if they come in three kinds? According to our school's teaching, certain goods are primary — joy, peace, the welfare of one's country. Others are of the second order, molded out of unhappy material — endurance under suffering, self-control during a severe illness. For the first kind we will pray outright; for the second, only if the need arises. And there is a third variety besides: a modest walk, a calm and honest face, a bearing that suits the wise man. Now how can these things be equal to one another, when we ought to pray for some of them and avoid others?

If we want to sort them out, we had better go back to the First Good and ask what its nature really is. It is this: the soul that gazes straight at truth; that is skilled in what should be sought and what should be avoided; that sets its values not by opinion but by nature; that ranges through the whole universe and turns its contemplating gaze on everything in it; that keeps strict watch over its own thoughts and actions, equally great and equally forceful in both; that stands above hardships and above flattery alike, surrendering to neither extreme of fortune; that rises above everything that comes, blessings and disasters both — a soul absolutely beautiful, perfectly furnished with grace and with strength together, healthy and lean, unruffled, undismayed, a soul no violence can shatter, a soul that no stroke of luck can puff up or beat down. A soul like that is virtue itself. That's what virtue would look like if it ever came into a single view and showed itself all at once, complete. But it has many aspects. They unfold as life varies and as actions differ; yet virtue itself never becomes greater or smaller. The Supreme Good cannot shrink, and virtue cannot slide backward; instead, it transforms itself — now into this quality, now into that — shaping itself to the role it has to play. Whatever it touches, it makes like itself; it dyes everything its own color. It beautifies our actions, our friendships, and sometimes whole households that it has entered and set in order. Whatever it has handled it makes, then and there, lovable, remarkable, admirable.

So the power and greatness of virtue cannot rise any higher, because what is already greatest has no room to increase. You will find nothing straighter than straight, nothing truer than true, nothing more temperate than perfect self-control. Every virtue is limitless — for limits belong to things with fixed measurements. Constancy cannot advance any further; neither can faithfulness, or truthfulness, or loyalty. What can be added to what is perfect? Nothing — otherwise the thing you added to wasn't perfect. And nothing can be added to virtue either; if anything could be added, something must have been missing. Honor, likewise, permits no additions: it is honorable precisely because of the qualities I have named. And what else? Don't you think propriety, justice, and lawfulness belong to the same type — held within fixed bounds? Being able to grow is proof that a thing is still imperfect.

The good, in every case, obeys these same laws. The advantage of the state and the advantage of the individual are yoked together; you can no more separate them than you can separate the praiseworthy from the desirable. Therefore the virtues are all equal to one another; and so are the works of virtue, and so are all the men lucky enough to possess them. The virtues of plants and animals, on the other hand, are perishable — frail, fleeting, uncertain. They spring up and they sink back down, and for that reason they aren't rated at the same value. But human virtues fall under one rule only, because right reason is single and of one kind only. Nothing is more divine than the divine, or more heavenly than the heavenly. Mortal things decay, fall, wear out, grow, drain away, refill; because their lot is uncertain, there is inequality among them. But the nature of divine things is one. And reason is nothing else than a piece of the divine spirit set inside a human body. If reason is divine, and no good is without reason, then every good is divine. Furthermore, there is no distinction between things divine — so there is none between goods, either. Therefore it follows that joy, and a brave unyielding endurance of torture, are equal goods; in both there is the same greatness of soul — relaxed and cheerful in the one case, combative and braced for battle in the other.

What — don't you think the courage of the man who bravely storms the enemy's stronghold equals the courage of the man who endures a siege with total patience? Scipio is great when he rings Numantia with his siege-works and squeezes it, driving an enemy he could not conquer to turn their own hands against themselves. But great, too, are the souls of the defenders — men who know that as long as the road to death lies open, no blockade is complete; men who breathe their last in the arms of liberty. In the same way all the other virtues are equal to one another: tranquility, simplicity, generosity, constancy, even-temperedness, endurance. For underneath them all lies a single virtue — the one that keeps the soul straight and unswerving.

"What then," you say, "is there no difference between joy and the unyielding endurance of pain?" None at all, as far as the virtues themselves are concerned; a very great one, however, in the circumstances in which each virtue gets displayed. In the one case there is a natural relaxing and loosening of the soul; in the other, an unnatural pain. So the circumstances, between which a great gulf can be drawn, belong to the category of indifferent things — but the virtue shown in each case is equal. Virtue is not changed by the material it works on. Hard, stubborn material does not make it worse; pleasant, joyful material does not make it better. So virtue necessarily stays equal. In each case, what is done is done with equal rightness, equal wisdom, and equal honor. The goodness on both sides is equal, and neither man can rise above it — not the one in his joy, not the other in his suffering; each is doing as well as a human being can do. And two goods, neither of which can possibly be better, are equal.

For if things outside virtue could shrink it or swell it, then the honorable would stop being the only good. Grant that, and honor has perished altogether. Why? Let me tell you: because no act is honorable that is done unwillingly, under compulsion. Every honorable act is voluntary. Mix in reluctance, complaints, cowardice, or fear, and it loses its best feature — its own approval of itself. What is not free cannot be honorable, for fear means slavery. The honorable is entirely free of anxiety; it is calm. If it objects, if it laments, if it counts anything as an evil, it falls into turmoil and starts floundering in deep confusion — on one side the appearance of right calls it forward; on the other, the suspicion of evil drags it back. So when a man is about to do something honorable, he should not count any obstacle as an evil — inconvenient, maybe, but not evil — and he should will the deed and do it willingly. Every honorable act is done without orders and without compulsion; it is pure, and mixed with no evil at all.

I know what you may say to me at this point: "Are you trying to make us believe it doesn't matter whether a man feels joy or lies on the rack wearing out his torturer?" I could answer: "Epicurus also maintains that the wise man, even while he is being roasted inside the bronze bull of Phalaris, will cry out, 'How pleasant! This doesn't touch me at all.'" Why should you be surprised that I call the man reclining at a banquet and the victim standing firm under torture possessors of equal goods, when Epicurus maintains something far harder to believe — that it is pleasant to be roasted? But the answer I actually give is this: there is a great difference between joy and pain. If I'm asked to choose, I will seek the one and avoid the other. The first is according to nature; the second is against it. Rated by that standard, a great gulf stands between them. But when it comes to the virtue involved, the virtue is the same in each case, whether it walks in through joy or through sorrow. Vexation and pain and the other inconveniences count for nothing, because virtue overpowers them. Just as the brightness of the sun dims every lesser light, so virtue, by its sheer greatness, shatters and overwhelms all pains, annoyances, and wrongs; wherever its radiance reaches, every light that shines without virtue's help goes out. Inconveniences, when they run into virtue, matter no more than a storm-cloud matters to the sea.

Here is the proof: the good man will rush without hesitation toward any noble deed. Put the executioner in his path, the torturer, the stake — he will keep going, looking not at what he must suffer but at what he must do. He will entrust himself to an honorable deed as readily as he would to a good man; he will count it profitable to himself, safe, lucky. And he will hold the same opinion of an honorable deed loaded with sorrow and hardship that he holds of a good man who is poor, or wasting away in exile. Come now, set a good man rolling in wealth beside a man who has nothing — except that in himself he has everything. They will be equally good, though their fortunes are utterly unequal. The same standard, as I said, applies to things as well as to men: virtue is just as praiseworthy living in a sound and free body as in one that is sick or in chains. So you will not praise your own virtue any more if Fortune has favored you with a sound body than if she has given you a body crippled in some limb — that would be like rating a master low because he is dressed like a slave. Everything Fortune rules over is mere property: money, the body, position — weak things, shifting, quick to perish, held on uncertain terms. But the works of virtue are free and unconquered — no more worth seeking when Fortune treats them kindly, and no less when adversity is weighing them down.

Now, friendship among men corresponds to desirability among things. You would not, I imagine, love a good man more for being rich than for being poor, or love a strong, muscular man more than a slender, delicate one. Then neither will you seek or love a good thing that is cheerful and peaceful more than one that is full of struggle and sweat. Or if you do, then of two equally good men you will care more for the neat, well-groomed one than for the dusty, unkempt one. Next you will go so far as to prefer a good man sound in all his limbs and without a blemish to one who is weak or half-blind; and little by little your pickiness will reach the point where, of two equally just and sensible men, you will choose the one with the long curling hair! Whenever the virtue in two men is equal, the inequality in everything else about them disappears. All those other things are not parts of them; they are accessories. Would any father judge his own children so unfairly as to love a healthy son more than a sickly one, or a tall child of striking stature more than a short or middle-sized one? Wild beasts play no favorites among their young; they lie down and suckle them all alike. Birds share out the food fairly. Ulysses hurries back to the rocks of his Ithaca as eagerly as Agamemnon speeds to the kingly walls of Mycenae. No man loves his homeland because it is great; he loves it because it is his own.

And what is the point of all this? For you to know that virtue looks on all her works with the same eyes, as if they were her children — equally kind to all of them, and more deeply kind to the ones that meet hardship. Even parents lean with extra affection toward the children they pity. Virtue does not exactly love her troubled, heavily burdened works more than the rest; but like a good parent, she gives them more of her care.

Why is no good greater than any other good? Because nothing is more fitting than what fits, and nothing more level than what is level. You cannot say that one thing is "more equal" to a given object than another thing is; and so nothing is more honorable than what is honorable. Accordingly, if all the virtues are equal by nature, then the three varieties of goods are equal too. This is what I mean: feeling joy with self-control and suffering pain with self-control are equal. The joy in the one case does not outrank the steadfastness in the other — the steadfastness that swallows the groan while the victim is in the torturer's grip. Goods of the first kind are desirable; goods of the second are worthy of admiration; and they are equal all the same, because whatever hardship comes attached to the second kind is outweighed by the good itself, which is so much greater. Anyone who believes them unequal has turned his gaze away from the virtues themselves and is inspecting the packaging. True goods have the same weight and the same measure; the counterfeit kind are full of empty space, so that when they are put on the scale they come up short, however imposing and grand they look to the eye.

Yes, my dear Lucilius: the good that true reason approves is solid and everlasting. It strengthens the spirit and lifts it up so that it stays on the heights forever. The things the mob thoughtlessly praises as goods merely puff us up with empty joy; and the things feared as if they were evils merely strike panic into men's minds — for the mind is spooked by the mere appearance of danger, the same way animals are. Both of them distract and sting the spirit for no good reason: the one does not deserve joy, and the other does not deserve fear. Reason alone is unchangeable; reason alone holds fast to its decisions. For reason is not the slave of the senses but their ruler. Reason is equal to reason, as one straight line is equal to another; therefore virtue is equal to virtue, since virtue is nothing else than right reason. All the virtues are reasons. Reasons are reasons if they are right reasons; and if they are right, they are equal. As reason is, so are its actions; therefore all actions are equal too — for since they resemble reason, they resemble one another. And I hold that actions are equal to each other exactly insofar as they are honorable and right. There will be great differences, of course, as the material varies — now broader, now narrower; now glorious, now humble; now wide-ranging, now confined. But the best part in every case is equal: they are all honorable. In the same way, all good men, precisely insofar as they are good, are equal. There are differences of age — one is older, another younger; of body — one is handsome, another ugly; of fortune — this man is rich, that one poor; this one is influential, powerful, known to whole cities and peoples, that one is unknown to almost everybody and obscure. But in the one respect in which they are good, they are equal.

The senses cannot judge what is good and what is evil. They do not know what is useful and what is not. They cannot file an opinion unless the thing is right in front of them; they can neither see into the future nor remember the past; and they do not know what follows from what. Yet it is exactly from that kind of knowledge that a coherent sequence and succession of actions is woven, and a unity of life is created — a unity that will run on a straight course. Reason, then, is the judge of good and evil. Whatever is foreign and external she counts as dross; whatever is neither good nor evil she rates as a mere accessory, insignificant and trivial. For all her good resides in the soul.

But there are certain goods that reason counts as primary and goes after on purpose — victory, for example, good children, the welfare of one's country. Certain others she counts as secondary; these only show themselves in adversity — for example, an even mind in enduring a severe illness or exile. And certain goods are indifferent, no more according to nature than against it — a discreet walk, say, or a composed way of sitting in a chair. For sitting is no less natural an act than standing or walking. The two higher kinds of goods differ from each other. The primary ones are according to nature — such as taking joy in the devotion of your children and in the well-being of your country. The secondary ones are contrary to nature — such as courage under torture, or endurance of thirst when illness sets fever burning through a man's vitals. "What," you say, "can anything contrary to nature be a good?" Of course not. But the thing this good springs from is sometimes contrary to nature. Being wounded, wasting away over a fire, being broken down by disease — those things are contrary to nature. But to keep an unconquerable soul in the middle of such distresses — that is fully in accordance with nature. To put my point briefly: the material a good is concerned with is sometimes against nature, but a good itself never is — because no good exists without reason, and reason follows nature.

"Then what is reason?" you ask. It is copying nature. "And what is the greatest good a man can possess?" It is to conduct yourself according to what nature wills.

"There is no doubt," says the objector, "that peace which was never attacked brings more happiness than peace won back at the cost of great slaughter. There is no doubt, either, that unbroken health brings more happiness than health forced back to soundness, as it were, through endurance and suffering, after an illness that threatened life itself. And in the same way, there will be no doubt that joy is a greater good than a soul grinding to the bitter end through the torment of wounds or the stake." Not at all. Things that come by chance admit of enormous differences, because they are rated by their usefulness in the eyes of the people who experience them. But with goods, the only point to consider is that they agree with nature — and this is equal in all of them. When we vote for someone's motion in the Senate, it cannot be said that one senator is "more in accord with the motion" than another; they all vote for the same motion alike. I say the same of the virtues: they all accord with nature. And I say it of goods too: they all accord with nature. One man dies young, another in old age, still another in infancy, having caught nothing more than a first glimpse out into life. All of them were equally subject to death, even if death let one of them travel further along the road of life, cut off the second in his flower, and broke off the third at the very beginning. One man gets his release at the dinner table. Another's sleep slides straight over into the sleep of death. Another is snuffed out in the middle of his pleasures. Now set beside these the people who have been run through by the sword, or bitten to death by snakes, or crushed under falling buildings, or twisted piece by piece out of existence on the rack. Some of these exits can be counted better, some worse; but the dying itself is equal in all of them. The roads to the end are different; the end is one and the same. Death has no degrees of greater or less. It has the same limit in every case: the closing of a life.

The same is true of goods, I assure you. You will find one good in the middle of pure pleasure, another in the middle of sorrow and bitterness. One manages Fortune's favors; the other overpowers her assaults. Each is equally a good, though one travels a level, easy road and the other a rough one. And the end of them all is the same: they are goods, they deserve praise, they walk in company with virtue and reason. Virtue makes everything it acknowledges equal to everything else.

You needn't be surprised that this is one of our principles. In the writings of Epicurus you will find two goods that make up his Supreme Good, his blessedness: a body free from pain and a soul free from disturbance. These goods, once complete, do not increase — for how can what is complete increase? Suppose the body is free from pain: what can be added to the absence of pain? Suppose the soul is composed and calm: what can be added to that tranquility? Perfectly fair weather, polished to its purest brilliance, does not admit of becoming any clearer. Just so, when a man takes care of his body and of his soul, weaving his good out of both, his condition is perfect; he has found the complete answer to his prayers, so long as there is no storm in his soul and no pain in his body. Whatever extra delights fall to him beyond these two things do not increase his Supreme Good; they merely season it, so to speak — a little added spice. For the absolute good of human nature is satisfied with peace in the body and peace in the soul.

I can show you, right now, a graded list of goods in Epicurus's own writings, very like the one in our own school. There are some things, he declares, that he would prefer to have happen to him — bodily rest free from every discomfort, and a soul at leisure, delighting in the contemplation of its own goods. And there are other things which he would prefer not to happen, but which he praises and approves all the same — for example, the kind of resignation in the face of failing health and terrible suffering that I mentioned a moment ago, and that Epicurus himself showed on the last and most blessed day of his life. He tells us that he had to endure excruciating agony from a diseased bladder and an ulcerated stomach — pain so acute that it could not possibly get worse — "and yet," he says, "that day was none the less happy." And no man can spend such a day in happiness unless he possesses the Supreme Good.

So even in Epicurus we find goods that one would prefer not to experience — goods which, when circumstances decide the matter, must nevertheless be welcomed, approved, and ranked with the highest. We cannot say that the good which rounded out a happy life — the good for which Epicurus gave thanks with the last words he ever spoke — is unequal to the greatest goods there are.

Allow me, my excellent Lucilius, one bolder statement still: if any goods could be greater than others, I would choose the ones that look harsh over the mild and alluring ones — and I would pronounce them greater. Breaking your way through difficulties is a bigger accomplishment than keeping your joy within bounds. It takes the same reason, I know perfectly well, for a man to bear prosperity honorably and to bear misfortune bravely. The soldier who sleeps calmly in front of the ramparts when no enemy is attacking the camp may be every bit as brave as the man who, with the tendons of his legs cut through, props himself up on his knees and does not let his weapons fall. But it is the blood-stained soldier coming back from the front who hears the shout: "Well done, hero!" And so I would give the greater praise to the goods that have stood trial, that show courage, that have fought it out with Fortune. Would I hesitate whether to praise the maimed and shriveled hand of Mucius above the uninjured hand of the bravest man in the world? There Mucius stood, despising the enemy and despising the fire, watching his own hand drip blood over the flames on his enemy's altar — until Porsenna, the very king who had been demanding his punishment, grew envious of the hero's glory and ordered the fire carried away against the victim's will.

Why shouldn't I count this good among the primary goods — and count it greater, in this one respect, than those safe, untested goods that have never had a run-in with Fortune — exactly as it is a rarer thing to have beaten an enemy with a hand lost than with a hand armed? "What," you say, "will you desire that good for yourself?" Of course I will. This is a thing a man cannot achieve unless he can also desire it. Should I desire instead to stretch out my limbs for my slaves to knead — or to have a woman, or a man made over into the likeness of a woman, pull my finger-joints for me? I can't help believing Mucius was the luckier man, because he handled the flames as calmly as if he were holding out his hand to the masseur. He burned away all his old mistakes. He finished the war unarmed and maimed; and with that stump of a hand, he conquered two kings.

Farewell.

Letters 67–69

Letter 67: On Ill-health and Endurance of Suffering

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Let me open with small talk: spring is finally showing itself. But even as it rounds the corner toward summer, when you'd expect real heat, it has stayed cool, and you can't trust it yet — it keeps sliding back into winter. Want proof of how unreliable it still is? I don't dare get into a genuinely cold bath; even now I take the chill off the water first. "That's no way to build up your tolerance for heat or cold," you'll say. True enough, dear Lucilius, but at my age a man's own natural chill is all the cold he needs. I can barely thaw out in midsummer. So I spend most of my time bundled up, and I actually thank old age for pinning me to my bed. Why shouldn't I thank it? Everything I ought not to want to do, I no longer can do. Most of my conversation these days is with books. Whenever your letters arrive, I feel as if I'm with you — as if I'm about to speak my reply instead of writing it. So let's take up this question of yours together, as if we were talking face to face.

You ask me whether every good is desirable. You put it this way: "If it's a good to be brave under torture, to walk to the stake with a steady heart, to endure sickness patiently, then it follows that these things are desirable. But I don't see that any of them is worth praying for. At least I've never heard of a man paying off a vow of thanks because he'd been cut to ribbons by the whip, or twisted out of shape by gout, or stretched taller on the rack." My dear Lucilius, you have to pull these cases apart; do that, and you'll see there is something in them worth wanting. I'd prefer to be spared torture; but if the time comes when it must be endured, then I'll want to bear it with bravery, honor, and courage. Of course I'd prefer that war never come; but if war does come, I'll want to bear the wounds, the hunger, and everything else war's necessity brings — and bear them nobly. I'm not so crazy as to wish for sickness; but if I must be sick, I'll want to do nothing weak, nothing unworthy of a man. The conclusion isn't that hardships are desirable. It's that virtue is desirable — and virtue is what lets us endure hardships patiently.

Some members of our own school think that of all these qualities, brave endurance is not actually desirable — not to be rejected either, but not to be prayed for — because we should ask heaven only for goods that are pure, peaceful, and out of trouble's reach. Personally, I disagree. Why? First, because nothing can be good without also being desirable. Second, because if virtue is desirable, and nothing good is without virtue, then everything good is desirable. Third, because brave endurance even under torture is desirable in itself. Here let me ask you: isn't bravery desirable? And yet bravery scorns danger and dares it. The most beautiful, most admirable thing about bravery is that it doesn't flinch from the stake, that it advances to meet its wounds, that sometimes it doesn't even dodge the spear but takes it chest-first. If bravery is desirable, then patiently enduring torture is desirable too, for that is a part of bravery. Just sort these things out, as I've suggested, and nothing will lead you astray. For it's not mere endurance of torture that is desirable — it's brave endurance. That "brave" is what I desire; and that is virtue.

"But who ever prayed for such a thing?" you say. Some prayers are open and explicit, when we ask for things one by one; others are folded up, when many requests hide under a single heading. For example: I pray for an honorable life. Now an honorable life is made up of many kinds of action. It may include the chest Regulus was locked into, or the wound Cato tore open with his own hands, or the exile of Rutilius, or the cup of poison that moved Socrates from prison to heaven. So in praying for an honorable life, I have also prayed for the things without which, on some days, no life can be honorable.

  Three times and four times blessed were the men
  who died before their parents' eyes, beneath
  the high walls of Troy!

What difference does it make whether you pray for this outcome for someone, or admit that it was desirable when it happened? Decius sacrificed himself for the state: he put spurs to his horse and charged into the middle of the enemy, hunting for death. The second Decius, matching his father's courage, repeated the words that had become sacred, already a household prayer, and crashed into the thickest of the fighting, worried about only one thing — that his sacrifice bring a good omen with it — and counting a noble death as something to be desired. Do you doubt, then, that it is best to die gloriously, in the act of doing something brave? When a man endures torture bravely, he is using every virtue at once. Endurance may be the one out front, the most visible; but bravery is there too — endurance and resignation and patient suffering are its branches. Foresight is there, because without foresight no plan gets made, and it is foresight that advises us to bear as bravely as we can what we cannot avoid. Steadfastness is there, which can't be pushed off its ground, which no force can wrench away from its purpose. The whole inseparable company of the virtues is there. Every honorable act is the work of a single virtue, but it is done with the approval of the entire council. And whatever all the virtues approve — even if one alone seems to have done the work — is desirable.

What — do you think the only desirable things are the ones that arrive amid pleasure and ease, the ones we decorate our doorways to welcome? Some goods have grim faces. Some prayers are offered not by a cheering crowd, but by a crowd that bows down in reverence and awe. Don't you think that is how Regulus prayed when he prayed to reach Carthage? Put on a hero's courage, and step back for a moment from the opinions of ordinary men. Get a true picture of virtue — a thing of surpassing beauty and grandeur, an image we worship not with incense and garlands but with sweat and blood. Look at Marcus Cato, laying his clean hands on that sacred breast and tearing open the wounds that hadn't gone deep enough to kill him. Which will you say to him — "I'm sorry for you" and "I hope it all turns out as you wish"? Or "Good fortune in your undertaking!"?

This brings to mind our friend Demetrius, who calls an easy life untroubled by any attack of Fortune a "Dead Sea." If you have nothing to stir you up, nothing to rouse you to action, nothing to test your resolve with threats and assaults; if you lie back in unshaken comfort — that isn't tranquility. That's just a flat calm. The Stoic Attalus used to say: "I would rather Fortune kept me in her camp than in the lap of luxury. If I'm tortured but bear it bravely, all is well. If I die, but die bravely, that is well too." Listen to Epicurus, and he'll tell you it's actually pleasant. I myself will never hang so soft a word on an act so honorable and austere. If I go to the stake, I'll go unbeaten. Why shouldn't I count that desirable — not because the fire burns me, but because it does not conquer me? Nothing is more excellent or more beautiful than virtue; whatever we do at her command is both good and desirable.

Farewell.

Letter 68: On Wisdom and Retirement

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I agree with your plan: retire, and hide yourself in rest. But hide the retirement itself as well. In doing this you'll be following the Stoics' example, if not their official teaching — though in fact you'll be following the teaching too, so you can satisfy both yourself and any Stoic you like. We Stoics don't push men into public life in every case, at all times, with no exceptions. Besides, once we have assigned our wise man the public sphere that's actually worthy of him — namely, the universe — he is never outside public life, even when he withdraws. If anything, he has quit one small corner of it and crossed over into greater, wider territory; set among the heavens, he understands how low a seat he occupied when he climbed onto the magistrate's chair or the judge's bench. Take this to heart: the wise man is never more active than when things divine as well as things human have come into his view.

Now back to the advice I started to give you: keep your retirement quiet. There's no need to pin a sign on yourself reading "Philosopher and Quietist." Give your project some other name — call it poor health, physical weakness, or plain laziness. Bragging about retirement is just vanity in hiding. Certain animals conceal their dens by scuffing up the tracks around the entrance. You should do the same; otherwise there will always be somebody following your footprints. Many people walk right past what's in plain sight and go poking after what's hidden and locked away. A locked room invites the thief. What lies in the open looks cheap; the housebreaker passes by what's on display. That's the way of the world, and the way of every ignorant man: they itch to break in on secrets. So it's best not to advertise your retirement — but making too much of your concealment, of your withdrawal from human sight, is itself a kind of advertising. So-and-so has gone into seclusion at Tarentum; that fellow has shut himself up at Naples; this one hasn't crossed his own threshold in years. To advertise your retirement is to collect a crowd. When you withdraw from the world, your business is to talk with yourself — not to have men talk about you.

And what should you talk about? Do exactly what people love to do when they gossip about the neighbors: speak ill of yourself when you're by yourself. You'll get used to telling the truth — and to hearing it. Above all, dwell on whatever you come to feel is your greatest weakness. Every man knows his own body's defects best. That's why one man relieves his stomach by vomiting, another props his up with frequent small meals, another drains and purges himself with regular fasting. Men with painful feet give up wine, or the baths. Careless about everything else, men go out of their way to treat the ailment that keeps coming back. It's the same with our souls: they have certain parts on the sick-list, so to speak, and it's to those parts that the cure must be applied.

What am I doing with my own leisure, then? I'm treating my own sores. If I showed you a swollen foot, an inflamed hand, or the shriveled tendons of a withered leg, you'd give me leave to lie quiet in one place and put compresses on the damaged limb. My trouble is bigger than any of those, and I can't show it to you: the abscess, the ulcer, is deep in my chest. Please, please — don't praise me, don't say, "What a great man! He has learned to despise it all; he has condemned the madness of men's lives and made his escape!" I have condemned nothing except myself. There's no reason to come to me hoping to make progress. You're mistaken if you expect any help from this quarter: no physician lives here, only a patient. I'd rather you said, on your way out: "I used to think him a happy man and a learned one, and I came with my ears pricked to listen. I've been cheated. I saw nothing, heard nothing that I wanted and would come back for." If you feel that way, and say so, some progress has been made. I'd rather you pardoned my retirement than envied it.

"So it's retirement you're recommending, Seneca? You'll be falling back on Epicurus's maxims next!" I do recommend retirement — but only so you can use it for greater and more beautiful work than what you're giving up. Knocking at the proud doors of the powerful, keeping alphabetical lists of childless old men with money, wielding the biggest influence in public life — that kind of power earns you hatred, doesn't last, and, if you price it honestly, is cheap stuff. One man will be far ahead of me in political clout, another in an officer's pay and the rank that comes with it, another in the size of his crowd of clients. It's worth being beaten by all of them, provided I myself can beat Fortune. And in a crowd I'm no match for her — she has the bigger following.

If only you had set your mind on this course years ago! If only we weren't discussing the happy life within plain sight of death! But even now, no delays. Experience has now confirmed what reason should have convinced us of long ago: that a great many things are useless, and many are hostile. Let's do what travelers do when they've started late and want to make up time by speed — let's use the spur. Our time of life is actually the best possible for this work: the boiling and foaming stage is over. The faults that raged unchecked in the first hot blast of youth have weakened; it will take only a little more effort to put them out.

"And when," you ask, "will learning do you any good, if you only learn it on your way out the door — and what good will it do?" Just this good: I shall leave a better man. But don't imagine any age is better suited to a sound mind than the age that has conquered itself through long testing — that has tamed its passions through long and repeated regret for its mistakes, and so arrived at health. This is precisely the season for acquiring that good. Whoever reaches wisdom in old age has reached it by means of his years.

Farewell.

Letter 69: On Rest and Restlessness

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I don't like you changing headquarters and scurrying from place to place. My first reason: all this flitting around is the sign of an unsteady spirit. And the spirit can never settle into unity through retirement until it has stopped its wandering and its sightseeing. To hold your spirit in check, you must first stop the body's runaway flight. My second reason: the cures that help most are the ones that aren't interrupted. Don't let anything break in on your quiet, or on the forgetting to which you've consigned your old life. Give your eyes time to unlearn what they've seen, and your ears time to get used to healthier words. Every time you go out, even just in passing from one place to the next, you'll run into things that wake your old cravings. A man trying to get over an old love must avoid every reminder of the person he once adored — nothing regrows as easily as love — and in the same way, a man trying to lay down his desire for all the things he once craved so hotly must turn both eyes and ears away from what he has left behind. The passions are quick to counterattack; wherever they turn, they'll spot something in front of them worth their attention. There is no evil that doesn't offer a signing bonus. Greed promises money; luxury, a whole menu of pleasures; ambition, the purple robe and the applause, and the influence applause brings, and everything influence can do. Vices recruit you with wages; in the life I'm describing, you must work unpaid. A whole lifetime is barely enough to break our vices and bring them under the yoke, swollen as they are from long indulgence — much less if we chop our short span into pieces with interruptions. Even constant, unbroken attention can barely carry a single undertaking through to the finish.

If you'll take my advice, ponder and practice this: how to welcome death — or even, if circumstances call for it, to invite it. It makes no difference whether death comes to us or we go to death. Convince yourself that the ignorant are wrong when they say, "It is a beautiful thing to die one's own death." There is no man who doesn't die his own death. And here is a further thought to chew on: no one dies except on his own day. You are throwing away none of your own time — for what you leave behind was never yours.

Farewell.

Letter 70

Letter 70: On the Proper Time to Slip the Cable

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

After a long absence I have seen your beloved Pompeii again, and it brought me face to face with my own youth. Everything I did there as a young man, it seemed to me I could still do — had done, in fact, only a little while ago. We have sailed past life, Lucilius, the way sailors sail past a coastline. At sea, as our poet Virgil puts it,

  Lands and towns drop away astern,

and just so, on this voyage where time runs at full speed, we put below the horizon first our boyhood, then our youth, then those years that lie between young manhood and middle age and border on both, and then the best years of old age itself. Last of all we begin to sight the one landfall appointed for the whole human race. Fools that we are, we take it for a dangerous reef. It is the harbor. Some day we must put in there; we are never entitled to refuse it; and if a man reaches that harbor in his early years, he has no more right to complain than a sailor who has made a fast crossing. Some sailors, as you know, are teased and held back by sluggish winds until they grow weary and sick of the flat calm; others are carried home by a steady gale.

Assume the same thing happens to us. Life has rushed some men to the harbor — the harbor they were bound to reach anyway, even dawdling — while others it has chafed and battered the whole way. And to a life like that, as you know, one should not always cling. Merely living is not the good; living well is. So the wise man will live as long as he ought, not as long as he can. He will note where he is to live, and with whom, and how, and what he is to be doing. He thinks always about the quality of his life, never the quantity. If life presents him with too many things that torment him and wreck his peace of mind, he sets himself free. And this right is his not only in the final emergency, but the moment Fortune starts to look untrustworthy: then he surveys the ground carefully and considers whether this is the place to stop. It makes no difference to him, he holds, whether he makes his end or merely meets it, whether it comes late or early. He does not fear it as some great loss: no one can lose much when only a trickle is left. It is not a question of dying earlier or later, but of dying well or badly. And dying well means escaping the danger of living badly.

That is why I find the words of the famous Rhodian utterly spineless. This man had been thrown into a cage by his tyrant and was fed there like a wild animal, and when someone advised him to end it by starving himself, he answered: "While a man has life, he may hope for anything." Perhaps so; but life is not worth buying at every price. Let the prizes be as great and as guaranteed as you please: I will not crawl to them through a shameful confession of weakness. Shall I dwell on the fact that Fortune holds every power over a man while he lives — rather than on the fact that she holds none over a man who knows how to die?

And yet there are times when a man, though death is certain and he knows torture is scheduled for him, will refuse to lend a hand to his own punishment. To himself he would lend it. It is folly to die for fear of dying. The executioner is coming: wait for him. Why get there first? Why volunteer to manage a piece of cruelty that belongs to someone else? Do you begrudge your executioner his job, or are you just sparing him the trouble? Socrates could have ended his life by fasting — died of hunger rather than hemlock. Instead he spent thirty days in prison waiting for death, not with the thought "anything might happen yet" or "so long a stretch has room for plenty of hopes," but so that he might show himself obedient to the laws, and so that his friends might have the last hours of Socrates to live on. What would have been more foolish than to despise death, yet be afraid of poison?

Scribonia, a woman of the stern old school, was the aunt of Drusus Libo, a young man as stupid as he was well-born, with ambitions higher than anyone could afford in that age — or a man like him in any age. He had been carried out of the senate-house sick, in his litter, with a very thin escort — for all his relatives had undutifully abandoned him once he was no longer a defendant but as good as a corpse — and he began to debate whether to kill himself or wait for death. Scribonia said to him: "What pleasure do you find in doing another man's work?" He did not take her advice; he laid hands on himself. And he was right after all. When your enemy has scheduled your death for two or three days out, staying alive really is doing another man's work.

So there is no blanket answer to the question of whether, when a power beyond our control decrees our death, we should go to meet it or wait for it. Plenty of arguments pull in each direction. If one death comes with torture and the other is simple and easy, why not grab the easy one? Just as I will choose my ship when I am going on a voyage, and my house when I am moving in, so I will choose my death when I am leaving life. What's more, a longer life is not automatically a better one, but a longer death is automatically a worse one. In nothing should we humor the soul more than in dying. Let it leave by whatever way it feels the pull: whether it reaches for the sword, or the rope, or a drug that empties the veins, let it go, and snap the chains of its slavery. A man should make his life acceptable to others as well as himself; his death, to himself alone. The best death is the one we like. It is foolish to sit thinking: "One person will say I wasn't brave enough; another, that I was too rash; a third, that some other death would have shown more spirit." What you should think instead is: the decision before me is one that public opinion holds no share in. Your single aim should be to get clear of Fortune as fast as you can; otherwise, there will never be a shortage of people who think badly of what you did.

You can even find men who profess philosophy and yet insist that no violence should be done to one's own life, and who call it an abomination for a man to be his own destroyer: we should wait, they say, for the end nature has decreed. A man who says this does not see that he is closing the road to freedom. Of everything the eternal law ever arranged, the best is this: it gave us one entrance into life, but many exits. Should I wait on the cruelty of disease, or the cruelty of men, when I can walk out through the middle of my tortures and shake off my troubles? This is the one count on which we cannot complain of life: it holds no one against his will. Humanity is well situated: no one is unhappy except by his own fault. If you like life, live; if not, you may go back where you came from. You have often been cupped to relieve a headache; you have had veins opened to bring your weight down. To pierce the heart, no gaping wound is required: a lancet opens the way to that great freedom, and tranquility costs a pin-prick.

What is it, then, that makes us sluggish and slow? None of us reflects that some day he must leave this house of life. It is just the way old tenants are: fondness for the place and force of habit keep them on, even when the place mistreats them. Do you want to be free with respect to your body? Then live in it as a tenant about to move out. Keep it before your mind that some day this lease will end; then you will be braver when moving day becomes necessary. But how will a man ever think about his own end if he craves everything without end? And yet there is nothing we need to rehearse more. Our other training may never be called on. Our souls stand ready for poverty — but our wealth has held out. We have armed ourselves to despise pain — but the good luck of a sound and healthy body has never asked us to prove it. We have taught ourselves to bear bravely the loss of those we love — but Fortune has kept alive everyone we loved. This is the one drill where the day of the test is guaranteed to come.

Do not imagine that only great men have had the strength to burst the bars of human servitude; do not believe this is something only a Cato can do — Cato, who with his bare hand dragged out the soul his sword had failed to set free. Men of the lowliest station have flung themselves to safety by one mighty effort, and when they were not allowed to die at their own convenience, or to pick and choose the instrument of death, they have snatched up whatever lay at hand and by sheer force turned objects harmless by nature into weapons of their own. Recently, in a training-school for beast-fighters, there was a German who was getting ready for the morning show. He withdrew to relieve himself — the only thing he was allowed to do in private, without a guard. There he seized the stick of wood tipped with a sponge that is kept for the filthiest of uses, and rammed the whole thing down his throat, blocking his windpipe and choking the breath out of his body. That was throwing an insult in death's face. Exactly so — not an elegant or a decent way to die; but what is more foolish than being fastidious about dying? What a brave man! He deserved to be given a choice of fate. How boldly he would have used a sword! With what spirit he would have hurled himself into the depths of the sea, or off a cliff! Cut off from every resource, he still found a way to furnish himself with a death, and with a weapon for it — proof that nothing but the will stands between a man and his dying. Let each of us judge this fierce fellow's act as he pleases, so long as we agree on this much: the foulest death is preferable to the cleanest slavery.

Since I began with humble examples, I will keep on with them. Men will demand more of themselves when they see that even the most despised class of men can despise death. We treat the Catos and the Scipios and the other names we are trained to admire as beyond imitation; but I will now show you that the virtue I am speaking of turns up as often in the gladiators' barracks as among the commanders of a civil war. Recently a gladiator, sent off to the morning show, was riding in a cart with the other prisoners. Nodding as if he could not fight off sleep, he let his head droop lower and lower until it slipped between the spokes; then he held his body rigid just long enough for the turning wheel to break his neck. The very wagon that was carrying him to his punishment carried him out of it.

When a man wants to break out and be gone, nothing stands in his way. Nature keeps us in an open enclosure; the way out is unbarred. When our situation permits it, we may look around for an easy exit. If many means of freedom lie ready to hand, we can take our pick and consider the best way out; but if chances are hard to come by, seize the nearest as if it were the best, however unheard-of, however new. A man who does not lack the courage to die will not lack the ingenuity. Look how even the lowest of slaves, when suffering spurs him, rouses himself and outwits the most watchful guards! The truly great man is not just the one who has given himself the order to die, but the one who has found the means.

I promised you a few more illustrations from the same games. During the second event of a mock sea-battle, one of the barbarians sank the spear he had been issued against his enemy deep into his own throat. "Why, oh why," he said, "did I not escape long ago from all this torture and all this mockery? Why do I stand here armed, and wait for death to come?" The show was all the more striking for the lesson it taught: that dying is more honorable than killing.

Well then: if desperate and dangerous men can possess such a spirit, shall it be missing in men who have prepared for these emergencies by long rehearsal — and by reason, the mistress of everything? Reason teaches us that fate has many roads in, but one destination, and that it makes no difference where the inevitable begins. Reason also gives this advice: die as you please, if you can; if you cannot, die as you are able, and seize whatever offers itself for doing yourself that violence. To live by robbery is a crime; but to die by robbery — that is the noblest act of all.

Farewell.

Letter 71

Letter 71: On the Supreme Good

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You keep sending me your particular problems, one after another, forgetting that a whole stretch of sea lies between us. Advice is worth most at the moment it's given, so by the time my opinion on some question reaches you, the opposite opinion may be the better one. Advice has to fit circumstances, and our circumstances are not just moving along — they're being whirled along. So advice should be produced on the spot; and even that is too slow. It should "grow as we work," as the saying goes. What I want to show you instead is the method for finding it yourself.

Whenever you want to know what to avoid or what to pursue, look at how it relates to the Supreme Good — to the purpose of your whole life. Everything we do ought to be in harmony with that. No one can put the details of his life in order until he has set the overall aim of his life in front of him. A painter may have all his colors mixed and ready, but he can't produce a likeness until he has decided what he wants to paint. The reason we go wrong is that we all think about the pieces of life, but never about life as a whole. An archer has to know what he's trying to hit; only then can he aim and guide the arrow with his skill. Our plans miscarry because they have no target. If you don't know which harbor you're sailing for, no wind is the right wind. No wonder Fortune has such power over our lives: we live by chance. And with certain people there's an odd twist — they don't know that they know things. Just as we sometimes hunt everywhere for the person standing right beside us, we tend to forget that the goal, the Supreme Good, is close at hand.

To grasp the nature of this Supreme Good, you don't need many words or any roundabout discussion. You should be able to point at it with one finger, so to speak, not scatter it into pieces. What's the use of chopping it into little bits when you can simply say: the Supreme Good is what is honorable? Better yet — and this may surprise you even more — what is honorable is the only good; every other so-called good is alloyed and debased metal. Once you convince yourself of this, and once you come to love virtue devotedly (merely loving it isn't enough), then everything virtue has touched will turn out blessed and prosperous for you, no matter how it looks to anyone else. Torture — if you lie there calmer in mind than the man torturing you. Illness — if you don't curse Fortune and don't surrender to the disease. In short, everything other people call an evil becomes manageable, and ends in good, if you rise above it.

Let this one thing be settled — that nothing is good except what is honorable — and every hardship will have a fair claim to the name of "good," once virtue has made it honorable. Many people think we Stoics are promising more than the human lot allows, and they have a right to think so: they're looking only at the body. Let them turn back to the soul, and they'll soon be measuring man by the standard of God. Rouse yourself, my excellent Lucilius, and drop the philosophers' word-games — the ones that shrink the most glorious subject in the world down to a matter of syllables, that lower and wear out the soul by teaching it in scraps.
