Then you'll become like the men who discovered these teachings, not like the men whose teaching does its best to make philosophy look difficult rather than great.

Socrates — who called all of philosophy back to conduct, and said the highest wisdom is telling good from evil — put it this way: "Follow these rules, if my words carry any weight with you, so that you may be happy — and let some people think you a fool. Let anyone who wants to insult you and do you wrong; if virtue lives with you, you will suffer nothing. If you wish to be happy, if you want to be, in good faith, a good man, then let this person or that person look down on you." No one can manage this until he has come to regard all goods as equal — because no good exists without the honorable, and the honorable is in every case equal.

"What?" you say. "Is there no difference between Cato winning the praetorship and Cato losing the election? No difference whether Cato is conquered or conqueror on the battle line at Pharsalia? When his party was defeated but he could not be defeated, was that goodness really equal to what he'd have had if he'd returned home victorious and arranged a peace?" Of course it was. It is the same virtue that overcomes bad fortune and keeps good fortune under control; and virtue cannot be increased or decreased. It stands at one height.

"But," you'll object, "Gnaeus Pompey will lose his army! The patricians, the noblest patterns the state ever produced, the front rank of Pompey's cause — a senate under arms — will be routed in a single battle. The wreckage of that great aristocracy will be scattered across the whole world: one part will fall in Egypt, another in Africa, another in Spain. And the poor state won't even be granted the privilege of being ruined once and for all!" Yes, all of it may happen. Juba's knowledge of every position in his own kingdom may do him no good, and no good the stubborn bravery of his people fighting for their king. Even the men of Utica, crushed by their troubles, may waver in their loyalty; and the good fortune that always followed the name of Scipio may desert Scipio in Africa. But long ago destiny "saw to it that Cato should come to no harm."

"He was conquered all the same!" Fine — put that down among Cato's "failures." Cato will bear whatever cheats him of victory with the same stout heart he bore what cheated him of the praetorship. The day he lost the election, he spent playing games; the night he intended to die, he spent reading. To him, losing the praetorship and losing his life belonged in the same category: he had convinced himself that a man must endure whatever can happen. And why shouldn't he suffer a revolution in the state bravely and calmly? What is free from the risk of change? Not the earth, not the sky, not the whole fabric of the universe — even though God's hand steers it. It will not keep its present order forever; some day will throw it from its course. Everything moves on its appointed schedule; all things are destined to be born, to grow, and to be destroyed. The stars you see wheeling above us, and this seemingly immovable earth we cling to and stand on — they will be consumed, and they will cease to exist. Everything has its old age; Nature simply sends all things toward the same finish line at unequal intervals. Whatever is will cease to be — and yet it will not perish; it will be dissolved back into its elements. To our minds, dissolving means perishing, because we see only what is nearest; the sluggish mind, sworn into the service of the body, doesn't see past its own borders. Otherwise it would bear its own ending, and the ending of everything it owns, with more courage — if it could hope that life and death take their turns like everything else in the universe: that whatever is put together is broken apart, whatever is broken apart is put together again, and that the eternal craftsmanship of God, who governs all things, is at work on exactly this task.

So the wise man will say just what a Marcus Cato would say, looking back over his life: "The whole human race, all who are and all who will be, stands condemned to die. Of all the cities that have ever ruled the world, and all that have been the shining ornaments of empires not their own, people will one day ask where they were — and they will have been swept away by destructions of every kind. Some will be ruined by wars; others will be eaten away by idleness, by the kind of peace that ends in sloth, and by the vice that brings down even mighty dynasties — luxury. All these fertile plains will be buried out of sight by a sudden flood of the sea, or the ground will settle and slip and drag them down into a yawning chasm. Why then should I be angry, or grieve, if I go a tiny interval of time ahead of the universal destruction?" Let great souls comply with God's wishes, and accept without hesitating whatever fate the law of the universe ordains. At death the soul is either sent out into a better life, to dwell with the divine in greater radiance and calm — or, at the very least, it suffers no harm: it is mingled back into nature and returns to the universe.

So Cato's honorable death was no less a good than his honorable life, because virtue does not stretch. Socrates used to say that truth and virtue are the same thing. Just as truth does not grow, virtue does not grow either; it has its full proportions; it is complete. So don't be surprised that goods are equal — both the ones we choose deliberately and the ones circumstances impose on us. Because if you once accept that they're unequal — if you rank the brave endurance of torture among the lesser goods — you'll end up ranking it among the evils too. You'll call Socrates unhappy in his prison; you'll call Cato unhappy as he tears his wound open again with his bare hands, more courageously than he inflicted it; you'll call Regulus the unluckiest man of all because he paid the penalty for keeping his word — even to his enemies. And yet no one, not even the softest creature alive, has ever dared to say that. People deny that a man like Regulus is happy; but for all that, they also deny that he is wretched. The earlier Academics do admit that a man is happy even under such tortures — but not completely, not fully happy. With that we cannot agree at all. Unless a man is happy, he has not attained the Supreme Good; and the good which is supreme has no higher degree above it — provided virtue exists in the man, provided adversity does not impair it, provided the virtue stands unharmed even when the body is injured. And it does stand. For as I understand virtue, it is high-spirited and exalted; whatever harasses it only rouses it. That spirit that young men of noble character sometimes catch — when the beauty of some honorable thing strikes them so deeply that they despise all the gifts of Fortune — wisdom pours that spirit into us and makes it ours for good. Wisdom brings the conviction that there is only one good, the honorable; that it can be neither shortened nor extended, any more than a carpenter's rule — the tool we test straight lines with — can be bent. Bend the rule at all, and you spoil the straight line. Apply the same figure to virtue, and we say: virtue too is straight, and it does not bend. Can you tighten what is already rigid? Virtue passes judgment on everything, and nothing passes judgment on virtue. And if the rule itself cannot be made any straighter, then the things virtue produces cannot be straighter in one case and less straight in another. They must correspond to virtue; therefore they are equal.

"What!" you say. "You're calling it equally good to recline at a banquet and to be stretched on the rack?" Does that shock you? Then here is something more shocking: reclining at a banquet can be an evil, and reclining on the rack can be a good — if the first is done shamefully and the second honorably. It isn't the material that makes an act good or bad; it's the virtue. Every act in which virtue shows itself has the same measure and the same worth. Right now the man who measures every soul by his own is shaking his fist in my face, because I hold that there is parity between the goods of the man who passes sentence honorably and the man who suffers sentence honorably; because I hold there is parity between the man celebrating a triumph and the man — unconquered in spirit — being carted along in front of the victor's chariot. Critics like that assume that whatever they can't do, can't be done; they pass judgment on virtue in the light of their own weaknesses. Why marvel that it can help a man — sometimes even please him — to be burned, wounded, killed, or chained in prison? To a man of luxury, a simple life is a penalty; to a lazy man, work is punishment; the dandy pities the hard worker; to the idle, study is torture. In the same way, we call anything we're all too weak for "hard" and "beyond endurance" — forgetting what a torment it is for plenty of men to go without wine, or to be rolled out of bed at dawn. None of these things is difficult by nature. We ourselves are soft and flabby. Great matters must be judged with greatness of soul; otherwise we'll blame them for what is really our own fault. It's like perfectly straight objects that look bent or broken when you sink them in water. It matters not only what you see, but with what eyes you see it: our souls are too dim-sighted to make out the truth. But give me an unspoiled, sturdy-minded young man, and he'll tell you the more fortunate person is the one who carries the whole weight of adversity on unbending shoulders — the one who stands taller than Fortune. There's nothing remarkable about not being tossed around when the sea is calm. Save your wonder for the man who is lifted up when everyone else sinks, who stays on his feet when everyone else is flat on the ground.

What element of evil is there in torture, or in the other things we call hardships? As far as I can see, the evil is this: that the mind sags, and bends, and collapses. None of that can happen to the wise man. He stands upright under any load. Nothing subdues him; nothing he must endure annoys him. He doesn't complain that he has been struck by something that can strike any man. He knows his own strength; he knows he was born to carry burdens. I don't remove the wise man from the human race, and I don't deny him the sense of pain, as if he were a rock that feels nothing. I remember that he is made of two parts. One part is irrational — that's the part that can be bitten, burned, hurt. The other part is rational — that's the part that holds firmly to its convictions, that is courageous and unconquerable. That is where man's Supreme Good lives. Until it is fully attained, the mind wavers in uncertainty; only when it is fully achieved does the mind become fixed and steady. So the man who has just begun — the man climbing toward the heights and cultivating virtue, even the man drawing near the perfect good who hasn't yet put the finishing touch on it — will slip back at times, will feel his mental effort slacken. He hasn't crossed the uncertain ground yet; he's still standing in a slippery place. But the happy man, whose virtue is complete, loves himself most of all when his bravery has passed the severest test — when what everyone else dreads he not only endures but welcomes, if that is the price of a duty honor imposes. He would far rather have people say of him "How much nobler!" than "How much luckier!"

And now I've reached the point your patient waiting has been calling me toward. Don't imagine that our human virtue rises above nature. The wise man will tremble, will feel pain, will turn pale — those are all sensations of the body. Then where does real distress live — the thing that is truly an evil? In the other part of us, no doubt: but only if these trials drag the mind down, force it to confess itself a slave, and make it sorry it exists. The wise man conquers Fortune by his virtue; yet plenty of men who profess wisdom are frightened by the flimsiest threats. And at this stage it's our mistake to demand the same from the learner as from the wise man. I'm still urging myself to do what I recommend, and my urging isn't followed yet. Even if it were, I wouldn't have these principles so ready, so well drilled, that they would rush to my defense in every crisis. Wool takes some dyes the moment it touches them; others it won't absorb unless it is soaked and steeped again and again. It's the same with doctrines: some the mind can apply the moment it accepts them. But this one — unless it has gone deep and soaked in for a long time, unless it has not merely tinted the soul but thoroughly saturated it — delivers on none of its promises. The teaching itself can be handed over quickly, in very few words: virtue is the only good; at any rate, there is no good without virtue; and virtue itself is located in our nobler part — the rational part. And what will this virtue be? A true and never-swerving judgment. From it every impulse of the mind will spring, and by it every outward appearance that stirs an impulse will be brought into clear focus. And it follows from that judgment to count everything virtue has colored as good — and as equally good.

Bodily goods are, to be sure, good for the body; but they are not good, full stop. They will have some value, but no genuine worth, and they will differ widely — some smaller, some greater. And we have to admit there are great differences among the followers of wisdom themselves. One man has already made enough progress to dare to lift his eyes and look Fortune in the face — but not for long; her overwhelming glare dazzles him, and his eyes drop. Another has come far enough to hold her gaze — unless, that is, he has reached the summit and stands full of confidence. Whatever is short of perfection is bound to be unsteady: advancing at one moment, slipping or faltering at the next. And it will surely slip back unless it keeps fighting forward — slacken your zeal and your faithful effort at all, and you go backward. No one can resume his progress at the exact point where he dropped it. So let us press on and persevere. More of the road remains than we have put behind us — but wanting to go forward is the greater part of going forward.

Of this task I am fully aware. I want it, and I want it with my whole heart. I can see you've been roused too, racing with tremendous energy toward infinite beauty. So let us race. Only on those terms is life a blessing to us. Otherwise it is delay — and disgraceful delay at that, while we busy ourselves with things that should disgust us. Let's see to it that all time belongs to us. And it cannot, until we ourselves first belong to us. When will we get to despise both kinds of fortune? When will we get to say — every passion subdued and brought under our own control — the words "I have conquered"? Whom have I conquered, you ask? Not the Persians, not the far-off Medes, not any warlike tribe beyond the Dahae. I have conquered greed, ambition, and the fear of death — the fear that has conquered the conquerors of the world. Farewell.

Letters 72–73

Letter 72: On Business as the Enemy of Philosophy

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

The question you're asking me about was once perfectly clear in my mind. I had mastered it so thoroughly that I didn't even need to think. But I haven't tested my memory of it in a while, and it isn't coming back easily. I feel like a book whose rolls have stuck together from sitting unread. My mind needs to be unrolled; whatever has been stored away in there ought to be taken out and inspected from time to time, so it's ready for use the moment it's needed. So let's set that question aside for now — it demands real labor and real care. As soon as I can expect to stay put in one place for a while, I'll take it up properly. Some subjects you can write about while bouncing along in a carriage; others need a desk, quiet, and a closed door. Still, I ought to get something done even on days like these — days that are booked solid from morning to night. There is never a moment when fresh business won't come along. We sow it ourselves, and that's why one task always sprouts into several. Then we keep granting ourselves postponements: "As soon as I finish this, I'll settle down to serious work," or, "Once I get this annoying mess sorted out, I'll devote myself to study."

But philosophy is not something to put off until you have free time. Everything else should be neglected so that we can attend to philosophy, because no amount of time is long enough for it — not even if our lives stretched from boyhood to the furthest limit granted to man. And it makes little difference whether you abandon philosophy entirely or study it in fits and starts; it doesn't stay where you left it. Break its continuity and it snaps back to the beginning, like a taut cord let go. We have to resist the affairs that eat our time. Don't try to untangle them — shove them out of the way. There is no time that's actually unsuitable for useful study; and yet plenty of men fail to study in the very circumstances that make study necessary. "Something will come up to stop me," he says. Not for the man whose spirit is happy and alert no matter what business he's in. It's the people still short of perfection whose happiness can be interrupted. The joy of the wise man is unbroken and continuous — a fabric woven in one piece, torn by no accident and no turn of Fortune. At all times and in all places, he is at peace. His joy depends on nothing external and waits on no favor from man or Fortune. His happiness is homegrown: it would leave his soul if it had entered from outside, but it was born there. Now and then some event reminds him that he's mortal, but it's a light blow — it only grazes the skin. Some trouble may brush him, I admit, like a puff of wind; but that Supreme Good of his stands unshaken. What I mean is this: there are external nuisances, like pimples and boils breaking out on a body that is basically strong and sound — but there's no disease down deep. The difference between the man of perfect wisdom and the man still making progress is the difference between a healthy man and someone convalescing from a long, hard illness — a man for whom "health" just means a milder attack of his disease. If the convalescent isn't careful, he relapses at once, straight back into the old trouble. But the wise man can't slip back — can't slip into any sickness at all. Bodily health is a temporary arrangement; the doctor can't guarantee it even after he's restored it. Often enough he's rousted out of bed to see the very patient who called him before. The mind, though, once healed, is healed for good.

Let me tell you what I mean by a healthy mind: one that is content with itself; that trusts itself; that understands that everything people pray for, all the advantages men give and chase, count for nothing in a happy life. That mind is sound. Because anything that can be added to is incomplete; anything that can suffer loss won't last. The man who wants his happiness to last should rejoice in what is genuinely his own — and everything the crowd gapes at ebbs and flows. Fortune gives us nothing we can actually own. Even Fortune's gifts only please us once reason has tempered and blended them to our taste; it's reason that makes even external goods agreeable — gulp them down greedily and they turn sour. Attalus, my old teacher, used to use this picture: "Have you ever watched a dog snapping, jaws wide, at scraps of bread or meat his master tosses him? Whatever he catches he swallows whole on the spot, and instantly gapes again in hope of more. That's us. Whatever Fortune throws, we bolt it down without tasting it, and stand there tense and frantic for the next thing to snatch." Not the wise man. He is satisfied. If something does fall his way, he takes it casually and sets it aside. The happiness he enjoys is supremely great, it lasts, and it is his. Now take a man with good intentions who has made progress but is still far below the summit: his life is a series of ups and downs — one moment lifted to heaven, the next brought down to earth. For the untrained and inexperienced, there's no bottom to the fall; they drop into Epicurus's Chaos — empty and boundless. And there's a third class: the men who are close to wisdom. They haven't grasped it, but they have it in view — within striking distance, you might say. They aren't tossed around, and they aren't drifting backward either. They're not yet on dry land, but they're already in the harbor.

So, given the enormous gap between those on the heights and those in the depths, and given that even the men in the middle are chased by their own ebb and flow — and by the very real risk of sliding back into their old degenerate ways — we must not surrender ourselves to busyness. Shut it out. Once it gets a foot in the door, it will bring in replacements for itself. Let's resist it at the start. Better that it never begin than that we have to make it stop.

Farewell.

Letter 73: On Philosophers and Kings

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

It seems to me a mistake to believe that people loyally devoted to philosophy are stubborn and rebellious — scorners of magistrates or kings or anyone who runs public affairs. Just the opposite: no class of men appreciates a ruler more than the philosopher does, and rightly so, because rulers give no one a greater gift than the people they allow to enjoy peace and leisure. So those whose whole purpose — living rightly — profits from the security of the state are bound to cherish the author of that good like a father. Far more, at any rate, than the restless types who live in the public eye, who owe the ruler much but expect from him even more, and who are never so loaded with favors that their cravings — which grow with every feeding — are ever satisfied. A man whose mind is on favors to come has already forgotten the favors received; and there is no worse thing in greed than its ingratitude. Besides, no man in public life thinks of the many he has passed; he thinks only of those still ahead of him. And these men take less pleasure in seeing a crowd behind them than they take offense at seeing anyone in front. That's the disease of every kind of ambition: it doesn't look back. And ambition isn't the only restless thing — every craving is, because every craving begins where it ought to end.

But that other man — upright, clean, the one who has left the senate and the courts and all affairs of state to withdraw to nobler business — cherishes the people who make it possible for him to do so in safety. He alone thanks them unprompted; he alone owes them a great debt they know nothing about. Just as a man honors and reveres the teachers who freed him from his early wanderings, so the wise man honors these men too, under whose protection he can put his good theories into practice. "But other people are also protected by a king's power," you say. Perfectly true. But out of everyone who profits from the same stretch of calm weather, the man who reckons his debt to Neptune largest is the one whose cargo on that voyage was biggest and most valuable. The merchant pays his vow with more feeling than the passenger. And among the merchants themselves, heartier thanks come from the dealer in spices, purple cloth, and goods worth their weight in gold than from the man hauling cheap stuff that's little better than ballast. In the same way, the benefits of this peace, which reaches everyone, are felt most deeply by those who use it well.

For there are plenty of our toga-wearing citizens to whom peace brings more trouble than war. Do you really think the men who spend their peace on drunkenness, or lust, or other vices it would be worth even a war to interrupt, owe as much for it as we do? Not unless you imagine the wise man is so unfair that he believes he owes nothing personally for benefits he enjoys along with everyone else. I owe a great debt to the sun and the moon — and yet they don't rise for me alone. I am personally obliged to the seasons and to the god who governs them, though none of it was portioned out for my benefit. The foolish greed of mortals draws a line between possessing and owning, and counts nothing its own that the public has a share in. But our philosopher considers nothing more truly his own than what he shares in partnership with the whole human race. These things wouldn't be common property — which they are — unless every individual held his portion; even the slightest share in a thing makes you a partner. And here's the further point: the great and true goods are not divided up so that each person gets a sliver. They belong entire to each individual. At a grain distribution, men receive only the amount promised to each; a banquet, a meat-dole, anything a man can carry off in his hands, gets split into portions. But these goods are indivisible — I mean peace and liberty — and they belong whole to all men and whole to every man.

So the philosopher thinks about the person who makes it possible for him to use and enjoy these things — the person who exempts him when the state's dire need calls men to arms, to sentry duty, to defending the walls, and to all the endless demands of war — and he gives thanks to the helmsman of his state. This is what philosophy teaches above all: to acknowledge the debt of benefits received honorably, and to pay it honorably. Though sometimes the acknowledgment itself is the payment. Our philosopher will freely admit, then, that he owes a large debt to the ruler whose management and foresight let him enjoy rich leisure, control of his own time, and a quiet uninterrupted by public duties.

  Shepherd, a god gave me this peaceful ease,
  for he shall be a god to me forever.

And if even leisure like our poet's owes a great debt to the man who gave it — leisure whose greatest boon is only this:

  He let me turn my cattle out to graze, as you can see,
  and play whatever tune I pleased on my rustic reed —

then how highly should we value the philosopher's leisure, which is spent among the gods, and makes us gods? Yes, that is exactly what I mean, Lucilius; and I am inviting you to heaven by a shortcut.

Sextius used to say that Jupiter has no more power than the good man. Jupiter, of course, has more gifts to hand out to mankind. But when you're choosing between two good men, the richer one isn't the better one — any more than, between two pilots of equal skill at the tiller, you'd call the one with the bigger, showier ship the better sailor. In what way is Jupiter superior to our good man? His goodness lasts longer. But the wise man doesn't rate himself one bit lower just because his virtues run on a shorter clock. Take two wise men: the one who dies at a greater age is not happier than the one whose virtue was confined to fewer years. In the same way, a god has no advantage over a wise man in happiness, even though he has one in years. Virtue is not greater for lasting longer. Jupiter possesses everything — but he has handed the possession of it all over to others. The only use of it he keeps for himself is this: he is the cause of everyone else's use. The wise man surveys other people's possessions, and disdains them, as calmly as Jupiter does — and he thinks the better of himself for it, because Jupiter cannot make use of them, and the wise man does not want to. So let's believe Sextius when he points out the path of perfect beauty and cries: "This is 'the way to the stars' — the way of thrift, self-restraint, and courage!"

The gods are not snobbish or jealous. They open the door; they lend a hand as you climb. Are you amazed that a man goes to the gods? God comes to men — no, closer than that: he comes into men. No mind is good without God. Divine seeds are scattered through our mortal bodies. If a good farmer receives them, they spring up true to their source, the equals of the stock they came from. But if the farmer is bad — barren soil, or swampland — he kills the seeds and raises weeds instead of wheat.

Farewell.

Letter 74

Letter 74: On Virtue as a Refuge from Worldly Distractions

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Your letter gave me real pleasure, and it shook me out of a sluggish spell. It also gave my memory a nudge — it has been slack and drowsy for some time now.

You are right, of course, my dear Lucilius: the chief means of reaching the happy life is the conviction that the only good is what is honorable. Anyone who counts other things as good has put himself in Fortune's power and handed the controls to someone else. But the man who has defined the good, in every case, by what is honorable carries his happiness inside him.

One man is crushed when his children die; another is anxious when they fall sick; a third is embittered when they disgrace themselves or take a stain on their reputation. You will see one man tortured by passion for his neighbor's wife, another by passion for his own. You will find men wrecked by losing an election, and others actually tormented by the offices they won. But the biggest crowd of unhappy people in the whole throng of mortals are the ones driven to despair by the expectation of death — death that threatens them from every side. For there is no direction death cannot come from. So, like soldiers scouting in enemy country, they have to keep looking every way and turning their heads at every sound; unless the heart is rid of this fear, a man lives with his pulse pounding. You will easily call to mind the people driven into exile and stripped of their property. You will also call to mind — and this is the most serious kind of destitution — the people who are poor in the middle of their riches. You will call to mind men who have suffered shipwreck, or whose sufferings amount to shipwreck: they were untroubled and at ease when the anger, or perhaps the envy, of the crowd — a missile most deadly to those in high places — dismantled them, like a storm that tends to blow up exactly when you are most confident the calm will last, or like a lightning bolt that sets even the surrounding countryside trembling. For just as anyone standing near the strike is stunned and looks like a man who was hit, so in these sudden, violent disasters, only one person is crushed by the blow itself, but the rest are crushed by fear — the mere possibility of suffering leaves them as downcast as the man who actually suffered.

Every one of us is shaken in spirit by evils that fall suddenly on a neighbor. Like birds, who cower at the whirr of an empty sling, we are rattled by mere sounds as much as by blows. No one can be happy who gives himself up to fancies like these. For nothing brings happiness unless it also brings calm; a life spent in dread is a bad way to exist. Whoever has surrendered himself wholesale to Fortune's power has woven himself a huge web of anxiety, and he cannot get free of it. There is only one road to safety: despise externals and be content with what is honorable. For those who think anything is better than virtue, or that there is any good besides virtue, are spreading their arms to catch what Fortune throws to the crowd, and waiting nervously on her favors. Picture it: Fortune is holding a festival, showering honors, riches, and influence down on this mob of mortals. Some of the gifts have already been ripped to pieces in the hands of the people grabbing for them; some have been carved up by treacherous partnerships; some have been seized — to the great harm of the very people they landed on. A few of these favors fell to men who weren't even paying attention; others were lost to their seekers precisely because they snatched too eagerly, knocked from their hands by their own greed. And in that whole crowd there is not one man — not even the one who got lucky in the scramble — whose joy in his haul lasted until the next morning.

The sensible man, then, runs out of the theater the moment he sees the free handout being carried in, because he knows how high a price is paid for small favors. Nobody will wrestle with him on the way out; nobody will hit him as he leaves. The brawling happens where the prizes are. It is the same with the gifts Fortune tosses down to us. Wretches that we are, we get worked up, we are torn every which way, we wish we had a dozen hands, we glance back now this way, now that. The gifts seem to fall toward us far too slowly; all they do is inflame our cravings, since they can reach only a few and are awaited by all. We are desperate to intercept them as they drop. We celebrate if we grab hold of anything — while some are mocked by the empty hope of grabbing. Either we have paid dearly for worthless loot and damaged ourselves in the bargain, or we have been cheated and left standing there empty-handed. So let us walk out of this game and leave the field to the greedy rabble. Let them stare up at their "goods" dangling overhead — and dangle in suspense themselves.

Whoever makes up his mind to be happy should conclude that the good consists only in what is honorable. For if he counts anything else as good, then to begin with he is passing an unfavorable judgment on Providence — on the grounds that upright men often meet with misfortune, and that the time allotted to us is short and skimpy compared with the eternity allotted to the universe.

It is from complaints like these that we turn ungrateful in our commentary on heaven's gifts: we grumble that they are not granted always, that they are few, uncertain, and fleeting. And so we end up willing neither to live nor to die; we are gripped by hatred of life and fear of death. All our plans drift rudderless, and no amount of prosperity can satisfy us. The reason for all of it is that we have not yet reached the good that is beyond measure and beyond surpassing — the good at which all our wishing has to stop, because there is nowhere beyond the highest. Do you ask why virtue needs nothing? Because it is pleased with what it has and does not lust after what it lacks. In virtue's eyes, whatever is enough is abundant.

Break with this judgment, and duty and loyalty will not survive it. For anyone who wants to show those two qualities must endure much of what the world calls evil, and must sacrifice many of the things we are addicted to under the impression that they are goods. Gone is courage, which ought to be testing itself continually; gone is greatness of soul, which can never stand out clearly until it has learned to scorn as trivial everything the crowd covets as supremely important; gone are kindness and the repaying of kindness, if we fear hard work, if we have admitted that anything is more precious than loyalty, if our eyes are fixed on anything but the best.

But set those questions aside. Either these so-called goods are not goods, or else man is luckier than God — because God gets no enjoyment out of the things that are given to us. Lust has nothing to do with God, nor do elegant banquets, nor wealth, nor any of the things that entice mankind and lead him along by cheap pleasure. So either you must believe — hard as that is — that there are goods God does not possess, or else the very fact that God does not possess them is itself the proof that they are not goods. Besides, many things commonly counted as goods are granted to animals in fuller measure than to men. Animals eat their food with a heartier appetite, are not worn down to the same degree by sex, and have greater, steadier constancy in their strength. It follows that they are far luckier than man. For there is no wickedness in their way of living, no injuring of themselves. They enjoy their pleasures — and take them more often and more easily, without any of the fear that comes from shame or regret.

That being so, ask yourself whether anything deserves to be called good when God is outdone in it by man. Let us confine the Supreme Good to the soul; it loses its meaning if it is taken from the best part of us and handed to the worst — that is, transferred to the senses, for the senses are livelier in dumb beasts than in us. The sum total of our happiness must not be lodged in the flesh. The true goods are the ones reason bestows: substantial and eternal, they cannot fall away, cannot shrink, cannot be whittled down. Other things are goods only by opinion; they share a name with the true goods, but the essence of goodness is not in them. So let us call them "advantages" — or, to use our technical term, "preferred" things. And let us recognize that they are our property, not parts of us. Keep them in your possession, but keep remembering that they stand outside you. Even while we hold them, they should be filed under the subordinate and the second-rate — things no man has a right to preen himself on. For what is more foolish than being smug about something you never accomplished by your own effort? Let all such things be attached to us, not grafted into us, so that if one is taken away it comes off without tearing away any part of us. Use them, don't boast of them — and use them sparingly, as things handed over for safekeeping that will be called back. Anyone who holds them without reason never holds them long; prosperity, left unmanaged by reason, buries itself under its own weight. If a man has put his trust in the most fleeting of goods, he is soon stripped of them — and, to avoid being stripped, he suffers in advance. Few men have been allowed to set prosperity down gently. The rest fall together with everything they rose by, and the very things that once lifted them up come down on top of them. That is why foresight has to step in and impose a limit — a frugality in the use of these things — because license wrecks and squanders its own abundance. What has no limit has never lasted, unless reason, which sets limits, has kept it in check. The fate of many cities will prove the point: their power collapsed at its very peak because they had given themselves to luxury, and excess ruined everything virtue had won. We should fortify ourselves against such calamities. But no wall can be built that Fortune cannot take by storm. Let us strengthen the defenses within. If the inner citadel is safe, a man can be attacked — but never captured.

Do you want to know what this defensive weapon is? It is the ability not to chafe at whatever happens to you — the knowledge that the very forces which seem to do harm are working for the preservation of the world, and are part of the plan that carries the universe to the fulfillment of its order and its functions. Let man be pleased with whatever has pleased God. Let him marvel at himself and his own resources for this very reason: that he cannot be conquered, that he holds the powers of evil themselves under his control, and that he brings chance and pain and wrong into subjection by means of that strongest of all powers — reason. Love reason! The love of reason will arm you against the worst that can happen. Wild beasts throw themselves onto the hunter's spear out of love for their young, and it is their wildness, their unthinking charge, that keeps them from being tamed. A hunger for glory has often driven young minds to despise both sword and stake. The mere vision, the mere semblance of virtue drives certain men to a self-chosen death. And reason is sturdier and steadier than any of these impulses — so with that much more force will she drive her way through the thick of terrors and dangers.

People say to us: "You are mistaken if you claim that nothing is good except what is honorable. A defense like that will not make you safe from Fortune and free from her attacks. You yourselves count dutiful children, a well-governed country, and good parents as goods — but you cannot watch these dear things in danger and stay at ease. Your calm will be shattered by a siege against your country, by the death of your children, by the enslavement of your parents." I will first give the answer we Stoics usually make to these objectors, and then add what I think should be said on top of it.

The situation is entirely different with goods whose loss brings some hardship in as a substitute. When good health is damaged, it changes into illness; when an eye is put out, blindness moves in; when the tendons of our legs are cut, we do not merely lose our speed — lameness takes speed's place. But no such danger attends the goods we were just talking about. Why not? If I have lost a good friend, no false friend is installed whom I must endure in his place; if I have buried a dutiful son, I do not have to face unfilial behavior in exchange. And in the second place, what has happened is not the taking away of a friend or a child — only the taking away of their bodies. A good can be lost in one way only: by changing into something bad. And that is impossible under the law of nature, because every virtue, and every work of virtue, endures uncorrupted. Besides, even if friends have perished, or children of proven goodness who lived up to everything their father prayed for, something exists that can fill their place. Do you ask what it is? The very thing that made them good in the first place: virtue. Virtue leaves no space in us unoccupied; it takes possession of the whole soul and removes all sense of loss. It is sufficient by itself, for the strength and the seed of every good lies in virtue herself. What does it matter if running water is cut off and drains away, so long as the spring it flowed from is unharmed? You will not claim that a man's life is more just if his children are alive than if they have died — nor better ordered, nor more intelligent, nor more honorable. Therefore not better, either. Adding friends does not make a man wiser, and losing them does not make him more foolish — therefore not happier or more wretched, either. As long as your virtue is unharmed, you will not feel the loss of anything that has been taken from you. "Come now," you say, "isn't a man happier when he is surrounded by a great company of friends and children?" Why should he be? The Supreme Good is neither diminished nor increased by them; it stays within its own bounds no matter how Fortune has behaved. Whether a long old age falls to your lot or the end comes well short of old age, the measure of the Supreme Good is the same, whatever the difference in years.

Draw a circle large or draw it small: its size affects its area, not its shape. One circle may sit undisturbed for a long time; the other you may rub out at once, merging it back into the sand it was drawn in — yet each circle had the same shape. What is straight is not judged by its size, or its number, or its duration; it can no more be stretched than it can be shortened. Scale the honorable life down as far as you like from the full hundred years — reduce it to a single day — and it is every bit as honorable. Sometimes virtue operates on a broad front: governing kingdoms, cities, and provinces, making laws, cultivating friendships, regulating the duties that hold between relatives and children. At other times it is hemmed in by the narrow bounds of poverty, exile, or bereavement. But it is no smaller for being brought down from proud heights to a private station, from a royal palace to a humble cottage, or for being gathered from a broad, general jurisdiction into the confines of a private house or a tiny corner. Virtue is just as great even when it has retreated into itself and is shut in on every side. Its spirit is no less great and upright, its judgment no less complete, its justice no less unbending. It is, therefore, equally happy. For happiness has its home in one place only — the mind itself: noble, steadfast, and calm. And that state cannot be reached without knowledge of things divine and human.

The other answer, the one I promised to add, follows from this reasoning. The wise man is not crushed by the loss of children or friends. He endures their death in the same spirit in which he awaits his own — and he fears the one as little as he grieves for the other. For the underlying principle of virtue is harmony: all the works of virtue are in tune and in agreement with virtue itself. And that harmony is lost if the soul, which ought to stand tall, is dragged down by grief and longing. It is always a disgrace for a man to be fretting and agitated, to be numb when the moment calls for action. What is honorable is free of anxiety and unhampered; it is unafraid, and it stands ready to act. "What," you ask, "will the wise man feel no stirring at all — no disturbance of spirit? Won't his face lose its color, his features twitch, his limbs go cold? And there are other reactions we produce not by choice but unconsciously, by a kind of natural reflex." I grant all of that. But the wise man will hold on to the settled conviction that none of these things is an evil, or weighty enough to make a healthy mind break down. Whatever still needs doing, virtue will do it with courage and readiness. For anyone would agree it is the mark of a fool to do what he has to do sluggishly and under protest — to send the body one way and the mind another, and be torn apart between contradictory impulses. Folly is despised for the very things she brags about and admires in herself; she does not even do gladly the things she takes pride in. And if folly fears some evil, she is weighed down by it in the very act of waiting for it, exactly as if it had already arrived — suffering in apprehension everything she is afraid she may suffer. Just as in the body the symptoms of hidden ill-health show up before the disease — a certain limp sluggishness, a tiredness no work has earned, a trembling, a shiver running through the limbs — so the feeble spirit is rattled by its troubles long before it is overwhelmed by them. It lives them in advance, and collapses ahead of schedule.

But what is greater madness than to be tortured by the future — not saving your strength for the actual suffering, but inviting your misery in and hurrying it along? If you cannot get rid of it, you can at least postpone it. Will you not understand that no man should be tormented by what is still to come? A man who is told he will have to face torture fifty years from now is not disturbed by it — unless he vaults over all the intervening years and plants himself in the middle of a trouble scheduled to arrive a generation later. In just the same way, souls that enjoy being sick, that hunt for excuses to be sorrowful, grieve over events long past and wiped from the record. Past and future are both absent; we feel neither one. And there can be no pain except from what you feel.

Farewell.

Letter 75

Letter 75: On the Diseases of the Soul

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You've been complaining that my letters are carelessly written. Well, who talks carefully unless he wants to sound affected? I want my letters to be exactly what my conversation would be if we were sitting together, or out taking a walk — spontaneous and easy. There is nothing strained or artificial in them. If I could, I would rather show you what I feel than tell you. Even if I were arguing a point, I wouldn't stamp my foot or wave my arms or raise my voice; I'd leave all that to the orators, and be content to have gotten my feelings across to you without dressing them up or dragging them down. The one thing I'd like to convince you of completely is this: I feel everything I say — and I don't just feel it, I'm wedded to it. A man kisses his mistress one way and his children another; but in the father's embrace too, holy and restrained as it is, there is plenty of love on display.

Not that I want our conversation about matters this important to be thin and dry — even philosophy doesn't refuse the company of wit. But you shouldn't spend much attention on mere words. Here is the kernel of my idea: let us say what we feel, and feel what we say; let speech harmonize with life. A man has kept his promise when he is the same person whether you see him or hear him. We will see exactly what sort of man he is, and how large a man he is — provided he is one and the same throughout. Let our words aim not to please, but to help. Of course, if eloquence comes to you without labor — if you're naturally gifted, or can pick it up at little cost — then take it and put it to the noblest possible use. But let it be the kind of eloquence that displays the facts rather than itself. Eloquence and the other arts are entirely a matter of cleverness; our business here is the soul.

A sick man doesn't call in a doctor for his speaking voice. If the doctor who can cure him also happens to talk elegantly about the treatment, fine — the patient will take it in good part. But he'll find no reason to congratulate himself on landing an eloquent physician. It's no different from discovering that a skilled ship's pilot is also handsome. Why are you tickling my ears? Why are you entertaining me? That is not the business at hand. I am here to be cauterized, operated on, put on a diet. That's what you were called in to do.

You are being asked to cure a disease that is chronic and serious — one that infects the whole human race. You have as big a job on your hands as a doctor during a plague. And you're worried about words? Be glad right now if you can cope with things. When will you learn everything there is to learn? When will you plant what you've learned so firmly in your mind that it cannot escape? When will you put it all into practice? Because these things can't simply be committed to memory, like other subjects; they have to be tested in action. The happy man is not the one who knows them but the one who does them.

"What?" you say. "Are there no degrees of happiness below your 'happy' man? Is it a sheer cliff just beneath wisdom?" I don't think so. The man who is making progress still counts, technically, among the fools — but a long interval separates him from them. And among the people making progress themselves there are wide gaps. They fall into three classes, as certain philosophers hold.

First come those who don't yet possess wisdom but have already taken up a position nearby — though even "nearby" is still outside. Who are they? If you ask me, they are the men who have already laid aside all their passions and vices, who have learned what to embrace — but whose assurance is still untested. They haven't yet put their good to use. Still, from now on they can't fall back into the faults they escaped. They have already reached a point from which there is no slipping backward — but they don't know it yet. As I remember writing in another letter, "They don't know that they know." It is now given to them to enjoy their good, but not yet to be sure of it. Some define this class — this class of men making progress — as people who have escaped the diseases of the mind but not yet its passions, and who are still standing on slippery ground; because no one is beyond the reach of evil unless he has cleared it away entirely, and no one has cleared it away entirely except the man who has put wisdom in its place.

I've explained before, more than once, the difference between the diseases of the mind and its passions. But let me remind you. The diseases are hardened, chronic vices, like greed and ambition; they have wrapped the mind in too tight a grip and started to become its permanent afflictions. A brief definition: a disease is a persistent distortion of judgment, which makes things that are only mildly worth wanting look intensely worth wanting. Or if you prefer, put it this way: it is straining too hard after things that deserve only mild pursuit or none at all, or prizing highly things that deserve little value or none. The passions are objectionable impulses of the spirit, sudden and violent; when they come often and go unattended, they settle into a state of disease. Think of a cold: one attack, not yet habitual, produces a cough; let it become regular and chronic, and it produces consumption. So we can say that the people who have made the most progress are beyond the reach of the diseases — but they still feel the passions, even when they are very near perfection.

The second class is made up of those who have laid aside both the greatest ills of the mind and its passions, but who don't yet hold their immunity securely. They can still slip back into their former state.

The third class are past many vices — the great vices especially — but not past all of them. They've escaped greed, say, but still feel anger; lust no longer troubles them, but ambition still does; they no longer desire, but they still fear. And because they fear, they stand firm against some things and give way to others: they scorn death and are terrified of pain.

Let's dwell on that for a moment. It will go well with us if we're admitted even to this third class. The second stage takes great good luck in your natural gifts, plus great and unceasing devotion to study. But even the third rank is nothing to sneer at. Think of the mass of evils you see all around you — look how there is no crime without its example, how far wickedness advances every single day, how much goes wrong in the home and in public life. You'll see we're making a considerable gain if we're not counted among the worst.

"But as for me," you say, "I hope I have it in me to rise higher than that!" I would pray for this rather than promise it — we've been beaten to the start. We hurry toward virtue with our vices hanging on us. I'm ashamed to say it, but we honor what is honorable only in our spare time. And yet what a rich reward is waiting if we would only break off the business that keeps cutting ahead of us and the evils that cling to us with such stubborn tenacity! Then neither desire nor fear will rout us. Undisturbed by fears, unspoiled by pleasures, we will dread neither death nor the gods; we will know that death is no evil and that the gods are not powers of evil. What harms is no stronger than what is harmed, and what is utterly good has no power to harm at all. If we ever escape from these low dregs to that high and lofty place, what awaits us is peace of mind and — once every error has been driven out — perfect freedom. You ask what that freedom is? Fearing no man and no god; wanting nothing shameful and nothing in excess; holding supreme power over yourself. And it is a priceless good to be master of yourself.

Farewell.

Letter 76

Letter 76: On Learning Wisdom in Old Age

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You have been threatening to become my enemy if I don't keep you posted on everything I do, day by day. See how frankly you and I live with each other: I am going to confide even this to you. I have been attending a philosopher's lectures. Four days now I have been going to his school and listening to the talk that starts at two in the afternoon. "A fine age for that!" you say. Fine indeed — and what could be sillier than refusing to learn a thing just because you've gone a long time without learning it? "So what are you telling me? Should I fall in line with the dandies and the schoolboys?" Well, if this is the only thing that embarrasses my old age, I'm doing pretty well. The classroom admits men of every age. "Is that why we grow old — so we can trail after the youngsters?" But if I, an old man, can go to the theatre, and have myself carried to the races, and can't bear to let a single fight in the arena be settled without me there to watch, shall I blush to show up at a philosopher's lecture?

You should keep learning as long as there is anything you don't know — which means, if the proverb has it right, as long as you live. And no proverb was ever better suited to its occasion: As long as you live, keep learning how to live. Mind you, I do some teaching in that school as well. What, you ask, could I possibly teach there? This: that even an old man should keep learning. But I am ashamed of the human race every time I walk into the lecture-hall. To get to Metronax's house, as you know, I have to pass right by the Theatre of Naples. The building is packed. Inside, men are deciding, with tremendous passion, who deserves to be called a good flute-player; even the Greek piper and the announcer draw a crowd. But in the other place — where the question on the table is "What is a good man?" and the lesson being taught is how to be one — the seats are nearly empty, and most people think the few who do sit in them are wasting their time. Empty-headed idlers, they get called. I hope I'm blessed with mockery like that. The jeers of the ignorant should be heard with an untroubled mind; when a man is marching toward the goal of honor, he should scorn scorn itself.

So push on, Lucilius, and hurry, or you'll end up like me — learning in your old age. Hurry all the more, in fact, because you have put the subject off for so long, and it is one you will barely master even as an old man. "How much progress will I make?" you ask. As much as you try to make. What are you waiting for? Wisdom doesn't fall to any man by luck. Money will come on its own; offices will be handed to you; influence and authority may well be forced on you; but virtue will not drop into your lap. And the knowledge of it is not won with light effort or a little sweat — though the sweating is worth it, when you are about to win every good there is at a single stroke. For there is only one good: the honorable. In all those other things that public opinion smiles on, you will find nothing true, nothing certain. Why there is only one good, and why it is the honorable, I will now explain — since you have ruled that my earlier letter didn't carry the argument far enough, and that the doctrine was recommended to you rather than proved. I'll also compress what other authors have said into a small space.

Everything is judged by the standard of its own particular good. We value a vine for its yield and the flavor of its wine, a stag for its speed. With pack animals we ask how strong their backs are, since carrying loads is their whole use. In a dog, if his job is to find a wild animal's trail, the first thing we want is a keen nose; if his job is to run the quarry down, fast legs; if to attack and grapple with it, courage. In each thing, the best quality should be the one it was born for — the one it is judged by. And what is the best quality in a human being? Reason. By reason man surpasses the animals and is surpassed only by the gods. Perfect reason, then, is the good that belongs peculiarly to man; everything else he shares, to some degree, with animals and plants. Man is strong: so is the lion. Man is handsome: so is the peacock. Man is swift: so is the horse. I am not saying he is outdone in all these categories. I am not looking for what is greatest in him, but for what is distinctly his. Man has a body: so do trees. Man can act deliberately and move at will: so can beasts, so can worms. Man has a voice — but how much louder is the dog's, how much more piercing the eagle's, how much deeper the bull's, how much sweeter and more musical the nightingale's! What, then, is peculiar to man? Reason. When reason is right and has been brought to perfection, a man's happiness is complete. So if everything is praiseworthy — has reached the goal its nature intended — when it has perfected its own particular good, and if man's particular good is reason, then a man who has perfected his reason is praiseworthy and has reached the goal his nature set for him. This perfect reason has a name: virtue. And virtue and the honorable are one and the same.

It follows that the only good in man is the good that belongs only to man. We are not asking, right now, what is a good; we are asking what is man's good. And if nothing belongs peculiarly to man except reason, then reason will be his one distinctive good — but a good worth all the others put together. If a man is bad, I suppose you will think ill of him; if good, well of him. So the quality in a man by which he earns approval or disapproval is his chief good — his only good. You don't doubt that it is a good; you only doubt that it is the sole one. Suppose a man has everything else — health, money, a long pedigree, a reception-hall full of callers — but is admittedly bad: you will disapprove of him. Suppose another man has none of what I've listed — no money, no train of clients, no rank, no line of grandfathers and great-grandfathers — but is admittedly good: you will approve of him. So this is man's one distinctive good. The man who has it deserves praise even if he lacks everything else; the man who lacks it stands condemned and rejected, though he has everything else in heaps. What holds for men holds for things. We call a ship good not when she is painted in expensive colors, not when her prow is plated with silver or gold or her figurehead carved in ivory, not when she is loaded with the imperial treasury or the wealth of kings — but when she is steady and sound and tight, with seams that keep the water out, sturdy enough to take the pounding of the waves, obedient to her helm, fast, and indifferent to the wind. You will call a sword good not because its belt is gold or its scabbard studded with gems, but because its edge cuts clean and its point will go through any armor. Of a carpenter's rule we do not ask how beautiful it is, but how straight it is. Each thing is praised by the standard it exists to meet — by the quality that is its own.

So in the case of a man, too, the relevant question is not how many acres he plows, how much money he has out at interest, how many callers crowd his morning receptions, how costly the couch he lies on or how transparent the cups he drinks from — but how good he is. And he is good if his reason is well-ordered and right and fitted to what his nature intended. That is what we call virtue; that is what we mean by "honorable"; and it is man's unique good. For since reason alone perfects a man, reason alone, perfected, makes him happy — and man's only good is the one thing that makes him happy. We do say, it's true, that the things virtue produces and gathers around itself — all the works of virtue, that is — are also goods. But virtue itself is the only good for this reason: there is no good without it. If every good resides in the soul, then whatever strengthens the soul, lifts it, enlarges it, is a good; and virtue is what makes the soul stronger, higher, larger. Everything else — all the things that set our desires itching — drags the soul down and weakens it. When we think those things are lifting the soul up, they are merely puffing it up, cheating it with so much air.

The whole of life's conduct is governed by one pair of considerations: the honorable and the base. It is with reference to those two that our reason decides to do a thing or leave it undone. Let me spell out what I mean. A good man will do what he believes it is honorable to do, even if it costs him labor; he will do it even if it costs him harm; he will do it even if it costs him danger. And he will not do what is base, even if it brings him money, or pleasure, or power. Nothing will scare him away from the honorable; nothing will tempt him into the base. So if he is resolved to follow the honorable without exception, to avoid the base without exception, and in every act of his life to keep his eye on those two things — counting nothing good but the honorable, nothing bad but the base — if virtue in him is the one thing uncorrupted, holding its steady course on its own, then virtue is that man's only good, and nothing can happen to it now that would make it anything else. It is past all risk of change. Folly can creep upward toward wisdom; wisdom never slips back into folly.

You may remember my telling you that the things people commonly desire and dread have been trampled underfoot by many a man in a moment of blind passion. Men have been found who would thrust their hands into the flames, whose smiles the torturer could not wipe away, who shed not one tear at their children's funerals, who walked to their deaths without flinching. Love, anger, lust — these have dared every danger. Now if a moment's stubbornness, spurred by some goad pricking the spirit, can manage all that, how much more can virtue manage — virtue, which acts not on impulse or on a sudden, but evenly, with a strength that lasts? It follows that the things which men in a fit of passion often despise, and which the wise despise always, are neither goods nor evils. Virtue itself, then, is the only good. She walks proudly between the two extremes of fortune, with a splendid contempt for both.

But once you accept the view that anything besides the honorable is good, every virtue suffers for it. No virtue can ever be won and held if it has to keep one eye on anything outside itself. And if any such thing exists, it stands in conflict with reason, from which the virtues spring — and with truth, which cannot exist without reason. And any opinion in conflict with truth is false. A good man, you'll grant, must show the utmost devotion to the gods. Therefore he will bear whatever happens to him with an untroubled spirit, knowing it has come about through the divine law by which the whole universe moves. Granted that, there will be one good for him and only one — the honorable — since the honorable itself commands us to obey the gods, not to flare up at sudden misfortune or bewail our lot, but to accept fate patiently and do what it orders. If anything besides the honorable is good, then we will be hounded by a greed for life, and a greed for all the furniture of life — an intolerable condition, endless, never settled. The only good, then, is the honorable: the good that knows its limits.

I have argued before that man's life would be more blessed than the gods' if things the gods have no use for — money, high office — were goods. Consider this too: if our souls survive when they are released from the body, a happier state awaits them than they knew while housed in it. But if the things we use for the body's sake are goods, then our souls will be worse off once set free — and that runs against everything we believe, that the soul is happier shut up and confined than free and away into the universe. I also said that if the things dumb animals possess as fully as man are goods, then dumb animals will lead happy lives — which is of course impossible. And one more point: for the sake of the honorable a man must be willing to endure anything — which would not be necessary if there existed any other good besides it.

I treated all this at more length in an earlier letter; here I have compressed it and run through the argument briefly. But a doctrine like this will never ring true to you until you lift your mind higher and put the question to yourself: if duty called, would you be willing to die for your country — to buy the safety of all your fellow-citizens at the price of your own — offering your neck not merely with patience but with gladness? If you would, then in your eyes there is no other good; for you are giving up everything else to get this one. See what power the honorable has: you will die for your country at a moment's notice, the moment you know you ought to. Sometimes a noble act yields great joy even in the shortest, most fleeting span of time; and though none of the fruits of the finished deed will reach its doer once he is dead and gone from human affairs, still the mere contemplation of the deed to come is a delight. The brave and upright man, picturing the rewards of his death — his country free, everyone he is paying out his life for delivered — feels the keenest pleasure and enjoys the fruit of his own peril. But even the man denied that joy, the joy of contemplating his last great effort, will leap to his death without a heartbeat's hesitation, content to be acting rightly and dutifully. Pile discouragements on him if you like. Tell him, "Your deed will be quickly forgotten," or "Your fellow-citizens will hardly thank you." He will answer: "All that lies outside my task. My thoughts are on the deed itself. I know it is honorable. So wherever honor leads and summons me, I go."

This, then, is the only good — and not only every soul that has reached perfection knows it, but every soul that is noble by nature and rightly inclined. All other goods are cheap and changeable. That is why owning them is such an anxious business. Even when Fortune's kindness has piled them high, they sit heavy on their owners, always pressing down, sometimes crushing. Not one of the men you see dressed in purple is happy — no more than the actors on whom the play bestows a scepter and a royal cloak for the length of the performance. They strut their hour before a packed house, chests out, on their high stage boots; the moment they exit, the boots come off and they shrink back to their real height. Not one of the men whom riches and honors have set on high is actually great. Then why does he look great to you? Because you are measuring the pedestal along with the man. A dwarf is not tall because he stands on a mountain-top; a colossus keeps its height even standing in a well. This is the mistake we labor under, the trick that keeps fooling us: we never price a man at what he is; we add on the trappings he comes dressed in. But when you want to know a man's true worth — what kind of man he really is — appraise him naked. Have him set aside his inherited estate, his titles, and all Fortune's other frauds; have him strip off his very body. Look at his soul: its quality, its stature. Then you will know whether his greatness is his own or borrowed.

If a man can watch the flash of a sword with steady eyes, if he knows it makes no difference to him whether his soul leaves through his mouth or through a wound in his throat, call him happy. Call him happy too if, when bodily torture is threatened — by accident or by the strength of the stronger — he can hear talk of chains and exile and all the empty terrors that rattle men's minds, and remain unconcerned, and say:

  Maiden, no shape of trouble rises here
  that is new to me, or sudden; I have gone
  before it all — faced and surveyed it, long ago,
  within my soul.

"Today it is you who threaten me with these horrors; but I have always threatened myself with them, and prepared myself, a man, for a man's destiny." An evil pondered in advance lands as a soft blow. To the fool, and to anyone who trusts in Fortune, each event arrives as a shape "new and sudden"; for the inexperienced, a large part of any evil is its novelty. The proof: things men first counted as hardships they bear more bravely once they have grown used to them. That is why the wise man accustoms himself to troubles before they come — lightening by long reflection what others lighten only by long endurance. We sometimes hear the inexperienced say, "I knew this was coming to me." The wise man knows that everything is coming to him. Whatever happens, he says: "I knew it."

Farewell.

Letter 77

Letter 77: On Taking One’s Own Life

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Today, without warning, the “Alexandrian” ships came into view — I mean the ones that are sent ahead to announce the fleet is coming; people call them mail-boats. All Campania is glad to see them. The whole crowd of Puteoli stands packed on the docks, and no matter how many vessels are out there, they can pick out the Alexandrian boats by the very set of their sails. Those ships alone are allowed to keep their topsails spread — the upper canvas that every ship flies on the open sea, because nothing drives a ship along like her topsails; that is where most of the speed comes from. So when the breeze stiffens and grows stronger than is comfortable, crews lower their yards, since the wind has less force close to the water. But once the ships have made Capri and the headland where

  Tall Pallas keeps her watch upon the stormy peak,

every other vessel is ordered to make do with her mainsail — and the topsail stands out, unmistakable, on the Alexandrian mail-boats.

While everyone else was bustling and hurrying down to the waterfront, I took great pleasure in my own laziness. I would soon have letters from my friends in hand, but I felt no rush to learn how my affairs were doing overseas, or what news the letters carried. For some time now I have had no losses — and no gains either. Even if I were not an old man, that thought would have pleased me; as things stand, it pleased me all the more. However little I might own, I would still have more travel-money left than road left to travel — especially since the road we are on is one we need not follow to the very end. A journey is incomplete if you stop halfway, or anywhere short of your destination; but a life is not incomplete if it is honorable. Wherever you stop living, if you stop nobly, your life is whole. And often a man must stop bravely, and not necessarily for momentous reasons — after all, the reasons that keep us here are not momentous either.

Tullius Marcellinus — a man you knew very well, quiet in his youth and old before his time — fell ill with a disease that was by no means hopeless, but long, troublesome, and demanding, and so he began to think about dying. He called many of his friends together, and each gave him advice. The timid friend urged him to do whatever he had already made up his mind to do. The flattering, wheedling friend offered the counsel he guessed Marcellinus would find most pleasing when he thought it over later. But our Stoic friend — a rare man, and, to give him the praise he deserves, a man of courage and vigor — advised him best of all, it seems to me. He began like this: “Don’t torment yourself, my dear Marcellinus, as if the question you are weighing were a great matter. Living is not a great matter; all your slaves live, and so do all the animals. What is a great matter is to die honorably, sensibly, bravely. Think how long you have been doing the same things: food, sleep, desire — around and around, the same daily circle. The wish to die can be felt not only by the sensible man, or the brave man, or the unhappy man, but even by the man who is simply full.”

Marcellinus did not need someone to urge him so much as someone to help him: his slaves refused to do what he asked. So the Stoic first took away their fear, showing them the household ran a risk only when it was uncertain whether the master’s death was his own choice — and that besides, it was as bad a practice to kill one’s master as to stop him by force from ending his own life. Then he suggested to Marcellinus himself that it would be a kindness, when life was finished, to hand out gifts to those who had attended him through the whole of it — just as, when a banquet ends, what remains is shared among the attendants standing around the table. Marcellinus was an easygoing and generous soul, even where his own property was concerned; so he distributed small sums among his grieving slaves, and comforted them too. He needed no sword and no bloodshed. For three days he fasted, and had a tent set up in his own bedroom. Then a tub was brought in; he lay in it a long while, and as the hot water was poured over him continuously, he slipped away little by little — not without a certain pleasure, as he himself remarked, the kind of pleasure a slow fading-out tends to bring. Those of us who have ever fainted know from experience what that feeling is.

I have wandered into this little story, but you will not be sorry to hear it, for you will see that your friend’s departure was neither difficult nor painful. Though he brought about his own death, he made the gentlest of exits — he simply glided out of life. And the story may be of some use, too; a crisis often calls for exactly such examples. There are times when we ought to die and are unwilling; and times when we are dying, and are unwilling still. No one is so ignorant as not to know that he must die someday — and yet when death comes near, a man turns to run, trembles, weeps. Wouldn’t you call him an utter fool if he cried because he was not alive a thousand years ago? Then he is just as much a fool if he cries because he will not be alive a thousand years from now. It is all the same: you will not be, and you were not. Neither stretch of time belongs to you. You have been set down on this single point of time — and if you could stretch it, how far would you stretch it? Why weep? Why pray? You are taking pains for nothing.

  Give up the hope that prayers of yours can bend
  what the gods have decreed from its appointed end.

Those decrees are fixed and unalterable, driven by a mighty, everlasting necessity. You will go where all things go. What is strange about that? You were born under this law. It happened to your father, your mother, your ancestors, everyone before you; it will happen to everyone after you. An unbreakable chain that no power can alter binds all things together and draws all things along. Think of the multitudes bound for death who will come after you; think of the multitudes who will go with you! You would die more bravely, I suppose, in the company of many thousands — well, there are many thousands, men and animals both, drawing their last breath at this very moment, in all their different ways, while you stand there hesitating. Did you really believe you would never arrive at the destination you have been traveling toward your whole life? There is no journey without an end.

You expect me, I suppose, to bring out examples of great men at this point. No — I will cite the case of a boy. The story of the Spartan lad has come down to us: taken captive while still very young, he kept shouting in his Doric dialect, “I will not be a slave!” — and he was as good as his word. The very first time he was ordered to do menial, degrading work — the command was to fetch a chamber-pot — he dashed his head against the wall and died. Freedom is that close at hand — and is anyone still a slave? Wouldn’t you rather your own son died like that than grew old by giving in weakly? Why be distressed, then, when even a boy can die so bravely? Refuse to follow, and you will be led. Take into your own hands what is now in the hands of another. Won’t you borrow that boy’s courage and say, “I am no slave”? Poor fellow — you are a slave to men, a slave to your business, a slave to life itself. For life, if the courage to die is missing, is slavery.

And what exactly are you waiting for? The very pleasures that make you linger, that hold you back — you have used them all up. Not one of them is new to you; not one of them hasn’t already turned stale from sheer excess. You know the taste of wine and of cordials. It makes no difference whether a hundred measures pass through your bladder or a thousand: you are nothing but a wine-strainer. You are a connoisseur of the oyster and the mullet; your luxury has left nothing untasted for the years ahead — and yet these are the things you are torn from unwillingly. What else is there you would hate to lose? Friends? Who can be a friend to a man like you? Country? Do you care enough about your country to be late for dinner? The light of the sun? You would blow it out if you could — for what have you ever done that could bear to be seen in the light? Admit the truth: it is not the senate chamber or the forum you are reluctant to leave, not even the natural world — you just can’t bear to leave the fish-market, though you have emptied its stalls.

You are afraid of death — but how heroically you scorn it over a plate of mushrooms! You want to live: do you even know how? You are afraid to die.
