But tell me: is this life of yours anything but death? Gaius Caesar was passing along the Via Latina when a man stepped out from a column of prisoners, his gray beard hanging to his chest, and begged to be put to death. “What!” said Caesar. “You call this being alive?” That is the answer for men to whom death would come as a relief. “You’re afraid to die? You call this being alive?” “But,” someone says, “I want to live — I am doing so much honorable work. I am reluctant to leave life’s duties; I am carrying them out loyally and tirelessly.” Well — don’t you know that dying is one of life’s duties too? You are deserting no duty, for there is no fixed quota you are obliged to complete.

There is no life that is not short. Measured against the universe, even Nestor’s life was short — even Sattia’s, the woman who had it carved on her tombstone that she had lived ninety-nine years. Some people, you see, boast about a long old age; but who could have stood that old lady if she’d had the luck to round out her hundredth year? Life is like a play: what matters is not how long the acting runs, but how good it is. It makes no difference where you stop. Stop wherever you like — only make sure the ending is well turned.

Farewell.

Letters 78–79

Letter 78: On the Healing Power of the Mind

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I'm sorry to hear that you keep being hit by congestion in the head and by the short fevers that follow when the congestion turns chronic — sorry above all because I've had this very illness myself. In its early stages I laughed it off. When I was young I could take hardship and stare a sickness down. But eventually it beat me. I sank to the point where I could do nothing but sniffle, wasted away to skin and bone. More than once I had the impulse to end my life right then. What held me back was the thought of my father — a kind old man. I considered not how bravely I could die, but how little brave he could be at losing me. So I ordered myself to live. Sometimes even staying alive is an act of courage.

Now let me tell you what consoled me in those days, saying up front that these comforts worked on me like medicine. Honest consolation becomes a cure; whatever lifts the soul helps the body too. My studies saved me. I credit philosophy with my recovery, with my getting back on my feet. I owe her my life — and that is the least of what I owe her. My friends also did a great deal for my health. Their encouraging words, the hours they sat by my bed, their conversation — all of it comforted me. Nothing, my excellent Lucilius, restores and strengthens a sick man like the affection of his friends; nothing so quietly steals away the expectation and the fear of death. With them around me, I honestly could not believe I was dying at all. I felt sure I would go on living — not with them, but through them. I imagined myself not surrendering my soul but handing it over to them for safekeeping.

All of this gave me the will to help myself and to endure anything. And besides, it is a miserable state to have lost your appetite for dying without having any appetite for living. These, then, are the remedies you should turn to. The doctor will prescribe how much to walk and how much to exercise. He will warn you not to sink into idleness, the way the inactive invalid tends to do. He will have you read aloud in a stronger voice to work the lungs, whose passages and hollows are the trouble; or take a sail and stir up your insides with a little gentle rocking. He will tell you what to eat, and when wine will build your strength and when to skip it so your cough doesn't get raw and hacking. But my prescription for you is this — and it is a cure not just for this illness but for your whole life: despise death. Once we have escaped the fear of death, there is no sorrow left in the world.

Every illness carries three serious evils: fear of death, bodily pain, and the interruption of our pleasures. About death enough has been said, and I'll add only this: the fear of it is not a fear of illness but a fear of nature. Illness has often postponed death, and looking like a dying man has saved more than one man's life. You will die not because you are sick, but because you are alive. Even cured, the same end waits for you; in recovering, you have escaped ill health, not death.

Now back to the evil that belongs to illness itself: it comes with great suffering. But the suffering is made bearable by its interruptions, because pain at its most extreme has to end. No one can suffer both intensely and for long. Nature, who loves us most tenderly, has built us so that pain is either bearable or brief. The sharpest pains strike in the slenderest parts of the body: nerves, joints, and the other narrow passages hurt most cruelly when trouble builds inside their cramped spaces. But those parts soon go numb, and the very pain robs them of the feeling of pain — whether because the life-force, blocked from its natural course and turned bad, loses the particular power by which it thrives and by which it warns us, or because the diseased fluids of the body, with nowhere left to flow, back up on themselves and deaden the parts they have flooded. That is why gout in the feet and hands, and every pain in the spine and the nerves, has its truces — the moments when it has stunned the parts it was torturing. In all these cases it is the first twinges that do the damage; time blunts the attack, and the pain ends when the numbness sets in. Pain in the teeth, eyes, and ears is so acute precisely because it starts in the body's narrowest spaces — no less acute, frankly, than pain in the head itself. But when it grows more violent than usual, it tips over into delirium and stupor. So here is the consolation for extreme pain: feel it too much and you are guaranteed to stop feeling it. What makes the untrained buckle under bodily suffering is that they have never learned to be content in spirit. They have been too intimate with the body. That is why a great-souled and sensible man separates soul from body and spends his time with the better, divine part, keeping only as much company as he must with this whining, fragile one.

"But it's hard," people say, "to give up the pleasures we're used to — to fast, to go thirsty and hungry." Yes, abstinence is hard at first. Later the craving dies down, because the appetites that drive the craving get tired and quit. Then the stomach turns fussy; then the food we once ached for turns our own stomach. The wants themselves die away. And there is no bitterness in doing without what you have stopped wanting. Besides, every pain stops sometimes, or at least eases up. Besides, you can take precautions against its return and, when it threatens, head it off with remedies. Every kind of pain sends warning signals — at any rate the habitual, recurring kind does. The suffering an illness brings can be endured, if you have learned to scorn what it threatens. But do not make your troubles heavier than they are and weigh yourself down with complaining. Pain is slight if opinion adds nothing to it. If instead you cheer yourself on the other way — "It's nothing, or nearly nothing; keep a stout heart, it will pass" — then by thinking it slight, you will make it slight. Everything hangs on opinion. Ambition, luxury, greed — all look to opinion. It is according to opinion that we suffer. A man is as wretched as he has convinced himself he is. So I say we should throw out all whining over past suffering, all talk like this: "No one was ever worse off. The agonies, the horrors I endured! Nobody thought I'd pull through. How many times my family wept over me, how many times the doctors gave up on me! Men on the rack aren't torn apart like that!" Even if all of it is true, it is over. What is the good of dragging up old sufferings and being miserable now because you were miserable once? Besides, everyone inflates his own ills and lies to himself about them. And what was bitter to bear is sweet to have borne; it is only natural to rejoice when your troubles end.

Two things, then, must be cut out once and for all: fear of future suffering and memory of past suffering. The past no longer concerns me; the future does not concern me yet. And a man caught in the very middle of troubles should say:

  Someday, perhaps, even this pain
  will be sweet to remember.

Let him fight them with everything he has. Give way once, and he is beaten; strain against his sufferings, and he wins. As it is, most men pull down on their own heads the very ruin they ought to be propping up. When something is toppling toward you, tottering, about to crash, step back from under it and it will follow you and lean on you harder; stand your ground and push back, and it will be forced away. Think of the blows athletes take to the face, to the whole body. For the sake of fame they endure every torment — and they take those blows not only while fighting but in order to be fit to fight. Their very training is torture. Let us win our victories in all our own struggles the same way — for our prize is not a garland or a palm branch or a trumpeter calling for silence while our name is announced, but virtue, steadiness of soul, and a peace won for all time, once Fortune has been beaten outright in even a single bout. "I'm in terrible pain," you say. And does the pain let up because you whimper through it? Just as an enemy is deadliest to an army in retreat, so every trouble Fortune sends hits hardest when we give ground and turn our backs. "But it's serious." What — is that what our strength is for, to carry light loads? Would you rather your illness be long, or quick and short? If it is long, it comes with intermissions: it gives you stretches to recover in, it grants you plenty of time; as it builds, it must also subside. A short, sharp illness will do one of two things: it will be snuffed out, or it will snuff you out. And what difference does it make whether the illness ends or I do? Either way, the pain is over.

This helps too: turn the mind to other things and walk away from the pain. Call up the honorable and brave things you have done; go over the good side of your own life; run through your memory of everything you have most admired. Then think of all the brave men who have conquered pain: the man who went on reading his book while he had his varicose veins cut out; the man who never stopped smiling, though that smile so enraged his torturers that they tried every instrument of their cruelty on him. If pain can be beaten by a smile, can it not be beaten by reason? Name whatever you please — head colds, coughing fits so hard they bring up pieces of our insides, fever that cooks us from the core, thirst, limbs wrenched until the joints stick out in different directions. Worse still are the stake, the rack, the red-hot plates, the device that reopens wounds while they are still swollen and drives its imprint deeper. And yet there have been men who never let out a groan under those tortures. "More!" says the torturer — and the victim doesn't beg off. "More!" — no answer. "More!" — and the victim smiles, and means it. After an example like that, can't you bring yourself to laugh at pain?

"But my illness won't let me do anything," you object. "It has pulled me away from all my duties." It is your body that is hampered by ill health, not your soul too. Illness will slow a runner's feet and tie up the hands of a cobbler or a craftsman; but if your soul has kept in training, you will still advise and teach, listen and learn, investigate and reflect. What more is needed? Do you think you are doing nothing when you keep your self-control while sick? You will be demonstrating that an illness can be overcome — or at least endured. There is room for virtue, I promise you, even on a sickbed. It is not only the sword and the battle line that prove a soul alert and unafraid; a man can show bravery wrapped in his blankets. You have work to do: wrestle well with your disease. If it forces you to nothing and talks you into nothing, you are setting a remarkable example. What a stage for glory it would be, if anyone came to watch us be sick! So be your own spectator; seek your own applause.

Again, there are two kinds of pleasures. Illness checks the pleasures of the body, but it does not abolish them. In fact, truth be told, it sharpens them: a drink delights a man more the thirstier he is; food delights him more the hungrier he is. Whatever comes after a fast is received with extra relish. And the other kind — the pleasures of the mind, which are higher and more reliable — no doctor can deny a sick man. Whoever pursues these, and knows what they really are, scorns all the flatteries of the senses. "Poor sick fellow!" people say. Why exactly? Because he doesn't chill his wine with snow? Because he doesn't chip ice into his cup to refresh the cold of a drink already mixed in a good-sized bowl? Because Lucrine oysters aren't shucked fresh at his table? Because there's no racket of cooks around his dining room, hauling in their stoves right along with the dishes? That is luxury's latest invention: the kitchen accompanies the dinner, so that no course goes lukewarm and nothing arrives insufficiently scalding for a palate already grown leathery. "Poor sick fellow!" — he will eat as much as he can digest. There will be no boar lying in state before his eyes, banished from the table as if it were too common a meat; his sideboard will not be heaped with the breast-meat of birds, because the sight of birds served whole turns his stomach. And what harm has been done to you? You will dine like a sick man — which is to say, once in a while, like a healthy one.

All of it is easy to endure — the gruel, the warm water, everything that seems unbearable to a fussy man gone soft with luxury, sick in soul rather than body — if only we stop shuddering at death. And we will stop, once we have learned the limits of good and evil. Then and only then will life not exhaust us or death frighten us. For weariness of self can never seize a life that surveys all the things that are — manifold, great, divine; it is idle leisure that makes men come to hate their lives. To one who roams the universe, the truth never grows stale; it is the falsehoods that cloy. And on the other side, if death comes calling early — even if it cuts a man down in his prime — he has already tasted everything the longest life has to give. Such a man has largely come to understand the universe. He knows that honorable things do not depend on time to grow. Any life must look short to people who measure it by pleasures that are empty and therefore bottomless.

Refresh yourself with thoughts like these — and meanwhile set aside some hours for our letters. A time will come when we are together again, reunited; however short that time is, we will make it long by knowing how to use it. For as Posidonius says: "A single day among the learned lasts longer than the longest life of the ignorant." In the meantime hold on to this, grip it tight: do not yield to adversity; do not trust prosperity; keep the full reach of Fortune's power before your eyes, as if she would surely do everything she has the power to do. What has been long expected arrives more gently.

Farewell.

Letter 79: On the Rewards of Scientific Discovery

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I have been waiting for a letter from you telling me what your tour around Sicily turned up — above all, a full report on Charybdis itself. Scylla, I know perfectly well, is just a rock, and not even a rock sailors fear; but about Charybdis I want a complete description, to see whether it matches the myths. And if you happen to have investigated it — it certainly deserves investigating — enlighten me on this: is it whipped into a whirlpool only by wind from one quarter, or do storms from every direction churn its depths alike? And is it true that whatever the whirlpool sucks down in that strait is carried underwater for miles and surfaces on the beach near Tauromenium? Write me a full account of all that, and then I'll have the nerve to ask a further favor: climb Etna, too, at my special request. Certain naturalists have concluded that the mountain is wearing away and gradually sinking, because sailors used to be able to see it from farther off. The reason may be not that its height is shrinking but that the fires have dimmed — the eruptions weaker and thinner, and the smoke, for the same reason, lazier by day. Either explanation is believable: that the mountain is growing smaller because it eats itself daily, or that it stays the same size because it is not devouring itself at all — the boiling matter collects in some underground hollow and is fed from elsewhere, using the mountain not as fuel but simply as an exit. There is a well-known spot in Lycia — the locals call it Hephaestion — where the ground is riddled with holes and ringed by a harmless fire that does no damage to the plants growing there. The place is actually fertile and lush, because the flames don't scorch; they merely glow, with a force too mild and feeble to burn.

But let's shelve that question until you have gone and can tell me just how far from the crater the snow lies — I mean the snow that doesn't melt even in summer, so safe is it from the fire next door. And don't bill this errand to my account: you were going to indulge your itch for fine writing with no commission from anybody. In fact, what would I have to pay you not to work Etna into your poem — not to touch the theme every poet treats as a required rite? Virgil had already covered it in full, and that didn't stop Ovid from taking it up; and neither of them scared off Cornelius Severus. Besides, the ground has been lucky for all of them, and to my eye the earlier poets did not use up what can be said — they merely opened the way.

It makes all the difference whether you take up a subject that is exhausted or one where the ground has only been broken. In the second case the topic grows day by day, and what is already discovered is no obstacle to discovering more. Besides, the one who writes last gets the best of the bargain: he finds the words lying ready, and arranged in a new order they show a new face. And he isn't stealing them, as if they belonged to somebody else — words are common property. Now, if Etna doesn't make your mouth water, I've misjudged you. You have been itching for a while to write something big, something on the scale of the old school. Your modesty won't let you hope any higher — indeed it's so pronounced that I think you would rein in your own talent if there were any danger of outdoing your betters. That is how much you revere the old masters. Wisdom has this advantage, among others: no one can be outdone except during the climb. Once you have reached the top, it's a draw — no room left to rise, the game is over. Can the sun grow bigger? Can the moon swell past her usual fullness? The seas do not increase. The universe keeps the same character, the same limits. Whatever has reached its full stature cannot get taller. So all who have attained wisdom will be equal, on level footing. Each will have his own particular gifts — one more genial, another quicker, another readier in speech, a fourth more eloquent — but in the thing that matters here, the thing that makes life happy, they are all equal. Whether this Etna of yours can collapse into ruins, whether that towering summit, visible for miles across the open sea, is being eaten away by the relentless fire, I do not know. But this I do know: virtue will never be brought lower, by flames or by ruins. Hers is the one greatness that knows no lowering; she can neither rise further nor sink. Her height, like the height of the stars, is fixed. Let us strive to raise ourselves to that altitude.

Much of the climb is behind me already — no; if I make myself tell the truth, not much. Goodness does not mean merely being better than the worst. Who would brag about his eyesight because he could catch a glimmer of daylight? A man who sees the sun through fog may be glad for now that he has escaped the dark, but he does not yet enjoy the blessing of light. Our souls will have no cause to celebrate until they have broken out of this darkness they grope in — until they have not merely glimpsed the brightness with weak eyes but absorbed the full light of day and been restored to their place in the sky, the place assigned them at birth. The soul is summoned upward by its very origin. And it can arrive there even before it is released from this prison below — as soon as it has shaken off its faults and leaps, pure and light, into celestial thoughts.

I am glad, dearest Lucilius, that this is the ideal that occupies us, that we pursue it with everything we have, even if few people know it — even if no one does. Fame is virtue's shadow: it follows her whether she wants it or not. But as a shadow sometimes walks ahead and sometimes trails behind, so fame sometimes goes before us in plain sight and sometimes hangs back — and is all the greater for coming late, once envy has retired from the field. How long did men take Democritus for a madman! Glory barely found Socrates in time. And how long did Rome fail to recognize Cato! The state rejected him and did not learn his worth until it had lost him. If Rutilius had not resigned himself to injustice, his innocence and virtue would have gone unnoticed; the hour of his persecution was the hour of his triumph. Didn't he give thanks for his lot and greet his exile with open arms? So far I have named men whom Fortune made famous in the very moment she was crushing them. But how many men's progress toward virtue came to light only after they died! And how many have been ruined by their reputations rather than raised by them! Take Epicurus: see how he is admired now, not just by the cultivated but by the ignorant crowd as well. Yet this man was unknown to Athens itself, though he had tucked himself away practically next door. So when he had outlived his friend Metrodorus by many years, he added these last words to a letter, celebrating their friendship with grateful pride: "So blessed were Metrodorus and I that it has done us no harm to be unknown, and almost unheard of, in this famous land of Greece." Isn't it true, then, that men did not discover him until after he was gone? And hasn't his renown blazed out all the same? Metrodorus admits the same thing in one of his letters — that he and Epicurus were hardly known to the public — but he declares that whoever chose to follow in their footsteps after they were gone would find a great name ready-made and waiting.

Virtue is never truly hidden; and yet to have been hidden is no loss to her. A day will come that reveals her, however buried or suppressed by the spite of her own times. The man who thinks only of his own generation was born for a mere handful. Many thousands of years, many thousands of peoples, are still to come — look to them. Even if envy has silenced every mouth of your own day, men will come who will judge you without malice and without favor. If fame is any reward virtue receives, that reward cannot perish either. True, the talk of posterity will not reach us; but posterity will cherish and celebrate us all the same, unaware though we are. Virtue has never failed to repay a man, in life and after it, provided he followed her honestly — provided he never dressed himself up or painted himself over, but was the same man whether people saw him announced and expected or suddenly and unprepared. Pretending accomplishes nothing. A mask thrown loosely over the face fools very few. Truth is the same through and through. Whatever deceives has no substance. Lies are thin stuff: hold them to the light and you see right through them.

Farewell.

Letters 80–81

Letter 80: On Worldly Deceptions

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Today I have some free time — thanks less to my own efforts than to the games, which have drawn off every bore in town to the boxing match. No one will barge in on me, no one will derail my train of thought, which moves ahead all the more boldly for that very confidence. The door hasn't been creaking on its hinges every five minutes; no one will be twitching my curtain aside; my thoughts can march on in safety. And that matters most for a man who travels under his own power and cuts his own path. Do I follow no predecessors, then? I do — but I give myself permission to discover something new, to change things, to throw things out. I am not their slave; I simply give them my vote.

And yet that was a bold thing I just said, promising myself quiet and uninterrupted seclusion. Because listen: a huge roar has just come up from the stadium. It doesn't scatter my thoughts, but it does shift them to a comparison the noise itself suggests. How many men train their bodies, I think to myself, and how few train their minds! What crowds pour into a show that is pure sham, staged for nothing but amusement — and what a desert reigns wherever the good arts are taught! How empty-headed are the athletes whose muscles and shoulders we all admire! Here is the question I keep chewing on: if the body can be drilled to such endurance that it stands up to the punches and kicks of several opponents at once, that a man can last out the whole day under a scorching sun in the burning dust, soaked the entire time in his own blood — if the body can do that, how much more easily could the mind be toughened to take Fortune's blows without going down for good, to struggle back to its feet after it has been flattened, after it has been trampled? The body needs a long list of supplies to be strong; the mind grows from within, feeding and exercising itself. Those athletes need mountains of food, rivers of drink, quantities of oil, and years of training on top of it all. Virtue comes to you without equipment and without expense. Everything it takes to make you a good man is already inside you.

And what do you need in order to become good? To want it. But what better thing could you possibly want than to break out of this slavery — a slavery that weighs on every one of us, and that even slaves of the lowest rank, born into that degradation, fight to strip off by every means they have? To buy their freedom they pay out savings scraped together by cheating their own stomachs. And you won't leap at liberty at any price — you, who claim you were born to it? Stop glancing at your strong-box. Freedom cannot be bought. There is no point entering "freedom" as an item in your ledger; freedom belongs neither to the men who have bought it nor to the men who have sold it. This good you must give to yourself; from yourself you must ask it.

Free yourself first from the fear of death — that is what sets the yoke on our necks — and then from the fear of poverty. If you want to know how little evil there is in poverty, compare the faces of the poor with the faces of the rich. The poor man smiles more often, and more honestly. His troubles don't go down deep; even when some anxiety hits him, it passes like a scrap of cloud. But the good cheer of the people we call happy is an act, while their sadness is heavy and festering — all the heavier because they are not allowed to show it. They must play the part of happiness while sorrows eat out their hearts. I find myself reaching for the same illustration again and again, because nothing captures so well this drama of human life, in which we are handed parts we then play so badly. There stands the actor, strutting the stage with his chest thrown out and his head thrown back, declaiming:

  Behold, I am the man whom Argos hails as lord,
  the heir of Pelops, master of the lands
  that run from the Hellespont and the Ionian sea
  down to the straits of the Isthmus.

And who is this fellow? A slave. His wages: five measures of grain and five denarii. And that other one — proud, imperious, swollen with confidence in his own power — who thunders:

  Silence, Menelaus, or this hand strikes you dead!

He earns a daily pittance and sleeps on rags. You can say the same of all those fine gentlemen you see riding in litters above the heads of the crowd: in every case their happiness is a costume, put on like an actor's mask. Tear it off, and you will despise them.

When you buy a horse, you order the blanket removed. When slaves are put up for sale, you have their clothes pulled off, so that no bodily flaw can hide from you. And when you judge a man, you judge him with the disguise still on? Slave-dealers use a bit of finery to cover any defect that might put buyers off, which is exactly why the finery itself makes a buyer suspicious. If you spotted a leg or an arm wrapped in bandages, you would demand the wrappings off and the limb itself shown to you. Do you see that Scythian or Sarmatian king over there, his head decorated with the badge of his office? If you want to appraise him — to know his full worth — take off the diadem. A great deal of evil hides underneath it. But why am I talking about other people? If you want to set a value on yourself, put away your money, your estates, your titles, and look inside — into your own soul. Right now, you are taking other people's word for what you are.

Farewell.

Letter 81: On Benefits

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You complain that you have run into an ungrateful man. If this is your first time, thank either your luck or your caution. Though in this case, caution can accomplish nothing except to make you stingy. If you want to dodge this particular danger, you will simply stop doing people favors — and so, to keep your benefits from being wasted on someone else, you will waste them yourself.

Better to get no return than to give no benefits. Even after a bad harvest, a man sows again; the losses of a stubborn, barren soil are often made good by one fertile year. To find one grateful person, it is worth testing many ungrateful ones. No one's hand is so sure in giving that he isn't frequently fooled — and it is good for the traveler to lose the road sometimes, so that he learns to hold to it. After a shipwreck, sailors go back to sea. The banker is not scared out of the forum by one swindler. If we had to drop everything that ever gave us trouble, life would soon go slack and dull with idleness. In your case, let this very setback make you more generous: when the outcome of an undertaking is uncertain, you have to try again and again to succeed in the end. But I have discussed all this at length in the books I wrote called On Benefits.

The question that really needs examining — one I don't think has ever been made clear enough — is this: whether a man who once helped us has squared the account and released us from our debt if he harms us later. Add this one too, if you like: what if the later harm outweighs the earlier help? If you want the formal verdict of a strict judge, you will find he offsets one act against the other and rules: "Though the injuries outweigh the benefits, still, whatever of the benefit survives the injury should be credited to the benefit's side." The harm was greater, yes — but the help came first, so time must be weighed as well. Other points are too obvious to need spelling out: How gladly was the help given? How reluctantly was the harm done? Benefits and injuries alike are matters of intent. "I didn't want to do him the favor, but respect for the man won me over" — or his pestering did, or hope of something in return. What we owe in every case depends on the spirit in which the gift was given: we weigh not the size of the gift but the quality of the goodwill behind it. So away with guesswork. The earlier act was a benefit; the later act, which outweighed it, was an injury. The good man arranges the two columns of his ledger so as to cheat himself on purpose — adding to the benefit, subtracting from the injury.

The more lenient judge, though — and I would rather be that judge — will order us to forget the injury and remember the favor. "But surely," you say, "justice means rendering each man his due: thanks for a benefit, and retribution, or at the very least resentment, for an injury!" That holds, I answer, when one man inflicted the injury and a different man conferred the benefit. When it is the same man, the benefit cancels the injury's force. A man who deserves pardon even with no good deeds on his record deserves something better than mere pardon when he does wrong with a benefit standing to his credit. I do not price benefits and injuries at the same rate; I rate a benefit higher. Not every grateful person understands what it means to be in debt for a benefit. Even a thoughtless, crude fellow out of the common herd may know it, especially just after the gift arrives; what he does not know is how deep the debt goes. Only the wise man knows the exact value of everything. The fool I just mentioned, however good his intentions, either pays less than he owes, or pays at the wrong time or in the wrong place; what he ought to return, he fumbles and loses.

There is a wonderfully precise vocabulary for certain things — a long-established terminology that captures whole duties in single expressions. We say, as you know: "So-and-so has made a return for the favor." Making a return means handing over what you owe of your own free will. We do not say he has "paid back" the favor — "pay back" is for a man who has been dunned, for men who pay unwillingly, or under any circumstances at all, or through a third party. We do not say he has "restored" the benefit, or "settled" it; we have never been content with a word that properly belongs to a money debt. Making a return means offering something to the man from whom you received something. The phrase implies a voluntary act: the man who has made a return has served the summons on himself.

The wise man will run through every circumstance in his own mind: how much he received, from whom, when, where, how. And so we declare that no one but the wise man knows how to return a favor — and, for that matter, no one but the wise man knows how to confer a benefit: I mean the man who enjoys the giving more than the receiver enjoys the getting. Someone will file this remark among the startling statements we Stoics are famous for, the ones the Greeks call "paradoxes," and will say: "So you maintain that only the wise man knows how to return a favor? Then no one else knows how to repay a creditor, or pay a seller the full price of his goods?" To keep the resentment off my own head, let me point out that Epicurus says the very same thing; Metrodorus, at any rate, remarks that only the wise man knows how to return a favor. Our objector is also astonished when we say, "Only the wise man knows how to love; only the wise man is a real friend." Yet returning favors is a part of love and of friendship — an ordinary part, in fact, more common than real friendship itself. He is astonished, too, at our saying, "There is no loyalty except in the wise man" — as if he didn't say the same thing himself! Or do you suppose there is loyalty in a man who doesn't know how to return a favor? So let these people stop accusing us of impossible boasting and understand this: the real substance of honor lives in the wise man; what circulates in the crowd is honor's ghost and imitation. None but the wise man knows how to return a favor. Even a fool can return one, up to the limit of his knowledge and his power; what he lacks would be understanding, not willingness. Wanting to is not something that can be taught.

The wise man will compare everything against everything else, for the very same object grows larger or smaller with the time, the place, and the reason. Often all the riches lavished on a palace accomplish less than a thousand denarii given at the right moment. It matters a great deal whether you hand a man a gift or come to his rescue — whether your generosity saves him or sets him up in life. Often the gift is small and its consequences huge. And what a gulf there is between taking something you lacked — something that was offered — and accepting a benefit precisely so you can confer one in return.

But let's not slide back into ground we have already covered. In this weighing of benefits against injuries, the good man will judge with perfect fairness, yes — but he will lean toward the benefit's side; that is the direction he turns more readily. And in matters of this kind, the person involved usually counts for a great deal. "You did me a favor in that business with my slave, but you injured me in my father's case." "You saved my son, but you robbed me of my father." So he will work through every point where comparison is possible, and if the difference is slight, he will pretend not to see it. Even if the difference is large, he will let it pass — provided the concession costs nothing in duty or loyalty, and provided the injury falls on the good man alone. The sum of it is this: the good man will be easygoing in striking the balance; he will let too much be charged against his own account. He will refuse to pay off a benefit by cancelling it against an injury. The side he leans toward, the verdict he wants to reach, is this: to be in debt for the favor, and to make a return for it. Anyone who receives a benefit more gladly than he repays it has it wrong. A man paying off a loan is more lighthearted than a man taking one out; by that same measure, a man unburdening himself of the greatest of all debts — a benefit received — should be more joyful than a man signing for the biggest of all obligations. Ingrates go wrong here too: they pay their creditors principal plus interest, but treat benefits as money they can use interest-free. The debt grows with every postponement, and the longer the payment is put off, the more remains to pay. A man is an ingrate if he repays a favor without interest. So count the interest too, when you compare what you have taken in against what you have paid out. We should try by every means to be as grateful as we can.

For gratitude is a good we do ourselves — unlike justice, which is commonly supposed to concern other people. Gratitude flows back, in large measure, to its source. There is no man who, in helping his neighbor, has not helped himself — and I don't mean because the man you aided will want to aid you, or the man you defended will want to protect you, or because a good example circles back to bless its author the way bad examples recoil on theirs (people get no pity when they suffer wrongs they have taught the world how to commit). I mean that the reward of all the virtues lies in the virtues themselves. They are not practiced for pay: the wage of a good deed is to have done it. I am grateful not so that my neighbor, encouraged by my earlier thanks, will be quicker to help me next time, but simply so that I may do a supremely pleasant and beautiful thing. I feel gratitude not because it profits me, but because it delights me. And to prove it: if I could be grateful only by appearing ungrateful — if I could return a benefit only by an act that looked like an injury — even then I would go calmly ahead toward what honor demands, straight through the disgrace. No one, I think, holds virtue dearer or is more devoted to it than the man who has thrown away his reputation for goodness rather than throw away the approval of his own conscience. So, as I said, your gratitude does more good to you than to your neighbor. He has had a common, everyday experience — getting back what he gave. You have had a great one, the fruit of a soul in its happiest condition: you have felt gratitude. If wickedness makes people miserable and virtue makes them blessed, and if being grateful is a virtue, then what you handed back was merely the customary payment, but what you gained is beyond price — the consciousness of gratitude, which visits only a soul that is godlike and blessed. And the opposite feeling is punished at once, and punished to the limit. No ungrateful man will be unhappy someday. I grant him no grace period. He is unhappy now.

So let us avoid ingratitude — not for other people's sake, but for our own. When we do wrong, only the smallest and lightest part of it splashes onto our neighbor; the worst part — the densest, if I may put it that way — stays home and torments the owner. My master Attalus used to say: "Evil herself drinks the largest portion of her own poison." Snake venom is not like this poison: what the serpent carries for the destruction of others it stores without harm to itself, but this kind ruins its possessor. The ungrateful man racks and torments himself. He hates the gifts he has accepted, because he will have to return them, so he talks their value down — while the injuries done to him he inflates and exaggerates. And what is more wretched than a man who forgets his benefits and clings to his injuries?

Wisdom does the opposite: she lends grace to every benefit, presses it warmly on her own attention, and delights her soul by remembering it again and again. Bad men get one pleasure out of a benefit, and a short-lived pleasure at that: it lasts only while they are receiving it. The wise man draws from it a steady, permanent joy, for his delight is not so much in receiving the gift as in having received it — and that joy never dies; it stays with him always. The wrongs done to him he despises; he forgets them — not by accident, but on purpose. He does not put the worst construction on everything, or go hunting for someone to blame for every mishap; he charges even men's sins to chance. He will not read malice into a word or a look; whatever goes wrong, he softens it by interpreting it generously. He does not remember an injury sooner than a service. As far as he can, he lets his memory settle on the earlier and better deed, and he never changes his attitude toward those who have deserved well of him — except when the bad deeds far outrun the good, and the gap is obvious even to a man trying to shut his eyes to it. And even then he goes only this far: after the injury has tipped the scale, he tries to return to the attitude he held before the benefit. For when the injury merely equals the benefit, some kindly feeling is still left over. Just as a defendant is acquitted when the votes are tied, and just as kindness always bends a doubtful case toward the better reading, so the wise man's mind, when another man's merits merely equal his misdeeds, will stop feeling the obligation — but not stop wanting to feel it. He acts exactly like the man who goes on paying his debts after the court has cancelled them.

But no one can be grateful who has not learned to scorn the things that drive the common herd out of its mind. If you want to make return for a favor, you must be willing to go into exile, to pour out your blood, to accept poverty — even, and this happens often, to let your very innocence be spattered and dragged through shameful slanders. Gratitude does not come cheap. We hold nothing dearer than a benefit while we are chasing it, and nothing cheaper once we have caught it. Do you ask what makes us forget the benefits we have received? Our hunger for the next ones. We think not about what we have obtained but about what we are going to pursue. We are pulled off course by riches, titles, power — everything that is precious in our opinion and worthless at its true price. We do not know how to weigh things. We should take our counsel about them not from their reputation but from their nature. Those glittering things have no grandeur to enchant our minds beyond the fact that we have gotten used to marveling at them. They are not praised because they deserve to be desired; they are desired because they have been praised — and once the error of individuals has created a public error, the public error turns around and creates the errors of individuals.

But just as we take those valuations on faith, let us also take this truth on the faith of the people: nothing is more honorable than a grateful heart. Every city will echo the sentence, every race, even the ones from savage countries. On this point good men and bad will agree. Some praise pleasure, some prefer hard work; some call pain the greatest of evils, some call it no evil at all; some will count riches into the supreme good, others will say their discovery was a disaster for the human race, and that no man is richer than the one for whom Fortune has found nothing left to give. Amid all this squabbling of opinions, every voter, as the saying goes, will shout "aye" to one proposition: that thanks should be returned to those who have deserved well of us. On this question the whole quarrelsome herd agrees — and meanwhile we keep paying back injuries instead of benefits, and the primary reason a man turns ungrateful is that he found it impossible to be grateful enough. Our madness has gone so far that it is now downright dangerous to do a man a great favor: because he thinks it shameful not to repay it, he would prefer his benefactor out of the way entirely. "Keep what you have received. I am not asking for it back; I am not demanding anything. Just let it be safe to have done you a favor." No hatred is deadlier than the hatred born of shame over a benefit betrayed.

Farewell.

Letter 82

Letter 82: On the Natural Fear of Death

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I have stopped worrying about you. "And which of the gods," you ask, "did you get to guarantee that?" One that deceives no one, I assure you: a soul in love with what is upright and good. The better part of you is on safe ground. Fortune can do you injury; what matters more is that I have no fear of you doing injury to yourself. Go on the way you have begun, and settle yourself into this way of living — not luxuriously, but calmly. I would rather be in trouble than in luxury; and take "in trouble" here the way people usually mean it: living a hard, rough, toilsome life. We often hear a man's life praised with a little envy in the voice — "So-and-so lives luxuriously" — and what the words really mean is: "He has gone soft." For the soul goes soft by degrees, weakening until it matches the ease and laziness it lies in. Tell me — isn't it better for a real man to end up hard instead? But then these same pampered fellows are terrified of the very thing they have made their lives resemble. There is a great deal of difference between lying idle and lying buried! "But," you say, "isn't it better to lie idle than to be whirled around in these eddies of business?" Both extremes deserve blame — the strain and the sluggishness alike. I hold that the man stretched out on a perfumed couch is no less dead than the man dragged off by the executioner's hook.

Leisure without study is death — a tomb for a living man. What is the advantage of retirement, in the end? As if the real causes of our anxiety didn't follow us across the sea! What hiding place is there that the fear of death cannot enter? What retreat is so fortified, so far withdrawn, that pain doesn't fill it with dread? Wherever you hide yourself, human ills will raise their racket all around you. Plenty of external things circle us, to deceive us or to crush us; and plenty of internal ones boil and fret even in perfect solitude.

So wrap philosophy around yourself — an impregnable wall. Fortune can batter it with every siege engine she has and never find a way in. The soul that has let go of external things stands on unassailable ground; it defends itself in its own fortress; every weapon hurled at it falls short. Fortune does not have the long reach we give her credit for: she can seize no one who is not clinging to her. So let us spring back from her as far as we can — and the only thing that makes that possible is knowledge of ourselves and of nature. The soul should know where it is going and where it came from, what is good for it and what is evil, what it seeks and what it avoids, and what that Reason is which sorts the desirable from the undesirable — the Reason that tames the madness of our desires and calms the violence of our fears.

Some men flatter themselves that they have beaten these evils on their own, no philosophy required; then some accident catches them off guard, and the confession of error is wrung out of them — too late. The boastful words die on their lips the moment the torturer orders them to stretch out their hands, the moment death draws closer. You could say to such a man: "It was easy for you to challenge evils that were nowhere in sight. But here comes pain — the pain you declared you could endure. Here comes death, the thing you hurled so many brave boasts against. The whip cracks, the sword flashes:

  Now, Aeneas, you will need your courage,
  now you will need a sturdy heart!"

That sturdy heart comes from constant study — provided you practice with your soul and not just your tongue, and provided you prepare yourself to meet death. And for that preparation, expect no encouragement, no cheer, from the men who try to argue you into believing, by hair-splitting logic, that death is no evil. Because I do take pleasure, my excellent Lucilius, in poking fun at the absurdities of the Greeks — absurdities I am continually surprised to find I haven't shaken off myself. Our own master Zeno uses a syllogism like this: "No evil is glorious; but death is glorious; therefore death is no evil." There's a cure, Zeno! Consider me freed of fear — after that, I won't hesitate to stretch my neck out on the block. You're quibbling a man into scorning death? Couldn't you find sterner words, instead of making a dying man laugh? And honestly, Lucilius, I couldn't easily tell you which of the two was the bigger fool: the man who thought this syllogism would quench the fear of death, or the man who took the trouble to refute it — as if any of it had anything to do with the matter. For the refuter set up a counter-syllogism of his own, built on our classing death as "indifferent" — one of the things the Greeks call adiaphora. "Nothing indifferent," he says, "can be glorious; death is glorious; therefore death is not indifferent." You can see the trick sliding through that one: mere death is not, in fact, glorious; a brave death is glorious. And when you say "nothing indifferent is glorious," I'll grant it — with this addition: nothing is glorious except in its dealings with indifferent things. By "indifferent" I mean neither good nor evil: sickness, pain, poverty, exile, death. None of these is glorious in itself; but nothing can be glorious without them. For it is never poverty we praise — it is the man poverty cannot humble or bend. It is never exile we praise — it is the man who walks into exile in the same spirit in which he would have sent another man there. We do not praise pain; we praise the man pain never coerced. No one praises death; we praise the man whose soul death carried off before it could shake it. All these things are, in themselves, neither honorable nor glorious; but let virtue visit and touch any one of them, and virtue makes it honorable and glorious. They simply lie in the middle, and the deciding question is only this: does wickedness lay hold of them, or does virtue? The death that in Cato's case is glorious becomes, in the case of Brutus, instantly base and disgraceful. This is the Brutus who, condemned to death, kept angling for a postponement — who stepped aside a moment to relieve himself, and then, summoned to die and ordered to bare his throat, cried out: "I will bare my throat, if only I may live!" What madness, to run away when there is no turning back! "I will bare my throat, if only I may live!" He came very close to adding: "even under Antony!" There was a man who deserved to be sentenced to life!

But, as I was saying: you can see that death in itself is neither an evil nor a good. Cato used it most honorably; Brutus most shamefully. Add virtue to a thing and it takes on a glory it never had before. We call a room sunny even though the same room is pitch-black at night. The day fills it with light; the night steals the light away. So it is with the things we call indifferent — the "middle" things: riches, strength, beauty, titles, kingship, and their opposites — death, exile, sickness, pain, and all the other evils that frighten us to one degree or another. It is the wickedness or the virtue that hands out the names "good" and "evil." An object is not hot or cold by its own essence: throw it into the furnace and it heats; drop it into water and it chills. Death is honorable when it is joined to something honorable — I mean virtue, and a soul that despises the worst that can happen.

And there are vast distinctions among these "middle" things. Death is not as indifferent as, say, the question of whether your hair is trimmed evenly or unevenly. Death belongs to the class of things that are not evils, but still wear the look of evil. For we are born with love of self, with a desire to exist and preserve ourselves, and with a horror of dissolution — death seems to strip us of so many goods, to pull us away from the abundance we have grown used to. And one more thing estranges us from death: the present we already know, but the future we are crossing into is unknown, and we shrink from the unknown. It is natural, too, to fear the world of shades, where death is supposed to lead. So although death is an indifferent, it is not the kind of thing you can shrug off easily. The soul must be hardened by long practice before it can bear the sight of death and the approach of it.

Death deserves far more scorn than it usually gets — because we believe too many of the stories about it. Plenty of great minds have worked hard to blacken its reputation: they have painted the prison of the world below, the land drowned in everlasting night, where

  the huge warden of Hell,
  sprawled in his blood-stained cave on half-gnawed bones,
  keeps the bloodless ghosts in terror
  with a bark that never ends.

Even if you win that argument — even if you prove these are only stories, and the dead have nothing left to fear — a second fear comes creeping in. For the fear of going to the underworld is matched, exactly, by the fear of going nowhere.

With all these notions dinned into our ears by long tradition, how can the brave endurance of death be anything but glorious — anything but one of the greatest achievements of the human mind? The mind will never climb to virtue while it believes death is an evil; it will climb once it holds death to be a matter of indifference. Nature does not build men to march with a great heart toward what they believe is evil: a man will shuffle toward it, dragging his feet. And nothing glorious ever comes of reluctance and cowardice. Virtue does nothing under compulsion. Besides, no deed is honorable unless the whole heart is in it — unless the doer has given himself to it completely, with no part of him in revolt. But when a man goes to face an evil because he fears something worse, or because he hopes for goods that matter enough to make the one evil worth swallowing, then his judgment is pulled in two directions. On one side is the motive urging him to carry out his plan; on the other, the motive holding him back, making him flee from what looks suspicious or dangerous. He is torn both ways — and the moment that happens, the glory of the act is gone. For virtue carries out its plans only when the spirit is in harmony with itself. There is no fear in anything virtue does.

  Do not yield to evils. Meet them head-on, bolder still,
  wherever your fortune allows you a way.

You cannot meet them "bolder still" if you are convinced they are real evils. Dig that idea out of your soul by the roots; otherwise your fear will hang there, undecided, and choke the impulse to act. You will be shoved toward the thing you ought to charge at like a soldier.

Those of our school, it is true, would have you think that Zeno's syllogism is correct, and that the second one — the counter — is deceptive and false. For my part, I refuse to reduce such questions to the rules of logic, to the quibbles of an utterly worn-out system. Away with the whole business, I say — the kind of question that makes a man feel cornered the moment it is put to him, that forces him to concede a premise and then to say one thing in his answer while believing another. When truth is at stake, we must deal more frankly; when fear is to be fought, more bravely. These questions the dialecticians tangle up in subtleties, I would rather untangle and weigh in plain reason — aiming to win conviction, not to trap the judgment.

A general is about to lead an army into action — men prepared to die for their wives and children. How does he fire them for battle? I give you the Fabii, one clan taking a whole state's war onto its shoulders. I point you to the Spartans posted in the very pass of Thermopylae. They have no hope of victory, no hope of coming home. The ground they stand on is to be their tomb. In what language do you urge them to make a barricade of their bodies, to take the ruin of their whole people onto themselves, to retreat from life sooner than from their post? Will you say: "What is evil is not glorious; but death is glorious; therefore death is not an evil"? What a stirring speech! After words like those, who could hesitate to throw himself onto the massed spears of the enemy and die where he stood? But take Leonidas — how bravely he spoke to his men! "Fellow soldiers," he said, "eat your breakfast as men who will dine in the underworld!" And the food did not turn to lumps in their mouths, or stick in their throats, or slip from their fingers: they accepted the invitation cheerfully — to breakfast and to dinner both. And think of the famous Roman general who sent his soldiers to seize a position straight through an enormous enemy army, and addressed them so: "Men, you must go to that place. There is no must about your coming back."

You see how straightforward virtue is, how commanding. But what man on earth will your logical trickery make braver or more upright? It breaks the spirit instead — and there is no worse time to cramp the spirit and pin it to petty, thorny puzzles than when some great work is being planned. It is not the Three Hundred who need to be relieved of the fear of death — it is all mankind. And how will you prove to all of them that death is no evil? How will you overcome the notions of a whole lifetime, dyed into us from infancy? What rescue will you find for human helplessness? What will you say that sends men burning with zeal into the middle of danger? What speech will talk the whole race out of this universal fear — what feat of cleverness will overturn the settled conviction of the human race standing squarely against you? Are you going to build me catchwords, and string together petty little syllogisms? It takes great weapons to bring down great monsters. You remember the monstrous serpent in Africa — more terrifying to the Roman legions than the war itself — which arrows and slings attacked in vain. Even "Pythius," their great siege engine, could not wound it: its sheer size, and a hide as tough as its bulk, made spears and every weapon thrown by a human hand glance off. It was finally crushed by rocks the size of millstones. And you — is that the kind of toy weapon you are hurling at death? Will you stop a lion's charge with an awl? Your arguments are sharp, I grant you. So is a stalk of grain — nothing is sharper. And some arguments are made useless and unavailing by their very subtlety.

Farewell.

Letters 83–84

Letter 83: On Drunkenness

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You ask me for an account of each of my days — the whole day, start to finish. You must think well of me if you assume there's nothing in them to hide. And in fact that is how we ought to live: as if we lived in plain sight of everyone. That is how we ought to think, too: as if someone could look into our inmost hearts — because someone can. What good does it do to hide something from people? Nothing is shut away from the sight of God. He is the witness of our souls; he stands in the very middle of our thoughts — and I say "stands in" as one who may, at any moment, walk out. So I'll do as you ask, and gladly report by letter what I'm doing and in what order. I'm going to keep watch on myself continually and — a most useful habit — review each day when it's over. This is exactly what makes us bad: none of us looks back over his own life. We think only about what we're going to do next. And yet our plans for the future always hang on the past.

Today has come to me in one piece; no one has stolen the smallest scrap of it. The whole of it has been divided between rest and reading. A small slice went to physical exercise, and on that score I can thank old age: my exercise costs very little effort. The moment I move, I'm tired — and tiredness is the whole aim and end of exercise, no matter how strong you are. You want to know who my training partners are? One is enough for me: the slave Pharius, a pleasant fellow, as you know — though I'll be trading him for another. At my time of life I need someone of even tenderer years. Pharius, for his part, insists that he and I are at the same stage of life, since we're both losing our teeth. Even so, I can barely match his pace when he runs, and before long I won't be able to match it at all — see what daily exercise does for you! A wide gap opens fast between two people traveling in opposite directions. He is climbing at the very moment I'm coming down, and you know how much quicker coming down is. No — I put that wrong. My life isn't coming down anymore; it's dropping straight off. Still, you ask, how did today's race come out? A dead heat — something that rarely happens in a footrace.

After tiring myself out that way (I can't call it exercise), I took a cold bath — which, at my house, means just short of hot. I, the old cold-water fanatic, who used to salute the new year with a plunge into the canal, who used to open every first of January with a dive into the Aqua Virgo as naturally as I'd sit down to read or write or draft a speech — I have switched my allegiance, first to the Tiber, and then to my own tank, which is warmed by nothing but the sun, and only on days when I'm at my strongest and my body has no complaints. There's not much bathing left in me. After the bath, some stale bread, and breakfast without a table — no need to wash your hands after a meal like that. Then a very short nap. You know my habit: I take the barest scrap of sleep — just unharnessing, so to speak. It's enough for me to simply stop being awake. Sometimes I know I've slept; other times I merely suspect it.

Listen — there's the roar of the Races all around me now! A sudden, unanimous cheer slams against my ears. But it doesn't shake my thoughts or even break their thread. I can put up with an uproar with complete patience. All those voices blended into a single note sound to me like waves breaking, or wind lashing the treetops, or any other noise that carries no meaning.

What, then, have I been giving my attention to? I'll tell you. A thought has been stuck in my head since yesterday: what did the shrewdest men in the world mean when they offered flimsy, tangled little proofs for questions of the greatest importance — proofs that may be true, and yet look exactly like tricks? Zeno, that greatest of men, the revered founder of our brave and holy school of philosophy, wants to talk us out of drunkenness. So listen to his argument that the good man won't get drunk: "No one entrusts a secret to a drunken man; but people entrust secrets to a good man; therefore, the good man will not get drunk." Now watch how ridiculous Zeno looks when we set a matching syllogism beside his. There are many to choose from, but one will do: "No one entrusts a secret to a man who's asleep; but people entrust secrets to a good man; therefore, the good man does not sleep."

Posidonius defends our master Zeno in the only way possible — and even that way, I hold, won't hold up. Posidonius says the word "drunken" is used in two senses: of a man who is loaded with wine and has lost control of himself, and of a man who habitually gets drunk — a slave to the habit. Zeno, he says, meant the second: the man in the habit of getting drunk, not the man who is drunk right now; and nobody would trust that man with a secret, because he might blurt it out over his cups. That's a dodge. The syllogism is about the man who is actually drunk, not the man who is going to get drunk. And you'll surely grant there's a big difference between a man who's drunk and a drunkard. The man who's drunk right now may be drunk for the first time in his life, with no habit at all; and the drunkard is often stone sober. So I take the word in its ordinary meaning — especially since the syllogism comes from a man who makes a business of precision, who weighs every word he uses. Besides, if that's what Zeno meant, and what he wanted it to mean to us, then he was exploiting an ambiguous word to smuggle in a fallacy — and no one should do that when the object is truth.

But fine — let's grant he meant what Posidonius says. Even then the conclusion is false: that secrets are not entrusted to a habitual drinker. Think how many soldiers — hardly models of sobriety — have been trusted by a general or a captain or a centurion with messages that had to stay secret! Take the famous plot to kill Gaius Caesar — I mean the Caesar who defeated Pompey and got control of the state. Tillius Cimber was trusted with it every bit as much as Gaius Cassius. Cassius drank water his whole life; Cimber was a sot and a brawler besides. Cimber even joked about it himself: "Me, carry a master? I can't even carry my liquor!" So let each of us call to mind the men we know who can't be trusted with wine but can be trusted with a secret. One case comes to my mind that I'll pass along, so it doesn't slip into oblivion — life should be stocked with vivid examples, and we shouldn't always be reaching back into the dim past.

Lucius Piso, the man in charge of keeping order in Rome, was drunk from the very moment of his appointment. He spent the better part of every night at banquets and slept until noon — that was his morning. Yet he applied himself most diligently to his official duties, which included the safekeeping of the city. Even the sainted Augustus trusted him with secret orders when he gave him command of Thrace — which Piso conquered. Tiberius trusted him too, when he went off on his Campanian holiday, leaving behind in Rome plenty of unfinished business that stirred up both suspicion and hatred. And I suspect it was because Piso's drunkenness had worked out so well for the emperor that he later named Cossus city prefect — a man of weight and balance, but so soaked, so pickled in drink, that once, arriving at a Senate meeting straight from a banquet, he sank into a sleep nobody could rouse him from and had to be carried home. This is the man to whom Tiberius sent many orders written in his own hand — orders he decided he couldn't trust even to the staff of his own household. Cossus never let a single secret slip, personal or public.

So let's retire all the set speeches like this one: "The man in the grip of drink is not master of his own soul. As new wine bursts the very vats, and the strength of the fermentation heaves the dregs up from the bottom, so when the wine froths inside a man, whatever lies hidden in him is carried up and brought into the open. A man overloaded with wine can't keep down his food — and he can't keep down a secret either. He pours out his own and other people's alike." That does commonly happen — but so does this: we take counsel on serious matters with men we know to be steady drinkers. So the claim laid down in defense of Zeno's syllogism — that a secret is never entrusted to the habitual drinker — is false.

How much better to indict drunkenness openly and expose its vices! Even the middling good man avoids them — never mind the perfect sage, who is satisfied once his thirst is quenched, and who, even if a friend's celebration occasionally carries him a little far, always stops short of drunk. Whether too much wine can scramble even the sage's mind and push him into a fool's behavior, we can look into later. Meanwhile, if you want to prove that a good man shouldn't get drunk, why work it out by logic? Say how disgraceful it is to pour down more than you can carry, without knowing the capacity of your own stomach. Say how many things drunk men do that make them blush when they're sober. Say plainly that drunkenness is nothing but voluntary madness. Stretch the drunkard's condition out over several days — would you have any doubt then that he's insane? As it is, the madness is no smaller; it just doesn't last as long. Think of Alexander of Macedon, who stabbed Clitus, his dearest and most loyal friend, at a banquet; when Alexander understood what he had done, he wanted to die — and he certainly should have.

Drunkenness ignites every vice and puts it on display, and it strips away the shame that veils our worst undertakings. For more men hold back from what's forbidden because they're ashamed of sinning than because their desires are good. When the wine grows too strong and takes command of the mind, every evil that was lurking comes out of its hiding place. Drunkenness doesn't create vice; it merely brings it into view. That's when the lustful man doesn't even wait for the privacy of a bedroom, but grants his appetites everything on the spot; that's when the shameless man announces and advertises his disease; that's when the surly man restrains neither his tongue nor his fist. The arrogant man grows more arrogant, the ruthless man more cruel, the slanderer more spiteful. Every vice is off its leash and out in front. Add to this that we forget who we are; the words come halting and slurred; the eyes swim; the step falters; the head spins; the very ceiling starts to wheel around as if a cyclone had hold of the whole house; and the stomach is in torment as the wine turns to gas and bloats our very insides. All of that can be endured, at least, while the man still has his strength. But what is he to do when sleep has sapped it, and what was drunkenness has become indigestion?

Think what calamities drunkenness has brought down on whole nations! This is the evil that has betrayed the most spirited and warlike peoples to their enemies; that has opened breaches in walls defended through years of stubborn fighting; that has forced under a foreign yoke peoples who were utterly unyielding and refused all mastery; that has conquered with the wine-cup men who were unconquerable in the field. Alexander — I just mentioned him — came safely through all his marches, all his battles, all his winter campaigns fought through every disadvantage of season and terrain, all the rivers flowing down from unknown sources, all the seas. It was overdrinking that laid him low — that, and the notorious death-dealing "bowl of Hercules."

Where's the glory in holding a lot of liquor? When you've won the prize; when the other guests, sprawled out snoring or vomiting, have declined your challenge to yet another round; when you're the last man standing at the party; when you've vanquished everyone with your magnificent show of prowess and no one has a capacity to match yours — you have been vanquished by the cask. Mark Antony was a great man, a man of distinguished ability; and what ruined him, what drove him into foreign habits and un-Roman vices, if not drunkenness — and, no less potent than wine, his love of Cleopatra? Drink made him an enemy of the state; drink left him no match for his enemies; drink made him cruel — for he would sit at dinner while the heads of the leading men of Rome were carried in; amid the most elaborate feasts and royal luxury he would study the faces and hands of the men he had proscribed; and heavy with wine, he still thirsted for blood. That he was getting drunk while he did such things was intolerable; that he did such things while actually drunk — how much worse! Cruelty usually follows hard drinking, because a man's soundness of mind gets corrupted and turns savage. Just as a lingering illness makes men whiny and irritable, wild at the least crossing of their wishes, so continual bouts of drunkenness brutalize the soul. When men are often beside themselves, the habit of madness settles in, and the vices the liquor bred keep their strength even when the liquor is gone.

So state the reasons why the wise man ought not to get drunk. Show the ugliness of the thing, and the evils that haunt it, by facts and not by words. Do the easiest thing of all: demonstrate that what people call pleasures turn into punishments the moment they pass their proper limits. Because if you set out to prove that the wise man can soak himself in any amount of wine and still hold his course, still steady even in his cups, then you may as well go on to conclude, syllogism by syllogism, that he won't die if he swallows poison, won't sleep if he takes a sleeping draught, and won't vomit up what's clogging his stomach when you dose him with hellebore. But when a man's feet are tottering and his tongue is tripping, what reason do you have to call him half sober rather than half drunk?

Farewell.

Letter 84: On Gathering Ideas

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

These journeys you mention — the kind that shake the laziness out of my system — I count as good both for my health and for my studies. Why they help my health, you can see: since my passion for books makes me lazy and careless about my body, traveling lets me exercise by proxy. Why they help my studies, I'll show you: I haven't cut back on my reading in the slightest. And reading, I hold, is indispensable — first, so that I'm not satisfied with myself alone; and second, so that once I've learned what others have found out, I can judge their discoveries and think about the discoveries still waiting to be made. Reading feeds the mind, and refreshes it when it's worn out with study — though the refreshment isn't had without study either. We shouldn't confine ourselves to writing only, or to reading only: endless writing will darken and drain your strength; endless reading will make it soft and watery. Better to turn from one to the other, blending them, so that the pen works the gatherings of your reading into something solid.

We ought to imitate the bees, as people say: they wander about and raid the flowers best suited for making honey, then arrange what they've brought home and sort it through the combs, and — as our Virgil puts it —

  pack close the flowing honey,
  and swell their cells with nectar sweet.

It isn't certain whether the juice they draw from the flowers is honey the moment they gather it, or whether they change what they've collected into that delicious thing by blending something with it — some property of their own breath. Some authorities believe bees have no art of making honey, only of gathering it; they say that in India honey has been found on the leaves of certain reeds, produced by a dew peculiar to that climate, or by the juice of the reed itself, which has an unusual sweetness and richness. Our own grasses, they say, carry the same quality, fainter and less obvious, and a creature born for exactly this work hunts it out and collects it. Others hold that what the bees have culled from the tenderest of the blooming, flowering plants is transformed into this new substance by being packed away and carefully stored, helped along by something like fermentation, which fuses the separate elements into one.

But I mustn't get pulled off into another subject. The point is: we too ought to copy these bees. We should sort out whatever we've gathered from our varied reading — such things keep better when kept separate — and then, applying the supervising care our nature has given us (our own talent, in plain terms), blend all those separate flavors into one delicious compound, so that even though you can see where it came from, it is clearly a different thing from its source. This is what we watch nature do in our own bodies with no effort on our part: the food we eat is a burden only as long as it keeps its original form and sits whole in the stomach; only when it has been changed from what it was does it pass into muscle and blood. Let's do the same with the food that feeds our higher nature: whatever we take in, let's not allow it to sit inside us unchanged and foreign — then it would never be part of us. It has to be digested. Otherwise it goes into the memory and not into the reasoning power. Let's welcome these ideas wholeheartedly and make them our own, so that out of many things one thing is formed — the way one number is formed out of many smaller sums when a single reckoning gathers up the different amounts. This is what our mind should do: let the mind hide all the sources; show only the result. And even if a resemblance does show through — to some writer your admiration has stamped deep into you — I want you to resemble him the way a child resembles his father, not the way a portrait resembles its subject. A portrait is a dead thing.

"What," you say, "won't people be able to tell whose style you're imitating, whose way of arguing, whose sharp sayings?" Sometimes, I think, they can't tell at all — not if the copy is a true one; for a true copy stamps its own form on everything it takes from its model, so that it all fuses into a unity. Haven't you noticed how many voices there are in a chorus? Yet out of them all comes one voice. One takes the high part, another the low, another the middle; there are women's voices in among the men's, and flutes woven between them. The individual voices are hidden; what you hear belongs to all of them together. (I mean the chorus the old philosophers knew. At our modern spectacles there are more singers on stage than there used to be spectators in the old theaters: the aisles are packed with rows of them, brass rings the auditorium, the stage throbs with flutes and instruments of every kind — and out of all those clashing sounds comes a harmony.)

That's the kind of mind I want mine to be: stocked with many skills, many precepts, and patterns of conduct drawn from many ages of history — but all of it blended into harmony, into one. "How is that done?" you ask. By constant attention — by doing nothing without the approval of reason. And if you're willing to hear her voice, reason will tell you: "Abandon the pursuits that have kept you running here and there. Abandon riches; they are either a danger or a burden to whoever holds them. Abandon the pleasures of body and mind; they only soften and weaken you. Abandon the chase for office; it is a swollen, idle, empty thing with no finish line, as anxious that no one gets ahead of it as that no one comes up behind it. It is sick with envy — a double envy, in fact; and you see how wretched a man is when he is envied and envies too."

Do you see those mansions of the great, those doorways loud with the shoving of morning callers? They have plenty of insults for you on your way in the door, and more once you're inside. Walk past the staircases of the rich and the porches made hazardous by the crush — there you'd be standing not just on the edge of a cliff but on slippery ground. Set your course instead toward wisdom; seek her ways, which are ways of surpassing peace and plenty.
