Whatever looks prominent in human affairs — however small it really is, and prominent only next to what's lowest — is still reached by a hard, grinding climb. It is a rough road that leads to the heights of greatness. But if you choose to scale this peak, which stands far above Fortune's whole range, you will look down from there on everything men count loftiest — and yet you can reach the top over level ground.

Farewell.

Letter 85

Letter 85: On Some Vain Syllogisms

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I had meant to spare you. I was leaving out the knotty problems that still remained, satisfied to give you just a taste of our school's arguments — the ones designed to prove that virtue, all by itself, is enough to complete the happy life. But now you order me to bring in the whole arsenal: our syllogisms, and the ones rival schools have devised to belittle us. If I'm willing to do that, the result won't be a letter; it will be a book. And I say it again, for the record: I take no pleasure in this kind of quibbling. I'm ashamed to march into the arena and do battle on behalf of gods and men armed with nothing but an awl.

Still, you asked. Here is the chain: "Whoever has prudence also has self-restraint; whoever has self-restraint is steadfast; whoever is steadfast is unperturbed; whoever is unperturbed is free from sadness; whoever is free from sadness is happy. Therefore the prudent man is happy, and prudence is enough for the happy life."

Certain Peripatetics — Aristotle's school — answer this by redefining the terms. "Unperturbed," they say, means a man who is rarely perturbed, and only moderately — not a man who is never perturbed at all. Likewise a man is called "free from sadness" if he isn't prone to sadness, if he falls into that unfortunate state seldom and not too deeply. Human nature being what it is, they argue, no one's spirit can be exempt from sadness; the wise man isn't overwhelmed by grief, but he is touched by it — and so on, all according to the teachings of their school. They don't abolish the passions this way; they only moderate them. But what a petty superiority we're granting the wise man, if he's merely braver than the biggest coward, happier than the most dejected, more self-controlled than the most out-of-control, and greater than the lowest of the low! Would Ladas, the legendary runner, brag about his speed by comparing himself with the lame and the feeble?

  She could skim over the tips of the standing grain
  and never bruise the tender ears as she ran,
  or dart across open sea, poised above the swell,
  and never wet her flying feet in the water.

If the wise man, on your definition, has any passions at all, his reason will be no match for them; it will be swept along as if on a rushing current — especially if you assign him not one passion to wrestle with, but all of them. And a crowd of passions, even moderate ones, can do more to him than the violence of one powerful passion. He craves money, but moderately. He has ambition, but it isn't fully roused yet. He has a temper, but it can be talked down. He's fickle, but not wildly or restlessly so. He has lust, but not the violent kind. We'd have an easier time with a man who had one full-blown vice than with a man who has all the vices, none of them in extreme form.

Besides, the size of a passion makes no difference. Whatever its size, it doesn't know how to obey, and it doesn't take advice. No animal obeys reason — not the wild ones, not the tame and gentle ones either; nature made them deaf to counsel. In the same way the passions don't follow and don't listen, however slight they are. Tigers and lions never put off their wildness. They set it aside sometimes, and then, just when you're least prepared, that softened ferocity flares back into savagery. Vices are never genuinely tamed.

Again: if reason prevails, the passions never even get started. But if they get moving against reason's will, they'll keep moving against reason's will. It's easier to stop them at the start than to steer them once they've gathered force. So this halfway position is misleading and useless. Treat it exactly the way you'd treat the claim that we ought to be "moderately" insane, or "moderately" sick. Virtue alone possesses moderation; the evils that afflict the mind don't come in moderate sizes. You can remove them more easily than you can manage them. Can anyone doubt that the vices of the human mind, once they've become chronic and hardened — at that point we call them "diseases" — are beyond control? Greed, cruelty, brutality: uncontrollable. Then the passions are beyond control too, because the passions are the road we take to reach the vices.

Again: grant any allowance to sadness, fear, desire, and the rest of the wrong impulses, and they'll slip out of our jurisdiction entirely. Why? Because the triggers that rouse them lie outside our power. So they'll grow in proportion as their causes are greater or smaller. Fear will swell as the terrifying thing looks bigger or comes closer; desire will sharpen as the hope of a bigger prize calls it into action. If the existence of the passions isn't in our control, neither is their strength; once you let them get started, they'll grow right along with their causes, and they'll end up as big as they're going to grow. Besides, however small these evils are, they get bigger. Whatever is harmful never keeps within bounds. However trifling a disease is at the beginning, it creeps forward — and sometimes the slightest worsening of an illness is what finishes off a weakened body.

But what folly it is, when the beginnings of a thing lie outside our control, to believe that its endings lie within it! How can I have the power to shut something down when I didn't have the power to keep it out? It's easier to bar the door than to subdue the intruder once you've let him in.

Some have drawn this distinction: "The self-controlled and wise man is at peace in the settled attitude and habit of his mind, but not in what actually befalls him. As far as his habit of mind goes, he isn't perturbed or saddened or afraid; but plenty of outside causes strike him and bring disturbance with them." What they mean is this: "He's not a man of angry temperament, but he still sometimes gives way to anger," and "He's not a fearful man, but he still sometimes feels fear." He's free of the fault, in other words, but not free of the feeling. But let fear in once, and through repeated use it turns into a vice; let anger into the mind, and it corrupts what was formerly the habit of a mind without anger. Besides, if the wise man doesn't despise everything that comes at him from outside — if he ever fears anything at all — then when the moment arrives to march bravely against the spear or the flames for his country, his laws, and his liberty, he'll go out reluctantly, with his spirit dragging. And that kind of inner contradiction doesn't suit the wise man.

Then there's another point: we should be careful not to tangle together two principles that ought to be tested separately. It's one conclusion, reached on its own, that the honorable is the only good; and a separate conclusion that virtue is sufficient for the happy life. If the honorable is the only good, everyone agrees that virtue is enough for living happily. But it doesn't run backward: if virtue alone makes men happy, no one will concede on that basis that the honorable is the only good. Xenocrates and Speusippus, Plato's successors, hold that a man can become happy by virtue alone without holding that the honorable is the only good. Epicurus, too, judges that the man who has virtue is happy — but that virtue by itself isn't sufficient for the happy life, because what makes him happy is the pleasure that results from virtue, not the virtue itself. A useless distinction. For the same philosopher declares that virtue never exists without pleasure; so if virtue is always joined to pleasure, always inseparable from it, then virtue is sufficient all by itself. It keeps pleasure in its company; even alone, it is never without it. And it's absurd to say that a man will be happy by virtue alone, and yet not completely happy. I can't discover how that would even work. The happy life contains within itself a perfect good, a good that can't be surpassed; and if a man has that good, his life is completely happy.

Now, if the life of the gods contains nothing greater or better, and the happy life is divine, then there is no further height a man can be raised to. Moreover, if the happy life lacks nothing, then every happy life is perfect: it is happy and supremely happy at the same time. Do you have any doubt that the happy life is the Supreme Good? Then if it possesses the Supreme Good, it is supremely happy. Just as the Supreme Good doesn't admit of increase — what could stand above the supreme? — so the happy life can't be increased either, since it doesn't exist without the Supreme Good. Bring in one man who is "happier" than another, and you'll soon bring in one who is "much happier"; you'll end up slicing the Supreme Good into countless gradations — when, as I understand it, the Supreme Good is precisely the good that has no degree above it. If one man is less happy than another, it follows that he'd rather have the happier man's life than his own. But the happy man prefers no one's life to his own. Either alternative is unbelievable: that there should be something left for a happy man to wish for beyond what is, or that he should fail to prefer what's better than what he already has. Certainly the more prudent he is, the more he'll strive for the best and want to reach it by every possible means. But how can a man be happy who is still able — no, still bound — to crave something more?

I'll tell you where this error comes from: people don't understand that the happy life is a single whole. It's the quality of the life, not its size, that sets it on the highest plane. That's why there is complete equality between the long life and the short one, between the life spread wide and the life kept narrow, between the life whose influence is felt in many places and many directions and the life confined to a single pursuit. Whoever measures life by number, by size, or by parts strips it of what makes it distinctive. And in a happy life, what is the distinctive thing? Its fullness. Satiety, I take it, is the limit of our eating and drinking. A eats more and B eats less — what difference does it make? Each is now full. A drinks more and B drinks less — what difference? Neither is thirsty anymore. A lives many years and B lives few: no matter, so long as A's many years hold as much happiness as B's few. The man you insist is "less happy" isn't happy at all; the word doesn't come in smaller sizes.

"Whoever is brave is fearless; whoever is fearless is free from sadness; whoever is free from sadness is happy." This syllogism is our own school's work. The attempted refutation goes like this: we Stoics are treating as settled a premise that is false and hotly disputed — namely, that the brave man is fearless. "What!" they say. "The brave man won't fear the evils hanging over his head? That's the condition of a madman, a lunatic — not a brave man. The brave man feels fear, certainly, only very slightly; he isn't absolutely free of it." But people who say this are doubling back to their old move: treating smaller vices as the equivalent of virtues. The man who feels fear rarely and slightly isn't free of the evil; he just suffers a milder case of it. "Not so," comes the reply. "I say a man is mad if he doesn't fear the evils hanging over his head." What you say is perfectly true — if the things threatening him really are evils. But if he knows they aren't evils, if he believes the only evil is disgrace, then he'll be bound to face dangers without anxiety and to despise the things other men can't help fearing. Or, if not fearing evils is the mark of a fool and a madman, then it follows that the wiser a man is, the more afraid he ought to be!

"So it's Stoic doctrine, then, that the brave man will expose himself to dangers." Not at all. He won't fear them — but he will avoid them. Caution suits him; fear does not. "What then? Won't he fear death, prison, burning, and all the other missiles Fortune throws?" Not at all, because he knows they aren't evils; they only look like evils. He counts all these things among the bogeymen of human existence. Paint him the whole picture: slavery, the lash, chains, want, limbs mangled by disease or by torture — add whatever else you like. He'll file every one of them under panics of a deranged mind. These things are fearful only to the fearful. Or do you call that an evil which, some day, we may be driven to choose freely, of our own accord?

Then what is an evil, you ask? Giving in to the things that are called evils — surrendering your liberty into their hands, when you ought to suffer anything to preserve that liberty. Liberty is lost unless we despise the things that put the yoke on our necks. If people knew what bravery really was, they'd have no doubts about how a brave man should act. Bravery isn't thoughtless recklessness, or a love of danger, or an appetite for frightening situations. It is the knowledge that lets us tell what is an evil from what is not. Bravery takes the most attentive care of itself — and, at the same time, endures with the greatest patience everything that wears the false mask of evil. "What then?" comes the question. "If the sword is brandished over your brave man's neck, if he's run through in one place and then another, if he watches his own entrails spill into his lap, if the torture is paused and then renewed so he'll feel it more keenly, if the blood runs fresh from wounds that had only just stopped bleeding — is he not afraid? Will you tell me he hasn't felt pain, either?" He has felt pain; no human virtue can strip away feeling. But he has no fear. Unconquered, he looks down on his sufferings from a great height. Do you ask what spirit is in him at such a moment? The spirit of a man comforting a sick friend.

"What is evil does harm; what does harm makes a man worse; but pain and poverty don't make a man worse; therefore they are not evils." "Your premise is wrong," says the objector, "because what harms a man doesn't necessarily make him worse. The storm and the squall do harm to the pilot, but they don't make him a worse pilot for all that." Some of our own Stoics answer this as follows: "The pilot is made a worse pilot by storms and squalls, in the sense that he can't carry out his intention and hold his course. In his art he becomes no worse a pilot; in his work, he does become worse." To which the Peripatetics retort: "Then poverty will make even the wise man worse, and so will pain, and so will everything of that sort. Those things won't strip him of his virtue, but they will hinder virtue's work."

That would be a fair point, except that the pilot and the wise man are two different kinds of person. The wise man's aim in conducting his life is not to accomplish whatever he attempts at any cost, but to do everything rightly. The pilot's aim is to bring his ship into port at any cost. The arts are servants: they must deliver what they promise. Wisdom is mistress and ruler. The arts render a slave's service to life; wisdom gives the orders.

For my own part, I'd give a different answer altogether: the pilot's art is never made worse by the storm — and neither is his practice of it. The pilot never promised you a prosperous voyage; he promised a competent performance of his job — that is, expert knowledge of steering a ship. And the harder Fortune presses him, the more that expert knowledge shows. The man who can say, "Neptune, you will never sink this ship except on an even keel," has met the full requirement of his art. The storm doesn't hinder the pilot's work — only his success. "What," you say, "isn't a pilot harmed by whatever keeps him from making port, wrecks all his efforts, drags him out to sea, holds his ship pinned, or strips her masts?" No — it harms him as a voyager, not as a pilot; as a pilot, he's untouched. So far is the storm from hindering the pilot's art that it actually puts the art on display: anyone, as the proverb says, is a pilot on a calm sea. These disasters obstruct the voyage, not the steersman as steersman. A pilot plays a double role. One he shares with everyone on board, because he is a passenger too. The other is his alone, because he is the pilot. The storm harms him as a passenger, not as a pilot.

And there's more. The pilot's art is a good that belongs to other people: it concerns his passengers, the way a doctor's art concerns his patients. But the wise man's good is a common good — it belongs both to the people he lives among and to himself. So perhaps the pilot really is harmed, since the services he promised to others are blocked by the storm. But the wise man is not harmed by poverty, or by pain, or by any of life's other storms. Not all of his functions are shut down — only the ones directed at other people. He himself is always in action, and he is greatest in performance at the very moment Fortune has blocked his road, because that is when he's doing the business of wisdom itself — and wisdom, as I've already said, is both a good for others and his own good too. Nor is he barred from helping others even when hard circumstances are pressing him down. His poverty prevents him from showing how the state should be managed; but he teaches, all the same, how poverty should be managed. His work goes on through the whole of his life.

So no stroke of Fortune, no external circumstance, can shut the wise man off from action. The very thing that claims his attention is what keeps him from attending to other things. He stands ready for either outcome: if goods come, he governs them; if evils come, he conquers them. He has trained himself this thoroughly, I mean: to make his virtue visible in prosperity and in adversity alike, keeping his eyes on virtue itself, not on the materials virtue happens to be working with. So neither poverty nor pain nor anything else that turns the inexperienced aside and sends them stampeding will pull him off his course. Do you suppose he's weighed down by evils? He makes use of them. Phidias, the great sculptor, didn't know how to make statues only out of ivory; he made them out of bronze too. If you'd handed him marble, or some still cheaper material, he'd have carved the best statue that material allowed. In the same way, the wise man will develop virtue amid wealth if he can, and if not, in poverty; in his own country if possible, and if not, in exile; as a commander if possible, and if not, as a common soldier; in sound health if possible, and if not, enfeebled. Whatever Fortune hands him, he will make something remarkable out of it.

Animal-tamers never miss. They take the most savage beasts — creatures that would terrify anyone who stumbled on them — and bend them to man's will; and not content with driving out the ferocity, they gentle them until beast and man live under the same roof. The trainer puts his hand in the lion's mouth; the keeper kisses his tiger; a small Ethiopian boy orders the elephant down onto its knees, or has it walk the rope. Just so, the wise man is an expert at taming evils. Pain, want, disgrace, prison, exile — dreaded everywhere — when they come up against the wise man, they are tamed.

Farewell.

Letter 86

Letter 86: On Scipio’s Villa

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I am writing to you from the country house that once belonged to Scipio Africanus himself. I have been resting here, and before I sat down to write I paid my respects to his spirit, and to an altar which, I suspect, is actually the great soldier's tomb. That his soul went back to the sky it came from, I have no doubt — and not because he commanded mighty armies. Cambyses had mighty armies too, and Cambyses was a madman who simply happened to use his madness successfully. No, I believe it because Scipio showed moderation and a sense of duty to a degree that is hard to believe. And I admire those qualities more in the moment he walked away from his country than in all the years he defended her. Because by then it had come down to this: either Scipio stayed in Rome, or Rome stayed free. "I want nothing," he said, "that infringes our laws or our customs by so much as an inch. Let every Roman citizen have equal rights. My country, enjoy the good I have done you — but enjoy it without me. I have been the cause of your freedom; now I will be the proof of it. If it is true that I have grown too big for your good, then I go into exile."

How can I not admire that greatness of spirit — choosing voluntary exile just to take the weight off the state? Matters had reached the point where either liberty had to do harm to Scipio, or Scipio had to do harm to liberty. Either one would have been an offense against heaven. So he yielded to the laws and withdrew here to Liternum, putting the state in his debt for his own exile just as surely as for Hannibal's.

I have gone through the whole house. It is built of squared stone. A wall runs around an entire forest. There are towers buttressed out on both sides to defend the house, and a well hidden among the buildings and shrubbery — big enough to supply an army. And then there is the bath: tiny, and buried in darkness, in the old style, because our ancestors did not believe a bath could be hot unless it was dark. It gave me real pleasure to stand there and compare Scipio's ways with ours. Think of it. In this cramped little recess, the "terror of Carthage" — the man Rome must thank for being captured only once — used to wash a body worn out by work in the fields. For he kept himself busy, and he tilled the soil with his own hands, the way the good old Romans did. Under this dingy roof he stood; this floor, cheap as it is, bore his weight.

But who could bear to bathe like that now? A man thinks himself poor and shabby these days unless his walls gleam with huge, expensive mirrors; unless his Alexandrian marbles are set off with mosaics of Numidian stone, their borders worked over on every side with elaborate patterns in as many colors as a painting; unless the vaulted ceiling is buried in glass; unless the swimming pools are lined with Thasian marble — a sight that was once rare and wonderful in any temple — pools into which we lower bodies already drained weak by heavy sweating; and unless, to finish it off, the water pours out of silver spigots. And so far I have only been describing the ordinary bathhouses. What am I supposed to say when I get to the baths of the freedmen? The sheer crowds of statues! The columns that hold up nothing at all, installed purely for decoration, purely for the sake of spending money! The masses of water crashing noisily down from level to level! We have grown so luxurious that we refuse to walk on anything but precious stones.

In this bath of Scipio's there are little chinks — you cannot really call them windows — cut through the stone wall in a way that lets in light without weakening the defenses. Nowadays, though, people say a bath is fit only for moths unless it has been designed to catch the sun all day long through the widest possible windows, unless a man can bathe and get a tan at the same time, unless he can lie in his tub and gaze out over stretches of land and sea. And so it goes: the establishments that drew crowds and won gasps of admiration when they first opened get deserted and filed away with the venerable antiques the moment luxury works out some new trick — luxury busily inventing her own undoing. In the early days, though, there were few baths, and nobody dressed them up at all. Why should anyone lavish decoration on a thing that costs a penny to enter and was invented for use, not for delight? The bathers of those days did not have water poured over them, and it did not run endlessly fresh as if from a hot spring; they did not think it mattered in the least how perfectly clear the water was that they were about to leave their dirt in. Good gods, what a pleasure it is to step into that dark bath, under its plain common roof, and know that your hero Cato, as aedile — or Fabius Maximus, or one of the Cornelii — warmed its water with his own hands! For even that used to be part of the noblest aediles' job: to walk into the places where ordinary people gathered and demand that they be kept clean and heated to a temperature fixed by use and health — not the temperature that has lately become fashionable, as hot as a house fire, so hot that a slave convicted of some crime could now simply be sentenced to be bathed alive. As far as I can tell, there is no longer any difference between "the bath is warm" and "the bath is on fire."

And how people today sneer at Scipio as a country bumpkin — because he did not let daylight into his sweating-room through wide windows, because he did not roast himself in the blazing sun and dawdle around until he could stew in the hot water! "Poor fool," they say, "he didn't know how to live! His water wasn't even filtered. It was often cloudy, and after a heavy rain it was practically mud!" But it did not matter much to Scipio if his bathwater looked like that. He came there to wash off sweat, not ointment. And how do you suppose certain people will answer me? They will say: "I don't envy Scipio. A bath like that — now that was truly an exile's life." Friend, if you were wiser, you would know that Scipio did not even bathe every day. The writers who have handed down the old Roman ways tell us that the Romans washed their arms and legs daily — those being the parts that picked up dirt in a day's work — and bathed all over just once a week. At this point somebody will snort: "Well! They must have been filthy fellows. Imagine what they smelled like!" They smelled of the camp, the farm, and heroism. Now that spotless, glittering bathhouses have been invented, men are actually fouler than they used to be. When Horace wants to describe a scoundrel, a man notorious for over-the-top luxury, what does he say? "Buccillus smells of perfume." Show me a Buccillus today: his perfume would rank as pure goat-stink, and he would have to take the place of Gargonius, the smelly fellow Horace set opposite him in the same passage. Nowadays it is not enough merely to wear scent; you have to slap on a fresh coat two or three times a day so it will not evaporate off your body. And why on earth does a man preen over that perfume as if the smell were his own?

If all this strikes you as too gloomy, charge it to Scipio's villa — where I have also learned a lesson from Aegialus, the most careful of farmers and the current owner of this estate. He has taught me that a tree can be transplanted no matter how far along in years it is. We old men need to learn that lesson, since there is not one of us who is not planting an olive orchard for his successor. I have seen those orchards bearing fruit in due season after three or four unproductive years. And you too, one day, will be shaded by the tree that

  is slow to grow, but will bring shade to cheer
  your grandsons in the far-off years,
  Sow beans in the spring; then you too, clover,
  are welcome in the crumbling furrows; and
  the millet calls for its yearly care.

You can judge from a little incident whether those crops should really be planted at the same time, or whether both belong to spring. It is June as I write this — we are well along toward July — and this very day I have watched farmers harvesting beans and sowing millet.

But back to our olive orchard. I saw it planted in two ways. If the trees were large, Aegialus took the trunks and cut the branches back to a foot each; then he transplanted the tree with its root-ball, first trimming off the roots and leaving only the thick knob they hang from. He smeared this with manure, set it into the hole, and then not only heaped the earth up around it but stamped and pressed it down hard. Nothing, he says, works better than this packing. It keeps out the cold and the wind; and besides, the trunk is not shaken about so much, which gives the young roots a chance to come out and take hold in the soil. Roots that new are necessarily still soft; they have only a slight grip, and a very little shaking tears them loose. The root-ball itself he shaves clean before he covers it up, because, he maintains, new roots spring from every part that has been shorn. And the trunk should stand no more than three or four feet out of the ground. That way it greens over thickly right from the base, and there is none of that big, dry, withered stump you see in old olive orchards. The second way of planting was this: he set out, in the same fashion, branches that were strong and soft-barked, the way young saplings' branches usually are. These grow a little more slowly, but since they spring from what is practically a cutting, there is nothing rough or ugly about them.

One more thing I have seen recently: an aged vine transplanted out of its own vineyard. With a vine, the little fibers should be gathered up too, if possible, and then you should cover the stem more generously with earth, so that roots will sprout even from the stock itself. I have seen such plantings made not only in February but at the very end of March — and the vines take hold of elms that are strangers to them, and embrace them. All the trees that are, so to speak, "thick-stemmed," Aegialus declares, should be helped along with water from the tank. If we have that help, we are our own rain-makers.

I do not intend to pass along any more of these secrets — otherwise I will do to you what Aegialus did to me, and train up my own competitor.

Farewell.

Letter 87

Letter 87: Some Arguments in Favor of the Simple Life

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

"I was shipwrecked before I ever got on board." I won't explain how that happened, or you'll file it away as one more Stoic paradox. But I will prove to you — whenever you're willing to listen, and even if you're not — that the words are neither false nor as surprising as they sound at first. In the meantime, here is what the journey showed me: how much we own that we don't need, and how easily we could decide to let go of things whose loss, whenever necessity does take them from us, we never even feel.

My friend Maximus and I have just spent two of the happiest days imaginable on the road, taking along very few slaves — one carriage-load — and no gear beyond what we wore on our backs. The mattress lies on the ground; I lie on the mattress. Of our two rugs, one goes under us and one over. Nothing could have been trimmed from our lunch: it took less than an hour to prepare, and we were nowhere without dried figs, never without writing tablets. If I have bread, the figs are a relish; if I don't, the figs are the bread. They make every day a New Year's feast for me, and I make the New Year happy and prosperous with good thoughts and greatness of soul — for the soul is never greater than when it has set aside everything that isn't its own and made its own peace by fearing nothing, its own riches by craving none.

The vehicle I'm riding in is a farmer's cart. The mules prove they're alive only by walking. The driver is barefoot — and not because it's summer, either. And yet I can barely force myself to want anyone to think this cart is mine. My false embarrassment about the truth is still holding out, you see. Whenever we meet a more elegant party on the road, I blush in spite of myself — proof that the conduct I approve and applaud has not yet found a firm, settled home in me. The man who blushes at riding in a rattletrap will brag when he rides in style.

So my progress is still too small. I don't yet have the courage to own my thrift in public. I still worry about what other travelers think of me. What I should have done instead was throw my opinion straight in the face of the whole human race: "You are mad. You are deceived. You pour your admiration on superfluous things. You value no man at what he's really worth. When property is at stake, you know exactly how to calculate. Before you lend a man money or favors — because by now you enter favors in your account-book too — you run the numbers with the most scrupulous care. 'His estates are wide, but his debts are heavy.' 'A fine house — built on borrowed capital.' 'No one can field a more brilliant retinue on short notice, but he can't cover what he owes.' 'If he paid off his creditors, he'd have nothing left.'" Well, you should do the same in every other case too: subtract, and find out what each man actually owns.

You call a man rich, I suppose, because his gold dinner service travels with him wherever he goes, because he farms land in every province, because the account-book he unrolls is a long one, because he owns estates so vast near the city that people would grudge him that much acreage even in the empty wastelands of Apulia. But when you've listed all of it — he is poor. Why? He's in debt. "For how much?" you ask. For everything he has. Or do you think it matters whether a man has borrowed from another man or from Fortune? What good are mules matched in identical livery? Or decorated chariots, and

  Horses draped in purple and embroidery,
  gold harness hanging at their necks,
  champing gilded bits, in trappings all of gold?

Neither master nor mule is improved by any of it.

Marcus Cato the Censor — whose life did the state as much good as Scipio's, for while Scipio made war on our enemies, Cato made war on our bad morals — used to ride a donkey; and a donkey, at that, loaded with saddlebags carrying the master's necessities. How I'd love to see him meet on the road today one of our fashionable dandies, with his outriders and his Numidian couriers and a great cloud of dust rolling ahead of him. Your dandy would look refined and well-attended next to Marcus Cato, no question — your dandy who, in the middle of all his luxurious equipment, is chiefly debating whether to hire himself out to the sword or to the hunting-knife. What a glory to his times: a general who had celebrated a triumph, a censor, and — most remarkable of all — a Cato, content with a single nag, and with less than a whole nag at that, since the baggage hanging down over both flanks had claimed part of the animal for itself. So wouldn't you rather have Cato's horse — that one horse, saddle-worn by Cato himself — than the dandy's whole string of plump ponies, Spanish cobs, and trotters? I can see there will be no end to this subject unless I make one myself. So I'll say no more about superfluous things — surely the man who first called them "hindrances" had a prophet's inkling that they would become exactly what they are now. Instead, let me deliver to you the syllogisms of our school — still a very small number — that bear on the question of virtue, which in our judgment is sufficient for the happy life.

"What is good makes men good. In the art of music, for example, what is good makes the musician. But chance events do not make a good man; therefore chance events are not goods." The Peripatetics answer that the premise is false: men are not in every case made good by what is good. In music there is something good — a flute, a harp, an organ tuned to accompany singing — but none of those instruments makes the musician. To which we reply: "You misunderstand what we mean by 'the good in music.' We don't mean what equips the musician; we mean what makes him. You are pointing at the tools of the art, not at the art itself. If anything in the art of music itself is good, that thing will make the musician — every time." Let me put it more clearly still. "Good in the art of music" can mean two things: what helps the musician's performance, and what helps his art. The instruments — flutes and organs and harps — belong to the performance; they have nothing to do with the art itself. A man is an artist without them; at most he lacks the means to exercise his art. But the good in a man is not double in that way, because the good of a man and the good of his life are one and the same.

"What can fall to the lot of the basest and most despised of men is not a good. But wealth falls to the lot of the pimp and the gladiator-trainer; therefore wealth is not a good." "Another false premise," they say, "for we see goods fall to very low sorts of men — in scholarship, and in the art of healing, and in the art of navigation too." But those arts make no claim to greatness of soul. They rise to no heights, and they don't frown on whatever Fortune brings. It is virtue that lifts a man up and sets him above everything mortals hold dear; virtue neither craves too much what people call good nor fears too much what people call bad. Chelidon, one of Cleopatra's eunuchs, held enormous wealth; and more recently Natalis — a man whose tongue was as shameless as it was filthy, a man whose mouth performed the vilest services — was heir to many men and made many men his heirs. Well? Was it the money that made him unclean, or did he dirty the money? Money tumbles into some men's hands the way a coin tumbles down a sewer. Virtue stands above all that. It is appraised in coin of its own minting, and it counts none of these random windfalls as good. But medicine and navigation don't forbid themselves or their practitioners to admire such things. A man who is not good can still be a doctor, a pilot, a scholar — yes, just as easily as he can be a cook. A man who comes to possess something that is not of a random sort cannot be called a random sort of man; a person is the same sort as what he possesses. A strongbox is worth exactly what it holds — or rather, it's a mere accessory of what it holds. Who ever prices a full purse at anything except the count of the money inside it? The same goes for the owners of great estates: they are accessories and afterthoughts to their own possessions.

Why, then, is the wise man great? Because he has a great soul. So it stands: what falls even to the most contemptible person is not a good. That's why I would never call inactivity a good — even the tree-frog and the flea have that. Nor would I call rest and freedom from bother a good — what has more leisure than a worm? Do you ask what produces the wise man? The same thing that produces a god. You must grant that there is something godlike in the wise man, something heavenly, something grand. The good doesn't come to everyone, and it doesn't tolerate just any owner. Look:

  See what each country bears, and what it won't:
  here grain grows richer, there the vine;
  elsewhere young trees and grasses, all unbidden,
  clothe themselves in green. See how Tmolus ships
  its saffron perfume out, how ivory comes
  from India; soft Sheba sends its incense,
  and the naked Chalybes their iron.

These products were parceled out to separate countries so that human beings would be forced to trade with one another, each needing something from his neighbor in turn. The Supreme Good has its own homeland too. It does not grow where ivory grows, or iron. Where does the Supreme Good live, then? In the soul. And unless the soul is pure and holy, there is no room in it for God.

"Good does not come from evil. But riches come from greed; therefore riches are not a good." "It isn't true," they say, "that good doesn't come from evil. Money comes from sacrilege and theft. Sacrilege and theft are evils, yes — but only because they work more evil than good. They bring profit; but the profit comes packaged with fear, anxiety, and torment of mind and body." Whoever says this has to admit that sacrilege, though an evil because it works much evil, is also partly good because it accomplishes a certain amount of good. What could be more monstrous than that? Though, to be honest, we have actually convinced the world that sacrilege, theft, and adultery belong among the goods. How many men feel no blush at theft; how many boast of their adulteries! Petty sacrilege gets punished; sacrilege on the grand scale rides in a triumphal procession. Besides, if sacrilege were wholly good in any respect, it would also be honorable and would count as right conduct — for it is conduct, and conduct concerns us. And no human being, thinking it over seriously, admits that.

So goods cannot spring from evil. For if sacrilege is an evil, as you object, only because it brings on much evil, then absolve sacrilege of its punishment, guarantee it immunity, and sacrilege becomes wholly good. And yet the worst punishment for a crime is inside the crime. You are mistaken, I insist, if you save your punishments for the hangman or the prison. The crime is punished the moment it is finished — no, the moment it is committed. Good does not spring from evil any more than figs grow on olive trees. What grows corresponds to its seed; goods cannot wander from their stock. Just as the honorable does not grow from the base, good does not grow from evil. For the honorable and the good are identical.

Some of our own school push back against this as follows: "Suppose money from any source whatsoever is a good. Then even when it is taken by an act of sacrilege, the money doesn't owe its origin to the sacrilege. Take this illustration. In one jar there is a piece of gold and there is a serpent. If you take the gold out of the jar, the jar doesn't yield me the gold because it contains the serpent too; it yields the gold in spite of containing the serpent. In the same way, gain results from sacrilege not because sacrilege is a base and cursed act, but because the act contains gain as well. In the jar, the serpent is the evil, not the gold lying beside it; in the act of sacrilege, the crime is the evil, not the profit." I disagree with these men, and here's why: the two cases are not alike at all. In the one, I can take the gold without the serpent; in the other, I cannot take the profit without committing the sacrilege. The gain in that case doesn't lie beside the crime; it is blended into the crime.

"What entangles us in many evils while we are trying to get it is not a good. But while we are trying to get riches, we are entangled in many evils; therefore riches are not a good." "Your first premise," they say, "carries two meanings. One is: we become entangled in many evils while trying to get riches. But we also become entangled in many evils while trying to get virtue — one man is shipwrecked traveling for his studies, another is captured by pirates. The second meaning is: that through which we become entangled in evils is not a good. And it won't logically follow from your proposition that it is through riches or through pleasure that we're entangled in evils. Otherwise, if riches are the route to many evils, riches are not merely not-a-good; they are a positive evil. You claim only that they are not a good. Besides," the objector adds, "you grant that riches have some use. You count them among the advantages. But on that same reasoning they can't even be an advantage, since the pursuit of them costs us so much disadvantage." Certain men answer the objection this way: "You are wrong to charge the disadvantages to riches. Riches injure no one. What harms a man is his own folly or his neighbor's wickedness — just as a sword by itself kills nobody; it is merely the tool in the killer's hand. Riches themselves don't harm you just because you come to harm on account of riches."

I think Posidonius reasons better. He holds that riches are a cause of evil not because they do any evil themselves, but because they goad men on until they are ready to do it. The efficient cause — the one that necessarily does harm directly — is one thing; the antecedent cause is another. It is the antecedent cause that riches carry inside them: they puff up the spirit and breed pride, they attract resentment, and they unsettle the mind so thoroughly that we delight in the mere reputation of being wealthy, even though that reputation is bound to hurt us. Goods, properly speaking, should be beyond all blame. They are pure; they don't corrupt the spirit; they don't tempt us. They lift and enlarge the spirit, yes — but without inflating it. Goods produce confidence; riches produce brazenness. Goods give us greatness of soul; riches give us arrogance. And arrogance is nothing but a false front of greatness.

"By that account," says the objector, "riches are not merely not-a-good; they are a positive evil." They would be an evil if they did harm by themselves — if, as I said, the efficient cause were what they carried. In fact what they carry is the antecedent cause — and a cause that doesn't merely rouse the spirit but drags it along by force. For riches shower on us a semblance of the good, so like the real thing that most people take it on trust. Virtue carries an antecedent cause too: the envy it draws — for many men are resented for their wisdom, and many for their justice. But that cause, though it comes with virtue, is not virtue's own product, and it is no mere semblance of reality. On the contrary: far closer to reality is that vision which virtue flashes into men's spirits, summoning them to love it and marvel at it.

Posidonius thinks the syllogism should be framed like this: "Things that give the soul no greatness, no confidence, no freedom from care are not goods. But riches and health and conditions like them do none of these things; therefore riches and health are not goods." Then he extends it further: "Things that give the soul no greatness, no confidence, no freedom from care — and that furthermore breed in it arrogance, vanity, and insolence — are evils. But the gifts of Fortune push us into those evil ways. Therefore the gifts of Fortune are not goods." "But by that reasoning," says the objector, "the gifts of Fortune won't even be advantages." No — advantages and goods stand on different footing. An advantage is what contains more usefulness than annoyance. But a good must be unmixed, with no harmfulness anywhere in it. A thing is not good if it does more benefit than injury — only if it does nothing but benefit. Besides, advantages can belong to animals, to imperfect men, to fools. So an advantage may have some disadvantage stirred into it; we call the mixture an "advantage" by judging its dominant element. The good can belong to the wise man alone, and it must be without alloy.

Cheer up: there's only one knot left for you to untangle, though it's a knot fit for a Hercules. "Good does not come from evil. But riches come from many cases of poverty; therefore riches are not a good." Our school doesn't recognize this syllogism; the Peripatetics both concoct it and solve it. But Posidonius reports that this fallacy, batted around every school of logic there is, was refuted by Antipater as follows: "The word 'poverty' denotes not the possession of something but the non-possession of it — or, as the ancients put it, deprivation (the Greeks say 'by deprivation,' meaning negatively). 'Poverty' states not what a man has, but what he does not have. So no fullness can come from a multitude of empties; riches are made up of many positive possessions, not many deficits. You have," he says, "the wrong notion of what poverty means. Poverty is not the possession of little; it is the non-possession of much. The word applies not to what a man has, but to what he lacks." I could say all this more easily if Latin had a word for the Greek term meaning "not-possessing." Antipater assigns that quality to poverty; for my own part, I can't see what poverty is except the possession of little. If we ever find ourselves with plenty of leisure, we can investigate what the essence of riches is and what the essence of poverty is. But when that day comes, let's also consider whether it might be better to soften poverty, and to take the arrogance out of wealth, than to quibble over the words as if the question of the things were already settled.

Imagine we've been summoned to an assembly, and a bill to abolish riches is on the floor. Is this how we'd argue for it or against it — with these syllogisms? Will syllogisms make the Roman people demand poverty and praise it — poverty, the foundation and the cause of their empire — and shrink in fear from their own wealth instead? Will they make the people reflect that they found that wealth among the peoples they conquered; that wealth is the source from which the vote-hunting and the bribery and the disorder burst into a city once famous for scruple and sobriety; that the spoils of conquered nations are put on display far too lavishly; and that whatever one people has snatched away from all the rest can be snatched even more easily by all the rest from one? Better to vote for that law with our conduct, and to take our desires by direct assault, than to outflank them with logic. If we can, let us speak more bravely; if not, let us speak more frankly.

Farewell.

Letter 88

Letter 88: On Liberal and Vocational Studies

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You have been wanting to know what I think of the liberal studies. Here is my answer: I have no respect for any study, and I count no study good, whose end is making money. Those are trades — useful exactly as far as they train the mind for something greater, and no farther. You should stay with them only as long as your mind can manage nothing better; they are our apprenticeship, not our real work. You can see why they are called "liberal" studies: they are the studies thought worthy of a free man. But there is only one study that is truly liberal — the one that makes a man free. That is the study of wisdom, and it is lofty, brave, and great-souled. All the others are small and childish. You surely don't believe there is any real good in subjects whose teachers are — look at them — men of the cheapest and shabbiest stamp? We shouldn't be learning these things; we should be done learning them.

Some people have decided that the real question about the liberal studies is whether they make men good. But the teachers of these subjects don't even claim to know anything about that. The scholar busies himself with language; if he wants to range farther, he takes up history; if he wants to push out to the farthest limits, poetry. Which of these paves the road to virtue? Sounding out syllables, investigating words, memorizing plays, drawing up rules for the scansion of verse — what is there in any of it that removes fear, roots out desire, or bridles passion? The question is simple: do these men teach virtue, or don't they? If they don't teach it, they can't pass it on. If they do teach it, they are philosophers. Would you like to know how far their lecture chairs are from being chairs of virtue? Look how unlike their subjects are — and they would resemble one another if they were all teaching the same thing.

Perhaps they persuade you that Homer was a philosopher — though they disprove it with the very arguments they use to prove it. Sometimes they make him a Stoic, approving nothing but virtue, shunning pleasure, refusing to trade honor even for immortality. Sometimes they make him an Epicurean, praising the quiet city that passes its days in feasting and song. Sometimes he is a Peripatetic, sorting goods into three classes; sometimes an Academic, holding that everything is uncertain. Clearly none of these doctrines belongs to Homer, precisely because all of them are in him — for they contradict one another. But let us grant these men their point: Homer was a philosopher. Then surely he was wise before he ever knew a line of poetry. So let us learn the things that made Homer wise.

Asking whether Homer or Hesiod was the older poet is no more to the point than knowing why Hecuba, though younger than Helen, wore her years so badly. And what, I ask you, would be the point of settling the ages of Achilles and Patroclus? Do you ask, "Where did Ulysses wander?" instead of working to keep ourselves from wandering all the time? We have no leisure for lectures on whether he was tossed between Italy and Sicily or blown beyond the known world — a wandering that long could hardly have fit inside such narrow bounds. We meet storms of the spirit every day of our lives, and our own badness drives us into every trouble that ever plagued Ulysses. For us there is never any shortage of beauty to tempt the eye, or of enemies to attack us; here are savage monsters that delight in human blood, there the treacherous songs that seduce the ear, and out beyond, shipwreck and the whole varied catalogue of misfortune. Teach me this instead, by the example of Ulysses: how to love my country, my wife, my father — and how, even after shipwreck, to keep sailing toward those honorable harbors. Why try to settle whether Penelope was a model of purity or just fooled her generation? Whether she suspected the man standing in front of her was Ulysses before she knew it? Teach me instead what purity is, and how great a good we have in it — and whether it lives in the body or in the soul.

Now I turn to the musician. You, sir, are teaching me how the treble and the bass harmonize, how strings sounding different notes can still produce concord. Teach my soul harmony instead — bring it into accord with itself, and don't let my purposes fall out of tune. You are showing me which keys are mournful; show me instead how to keep from sounding a mournful note in the middle of adversity. The mathematician teaches me how to survey my estates; I would rather be taught how to measure what is enough for a man. He teaches me to count, and lends my fingers to greed; I would rather he taught me that all that arithmetic is beside the point — that a man is no happier for possessions that wear out his bookkeepers. Or rather, how useless property is to a man for whom it would be the worst of misfortunes to have to total up his own holdings using his own head. What good is knowing how to divide a field into parcels, if I don't know how to share it with my brother? What good is calculating an acre to the inch, and catching the sliver that slipped past my measuring rod, if I turn bitter the moment a bad-tempered neighbor scrapes off a corner of my land? The mathematician teaches me how to lose none of my boundaries; what I want to learn is how to lose them all with a light heart. "But I'm being driven off the farm my father and grandfather owned!" So? Who owned the land before your grandfather? Can you name the people — never mind the person — who held it first? You didn't enter it as a master; you entered as a tenant. And whose tenant are you? If your case goes well, your heir's. The lawyers say public property can't become private by squatting on it; well, what you hold and call your own is public property — it belongs to the human race. What marvelous skill you have! You can measure the circle; you can square any shape set in front of you; you can name the distances between the stars; nothing in the universe escapes your calculations. If you are really a master of your craft, then measure a human soul. Tell me how great it is — or how small. You know a straight line when you see one; what good is that, if you don't know what is straight in this life of ours?

Next I come to the man who boasts of knowing the heavens — who knows

  Where Saturn's chilly star conceals itself,
  And through what orbit Mercury goes straying.

What is the benefit of knowing this? That I should worry when Saturn and Mars stand in opposition, or when Mercury sets in the evening in plain view of Saturn — instead of learning that those stars, wherever they stand, are kindly, and cannot change? They are driven along by an unending round of destiny, on a course they cannot leave. They return on schedule; they either set the whole world's work in motion or mark off its intervals. But if they are responsible for everything that happens, what does it help you to know the secrets of what cannot be altered? And if they merely give signs, what is the use of foreseeing what you cannot escape? Know these things or don't — they will happen anyway.

  But watch the racing sun, and watch the stars
  That follow in his train, and then tomorrow
  Will never take you in, and no clear night
  Will ever lead you wrong.

Provision enough has already been made to keep me safe from anything that could take me in. "What," you say, "tomorrow never plays me false? Whatever happens without my knowledge plays me false." I don't know what will happen; but I do know what can happen. And about that I will file no complaints. I expect the future in its entirety; if any of its severity is remitted, I take it as a gift. Tomorrow deceives me if it treats me kindly — and even then it doesn't really deceive me. For just as I know that anything can happen, I also know that it won't all happen. So I count on the favorable and stand ready for the bad.

In this discussion you must bear with me if I don't follow the standard course. For I refuse to admit painting into the list of liberal arts, and sculpture too, and marble-work, and the other servants of luxury. I also strike off wrestling and the whole science compounded of oil and mud — otherwise I would have to admit the perfumers as well, and the cooks, and everyone else who hires out his wits to the service of our pleasures. For what is "liberal," tell me, about those ravenous gulpers of emetics, whose bodies are fattened while their minds stay thin and dull? Or do we seriously believe that sort of training is "liberal" for the young men of Rome — the young men our ancestors used to teach to stand straight and hurl a spear, to wield a pike, to manage a horse, to handle weapons? Our ancestors taught their children nothing that could be learned lying down. But neither the new system nor the old one teaches or nourishes virtue. What good does it do us to guide a horse and check his speed with the curb, and then discover that our own passions, completely uncurbed, have bolted with us? What good is beating opponent after opponent at wrestling or boxing, and then being beaten by our own temper?

"What then," you say, "do the liberal studies contribute nothing to our welfare?" A great deal in other respects — nothing at all toward virtue. Even the admittedly low-grade arts that work with the hands contribute plenty to the equipment of living, yet they have nothing to do with virtue. "Then why do we educate our children in the liberal studies?" Not because these studies can bestow virtue, but because they prepare the soul to receive it. Just as the old "primary course," as the ancients called it, which taught boys their letters, does not teach the liberal arts but clears the ground for learning them, so the liberal arts do not carry the soul all the way to virtue; they merely set it moving in that direction.

Posidonius divides the arts into four classes. First come the common and low; then the arts of amusement; then the arts for educating boys; and finally the liberal arts. The common ones belong to workmen and are plain handwork; their business is equipping life, and they make no pretense of beauty or honor. The arts of amusement are the ones that aim to please the eye and the ear. Here you may file the stage-machinists, who invent scaffolding that rises on its own, floors that climb silently into the air, and plenty of other surprises — things that fit together and then fall apart, things that stand separate and then join by themselves, things that tower up and then slowly collapse. Such tricks stun the inexperienced eye; people who don't know the causes marvel at everything that happens without warning. The arts for educating boys, which come somewhat nearer the liberal arts, are the ones the Greeks call the "cycle of studies" and we Romans call the "liberal." But the only arts that are really liberal — or, to give them their truer name, really free — are the ones whose business is virtue.

"But," someone will say, "just as philosophy has one part dealing with nature, one with ethics, and one with reasoning, so this family of liberal arts claims its own place in philosophy. When you come to questions about nature, the decision rests on a word from the mathematician; therefore mathematics is a department of the branch it assists." But many things assist us without being parts of us — indeed, if they were parts of us, they wouldn't assist. Food is an aid to the body, but it is not a part of the body. Mathematics performs a service for us; it is as necessary to philosophy as the carpenter is to the mathematician. But carpentry is not a part of mathematics, and mathematics is not a part of philosophy. Besides, each has its own boundaries. The wise man investigates and learns the causes of natural things; the mathematician follows after and computes their numbers and measurements. The wise man knows the laws by which the heavenly bodies hold their courses, what powers they have and what natures; the astronomer merely logs their comings and goings, the rules of their rising and setting, and the occasional stretches when they seem to stand still — though in fact no heavenly body can stand still. The wise man will know what causes the reflection in a mirror; the mathematician can only tell you how far the object must be from the image, and what shape of mirror will produce what reflection. The philosopher will prove that the sun is a large body; the astronomer will compute exactly how large, working forward by trial and observation — but to work forward at all he must borrow certain principles. And no art stands on its own feet if its foundation is held on loan. Philosophy asks no favors of anyone; it raises its whole structure on its own ground. Mathematics is, so to speak, built on the next man's property — it builds on borrowed soil. It accepts its first principles as a favor, and only by that favor reaches its further conclusions. If it could march unassisted to the truth, if it could grasp the nature of the whole universe, I would say it offered real help to our minds — for the mind grows by contact with things heavenly, and draws down something from above. But there is only one thing that brings the soul to perfection: the unshakable knowledge of good and evil. And no other art investigates good and evil.

I would like to run through the virtues one by one. Bravery scorns everything that inspires fear; it looks down on, challenges, and crushes the powers of terror and everything that would drive our freedom under the yoke. Do "liberal studies" strengthen that virtue? Loyalty is the holiest good in the human heart. No compulsion can force it into betrayal; no reward can bribe it. Loyalty cries: "Burn me, cut me, kill me — I will not betray my trust; and the harder torture digs for my secret, the deeper I will bury it!" Can the "liberal arts" produce that spirit in us? Self-control governs our desires: some it hates and routs outright; others it disciplines back to a healthy measure; and it never goes near a desire for the desire's own sake. It knows that the best measure for appetite is not what you want to take but what you ought to take. Kindness forbids you to be overbearing with your companions, and forbids you to be grasping. In words, in deeds, in feelings, it shows itself gentle and courteous to everyone. It counts no trouble as merely someone else's. And the chief reason it loves its own good is that someday that good will belong to someone else. Do "liberal studies" teach a man this kind of character? No — no more than they teach simplicity, moderation and self-restraint, thrift and economy, or the mercy that spares a neighbor's life as if it were its own and knows that no man is a thing for other men to waste.

"But," comes the objection, "since you declare that virtue can't be attained without the liberal studies, how can you deny that they assist virtue?" Because you can't attain virtue without food either, and food has nothing to do with virtue. Wood gives no assistance to a ship, even though a ship can't be built without wood. There is no reason, I tell you, to think a thing is made by the help of whatever it can't be made without. And we could actually go further: it is possible to attain wisdom without the liberal studies at all. Virtue has to be learned, yes — but it isn't learned through them.

And really, why should I assume that a man who doesn't know his letters can never be wise, when wisdom isn't found in letters? Wisdom deals in facts, not words — and perhaps memory is more dependable when it has nothing to lean on outside itself. Wisdom is a large and spacious thing. It needs room to breathe. One must learn about things divine and human, the past and the future, the perishable and the eternal — and about Time. Look how many questions arise about time alone: first, whether it is anything in and of itself; second, whether anything exists before time and apart from time; and again, did time begin along with the universe — or, since something existed even before the universe began, did time exist then too? About the soul alone the questions are countless: where it comes from, what it is, when it begins to exist, how long it lasts; whether it moves from place to place, changing its lodgings, passed on from one animal shape to another, or serves only a single term and then roams the universe once it is set free; whether it is corporeal or not; what it will do when it stops using us as its instrument; how it will spend its freedom once it has escaped this present prison; whether it forgets its whole past, and begins to know itself only at the moment when, released from the body, it has withdrawn to the skies.

So whatever province of things human and divine you take up, you will be worn out by the sheer mass of what must be answered and what must be learned. And to give these vast and weighty subjects free lodging in your soul, you must throw out everything superfluous. Virtue will not squeeze herself into our narrow spaces; a great thing needs wide room to move in. Drive everything else out; empty the whole chest for virtue.

"But it's a pleasure to be acquainted with many arts." Then let us keep only as much of them as we actually need. Do you blame the man who ranks the superfluous alongside the useful and fills his house with a parade of costly objects — and then not blame the man who has buried himself in the useless furniture of learning? Wanting to know more than enough is its own kind of intemperance. Why? Because this unseemly chase after the liberal arts makes men tiresome, wordy, tactless, self-satisfied bores, who fail to learn the essentials precisely because they've learned the non-essentials. Didymus the scholar wrote four thousand books. I would pity him if he had merely read that many useless volumes. In those books he investigates Homer's birthplace, who Aeneas's real mother was, whether Anacreon was more of a lecher or more of a drunkard, whether Sappho was a loose woman — and other questions whose answers, if ever found, ought to be forgotten on the spot. Now don't tell me that life is long! And when you turn to our own countrymen, I can show you plenty of works there too that deserve the axe.

It costs an enormous outlay of time, and enormous wear on other people's ears, to win the compliment: "What a learned man you are!" Let's settle for the plainer, more countrified one: "What a good man you are!" Do I mean it? Should I unroll the annals of the world's history and hunt for the first man who wrote poetry? Should I estimate, without any written records, the number of years between Orpheus and Homer? Should I study Aristarchus's absurd notations, in which he branded the text of other men's verses, and wear my life away on syllables? Shall I go wallow in the geometer's dust? Have I so completely forgotten that useful saying, "Save your time"? Must I know these things? Then what may I choose not to know? Apion the scholar — who drew crowds to his lectures all over Greece in the days of Gaius Caesar, and whom every city hailed as a second Homer — used to maintain that Homer, after finishing his two poems, the Iliad and the Odyssey, added a preliminary poem embracing the whole Trojan war. His proof? That Homer had deliberately planted in his opening line two letters containing a key to the number of his books. A man who wants to know many things must know things like that — and must take no account of all the time stolen from him by illness, public duties, private duties, daily duties, and sleep. Take the measure of your years; they have no room for all of this.

So far I have been speaking of the liberal studies. But think how much superfluous, unusable matter the philosophers carry around! They too have stooped to drawing fine divisions between syllables, to fixing the true meanings of conjunctions and prepositions — they have grown jealous of the grammarians, jealous of the mathematicians. Everything superfluous in those arts they have imported into their own, and the result is that they know more about speaking carefully than about living carefully. Let me tell you what evil this over-fine exactness does, and what an enemy of truth it is! Protagoras declares that you can argue either side of any question with equal success — including this very question, whether every question can be argued from either side. Nausiphanes holds that among the things which seem to exist, there is no difference between existing and not existing. Parmenides maintains that of everything which seems to exist, nothing exists except the universe alone. Zeno of Elea swept away all the difficulties by sweeping away one: he declares that nothing exists. The Pyrrhonean, Megarian, Eretrian, and Academic schools are all working at much the same job; they have introduced a new branch of knowledge — non-knowledge. Sweep the whole lot of them in with the superfluous troop of "liberal" studies. The one group of men gives me knowledge that will be of no use to me; the other takes away all hope of knowledge whatsoever. Better, of course, to know useless things than to know nothing. One set offers no lamp by which I might steer my gaze toward the truth; the other digs out my very eyes and leaves me blind. If I follow Protagoras, nothing in the whole of nature is anything but doubtful. If I follow Nausiphanes, only one thing is sure — that nothing is sure. If Parmenides, there is nothing but the One; if Zeno, not even the One.

Then what are we? What becomes of all these things that surround us, support us, sustain us? The whole universe turns out to be an empty or deceptive shadow. And I honestly cannot say which men annoy me more — the ones who would have it that we know nothing, or the ones who won't leave us even that.

Farewell.

Letter 89

Letter 89: On the Parts of Philosophy

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

What you want to know is genuinely useful — essential, in fact, for anyone in a hurry to reach wisdom: the parts of philosophy, and how that enormous body divides into its separate limbs. Study the parts, and the whole comes easier. I only wish philosophy could appear before our eyes entire, the way the whole expanse of the sky spreads out for us to gaze at — it would be a sight very much like the sky. Every mortal who saw her would fall helplessly in love, and we would abandon all the things we now believe to be great because we don't know what greatness is. But since that view isn't granted to us, we have to study philosophy the way we study the secrets of the heavens: one piece at a time.

The wise man's mind, it's true, takes in the whole structure of philosophy at a glance, sweeping over it as quickly as our eyes sweep the sky. But the rest of us are still breaking through fog; our vision blurs even for what's right in front of us. For people like us, it's easier to be shown each object separately while the universe as a whole is still beyond our grasp. So I'll do what you ask and divide philosophy into parts — but not into scraps. Dividing it is useful; mincing it is not. What is impossibly large is hard to take in, and so is what is impossibly small. A people is divided into tribes, an army into companies; anything that has grown large becomes easier to see once it's broken up — but the pieces, as I say, must not be countless and tiny. Over-analysis is faulty in exactly the same way as no analysis at all: grind anything down to dust and you might as well have left it a lump.

First, then, if you agree, let me draw the line between wisdom and philosophy. Wisdom is the perfect good of the human mind; philosophy is the love of wisdom, and the striving to reach it. Philosophy aims at the goal wisdom has already reached. And it's obvious why philosophy got its name: the name itself confesses what it loves. Some people have defined wisdom as the knowledge of things divine and things human. Others add: "and their causes as well." The added phrase seems unnecessary to me, since the causes of things divine and human are themselves part of the divine order. Philosophy, too, has been defined in all sorts of ways. Some have called it "the study of virtue," others "the study of straightening out the mind," others "the pursuit of right reason." But one thing is as good as settled: philosophy and wisdom are not the same. The seeker and the thing sought cannot be identical. Greed and money are different things — one does the craving, the other is what's craved — and philosophy and wisdom differ in the same way: one is the result and reward of the other. Philosophy does the traveling; wisdom is the destination.

Wisdom is what the Greeks call sophia. The Romans used that word too, in the old days, in the same sense in which they now also say "philosophy." Our old national plays will prove it to you, and so will the epitaph carved on the tomb of Dossennus:

  Pause, stranger, and read the wisdom of Dossennus.

Some of our own school, though — even though philosophy meant to them "the study of virtue," with virtue the thing sought and philosophy the seeker — have insisted that the two cannot be pulled apart. Philosophy cannot exist without virtue, nor virtue without philosophy. Philosophy is the study of virtue, but a study carried on by means of virtue itself; and there can be no virtue without the study of virtue, and no study of virtue without virtue. This isn't like shooting at a target far off, where the archer stands in one place and the mark in another. The approaches to virtue don't lie outside her the way roads into a city lie outside the city; the path that leads to virtue leads through virtue herself. Philosophy and virtue hold on to each other.

The greatest authors — and the greatest number of them — have held that philosophy has three divisions: moral, natural, and rational. The first keeps the soul in order. The second investigates the universe. The third works out what words really mean, how they combine, and the proofs that keep falsehood from creeping in and shoving truth aside. But there have also been those who carved philosophy into fewer parts, and those who carved it into more. Certain Peripatetics, Aristotle's school, added a fourth division, "civil philosophy," on the grounds that it demands its own sphere of activity and deals with its own subject matter. Some added a department they call by the Greek name "economics" — the science of managing one's own household. Still others made a separate heading for the various kinds of life. Yet there isn't one of these subdivisions that won't be found already filed under "moral" philosophy.

The Epicureans held that philosophy was twofold, natural and moral; they threw out the rational branch. Then, when the facts themselves forced them to sort out ambiguous ideas and expose the fallacies that hide under the costume of truth, they too smuggled in a heading — they call it "on judgment and rule" — which is simply "rational" under another name, though they treat it as an appendix to the natural department. The Cyrenaic school abolished the natural and the rational departments both, and settled for the moral side alone — and then they too readmitted, under other titles, exactly what they had rejected. For they divide moral philosophy into five parts: first, what to avoid and what to seek; second, the passions; third, actions; fourth, causes; fifth, proofs. But the causes of things really belong to the natural division, and the proofs to the rational. Aristo of Chios declared that the natural and the rational were not only superfluous but actually got in the way. Then he trimmed even the moral part, the only one he had left: he abolished the department that gives advice, insisting that advice was a tutor's business, not a philosopher's. As if the wise man were anything other than the tutor of the human race!

Since philosophy is threefold, then, let's begin by setting the moral side in order. It has been agreed that this, too, divides into three parts. First comes appraisal — the part that assigns each thing its proper function and weighs what each is worth — and in usefulness it ranks highest. For what is so indispensable as putting the right price on everything? The second part deals with impulse, the third with action. The first duty is to determine what things are worth; the second, to form toward them an impulse that is measured and orderly; the third, to make your impulse and your actions agree, so that in all these matters you are consistent with yourself. Let any one of the three go wrong, and the others fall into confusion with it. What good is it to have everything correctly appraised, each in its proper place, if your impulses run to excess? What good is it to have checked your impulses and to hold your desires in your own hands, if when the moment for action arrives you don't know the right times and seasons — don't know when, where, and how each thing should be done? It is one thing to understand the worth and merit of facts, another to know the precise moment for acting, and still another to curb your impulses and walk, rather than rush, toward what has to be done. So life is in harmony with itself only when action has not deserted impulse, and when impulse arises in each case from the worth of its object — languid or eager as the case may be, depending on whether the object is worth pursuing.

The natural side of philosophy is twofold: bodily things and bodiless things. Each divides into its own ranks of importance, so to speak. The topic of bodies deals first with these two ranks: what creates and what is created — and the created things are the elements. Now this very topic of the elements, some writers hold, is a single integral subject; others divide it into matter, the cause that moves all things, and the elements themselves.

It remains for me to divide rational philosophy into its parts. All speech is either continuous or split up between questioner and answerer. It has been agreed that the first should be called rhetoric and the second dialectic. Rhetoric deals with words, meanings, and arrangement. Dialectic divides into two: things said, and the words they are said in. Then comes a subdivision of each — and it is vast. So I shall stop at this point, and

  only treat the climax of the story;

because if I took a fancy to list all the subdivisions, this letter would turn into a debater's handbook. I'm not trying to discourage you from reading on the subject, excellent Lucilius — provided only that whatever you read, you promptly refer to conduct.

It's your conduct you must hold in check.
