Rouse what has gone slack in you, bind fast what has come loose, conquer what is stubborn, make war on your own appetites — and on mankind's — as hard as you can. And to those who say, "How long will this unending talk go on?" answer: "I ought to be asking you, 'How long will these unending sins of yours go on?'" Do you really want my remedies to quit before your vices do? I'll speak of them all the more — and precisely because you object, I'll keep on talking. Medicine begins to do good at the moment a touch makes the diseased body tingle with pain. I'll say things that help men even against their will. Now and then something other than compliments should reach your ears, and since as individuals you refuse to hear the truth, hear it all together.

How far will you extend the boundaries of your estates? An estate that once held a whole nation is too narrow for a single owner. How far will you push your plowed fields — you who won't confine even a single farm within the borders of a province? Noble rivers flow down through your private grounds; mighty streams that once marked the border between nations now run through your property from source to mouth. Even that is too little for you, unless your estates fence in whole seas, unless your steward holds sway on the far side of the Adriatic, the Ionian, and the Aegean, unless islands that were home to famous chieftains are counted among your most trifling possessions. Spread them as wide as you like; have as a "farm" what was once called a kingdom; make everything yours that you can — provided only that it's more than your neighbor has.

And now a word with you, whose luxury sprawls as wide as those men's greed. To you I say: "Will this go on until there is no lake your rooftop towers don't loom over? No river whose banks aren't lined with your lordly buildings? Wherever hot springs gush from the ground, you'll be raising new resorts of luxury. Wherever a shore bends into a bay, you'll be laying foundations on the spot — and, not content with any land you haven't manufactured yourselves, you'll bring the sea inside your boundaries. Let your rooftops flash in the sun on every side — perched on mountain peaks commanding wide views of sea and land, or hoisted from the plain to the height of mountains; build your endless structures, your enormous piles — you are still only individual men, and puny ones at that. What good are all your bedrooms? You sleep in one. No place is yours where you yourselves are not."

"Next I come to you, whose bottomless, insatiable gullet ransacks the seas on one side and the earth on the other, hunting down its prey with enormous toil — now with hook, now with snare, now with nets of every kind; no animal is left in peace unless you're already stuffed with it. And of those banquets so many hands prepare for you, how small a portion does your pleasure-jaded palate actually taste! Of all that game, so dangerous to take, how small a portion does the master's sick and squeamish stomach relish! Of all those shellfish, imported from so far away, how small a portion slips down that insatiable gullet! Poor wretches — don't you know that your appetites are bigger than your bellies?"

Talk this way to other men — provided that while you talk, you also listen. Write this way — provided that while you write, you read; and remember that everything you hear or read is to be applied to conduct, and to the calming of passion's fury. Study, not to add anything to your knowledge, but to make your knowledge better.

Farewell.

Letter 90

Letter 90: On the Part Played by Philosophy in the Progress of Man

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Who can doubt, my dear Lucilius, that life is the gift of the immortal gods, but that living well is the gift of philosophy? It would follow that we owe philosophy more than we owe the gods — by exactly as much as a good life outweighs mere life — if philosophy itself were not something the gods gave us. The knowledge of it they have given to no one; the capacity for it they have given to everyone. If they had made philosophy a blanket gift too — if we were all born understanding — then wisdom would have lost her finest quality: that she is not one of Fortune's handouts. As things stand, this is precisely what is precious and noble about her: she does not come out to meet us. Each man owes her to himself alone. We do not get her secondhand.

What would there be in philosophy worth respecting if she were passed out like charity? Her one job is to discover the truth about things divine and things human. Religion never leaves her side, nor duty, nor justice, nor the whole company of virtues that travel together, arm in arm. Philosophy has taught us to worship what is divine and to love what is human; she has told us that dominion belongs to the gods, and fellowship to men. And that fellowship stayed whole for a long time — until greed tore the community apart and became the cause of poverty even for the people it had made richest. For men stop possessing everything the moment they want everything for themselves.

But the first men, and the generations that sprang from them, were still unspoiled; they followed nature. They took one man as both their leader and their law, and put themselves in the hands of someone better than they were. Nature makes a habit of subjecting the weaker to the stronger. Even among the dumb animals, the biggest or the fiercest hold sway. It is no weakling bull that leads the herd; it is the one that has beaten every other male with muscle and might. Among elephants, the tallest walks first. Among men, the best man ranked highest. So the ruler was chosen for his mind — and that is why the happiest peoples were the ones where a man could not become more powerful without being better. A man can safely be given all the power he wants when he believes he can do nothing except what he ought to do.

So in that age we call the golden age, Posidonius holds that government was in the hands of the wise. They kept their own hands in check and protected the weak from the strong. They advised people what to do and what not to do; they showed what was useful and what was useless. Their foresight kept their people from lacking anything; their bravery kept dangers away; their generosity made their subjects prosperous and their lives handsome. Ruling was a service, not a throne. No ruler tested his power against the very people who had given him his power in the first place; and no one had the urge — or the excuse — to do wrong, because the ruler ruled well and the ruled obeyed well, and the sternest threat a king could make against a disobedient subject was banishment from the kingdom.

But once vice crept in and kingdoms turned into tyrannies, laws became necessary — and at first the wise framed those too. Solon, who set Athens on a foundation of just laws, was one of the seven men famous for their wisdom. If Lycurgus had lived in the same era, an eighth would have joined that hallowed number. The laws of Zaleucus and Charondas are still praised; and those two men learned the principles of justice they would establish in Sicily (flourishing, in those days) and through the Greek cities of Italy not in the forum, not in the offices of legal experts, but in the silent, holy retreat of Pythagoras.

Up to this point, I agree with Posidonius. But that philosophy discovered the crafts our daily life depends on — that I refuse to admit. I will not hand her a workman's diploma. Posidonius says: "When men were scattered across the earth, sheltering in caves, or in hollows dug out of cliffs, or in the trunks of hollow trees, it was philosophy that taught them to build houses." I say philosophy no more devised these clever stacked dwellings of ours — story piled on story, city crowding against city — than she invented the fish-ponds, walled in so that no man's gluttony has to take its chances with the weather, so that however wildly the sea is raging, luxury has its own safe harbors where it can fatten exotic breeds of fish. What! Did philosophy teach men the use of locks and keys? What was that but a hint dropped to greed? Was it philosophy that stacked up all these towering tenements, so dangerous to the people who live in them? Wasn't it enough for a man to roof himself with whatever came to hand, and find himself some natural shelter without engineering and without fuss? Believe me, that was a happy age — before there were architects, before there were builders. All of that was born when luxury was being born: this business of cutting timber square, of sawing a beam with a practiced hand along the marked-out line.

  The earliest men split wood with wedges.

They were not framing a roof for a future banquet-hall; they did not haul pines and firs down trembling streets in long trains of wagons just to hang paneled ceilings, heavy with gold, from the rafters. Two forked poles, one at either end, propped up the house. Packed branches and leaves, heaped and laid on a slope, let even the heaviest rain run off. Beneath such roofs they lived — but they lived in peace. A thatched roof once covered free men; under marble and gold lives slavery.

I part ways with Posidonius on another point too: he holds that the wise invented our mechanical tools. On that logic you might as well say the wise were the men who taught

  The setting of the snare for game, the liming
  Of twigs for birds, the circling of great woods with dogs.

It was man's ingenuity that discovered all these tricks, not his wisdom. And I disagree with him again when he says it was wise men who discovered our mines of iron and copper — "when the earth, scorched by forest fires, melted the shallow veins of ore and the metal gushed out." No: the sort of men who discover such things are the sort of men who work at them. Nor does the question strike me as subtle as it strikes Posidonius — whether the hammer or the tongs came first into use. Both were invented by some man whose mind was nimble and sharp, but not great and elevated. And the same goes for anything else that can only be found by a man whose back is bent and whose eyes are on the ground.

The wise man lived simply. Of course he did — even in our own day he would prefer to travel as light as possible. How, I ask you, can you admire both Diogenes and Daedalus? Which of the two looks like a wise man to you: the one who invented the saw? Or the one who saw a boy drinking water out of his cupped hand, immediately pulled the cup from his knapsack and smashed it, scolding himself — "What a fool I am, hauling around superfluous baggage all this time!" — and then curled up in his tub and went to sleep? And in our own times, which man do you call wiser: the one who invents a system for spraying saffron perfume to a tremendous height from hidden pipes, who fills and empties canals in a single rush of water, who builds a dining room with a ceiling of sliding panels that shows one pattern after another, a new roof for every course of the meal — or the one who proves, to himself and to everyone else, that nature has laid down no harsh or difficult law? That we can live without the marble-cutter and the engineer; that we can clothe ourselves without a silk trade; that we can have everything we actually need if we will only be content with what lies on the earth's surface? If mankind were willing to listen to this sage, they would learn that the cook is as superfluous as the soldier. The men who made caring for the body a simple problem were wise men, or something like them. What is indispensable takes no elaborate effort to get; it is the luxuries that demand the labor. Follow nature and you will need no craftsmen.

Nature did not want us harassed. Whatever she required of us, she equipped us for. "But the naked body can't endure cold." Well? Aren't there the hides of wild beasts and other animals, which can keep off the cold well enough and more than enough? Don't plenty of tribes cover themselves with the bark of trees? Aren't birds' feathers sewn together to serve as clothing? To this very day, doesn't a large part of the Scythian people dress in the skins of foxes and mice — soft to the touch, and windproof? "Even so, men need something thicker than skin to keep off the summer sun." Well? Hasn't time itself hollowed out plenty of retreats — caverns opened up by decay or by some accident or other? Well? Didn't the very first men weave twigs into wicker mats by hand, plaster them with common mud, roof them over with straw and wild grasses, and pass their winters snug and dry, the rain sliding off the sloping gables? Well? Don't the peoples along the edge of the Syrtes live in dug-out houses — along with every other tribe whose sun burns too fiercely for any shelter to keep out the heat except the parched earth itself?

Nature was not so spiteful that, having given every other animal an easy time of it, she made man alone unable to live without all this apparatus. None of it was imposed on us by her; none of it had to be painfully invented to keep us alive. Everything was ready for us at birth. It is we who have made everything difficult for ourselves by turning up our noses at what is easy. Housing, shelter, comfort, food — all the things that have now become a colossal industry of trouble — were ready to hand, free to all, obtainable for trivial effort. In those days the limit of everything matched the need. It is we who have made these things expensive, we who have made them marvelous, we who have made them the objects of elaborate, endlessly multiplying arts of pursuit. Nature is sufficient for everything she asks of us. Luxury has turned her back on nature; every day she swells herself further, gathering strength through all the centuries, her cleverness in the service of the vices. First luxury began craving what was superfluous; then what was contrary to nature; and finally she sold the soul to the body and ordered it to be the utter slave of the body's appetites. All these trades that keep the city bustling — or should I say, in an uproar — are doing the body's business. There was a time when everything was supplied to the body as to a slave; now everything is procured for it as for a master. So we get the workshops of the weavers and the carpenters; so we get the smells of the professional kitchens; so we get the dancing-masters with their suggestive poses, and the suggestive, affected singing. The moderation nature prescribes — desires limited to means, and means limited to needs — has left the field. It has now come to this: wanting only what is enough is taken as proof that you are a bumpkin, or a beggar.

It is hard to believe, my dear Lucilius, how easily the charm of eloquence can lure even great men away from the truth. Look at Posidonius — in my judgment one of the men who have given the most to philosophy — when he sets out to describe the art of weaving. He tells how some threads are twisted and others drawn out from the soft, loose mass of wool; how the upright warp holds its threads taut with hanging weights; how the inserted thread of the woof, which softens the stiff texture of the web gripping it on either side, is driven home by the batten and pressed into a tight union with the warp. And he maintains that even the weaver's art was discovered by wise men — forgetting that the elaborate loom he is describing was invented much later, the art in which

  The web is bound to the frame; the reed divides
  The warp; between the threads the pointed shuttles
  Carry the woof, and the broad comb's notched teeth
  Drive it home.

What if he had lived to see the weaving of our own day, which produces clothing that conceals nothing — clothing that offers no protection, I won't say to the body, but even to modesty!

Posidonius then moves on to the farmer. With just as much eloquence he describes the ground broken up by the plough and crossed again, so that the loosened earth gives the roots freer play; then the seed sown, and the weeds pulled by hand so that no stray growth or wild plant can spoil the crop. This trade too, he declares, is the creation of the wise — as if farmers were not discovering countless new ways to improve the soil's yield at this very moment! And not content with these arts, he actually demotes the wise man to the mill. For he tells us how the sage began making bread by copying nature's own processes. "The grain," he says, "once taken into the mouth, is crushed by the hard teeth meeting in collision, and whatever escapes them the tongue pushes back to those same teeth. Then it is blended into a mass, so it can slide more easily down the throat. When it reaches the stomach, it is cooked by the stomach's even heat, and then — only then — is it taken up into the body. Following this model," he goes on, "someone set two rough stones one on top of the other, in imitation of the teeth: one set fixed, waiting on the motion of the other. Then by rubbing stone against stone the grain is crushed, and brought back again and again, until repeated grinding reduces it to flour. Then this man sprinkled the flour with water, worked it and kneaded it into obedience, and shaped the loaf. At first it was baked in hot ashes, or in a red-hot earthen vessel; later, ovens were gradually discovered, and the other devices whose heat obeys the sage's will." One step more and Posidonius would have declared that the cobbler's trade was the discovery of the wise man.

All these things were devised by reason — but not by right reason. They were invented by man, but not by the wise man. The same goes for ships, in which we cross rivers and seas — fitted with sails to catch the force of the wind, with rudders hung at the stern to swing the vessel's course this way or that. The model was the fish, which steers itself with its tail and by the slightest flick to one side or the other bends its darting course. "But," says Posidonius, "the wise man did discover all these things; they were just too petty for him to handle himself, so he handed them over to his humbler assistants." No. These early inventions were thought up by exactly the class of men who run them today. We know that certain devices have appeared within living memory — windows of transparent tile that let in clear light; vaulted baths with pipes set into the walls to spread the heat and keep the temperature even from floor to ceiling. Need I mention the marble that makes our temples and our private houses gleam? Or the huge rounded and polished blocks of stone we use to raise colonnades and halls roomy enough for nations? Or our signs for whole words, which let us take down a speech however fast it is delivered, the hand keeping pace with the tongue? All of this has been devised by the lowest grade of slaves. Wisdom sits higher up. She does not train the hands; she is the teacher of minds.

Would you like to know what wisdom has actually brought to light — what she has accomplished? Not the body's graceful poses. Not the varied notes of horn and flute, where the breath goes in and comes out transformed into voice. Not weapons, not walls, not the machinery of war — her voice is for peace, and she calls all mankind to live in concord. She is not, I insist, the manufacturer of our everyday tools, however indispensable. Why assign her such small things? What you see in her is the master craftsman of life itself. The other arts do fall under her authority, it's true — for the servant of life is also served by the things that equip life. But wisdom's course is set toward the happy life. That is where she leads us; that is the road she opens for us. She shows us what things are evil and what things only look evil; she strips the mind of empty illusion. She gives us a greatness that is solid, and deflates the greatness that is puffed up, all show and hollowness; and she never lets us stay confused about the difference between what is great and what is merely swollen. She delivers to us the knowledge of the whole of nature, and of her own nature. She reveals what the gods are, and of what sort; what the gods below are, and the household deities, and the guardian spirits; what those souls are that have won lasting life and been admitted into the second rank of divinities; where they dwell, and what they do, and what they can do, and what they will.

These are wisdom's rites of initiation — and what they unlock is not some village shrine but the vast temple of all the gods, the universe itself, whose true images and true faces she offers to the gaze of our minds; for the eyes are too dull for sights that great. Then she goes back to the beginnings of things: to the eternal Reason poured into the whole, and to the force alive in every seed, shaping each thing after its kind. Then wisdom begins asking about the soul — where it comes from, where it lives, how long it lasts, into how many parts it falls. Finally she has turned from the bodily to the bodiless, and examined truth up close, and the marks by which truth is known — asking next how the ambiguous can be told apart from the true, in life and in language alike; for in both, the false is mingled with the true.

My own view is that the wise man did not withdraw from those arts, as Posidonius thinks. He never took them up in the first place. For he would have judged nothing worth inventing that he would not afterward judge worth using always. He would not pick up what he would have to put down.

"But Anacharsis," says Posidonius, "invented the potter's wheel, whose spinning gives shape to vessels." And then, because the potter's wheel turns up in Homer, people would rather believe the verses are spurious than that Posidonius's story is! I maintain Anacharsis was not the inventor of that wheel — and if he was, then a wise man invented it, but not as a wise man; just as there are plenty of things wise men do as men, not as wise men. Suppose a wise man happens to be exceptionally fast on his feet: he will beat every runner in the race because he is fast, not because he is wise. I would love to show Posidonius a glass-blower, whose breath moulds glass into shapes the most skillful hand could scarcely form. These discoveries have been made since we stopped discovering wisdom.

Posidonius again: "Democritus is said to have discovered the arch, in which a curved line of stones, leaning gradually inward, is locked together by the keystone." I am inclined to call this false. There must have been bridges and gateways before Democritus, curved toward the top. And it seems to have quite slipped your memory that this same Democritus discovered how to soften ivory, and how a pebble could be boiled into an emerald — the very process still used today for coloring stones that take the treatment. A wise man may well have discovered such things; but he did not discover them because he was wise. He does plenty of things that we see done just as well — or more deftly and skillfully — by men with no wisdom whatsoever.

You ask, then, what the wise man has actually tracked down, what he has brought into the light? First, truth, and nature — which he has not followed the way the other animals do, with eyes too dull to see the divine in it. Second, the law of life: he has made life conform to universal principles; he has taught us not merely to know the gods but to follow them, and to welcome whatever chance brings exactly as if it were a divine command. He has forbidden us to listen to false opinions, and has weighed the worth of everything on a true scale. He has condemned the pleasures that come mixed with remorse, and praised the goods that will always satisfy; and he has published this truth to the world: the happiest man is the man who needs no happiness, and the most powerful man is the man who has power over himself.

I am not talking about the philosophy that has set the citizen outside his country and the gods outside the universe, and handed virtue over to pleasure — but about the philosophy that counts nothing good except what is honorable; the philosophy that cannot be sweet-talked by the gifts of men or of Fortune; whose price is that she cannot be bought at any price. That this philosophy existed in that rough age, when the arts and crafts were still unknown and useful things could only be learned by using them — that I refuse to believe.

Next came the fortune-favored era when nature's bounty lay open to everyone, for everyone's use alike, before greed and luxury broke the bonds that held mortals together and men abandoned their common life, scattered, and turned to plunder. The men of that second age were not wise men, even if they did what wise men should do. There is no other condition of the human race anyone would rate more highly; and if God commissioned a man to fashion earthly creatures and hand institutions to peoples, that man would approve no other system than the one that held among the men of that age, when

  No ploughman worked the soil; it was not lawful
  To mark off land or fence one's property.
  Men pooled their gains, and the earth gave her riches
  More freely to sons who never asked for them.

Was any race of men ever more blessed than that one? They enjoyed all of nature in partnership. She was sufficient for them all, guardian now as she had been parent before; and her gift was this — the secure possession, by every man, of the common resources. Why shouldn't I call that race the richest in human history, since you could not have found a poor man in it?

But greed broke in on this happy arrangement, and in its eagerness to set something aside for its own private use, it made everything the property of somebody else, and shrank itself from boundless wealth down to cramped need. It was greed that introduced poverty: by craving much, it lost all. So now she tries to recover her losses; she adds estate to estate, buying out one neighbor and cheating out the next; she swells her country places to the size of provinces and calls it "ownership" when a tour of your own property counts as extensive travel — and for all that, no pushing back of boundary lines will ever bring us back to where we started.

When we have done everything that can be done, we shall possess much. We used to possess the whole world. The earth itself was more productive untilled — generous enough for peoples who did not rob one another. Whatever nature produced, a man took as much delight in showing it to someone else as in finding it. No one could have more than anyone else, and no one less; whatever there was, was divided among friends who did not quarrel. The stronger had not yet begun laying hands on the weaker; the miser had not yet begun hiding away what lay in front of him, shutting his neighbor off from the bare necessities. Each man cared as much for his neighbor as for himself. Weapons lay unused; hands unstained by human blood turned all their hostility against the wild beasts. Those men, sheltered from the sun by a thick grove, safe from the bite of winter and rain in their humble hideaways, spent their days under the branches and passed untroubled nights without a sigh. We toss with worry in our purple sheets — it prods us out of bed with the sharpest of goads; but how soft was the sleep the hard earth gave those men! No carved and paneled ceilings hung over them. They lay out under the open sky, and the stars glided overhead, and the great pageant of the night — the firmament — swept silently past, doing its mighty work without a sound. By day and by night alike, the views of that most glorious dwelling-place stood open and free. It was their joy to watch the constellations sinking down from mid-heaven, and others rising up out of their hidden places. What could it be but joy, to wander among marvels scattered so far and wide? But you people of today tremble at every sound your houses make; if anything creaks while you sit among your frescoes, you flinch and run. They had no houses the size of cities. The open air; the breezes blowing free across open ground; the drifting shade of a crag or a tree; springs crystal-clear, and streams not spoiled by man's meddling — no water-pipes, no forced channels, just water running where it liked; and meadows beautiful without a landscaper — this was the setting of their rough homes, trimmed by rustic hands. That was a dwelling in accordance with nature. It was a joy to live in it, afraid neither of the house nor for it. Today our houses make up a large share of our dread.

But however splendid and guileless the life of those men was, they were not wise men; that title is reserved for the highest achievement of all. Still, I would not deny they were men of lofty spirit — fresh from the gods, if I may put it that way. There is no doubt the world turned out better offspring before it was worn out. But though their native powers were sturdier than ours across the board, and better built for hard work, those powers had not been perfected in every case. For nature does not hand out virtue; becoming good is an art. They, at least, did not go rummaging through the lowest dregs of the earth for gold or silver or translucent gemstones; and they were still merciful even to the dumb animals — that age stood so far from the custom of man killing man not in anger, not in fear, but for the pleasure of the spectacle. They had no embroidered clothing yet; cloth was not yet woven with gold; gold was not yet even mined.

What is the upshot, then? Those men were innocent because they were ignorant — and it makes a very great difference whether a man refuses to sin or simply does not know how. They had no justice, no prudence, no self-control, no bravery. Their rough life had certain qualities that resembled all these virtues; but virtue itself comes only to a soul that has been trained and taught and brought to its full growth by unrelenting practice. We are born for virtue, but not with it. And even in the best of men, until you refine them by instruction, there is only the raw material of virtue — not virtue itself.

Farewell.

Letter 91

Letter 91: On the Lesson to Be Drawn from the Burning of Lyons

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Our friend Liberalis is heartbroken. He has just gotten the news of the fire that wiped out the colony of Lyons. A disaster like that would shake anyone, let alone a man who loves his home city as dearly as he does. And the blow has forced him to ask how strong his own character really is — a character he trained, I suppose, against the troubles he thought he had reason to fear. But this trouble was so unexpected, so nearly unheard of, that I'm not surprised it caught him without his guard up. There was no precedent for it. Fire has damaged plenty of cities; it has annihilated none. Even when an enemy hurls fire against the walls, the flames gutter out in a hundred places, and however often they are rekindled, they rarely devour so completely that nothing is left for the sword to finish. Even earthquakes have hardly ever been violent enough to flatten whole cities. And no blaze anywhere has ever raged so fiercely that a second fire would have found nothing left to burn. Yet here, so many beautiful buildings — any one of which would have made a town famous by itself — came down in a single night. In the deepest peace, something happened worse than anyone fears in wartime. Who can believe it? With weapons at rest everywhere, with the whole world at peace, Lyons — the pride of Gaul — is missing.

Fortune has usually given people warning when she meant to strike them all at once; she has let them feel the disaster coming. Every great thing has been granted some grace period before its fall. But here, a single night lay between a very great city and none. In short: it takes me longer to tell you the city perished than it took the city to perish.

All of this has bent even Liberalis' will, which stands so straight and steady against his own troubles. And there is a reason he was shaken: it is the unexpected that lands with the most weight. Strangeness adds heaviness to disaster; every mortal hurts more from a blow that also surprises him.

So nothing ought to be unexpected by us. Our minds should be sent out ahead to meet every problem, and we should consider not what usually happens, but what can happen. For what is there that Fortune, when she chooses, cannot drag down from the very peak of its prosperity? What is there that she doesn't attack all the more violently the more brilliantly it shines? What is hard or laborious for her? She doesn't always come at us the same way, or even at full strength. Sometimes she turns our own hands against us; sometimes, content with her own powers, she invents dangers that need no human agent. No moment is exempt. In the middle of our pleasures, the causes of our suffering are already sprouting. War breaks out in the middle of peace, and the very things we counted on to protect us become the things we fear; the friend turns enemy, the ally turns hostile. The summer calm whips itself into sudden storms wilder than winter's. With no enemy in sight, we suffer what enemies inflict — and if every other cause of disaster fails, too much good fortune will invent one on its own. Illness attacks the most temperate, wasting disease the strongest, punishment the most innocent, and the noisy mob the most secluded.

Fortune picks out some new weapon to remind us of her strength whenever she thinks we've forgotten her. Whatever has been built up by a long line of years, at the cost of enormous labor and by the great kindness of the gods, one single day scatters and destroys. No — whoever said "a day" has granted disaster too generous a schedule. An hour, an instant, is enough to overthrow an empire. It would be some consolation for our frailty, and the frailty of everything we make, if things perished as slowly as they came into being. But as it is, growth is sluggish and ruin is quick. Nothing is stable, public or private. The destinies of men, and of cities too, are spinning. In the middle of the greatest calm, terror rises; with nothing outside to stir up trouble, disasters burst out of the places we least expected. Thrones that stood firm through civil wars and foreign wars crash down with no one pushing them. How few states have carried their good fortune all the way to the end!

We should therefore think through every possibility, and fortify our minds against whatever evils may come. Exile, the torture of disease, war, shipwreck — rehearse them all. Fortune may tear you from your country, or your country from you; she may banish you to a wasteland — and this very place, where the crowds are suffocating, may become a wasteland itself. Let us set the whole of the human lot before our eyes. If we don't want to be overwhelmed — or even stunned — by unusual disasters, as though they were something new, then let us picture in advance not just the worst that commonly happens, but the very worst that can happen. We must think Fortune through fully and completely.

How often have cities in Asia, how often in Achaia, been leveled by a single shock of earthquake! How many towns in Syria, how many in Macedonia, have been swallowed whole! How often has that kind of devastation left Cyprus in ruins! How often has Paphos collapsed! Word reaches us constantly of entire cities utterly destroyed — and how small a part of the world are we, to whom such news comes so often!

So let us stand up to face whatever Fortune does, and whatever happens, let us be sure of this: it is never as great as rumor advertises. A rich city has been burned to ashes — the jewel of the provinces, counted among them and yet standing apart from them; but rich as it was, it sat on a single hill, and not a very large one. And of all those cities whose magnificence and grandeur you hear about today, time will erase even the traces. Don't you see how, in Achaia, the foundations of the most famous cities have already crumbled away, until nothing is left to show they ever existed? It's not only what human hands have made that totters to the ground; not only what human art and effort set in place that the passing days overthrow. Mountain peaks dissolve. Whole regions have subsided. Places that once stood far out of sight of the sea now lie under the waves. Fire's enormous power has eaten away the hills it once glowed through, and gnawed down to nothing the loftiest summits — once the sailor's comfort and his beacon. The works of nature herself take this battering; so we ought to bear the destruction of cities with untroubled minds. They stand only to fall. That end waits for every one of them. Maybe some force pent up inside, some blast of air made tremendous because its way is blocked, will throw off the weight that holds it down; maybe a whirlpool of raging currents, mightier for being hidden in the depths of the earth, will smash through whatever resists it; maybe the violence of fire will burst the framework of the earth's crust apart; maybe time, from which nothing is safe, will wear them down little by little; maybe an unhealthy climate will drive the inhabitants out and mold will eat the deserted walls. It would be tedious to count all the roads fate can take. But this one thing I know: everything mortals make is doomed to be mortal — and we live in the middle of things that are destined to die.

These are the thoughts, and thoughts like them, that I am offering as consolation to our friend Liberalis, who burns with an almost unbelievable love for his city. Perhaps it was destroyed only so it could be raised again to something better. Often a setback has simply cleared the ground for greater prosperity. Many buildings have fallen only to rise higher. Timagenes, who held a grudge against Rome and her success, used to say the only thing that grieved him about fires in Rome was knowing that better buildings would rise than the ones the flames had taken. In Lyons, too, most likely, every citizen will work to rebuild everything larger and safer than what was lost. May it be built to last — built, under happier omens, for a longer life! This colony is only in its hundredth year — not even the limit of a single human lifetime. Planted by Plancus, its founder, and favored by its natural site, it grew strong and swelled to the population it holds today. And yet think how many disasters, and how severe, it has endured within the span of one old man's life!

So let the mind be trained to understand its lot and to endure it. Let it know that there is nothing Fortune does not dare — that she has the same jurisdiction over empires as over emperors, the same power over cities as over the citizens who live in them. We must not cry out against any of this. We have entered into such a world; these are the laws we live under. If the terms suit you, obey; if not, leave by whatever door you please. Cry out in anger if some unfair rule is aimed at you personally; but if this inescapable law binds the highest and the lowest alike, then make your peace with fate, which dissolves all things. You should not measure us by our burial mounds, or by the monuments of unequal size that line the road; their ashes level all men. We are unequal at birth, but we are equal in death. And what I say about cities I say about the people in them: Ardea, the little town, was captured — and so was Rome. The great founder of human law made no distinctions among us for high lineage or famous names, except while we live. When we come to the end that waits for all mortals, he says: "Away with ambition! Let one and the same law apply to every creature that burdens the earth." For endurance, we are all equal: no one is more fragile than anyone else, and no one is more certain of being alive tomorrow.

Alexander, king of Macedon, took up the study of geometry. Unhappy man — because it would teach him how tiny the earth really was, this earth of which he had seized only a fraction. Unhappy man, I say again, because he was bound to discover that his title was a lie. Who can be "the Great" in something so small? The lessons were intricate and could only be mastered by careful attention — not the sort of thing a madman could grasp, a man whose thoughts went ranging out beyond the ocean. "Teach me something easy!" he cries. And his teacher answers: "These things are the same for everyone — just as hard for one as for another." Imagine that nature is saying the same to us: "The things you complain of are the same for all. I can make nothing easier for any man. But whoever wishes can make things easier for himself." How? Through an even mind. You will have to feel pain, and thirst, and hunger, and old age too — if a longer stay among men is granted you. You will have to be sick, and lose things, and die. But don't believe the noisy crowd around you: none of these things is an evil, none is beyond your strength to bear, none is truly heavy. It's only common opinion that makes them frightening. Fearing death, then, is like fearing gossip — and what is more foolish than a man afraid of words? Our friend Demetrius puts it cleverly: "The talk of ignorant men is like the rumblings of the belly. What difference does it make to me," he says, "whether the noise comes from above or below?" What madness, to fear a bad reputation among people whose reputations are bad! Just as you had no cause to be terrified of what people say, you have no cause now to fear these things — things you would never have feared if their talk hadn't forced the fear on you. Does it harm a good man to be spattered with unjust gossip? Then don't let gossip damage death in our estimation either — for death has a bad reputation too. Yet not one of the people who slander death has ever tried it.

Meanwhile it is reckless to condemn what you know nothing about. This much, though, you do know: death is a help to many people. It sets many free from torture, from want, from sickness, from suffering, from weariness. Nothing has any power over us, once we have death in our own power.

Farewell.

Letter 92

Letter 92: On the Happy Life

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You and I will agree, I think, on the following: that outward things are sought for the body's sake, that the body is cared for out of regard for the soul, and that the soul itself has service departments — the parts that let us move and stay alive — which exist only for the sake of the ruling part. That ruling part contains something irrational and something rational. The irrational part obeys; the rational part is the one thing that takes orders from nothing else and refers everything else to itself. Divine reason, after all, is set in supreme command over the universe and answers to nothing; and the reason we carry around is the same stuff, because it is derived from the divine.

If we agree on that, we must agree on what follows: the happy life depends on one thing and one thing only — our attainment of perfect reason. Reason alone keeps the soul from bowing down; reason alone stands its ground against Fortune. Whatever state a man's affairs are in, it keeps him untroubled. And the only real good is the one that can never be damaged. The happy man, I declare, is the one whom nothing can make less than he is. He holds the heights leaning on no one but himself — for whoever props himself up on anything can fall. If it's otherwise, then things that are not us begin to hold great power over us. And who wants Fortune holding the upper hand? What sensible man preens himself on what isn't his own?

What is the happy life? Peace of mind, and a tranquility that is unbroken and continuous. You will have it if you have greatness of soul; you will have it if you have the steadiness to hold onto a good judgment once you have reached it. How does a man get there? By seeing the truth whole; by keeping, in everything he does, order, measure, and fitness; by having a will that is kindly and gives no offense, that stays fixed on reason and never leaves it, that earns love and admiration at the same time. To give you the whole principle in one sentence: the wise man's soul should be the sort of soul that would suit a god.

What more can a man want who already has everything honorable? For if dishonorable things can contribute to the best condition, then a happy life becomes possible without an honorable one. And what is more debased, or more foolish, than tying the good of a rational soul to irrational things? Yet certain philosophers hold that the Supreme Good can be increased, on the grounds that it is hardly complete when the gifts of Fortune run against it. Even Antipater, one of the great leaders of that school, admits he gives some weight to externals — though only a very little. But you see the absurdity: it is adding a lamp to broad daylight. What can a spark matter in the blaze of the sun?

If honor alone doesn't content you, then you must want to add either the calm the Greeks call "undisturbedness," or else pleasure. The first can be had in any case: the mind is free of disturbance when it is fully free to contemplate the universe, and nothing pulls it away from the study of nature. The second, pleasure, is simply the good of cattle. To honor it is to bolt the irrational onto the rational, the shameful onto the honorable. A pleasant tingle in the body affects this life of ours — so why hesitate to say that all is well with a man because all is well with his appetite? And would you count — I won't say among heroes, but among men — a person whose Supreme Good is a matter of flavors and colors and sounds? Let him fall out of the ranks of the noblest class of living beings, second only to the gods. Let him go herd with the dumb beasts — an animal whose delight is fodder.

The irrational part of the soul is itself double. One part is spirited, ambitious, headstrong; it lives in the passions. The other is lowly, sluggish, and sold out to pleasure. Philosophers have neglected the first — which, unbridled as it is, is still the better of the two, certainly braver and more worthy of a man — and have declared the second, the nerveless and ignoble one, indispensable to the happy life. They have ordered reason to wait on it. They have made the Supreme Good of the noblest of living beings a mean, abject thing — and a monster besides, a hybrid stitched together from parts that don't match. As our Virgil says, describing Scylla:

  Above, a human face and a maiden's breast —
  a lovely breast — below, a shapeless monster,
  huge in bulk, a dolphin's tail
  joined to a belly like a wolf's.

And yet Scylla is at least assembled from wild animals — dreadful ones, swift ones. From what grotesque shapes have these wiseacres pieced together wisdom! Man's primary art is virtue itself; joined to it is the useless, short-lived flesh, fit only — as Posidonius remarks — for taking in food. That divine virtue ends in filth; onto its higher parts, which are holy and heavenly, they fasten a sluggish, flabby animal. As for the second item on the wish list — quiet — although by itself it would do the soul no actual good, it would at least clear hindrances out of the soul's way. Pleasure, on the other hand, actively destroys the soul and softens all its strength. Where will you find elements so mismatched yoked together? To the most vigorous thing is joined the most sluggish; to the austere, the frivolous; to the most holy, the unrestrained — unrestrained to the point of impurity.

"What, then," comes the retort, "if good health, rest, and freedom from pain won't hinder virtue — shouldn't you seek them?" Of course I'll seek them. Not because they are goods, but because they are according to nature, and because I will acquire them through the exercise of good judgment on my part. What will be good in them, then? Only this: that choosing them well is a good thing. When I put on suitable clothes, or walk as I should, or dine as I ought to dine, the dinner and the walk and the clothes are not goods; the good is the deliberate choice I show in them, keeping in each thing I do a measure that squares with reason. Let me add this too: choosing clean clothing is a fitting aim for a man's efforts, because man is by nature a clean and well-groomed animal. So the choice of neat clothes is a good, not the neat clothes themselves — the good is not in the thing selected, but in the quality of the selecting. It's our actions that are honorable, not the objects they handle.

And what I've said about clothing, you may apply to the body. Nature has wrapped the soul in the body as in a kind of garment; the body is the soul's cloak. But who ever priced clothes by the wardrobe that held them? A scabbard makes a sword neither good nor bad. So about the body I give you the same answer: if I have the choice, I'll choose health and strength — but the good involved will be my judgment about them, not the things themselves.

Another retort: "Granted, the wise man is happy. Still, he doesn't attain the Supreme Good as we've defined it unless the means nature provides for reaching it are at his disposal. A man who has virtue can't be unhappy; but he can't be perfectly happy either if he lacks natural gifts like health or sound limbs." In saying this, you grant the claim that seems harder to believe — that a man in unremitting, extreme pain is not wretched, is even happy — and you deny the far less serious one, that he is completely happy. But if virtue can keep a man from being wretched, it will find it easier still to make him completely happy; for the gap between happy and completely happy is smaller than the gap between wretched and happy. Can a power strong enough to snatch a man out of disaster and set him among the happy fail to finish the job and make him supremely happy? Does its strength give out at the very top of the climb?

Life contains advantages and disadvantages — both beyond our control. If a good man is not wretched though weighed down with every kind of disadvantage, how is he not supremely happy just because he lacks a few advantages? As the load of disadvantages does not drag him down to wretchedness, so the lack of advantages does not pull him back from supreme happiness. He is just as supremely happy without the advantages as he is free of wretchedness under the load of disadvantages. Otherwise, if his good can be damaged, it can be taken from him altogether.

A little way back I said that a tiny fire adds nothing to the sun's light. The sun's brightness blots out any light that shines on its own beside it. "But," someone says, "certain objects block even sunlight." The sun is unimpaired even among obstacles: an object may come between and cut off our view of it, but the sun sticks to its work and keeps its course. Whenever it shines out from behind the clouds, it is no smaller — and no less punctual either — than when the sky is clear; because there is a great difference between something merely standing in the way of its light and something interfering with its shining. In the same way, obstacles take nothing away from virtue. It is no smaller; it merely shines with less brilliance. To our eyes it may be dimmer and harder to see; in itself it is the same and, like the sun in eclipse, is still exerting its power in secret. Disasters, losses, and wrongs have only as much power over virtue as a cloud has over the sun.

Then we meet someone who maintains that a wise man who has suffered bodily misfortune is neither wretched nor happy. He too is wrong: he is putting the results of chance on a level with the virtues, and giving the honorable no more weight than things with no honor in them. And what is more detestable, more unworthy, than filing contemptible things in the same class as things that deserve reverence? Reverence belongs to justice, duty, loyalty, courage, and prudence. The other list is worthless — a sturdy leg, strong shoulders, good teeth, firm healthy muscles — attributes the most worthless men are often blessed with in fuller measure. Again, if the wise man whose body torments him is to be rated neither wretched nor happy, but parked in some halfway position, then his life will also be neither desirable nor undesirable. But what is as foolish as calling the wise man's life not desirable? And what is further past believing than a life that is neither desirable nor undesirable? Again: if bodily ills don't make a man wretched, then they leave him free to be happy; for things that have no power to change his condition for the worse have no power to disturb that condition at its best either.

"But," someone will say, "we know what cold is and what hot is; lukewarm lies between. In the same way, A is happy, B is wretched, and C is neither happy nor wretched." Let's examine this figure they deploy against us. If I add more cold water to your lukewarm water, it ends up cold; if I pour in more hot, it finally turns hot. But with your man who is neither wretched nor happy, no matter how many troubles I add, by your own argument he does not become unhappy — so your figure offers no analogy. Now, set before me this man of yours, neither miserable nor happy. I add blindness to his misfortunes: he is not made unhappy. I cripple him: not made unhappy. I pile on afflictions, severe and unceasing: not made unhappy. Well then — a man whose life all these ills cannot turn to misery cannot be dragged by them out of a happy life either. And if, as you admit, the wise man cannot fall from happiness into wretchedness, he cannot fall into non-happiness at all. Once you've started to slip, where exactly do you stop? Whatever keeps a man from rolling to the bottom keeps him at the summit. Why, you ask, can't the happy life possibly be destroyed? It can't even be loosened at the joints. And that is why virtue, all by itself, is sufficient for the happy life.

"But isn't the wise man happier if he has lived longer, with no pain to distract him, than one who has had to wrestle bad Fortune all his days?" Answer me this: is he any better, any more honorable? If not, then he isn't happier either. To live more happily, he must live more rightly; if he can't do that, he can't live more happily either. Virtue cannot be stretched any tighter, and so neither can the happy life, which depends on virtue. Virtue is so great a good that it doesn't feel petty assaults like shortness of life, pain, and the body's assorted complaints. As for pleasure — it doesn't deserve so much as a glance from virtue.

Now, what is the chief thing in virtue? That it needs not a single day beyond the present, and keeps no running count of the days that are ours. In the smallest sliver of time, virtue completes an eternity of good. These goods sound incredible to us, beyond human nature — because we measure virtue's grandeur by the yardstick of our own weakness, and we give our vices the name of virtue. But doesn't it sound just as incredible that a man in extreme suffering should say, "I am happy"? Yet that very sentence was heard in the factory of pleasure itself. "Today, and one other day," said Epicurus, "have been the happiest of my life" — and this though on the one day he was tortured by a blocked bladder, and on the other by the incurable pain of an ulcerated stomach; and yet, that day was none the less happy. Why, then, should the goods that virtue bestows be incredible to us, who cultivate virtue, when they turn up even among people who take pleasure for their mistress? They too, base and small-minded as they are, declare that even in extreme pain and misfortune the wise man will be neither wretched nor happy. But that is incredible too — more incredible, in fact, than the other claim. I cannot see how virtue, once knocked from her heights, can help being flung all the way to the bottom. Either she must keep a man happy, or, once driven from that post, she cannot stop him from becoming unhappy. As long as virtue stands her ground, she cannot be driven from the field. She must either conquer or be conquered.

But some say: "Virtue and the happy life belong to the immortal gods alone; we can grasp only the shadow, so to speak, and semblance of goods like theirs. We approach them; we never arrive." Yet reason is common to gods and men. In them it is already perfected; in us it is capable of being perfected. What drives us to despair is our vices. Man, the second class of rational being, is of a lower order — a guardian, so to speak, too unstable to hold fast to what is best, his judgment still wavering and unsure. He may need eyes and ears, good health, an outward appearance that isn't repellent, and, beyond that, a longer span of days with his constitution intact. Through reason he can lead a life he won't regret; yet there also lives in this imperfect creature, man, a certain force that makes for badness, because he has a mind easily tipped toward the perverse. Suppose the badness that is out in the open, already stirred into action, were removed — the man is still not a good man, but he is being molded toward goodness. And whoever is missing any quality that makes for goodness is bad.

But the man

  in whose body virtue dwells, and spirit
  ever present,

is the equal of the gods. Mindful of where he came from, he strives to return there. No man does wrong in attempting to climb back to the heights he once came down from. And why shouldn't you believe that something divine exists in one who is a part of God? This whole universe that surrounds us is one thing, and it is God; we are God's associates, God's limbs. Our soul has the capacity, and it is carried up to that place, if vices do not hold it down. Just as our bodies are built by nature to stand erect and look up at the sky, so the soul — which can reach out as far as it will — was framed by nature for this very end: to desire equality with the gods. And if it uses its powers and stretches upward into its own proper region, it is by no foreign road that it struggles toward the heights. It would be a great labor to journey to heaven; the soul merely returns there. Once it has found the road, it marches on boldly, scornful of everything else. It casts no backward glance at wealth. Gold and silver — things fully worthy of the darkness they once lay in — it values not by the sheen that dazzles ignorant eyes, but by the muck of the old days, out of which our greed first pried and dug them.

The soul, I insist, knows that riches are stored somewhere other than in men's heaped-up treasure-houses; it is the soul, not the strong-box, that should be filled. It is the soul that men may set in dominion over all things and install as owner of the universe, so that its wealth has no boundary short of East and West; so that, like the gods, it possesses all things; so that, with vast resources of its own, it looks down from on high upon the wealthy — not one of whom enjoys his own money as much as he resents another's. When the soul has lifted itself to this lofty height, it treats the body too not as a thing to love but as a thing to supervise — a burden that must be carried; it will not serve what it was set over as master. No man is free who is a slave to his body. Pass over all the other masters that come from fussing too much about the body: the rule the body itself exercises is fussy and fastidious enough.

From this body the soul goes forth — sometimes calm, sometimes exultant — and once it has gone, it does not ask what will become of the deserted clay. Just as we take no thought for the clippings of our hair and beard, so that divine soul, at the point of leaving the mortal man, cares no more where its earthly container ends up — burned by fire, sealed under stone, buried in earth, torn by wild beasts — than a newborn child cares about the afterbirth. Whether this body is cast out for the birds to pick at, or devoured when

  thrown to the sea-dogs as prey,

what is that to him who is nothing? Even while it is among the living, the soul fears nothing that may happen to the body after death; such things may have been brandished as threats, but they were never enough to terrify the soul before the moment of dying. It says: "The executioner's hook does not frighten me, nor the ugly mutilation of a corpse exposed to the sneers of spectators. I ask no one to perform the last rites for me; I entrust my remains to no one's keeping. Nature has made provision that none shall go unburied. Time will lay away whatever cruelty has cast out." Maecenas put it eloquently:

  I want no tomb: for Nature gives
  outcast bodies burial.

You would think that was said by a man of strict principles. He did indeed have a noble and vigorous nature — but in prosperity he let it go slack, and it spoiled. Farewell.

Letter 93

Letter 93: On the Quality, as Contrasted with the Length, of Life

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I was reading the letter in which you mourn the death of Metronax — as if he could have lived longer, and somehow should have. And I missed something in it: the fairness you show everywhere else. You have fairness in abundance when you deal with people and events; it deserts you on this one subject, as it deserts every one of us. I have met plenty of people who are fair to their fellow men. I have never met anyone who was fair to the gods. Every day we curse Fate: "Why was this man cut down in the middle of his career? Why not that one instead? Why does he get to drag out an old age that is a burden to himself and everyone around him?"

But tell me honestly — which seems fairer to you: that you obey Nature, or that Nature obey you? And what difference does it make how soon you leave a place you must leave sooner or later? Our aim should be not to live long, but to live rightly. Living long is Fate's department; living rightly is the soul's. A life is long if it is full — and it becomes full only when the soul has secured its own proper good, only when it has taken command of itself. What good are eighty years to a man who spent them doing nothing? A man like that hasn't lived; he has loitered awhile in life. He didn't die late; he just took a long time dying. He lived eighty years, did he? That depends on which date you count as his death. Your other friend, though, died in the bloom of his manhood — but he had fulfilled every duty of a good citizen, a good friend, a good son; he came up short in nothing. His age was incomplete, but his life was complete. The other man existed for eighty years — unless by "he lived" you mean what we mean when we say a tree "lives."

Let's see to it, my dear Lucilius, that our lives are like precious jewels: remarkable not for their size but for their weight. Let's measure them by their performance, not by their duration. Do you want to know the difference between the hardy man who despises Fortune, serves through every campaign of life, and reaches life's Supreme Good — and the man who has merely had many years pass over his head? The first still exists after his death; the second was dead before he died.

So let's praise, and count among the blessed, the man who invested well whatever time he was given, however little. He saw the true light. He was not one of the common herd. He didn't just live; he flourished. Sometimes he enjoyed clear skies; sometimes, as usually happens, the radiance of that mighty star reached him only in flashes through the clouds. Why do you keep asking, "How long did he live?" He is living still! In one bound he has crossed over into posterity and handed himself into the keeping of memory.

Not that I would turn down a few extra years for myself. But if my span is cut short, I will not say I lacked anything essential to a happy life. I never planned to live out the very last day my greedy hopes promised me; I have looked on every day as if it were my last. Why ask my birth date, or whether I'm still on the register of the younger men? What I have is my own. Just as a short man can be a complete man, a short life can be a complete life. Age belongs among the externals. How long I exist is not mine to decide; how long I go on truly living, in my present way, is entirely in my control. Ask only this of me — that I stop measuring out an inglorious old age in the dark, and devote myself to living instead of being carried along past life.

And what, you ask, is the fullest span of life? Living until you possess wisdom. Whoever reaches wisdom has arrived — not at the farthest goal, but at the most important one. That man may boldly rejoice and give thanks to the gods — and to himself as well — and count Nature in his debt for having lived. He has earned the right: he paid her back a better life than the one he received. He set up the pattern of a good man; he showed what a good man is, and how great he can be. One more year would only have been like all the rest.

And really, how long are we supposed to keep on living? We have had the joy of learning the truth about the universe. We know the beginnings from which Nature rises; how she orders the course of the heavens; by what regular changes she calls back the year; how she has set an end to everything that has ever existed, and made herself the only end of her own being. We know that the stars move by their own motion; that nothing stands still except the earth, while every other body races on without pause. We know how the moon overtakes the sun; why the slower body leaves the faster behind; how she receives her light and loses it again; what brings on the night and what brings back the day. Where you are going, you will see all of this from closer up. "And yet," says the wise man, "it is not this hope that makes me depart more bravely — not my judgment that the road lies clear to my own gods. I have earned admission to their company; in fact, I have already been in it. I sent my soul to them, as they had already sent theirs to me. But suppose I am wiped out completely, and nothing of a man remains after death — my courage is not one bit less, even if my road, when I go, leads nowhere at all."

"But," you say, "he could have lived so many more years." There are books of only a few lines that are admirable and useful despite their size — and then there are the Annals of Tanusius. You know how bulky that book is, and what people say about it. Some men's long lives are exactly that: the Annals of Tanusius. Do you think the fighter killed on the last day of the games is luckier than the one who dies in the middle of the festival? Do you believe anyone is so foolishly greedy for life that he would rather have his throat cut in the dressing-room than in the arena? The head start we have on one another is no longer than that. Death makes its rounds to everyone; the slayer soon follows the slain. What people discuss with so much anxiety is, in the end, a trifle. And anyway — what does it matter how long you avoid the one thing you cannot escape?

Farewell.

Letter 94

Letter 94: On the Value of Advice

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

There is a branch of philosophy that deals in precepts — advice fitted to the individual case rather than framed for the whole human race. It tells a husband how to behave toward his wife, a father how to bring up his children, a master how to govern his slaves. Some philosophers accept this branch as the only one worth having and throw out all the rest, on the grounds that the rest wanders off beyond anything we practically need — as if anyone could give advice about one portion of life without first gaining a knowledge of life as a whole!

Aristo the Stoic takes the opposite view. He thinks this branch counts for very little. It doesn't sink into the mind, he says; it holds nothing but old wives' precepts. The greatest benefit comes from philosophy's actual doctrines, from the definition of the Supreme Good. Once a man has completely understood that definition and thoroughly learned it, he can frame his own precept for whatever the situation demands. A student of javelin-throwing keeps aiming at one fixed target, training his hand to guide the throw; and once instruction and practice have given him the skill, he can use it against any target he pleases — he has learned to hit not some random object, but precisely the thing he aims at. In the same way, the man who has equipped himself for the whole of life needs no advising on each separate item, because he is now trained to meet his problem as a whole. He doesn't merely know how to live with his wife or his son; he knows how to live rightly — and how to live with wife and children is included in that knowledge.

Cleanthes takes a middle line: this branch of wisdom is useful, yes, but a feeble thing unless it flows from general principles — unless it rests on philosophy's actual doctrines and main headings. So the subject splits into two separate questions. First: is precept-giving useful or useless? And second: can it by itself produce a good man? In other words, is it superfluous — or does it make everything else superfluous?

Those who call this branch superfluous argue like this: "If an object held in front of the eyes blocks the vision, it has to be removed. As long as it's in the way, it is a waste of time to hand out precepts like 'Walk this way; stretch your hand out in that direction.' In the same way, when something blinds a man's soul and keeps him from seeing his line of duty clearly, there is no use in advising him: 'Live this way with your father, that way with your wife.' Precepts will do no good while the mind is clouded with error; only when the cloud is scattered will it become clear what his duty is in each case. Otherwise you are merely showing a sick man what he ought to do if he were well, instead of making him well. Suppose you're trying to teach a poor man the art of acting rich — how can that be managed while his poverty stays put? You are trying to explain to a starving man how to play the part of a man with a full stomach, when what he needs first is relief from the hunger gripping his gut.

"The same thing, I assure you, holds for every fault. The faults themselves must be removed, and no precepts should be given that cannot possibly be carried out while the faults remain. Unless you drive out the false opinions we suffer under, the miser will never take in a lesson on the proper use of money, nor the coward a lesson on scorning danger. You must make the miser see that money is neither a good nor an evil — show him rich men who are miserable to the last degree. You must make the coward see that the things that scare us out of our wits are less fearsome than rumor advertises them, whether the object of fear is suffering or death; that when death comes — the sentence law has fixed for every one of us — it is often a great comfort to reflect that it can never come again; that in the middle of suffering, a resolute soul will be as good as a cure, for the soul lightens any burden it endures with stubborn defiance. Remember that pain has this most excellent quality: if it is prolonged, it cannot be severe, and if it is severe, it cannot be prolonged. And we should bravely accept whatever commands the unbreakable laws of the universe lay upon us.

"Once doctrines like these have brought the erring man to a sense of his own condition — once he has learned that the happy life is not the life that conforms to pleasure but the life that conforms to Nature; once he has fallen deeply in love with virtue as man's only good and recoiled from baseness as man's only evil, and knows that everything else — riches, office, health, strength, power — falls in between and is not to be counted among goods or among evils — then he will not need a monitor at his elbow for every separate action, saying: 'Walk this way, eat that way. This is the conduct proper for a man and that for a woman; this for a married man and that for a bachelor.' Indeed, the people who take the greatest pains to hand out such advice are the very ones who can't practice it. It is the tutor lecturing the boy, the grandmother lecturing her grandson; it is the hottest-tempered schoolmaster who insists that one must never lose one's temper. Walk into any elementary school and you will find that these very pronouncements, straight from the high-browed philosophers, are sitting in the children's lesson-book!

"And will you offer precepts that are clear, or precepts that are doubtful? The clear ones need no counsellor, and the doubtful ones win no belief — so the giving of precepts is superfluous. Here's the proof. If you are counselling someone on a matter that is unclear or ambiguous in meaning, you will have to back your precepts with proofs; and if you have to fall back on proofs, then it is the proofs that do the work, and do it better on their own. 'This is how you must treat your friend, this your fellow citizen, this your associate.' And why? 'Because it is just.' But I can find all of that material under the heading of Justice. There I find that fair dealing is desirable for its own sake, that we are not forced into it by fear or hired into it for pay, and that no man is just who is drawn to anything in this virtue except the virtue itself. Once I have convinced myself of that view and thoroughly absorbed it, what good can I get from precepts that only teach a man who is already trained? To one who knows, giving precepts is superfluous; to one who does not know, it is insufficient — for he must be told not only what he is being instructed to do, but why. I ask again: are such precepts useful to the man with correct ideas about good and evil, or to the man without them? The man without them will get no benefit from you: some notion that clashes with your counsel has already monopolized his attention. And the man who has carefully decided what to seek and what to avoid knows what he ought to do without a single word from you. So that whole branch of philosophy can be abolished.

"There are two reasons we go astray: either the soul carries an evil quality planted in it by wrong opinions, or — even if not yet possessed by false ideas — it leans toward falsehood and is quickly corrupted by some outward appearance that pulls it the wrong way. So our duty is either to doctor the diseased mind carefully and free it from its faults, or to take possession of the mind while it is still unoccupied, though tilted toward evil. Philosophy's main doctrines accomplish both; therefore this giving of precepts accomplishes nothing. Besides, if we are to hand out precepts to each individual, the task is staggering. One class of advice for the financier, another for the farmer, another for the businessman, another for the man cultivating a king's good graces, another for the man seeking the friendship of his equals, another for the man courting those below him. In marriage, you will advise one man how to conduct himself with a wife who came to him a maiden, and another how to behave with a woman who was married to someone else before; how the husband of a rich woman should act, and how the husband of a dowerless one. Or do you think there is no difference between a childless wife and a mother of children, between a woman advanced in years and a mere girl, between a mother and a stepmother? We cannot cover all the types, and yet each type demands its own treatment. The laws of philosophy, by contrast, are concise, and they bind in every case. What's more, the precepts of wisdom ought to be definite and certain; where things cannot be defined, they lie outside wisdom's sphere — for wisdom knows the proper limits of things.

"We should therefore do away with this whole branch of precepts, because it cannot give to everyone what it promises only to a few — while wisdom embraces everyone. Between the madness of people in general and the madness that goes to a doctor there is no difference except this: the one suffers from disease, the other from false opinions. In the one case the symptoms of raving can be traced to ill health; the other is the ill health of the mind itself. If a man offered precepts to a lunatic — how he ought to speak, how he ought to walk, how to conduct himself in public and in private — he would be more of a lunatic than his patient. What is really needed is to treat the black bile and remove the root cause of the madness. And that is what must be done in the other case too, the case of the diseased mind. The madness itself must be shaken off; otherwise your words of advice will vanish into thin air."

That is what Aristo says, and I will answer his arguments one by one. First, his point about the obligation to remove whatever blocks the eye and hinders the vision. I admit that such a person needs no precepts in order to see; what he needs is treatment — a cure for his eyesight, the removal of the obstacle handicapping him. For it is Nature that gives us our eyesight, and whoever removes the obstruction simply restores to Nature her proper function. But Nature does not teach us our duty in every case. Again, a man whose cataract is cured cannot, the moment he recovers, give back their eyesight to other men as well; but when we are freed from evil, we can free others too. The eye needs no encouragement — no counsel, even — to distinguish different colors; black and white can be told apart without prompting from anyone. The mind, on the other hand, needs many precepts before it can see what to do in life. And notice that even in eye-treatment the physician doesn't just accomplish the cure — he throws in advice into the bargain. He says: "There's no reason to expose your weak vision to dangerous glare right away. Begin with darkness, then move into half-light, and only then grow bolder, accustoming yourself gradually to the bright light of day. There's no reason to read directly after eating; no reason to impose hard tasks on your eyes while they are swollen and inflamed. Keep winds and strong blasts of cold air off your face" — and other suggestions of the same sort, which are worth just as much as the drugs themselves. The physician's art supplements remedies with advice.

"But error is the source of sin," comes the reply; "precepts do not remove error, nor do they rout our false opinions about Good and Evil." I admit that precepts alone are not enough to overthrow the mind's mistaken beliefs; but that does not make them useless when they accompany other measures. In the first place, they refresh the memory. In the second place, matters that looked like a jumbled mass when viewed as a whole can, once sorted into their proper classes, be examined with far greater care. By our opponents' logic, you might as well call consolation and exhortation superfluous. Yet they are not superfluous; neither, therefore, is counsel.

"But it is folly," they retort, "to prescribe what a sick man ought to do as if he were well, when you should really be restoring his health; without health, precepts aren't worth a straw." But don't sick men and sound men have certain things in common, about which they need continual advice — not to grab greedily at food, for instance, and to avoid getting overtired? Poor and rich alike have certain precepts that fit them both. "Cure their greed, then," people say, "and you won't need to lecture either the poor man or the rich one, once the craving in each of them has subsided." But isn't it one thing to be free of the lust for money, and quite another to know how to use money? Misers don't know the proper limits in money matters — but even men who are not misers fail to understand its use. Then comes the reply: "Do away with error, and your precepts become unnecessary." Wrong.
