Suppose greed is slackened, luxury confined, rashness reined in, laziness pricked with the spur — even after the vices are removed, we must still go on learning what we ought to do, and how we ought to do it.

"Nothing will be accomplished," it is said, "by applying advice to the more serious faults." No — and medicine cannot master incurable diseases either; yet it is still used, in some cases as a cure, in others as a relief. Not even the power of philosophy entire, summoning all its strength for the purpose, will pull out of the soul a disease that has become stubborn and chronic. But Wisdom is not incapable of curing anything just because she cannot cure everything. People say: "What good does it do to point out the obvious?" A great deal of good — for we sometimes know facts without paying attention to them. Advice is not teaching; it engages the attention, rouses us, concentrates the memory and keeps it from losing its grip. We miss much that sits right before our eyes. Advice is really a kind of exhortation. The mind often works hard at not noticing even what lies in plain sight; so we must force upon it the knowledge of things it knows perfectly well. Think of what Calvus said at Vatinius' trial: "You all know that bribery has been going on — and everyone knows that you know it." You know friendship should be scrupulously honored, and you do not honor it. You know a man does wrong to demand chastity of his wife while carrying on with other men's wives; you know that just as she must have no dealings with a lover, you must have none with a mistress — and you do not act on it. That is why you must be brought back to these facts again and again: they should not sit in storage, but be ready for use. Whatever is wholesome should be discussed often and held before the mind often, so that it is not merely familiar to us but ready to hand. And remember, too, that in this way what is already clear often becomes clearer.

"But if your precepts are not obvious," comes the answer, "you will be bound to add proofs — so it is the proofs, not the precepts, that will be helpful." But can't the adviser's authority carry weight even without proofs? — the way a legal expert's opinions hold good even when he doesn't deliver the reasons behind them. Besides, the precepts themselves carry great weight of their own, whether woven into the fabric of verse or condensed into prose proverbs, like the famous wisdom of Cato: "Buy not what you need, but what you must have; what you do not need is dear at a penny." Or those oracles, and sayings like oracles: "Be thrifty with time!" "Know thyself!" Will you need somebody to explain the meaning when a person quotes you lines like these?

  Forgetting trouble is the way to cure it.
  Fortune favors the brave; the coward is defeated by his own faint heart.

Maxims like these need no special pleader. They go straight to our emotions, and they help us simply because Nature is exercising her proper function. The soul carries within itself the seed of everything honorable, and advice stirs that seed to growth, the way a spark fanned by a gentle breeze unfolds its natural fire. Virtue is roused by a touch, a jolt. Moreover, there are certain things that live in the mind and yet are not ready to hand; they begin to work easily only once they are put into words. Other things lie scattered about in various places, and the unpracticed mind cannot arrange them in order; so we should bring them into unity and join them together, to make them more powerful and more of a lift to the soul. Or, if precepts accomplish nothing at all, then every method of instruction should be abolished, and we should content ourselves with Nature alone.

The people who hold that view forget that one man is lively and quick of wit, another sluggish and dull — that some men simply have more intelligence than others. The strength of the mind is nourished and kept growing by precepts; they add new points of view to the inborn ones and straighten out warped ideas. "But suppose," people retort, "that a man does not possess sound doctrines — how can advice help him when he is chained down by vicious ones?" In this way, plainly: it frees him from them. His natural disposition has not been crushed, only overshadowed and held down. Even so it keeps trying to rise, struggling against the influences that push toward evil; and when it gets support, when the aid of precepts arrives, it grows stronger — provided the chronic trouble has not corrupted or annihilated the natural man. In that case, not even the training philosophy gives, striving with all its might, will restore him. And what is the difference, really, between the doctrines of philosophy and precepts, except that doctrines are general precepts and precepts are special ones? Both give advice — the one through the universal, the other through the particular.

Some say: "If a man is familiar with upright and honorable doctrines, advising him is superfluous." By no means. This man has indeed learned to do what he ought to do; but he does not see clearly enough what those things are. For it is not only our emotions that keep us from praiseworthy deeds; it is also lack of practice in discovering what a particular situation demands. Our minds are often under good control and yet, at the same time, idle — untrained in finding the path of duty. Advice makes that path plain. Again, it is written: "Cast out all false opinions about Good and Evil, replace them with true ones, and advice will have nothing left to do." Order in the soul can certainly be established that way — but that is not the only way. Even if proofs establish just what Good and Evil are, precepts still have their proper role. Prudence and justice consist of certain duties, and duties are set in order by precepts. What's more, our very judgment of Good and Evil is strengthened by following up our duties, and precepts lead us to that end. The two agree with each other; nor can precepts take the lead unless the duties follow. They keep their natural order — so precepts clearly come first.

"Precepts are numberless," it is said. Wrong again! On the important, essential things they are not numberless. Of course there are slight variations of time, place, and person; but even for these, precepts of general application are given. "But no one cures madness by precepts," it is said, "and therefore no one cures wickedness by them either." There is a difference. If you rid a man of insanity, his sanity returns on its own; but if we remove false opinions, insight into practical conduct does not immediately follow. And even if it did follow, counsel would still confirm his right opinion about Good and Evil. It is also wrong to believe that precepts are of no use to the mad. On their own they accomplish nothing, but they help the cure along: both scolding and correction rein a lunatic in. (I mean here lunatics whose wits are shaken, not hopelessly gone.)

"Still," it is objected, "laws do not always make us do what we ought — and what are laws but precepts with threats mixed in?" First of all: if laws fail to persuade, it is precisely because they threaten; precepts do not coerce — they correct men by pleading. Again, laws frighten a man away from crime, while precepts urge him toward his duty. And besides, laws too are a help toward good conduct — at least when they instruct as well as command. On this point I disagree with Posidonius, who says: "I do not think the preambles should have been added to Plato's Laws. A law should be brief, so that the uninstructed can grasp it the more easily. It should be like a voice sent down from heaven: it should command, not discuss. Nothing seems to me duller or more foolish than a law with a preamble. Warn me, tell me what you want me to do — I am not learning; I am obeying." No: laws framed with preambles are helpful — and notice that a state with defective laws will have defective morals. "But they do not work in every case." Well, neither does philosophy; and philosophy is not therefore ineffectual and useless in the training of the soul. And besides — what is philosophy but the Law of Life? But grant, if we like, that laws do not help; it does not follow that advice does not help either. On that ground you would have to say that consolation does not help, or warning, or exhortation, or scolding, or praise — for these are all varieties of advice, and it is by such means that we arrive at a perfected state of mind. Nothing succeeds better at bringing honorable influences to bear on the mind, or at straightening a wavering spirit inclined to evil, than the company of good men. Seen often and heard often, they sink little by little into the heart and take on the force of precepts.

The mere meeting of wise men lifts us up; a great man can help you even when he is silent. I could not easily tell you how it helps — I only know for certain that I have been helped that way. Phaedo says: "Certain tiny creatures leave no pain when they sting us; so subtle is their power, so deceptive for purposes of harm. Only a swelling reveals the bite, and even in the swelling there is no visible wound." That will be your experience in dealing with wise men: you will not discover how or when the benefit reaches you, but you will discover that you have received it. "And what is the point of this remark?" you ask. The point is that good precepts, welcomed within you often, will do you just as much good as good examples. Pythagoras declares that our souls undergo a change when we enter a temple and behold the images of the gods face to face and stand waiting for the word of an oracle. And who can deny that certain precepts strike even the rawest beginner with real force? Brief sayings, but weighty ones: "Nothing in excess." "The greedy mind no gain can satisfy." "Expect from others the treatment you have given them." We feel a kind of shock when we hear such sayings; no one ever thinks of doubting them or asking "Why?" — so strongly does plain truth attract us, even with no reason attached. If reverence reins in the soul and checks vice, why can't counsel do the same? If a rebuke can shame a man, why shouldn't counsel have the same power, even using bare precepts? And the counsel that supports its suggestion with reason — that adds why a thing should be done, and what reward waits for the man who does it and obeys — works better still and settles deeper in the heart. If commands are helpful, so is advice. But commands are helpful; therefore advice is helpful too.

Virtue divides into two parts: the contemplation of truth, and conduct. Training teaches contemplation; admonition teaches conduct. And right conduct both exercises virtue and reveals it. If a man about to act is helped by advice, he is helped by admonition too. Therefore, if right conduct is necessary to virtue, and admonition makes right conduct clear, then admonition is an indispensable thing. The soul has two strong supports — trust in the truth, and confidence — and both come from admonition. For men believe it; and once belief is established, the soul receives great inspiration and fills with confidence. Therefore admonition is not superfluous.

Marcus Agrippa — a great-souled man, and the only one of all those the civil wars raised to fame and power whose success was good for the state — used to say that he owed a great debt to the proverb: "Harmony makes small things grow; discord makes great things fall apart." He held that this saying had made him the best of brothers and the best of friends. And if proverbs of this kind, welcomed intimately into the soul, can mold the very soul, why shouldn't the branch of philosophy that consists of such proverbs carry equal influence? Virtue depends partly on training and partly on practice: you must first learn, and then strengthen what you have learned by action. If that is true, then it is not only the doctrines of wisdom that help us but the precepts too, which check and banish our passions like a kind of official decree.

It is said: "Philosophy divides into two things — knowledge, and a state of mind. For a man who has learned and understood what he should do and avoid is not yet a wise man until his mind has been remolded into the shape of what he has learned. This third department — precepts — is compounded out of the other two, from doctrine and from state of mind; so it is superfluous for the perfecting of virtue, since the other two are enough for the purpose." At that rate consolation would be superfluous too, since it is also a compound of the other two — and so would exhortation, and persuasion, and even proof itself, for proof also springs from a well-ordered and steady state of mind. But though these things flow from a sound state of mind, the sound state of mind also flows from them: it both creates them and results from them. Furthermore, what you describe is the mark of a man already perfect, one who has reached the summit of human happiness. The approach to that summit is slow; and in the meantime, in practical affairs, the path should be pointed out for the man who is still short of perfection but making progress. Wisdom may perhaps show herself this path without any prompting, once she has brought the soul to the point where it can only be moved in the right direction. Weaker characters, however, need someone to walk ahead of them and say: "Avoid this," or "Do that." Besides, if a man waits until he can know on his own what the best course of action is, he will sometimes go astray in the meantime, and going astray will keep him from ever arriving at the point where he can be content with himself. The soul must be guided at the very moment it is becoming able to guide itself. Schoolboys learn by direction: their fingers are held and guided by another hand so that they can trace the outlines of the letters; then they are told to imitate a model and form their handwriting on it. In the same way the mind is helped when it is taught by direction. Facts like these prove that this branch of philosophy is not superfluous.

The next question is whether this part alone is enough to make men wise. That problem I will take up at the proper time; but for now, setting all argument aside, isn't it obvious that we need someone to serve as our preceptor against the precepts of the world at large? No word reaches our ears without doing us harm. We are injured by good wishes and by curses alike. Our enemies' angry prayers plant false fears in us; and our friends' affection spoils us with its kindly wishes — for that affection sends us groping after goods that are far away, uncertain, and unsteady, when we could be opening the storehouse of happiness at home. We are not allowed, I tell you, to travel a straight road. Our parents draw us into wrong; our slaves draw us into wrong. Nobody confines his mistakes to himself: people sprinkle folly on their neighbors, and take it back from them in turn. That is why you find a nation's vices in each individual — because the nation gave them to him. Each man, in corrupting others, corrupts himself; he drinks in badness, and then pours it out again — and the result is one vast mass of wickedness, the worst of every separate person concentrated in a single heap.

We should therefore have a guardian, so to speak, to keep plucking us by the ear — to bat away the rumors and protest against the crowd's enthusiasms. For you are mistaken if you suppose our faults are born in us: they came from outside; they were heaped upon us. So by frequent admonitions we can push back the opinions that din around our ears. Nature allies us with no vice. She produced us whole and free. She set nothing before our eyes that might stir up the itch of greed. She put gold and silver beneath our feet, and gave those feet the job of stamping down and crushing everything that causes us to be stamped down and crushed. She lifted our gaze toward the sky, and willed that we look upward at her glorious and wonderful works: the rising and the setting sun; the whirling course of the onrushing world, which discloses the things of earth by day and the heavenly bodies by night; the movements of the stars — slow, if you compare them with the universe, yet enormously swift if you consider the size of the orbits they trace at unslackening speed; the eclipses of sun and moon, each in its turn; and all the other wonders, wonderful whether they arrive on schedule or leap suddenly into view — trails of fire by night, flashes across an open sky with no stroke or rumble of thunder, columns and beams and the varied shapes of flame. All these she ordained to travel above our heads. But gold and silver — and the iron which, because of the gold and silver, never brings peace — she hid away, as if they were dangerous things to trust to our keeping. It is we ourselves who dragged them into the light of day, so that we could fight over them. It is we ourselves who tore away the earth that covered them and dug up the causes and instruments of our own destruction. It is we ourselves who then blamed our misdeeds on Fortune, and are not ashamed to hold up as the loftiest of objects things that lately lay in the depths of the earth. Do you want to know how false the gleam is that has deceived your eyes? There is really nothing fouler, nothing more sunk in darkness, than this stuff of earth, buried and covered so long in the mud where it belongs. Of course it is foul — it was hauled up through a long, murky mine-shaft. There is nothing uglier than these metals while they are being refined and separated from the ore. And watch the workmen themselves, the ones who must handle and sift the barren grade of dirt that comes up from the bottom — see how caked with soot they are! Yet the stuff they handle dirties the soul more than the body; and there is more foulness in the owner than in the workman.

It is indispensable, then, that we be admonished — that we have some advocate of upright mind, and amid all the uproar and jangle of falsehood hear one voice only. But what voice shall it be? Surely a voice that, amid all the tumult of self-seeking, whispers wholesome words into the deafened ear, saying: "You have no need to envy the men the people call great and fortunate. Applause need not shake your composure or your sanity of mind. You need not grow disgusted with your own calm spirit at the sight of a great man in purple, ringed by the famous symbols of power. You need not judge the magistrate for whom the road is cleared to be any happier than yourself, whom his officer shoves off the road. If you would wield a command that profits you and injures nobody, clear your own faults out of the way. There are plenty of men who set fire to cities, who storm garrisons that stood impregnable for generations and safe through countless ages, who raise siege-mounds as high as the walls they are attacking, who shatter towers of wondrous height with battering-rams and engines. There are plenty who can send their columns ahead and press murderously on the enemy's rear, who can reach the Great Sea dripping with the blood of nations. But even these men, before they could conquer their enemy, were conquered by their own greed. No one withstood their attack — and they themselves could not withstand their own desire for power and impulse to cruelty. At the very moment they seemed to be hounding others, they were being hounded themselves. A mad desire to lay waste other men's territory hounded Alexander into misfortune and dispatched him to unknown countries. Do you call that man sane, who began by devastating Greece — the land where he was educated? Who snatched from each nation its dearest possession, ordering Spartans to be slaves and Athenians to hold their tongues? Not content with the ruin of all the states Philip had conquered or bribed into bondage, he knocked down commonwealth after commonwealth in place after place and carried his weapons across the whole world. His cruelty grew tired, but it never stopped — like a wild beast that tears to pieces more than its hunger demands. Already he has fused many kingdoms into one kingdom; already Greeks and Persians fear the same master; already nations Darius left free are bending under the yoke. Yet on he presses, past the Ocean, past the Sun, thinking it shame to turn his victorious course aside from the tracks of Hercules and Bacchus; he is ready to do violence to Nature herself. It is not that he wants to keep going; it is that he cannot stop — like a falling weight, whose plunge ends only when it lies motionless.

"It was not virtue or reason that persuaded Gnaeus Pompeius into foreign and civil wars; it was his mad craving for unreal glory. Now he attacked Spain and the faction of Sertorius; now he sailed off to put the pirates in chains and subdue the seas — excuses, all of it, pretexts for extending his power. What dragged him into Africa, into the North, against Mithridates, into Armenia and every corner of Asia? Nothing but a bottomless desire to grow bigger — for only in his own eyes was he not great enough. And what drove Gaius Caesar to the combined ruin of himself and the state? Glory, self-seeking, and a refusal to set any limit to his pre-eminence over all other men. He could not bear that even a single person outrank him — though the state was content to have two men standing at its head. And do you imagine that Gaius Marius — who held the consulship once by election and stole it every other time — was inspired by virtue as he slaughtered the Teutons and the Cimbri and chased Jugurtha through the wilds of Africa? Marius led armies, and ambition led Marius."

Men like these, while they were shaking the world, were shaken themselves — like cyclones, which whirl up everything they seize only because they are whirled first, and rush on with all the greater force because they have no control over themselves; and so, after wrecking so much for others, they come to feel in their own bodies the ruinous force that let them wreak havoc on so many. You must never believe that a man can become happy through the unhappiness of another. We must untangle all these examples that are forced before our eyes and crammed into our ears; we must clean out our hearts, for they are stuffed full of evil talk. Into the ground those opinions have seized, virtue must be led — the kind of virtue that roots out falsehood and every doctrine that contradicts the truth; that separates us from the crowd we trust far too much, and restores us to sound opinions. For this is wisdom: a return to Nature, a restoration to the condition from which our common errors have driven us. It is a great part of health to have deserted the counsellors of madness — to have fled far from a companionship in which each party poisons the other.

If you want proof of what I'm saying, look how differently each man lives in public and in private. A quiet home does not by itself give lessons in upright conduct; the countryside does not by itself teach plain living. But when the witnesses and the onlookers are removed, the faults that ripen on being seen and displayed sink into the background. Who puts on the purple robe with no one there to see it? Who dines off gold plate alone? Who, flinging himself down in the shade of some country tree, unpacks the splendor of his luxury in solitude? Nobody dresses up for his own eyes only, or even for the admiration of a few friends and relatives; a man spreads out his well-appointed vices in proportion to the size of the crowd admiring them. That is how it is: the claque and the witnesses are the irritants of all our mad cravings. Make us stop displaying, and you will make us stop craving. Ambition, luxury, and waywardness need a stage to perform on; you will cure them all if you keep out of sight.

Therefore, if our home must stand amid the din of a city, let there be an adviser standing at our side. When men praise great incomes, let him praise the man who can be rich on a small estate and measures his wealth by the use he makes of it. When men glorify influence and power, let him recommend, unprompted, a leisure devoted to study — a soul that has turned away from external things and found itself. Let him point out men who are happy by the crowd's reckoning but who totter on their envied heights of power — dismayed, holding an opinion of themselves very different from what everyone else holds. What others see as an eminence, they experience as a sheer drop. So their hearts sink and they flutter with fear whenever they look down the steep face of their own greatness; for they reflect that there are many ways of falling, and that the topmost point is the most slippery footing of all. Then they dread the very thing they fought for, and the good fortune that made them weighty in other men's eyes weighs still more heavily on themselves. Then they praise easy leisure and independence; they hate the glitter and look for an escape while their fortunes still stand. Then, at last, you may watch them turning to philosophy out of fear, and hunting for sound advice as their fortunes run aground. For good fortune and good sense stand, as it were, at opposite poles — that is why we are wiser in the midst of adversity. It is prosperity that takes away right judgment.

Farewell.

Letter 95

Letter 95: On the Usefulness of Basic Principles

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You keep asking me to take up, right now and without postponement, a topic I once said should wait for its proper time: whether the department of philosophy the Greeks call "paraenetic" and we Romans call "preceptorial" — the giving of practical advice — is enough by itself to make us perfectly wise. Now, I know you would take it in good part if I refused. But that is exactly why I accept your request all the more willingly, and I refuse to let the old saying lose its point:

  Don't ask for what you'll wish you hadn't got.

For sometimes we work hard to get things we would turn down if they were freely offered. Call that fickleness or call it childishness — either way, the cure for the habit is prompt compliance. There are plenty of things we want people to think we want, when we really don't. A lecturer sometimes hauls onto the platform an enormous work of research, written in the tiniest handwriting and rolled up tight; after reading off a good chunk of it he says, "I'll stop, if you like" — and up goes the shout, "Read on! Read on!" from the very people who are praying he'll shut his mouth then and there. We often want one thing and pray for another, and we don't tell the truth even to the gods — while the gods either don't listen, or take pity on us. Well, I shall be entirely without pity, and take my revenge by loading a huge letter onto your shoulders. If you find yourself reading it with reluctance, you may say, "I brought this on myself," and count yourself among the men driven frantic by the ambitious wives they schemed to marry, or the men tormented by riches they sweated blood to earn, or the men tortured by the titles they chased with every trick and every ounce of effort they had — and all the others who are the authors of their own misfortunes.

But enough preamble; let me come to the question. People argue like this: "The happy life consists in upright conduct; precepts guide us to upright conduct; therefore precepts are sufficient for the happy life." But precepts do not always guide us to upright conduct. They work only when the will is receptive; sometimes they are applied entirely in vain, when wrong opinions have the soul in their grip. Besides, a man may act rightly without knowing that he is acting rightly. Nobody, unless he has been trained from the start and equipped with complete reason, can develop to full proportions — understanding when he should do a given thing, and how far, and with whom, and how, and why. Without that training a man cannot strive wholeheartedly after what is honorable, or even steadily or gladly; he will forever be glancing back over his shoulder and wavering.

Then it is argued: "If honorable conduct results from precepts, then precepts are amply sufficient for the happy life; the first statement is true; therefore so is the second." Our reply: honorable conduct is indeed brought about by precepts — but not by precepts alone. "Well then," comes the answer, "if the other arts get along on precepts, wisdom can get along on them too; for wisdom is itself an art — the art of living. A pilot is made by precepts that tell him to turn the tiller this way and that, set his sails, use a fair wind, tack, make the best of shifting and contrary breezes — all in the proper manner. Other craftsmen are drilled by precepts too; so precepts can do the same job for our craftsman in the art of living." But notice: all those arts are concerned with the tools of life, not with life as a whole. That is why so much can clog them from the outside and complicate them — hope, greed, fear. But the art that professes to teach the art of living cannot be blocked by any circumstance from doing its work; it shakes off the tangles and drives straight through the obstacles. Would you like to know how different its standing is from that of the other arts? In their case, it is more forgivable to go wrong on purpose than by accident; in the case of wisdom, the worst fault of all is to sin deliberately. Here is what I mean. A scholar will blush for shame if he makes a grammatical blunder by accident — not if he makes one on purpose. A doctor who fails to notice that his patient is sinking is a far worse practitioner than one who notices it and hides what he knows. But in this art of living, it is the deliberate mistake that is the more shameful.

Furthermore, many arts — and the most liberal of them at that — have their own bodies of doctrine, not mere precepts of advice. Medicine, for example: hence the different schools of Hippocrates, of Asclepiades, of Themison. And no art with a theoretical side can exist without its own doctrines — the Greeks call them dogmas, while we Romans may say "doctrines" or "tenets" or "adopted principles," the sort of thing you find in geometry or astronomy. But philosophy is both theoretical and practical; it contemplates and acts at the same time. You are quite mistaken if you think philosophy offers you nothing but everyday assistance; her ambitions run far higher than that. "I investigate the whole universe," she cries. "I do not stay shut up in a mortal dwelling, content to hand you advice for or against. Great matters summon me — matters set far above your head." As Lucretius puts it:

  To you I shall reveal the ways of heaven
  And of the gods, and spread before your eyes
  The atoms — out of which all things are born,
  Grow, and are fed by nature's building power,
  And into which she breaks them at the end.

Philosophy, then, being theoretical, must have her doctrines. And why? Because no one can perform right actions properly except the man who has been entrusted with reason — reason that will let him, in every case, fulfill every category of duty. And he cannot honor those categories unless he receives precepts for every occasion, not just the present one. Precepts by themselves are weak — rootless, so to speak — if they are attached to the parts of life and not to the whole. It is the doctrines that fortify us, that keep us safe and calm, that take in at one sweep the whole of life and the whole of the universe. The difference between philosophical doctrines and precepts is the difference between elements and limbs: the limbs depend on the elements, while the elements are the source of the limbs and of everything else.

"But the old-style wisdom," people say, "did nothing but tell you what to do and what to avoid — and the men of those days were far better men. Ever since the scholars showed up, the sages have grown scarce. That frank, simple virtue has turned into hidden and crafty cleverness; we are taught how to argue, not how to live." No doubt the old wisdom, especially in its beginnings, was crude — just as the other arts were, which grew refined as they went along. But in those days there was not yet any need for carefully engineered remedies. Wickedness had not yet risen so high, or scattered itself so widely. Plain vices could be treated by plain cures. Now we need defenses built with all the more care, because the forces attacking us are so much stronger. Medicine was once the knowledge of a few herbs — to stop bleeding, to heal wounds; by degrees it reached its present tangled variety. And no wonder medicine had less to do in the early days! Men's bodies were still sound and strong; their food was light, unspoiled by art and luxury. But once they began to seek out dishes not to satisfy appetite but to arouse it, and devised countless sauces to whip up their gluttony, then what had been nourishment to a hungry man became a burden to a stuffed stomach. From that come pallor, and the trembling of wine-soaked muscles, and a wasted thinness that owes more to indigestion than to hunger. From that come weak, tottering steps, and a stagger like a drunkard's walk. From that comes dropsy, spreading under the whole skin, and the belly swelling into a paunch from the standing habit of taking in more than it can hold. From that come yellow jaundice, discolored faces, bodies rotting from within, fingers going knotty as the joints stiffen, muscles numbed past all feeling, and the heart pounding away without pause. Why should I mention the dizzy spells? Or the pain of the eye and the ear, the itching and throbbing of a fevered brain, and ulcers all through the digestive tract? Beyond these there are countless kinds of fever — some savagely acute, some creeping up on us with quiet damage, others arriving with chills and violent shaking. Why go through the other numberless diseases — the tortures that high living hands out?

Men used to be free of such ills, because they had not yet gone soft with indulgence, because they still governed themselves and supplied their own needs. They toughened their bodies with work and genuine toil, wearing themselves out with running or hunting or working the soil. They came home to food that only a hungry man could enjoy. Hence there was no need for our huge medical apparatus, all the instruments and the pill-boxes. Their health was plain for a plain reason: it takes elaborate courses to produce elaborate diseases. Just count the number of things — all destined for a single throat — that luxury mixes together after ransacking land and sea. So many different foods are bound to disagree with each other; they go down with difficulty and are digested with difficulty, each one jostling against the rest. No wonder the diseases bred by ill-assorted food are shifting and manifold; when so many unnatural combinations are dumped in together, something has to overflow. That is why there are as many ways of being sick as there are ways of living. The illustrious founder of the medical profession remarked that women never lose their hair or suffer pain in the feet. Yet nowadays their hair falls out and their feet ache with gout. Woman's physical nature has not changed; it has been conquered. By matching men's indulgences they have also matched men's diseases. They stay up just as late, they drink just as hard; they challenge the men at wrestling and at carousing; they match them in vomiting up from strained stomachs all the wine they poured in — drinking to vomit, and vomiting to drink again; they match them, too, in chewing ice to soothe a fevered digestion. Even in their lusts they yield nothing to the males — although they were born to the passive role (may the gods and goddesses confound them!) — devising the most contorted varieties of unchastity, and playing the man's part in men's company. Why be surprised, then, that we can catch out the greatest and most skilled of physicians in a false statement, when so many women are gouty and bald? By their vices they have forfeited the privileges of their sex; having put off their womanhood, they are condemned to suffer the diseases of men.

The doctors of the old days knew nothing of prescribing frequent feedings and propping up a feeble pulse with wine; they did not understand the drawing of blood, or easing chronic conditions with sweat-baths; they did not know how, by binding the ankles and arms, to draw back to the surface the hidden strength that had retreated to the core. They were not forced to hunt up so many varieties of relief, because the varieties of suffering were so few. But now — look how far the evils of ill-health have advanced! This is the interest we pay on the pleasures we coveted past all reason and all decency. Are you surprised that diseases are beyond counting? Count the cooks. All serious study is at a standstill; teachers of the liberal arts lecture to empty rooms in out-of-the-way corners. The halls of the professors and the philosophers stand deserted — but look at the crowd in the cookshops! Look how many young men besiege the kitchens of their gluttonous patrons! I pass over the troops of unhappy boys who, when the banquet is finally over, must endure other humiliations in the bedroom. I pass over the regiments of slave-boys kept for lust, sorted by nation and by color — all required to have the same smooth skin, the same first down on the cheek, the same style of hair, so that no straight-haired boy gets mixed in among the curly-heads. I pass over the swarm of bakers, and the mob of waiters who scurry in with the courses at a signal. Ye gods — how many men kept busy in the service of a single belly! What — do you imagine those mushrooms, the epicure's poison, are doing no secret damage, even if they haven't struck yet? Do you suppose your snow in summer doesn't harden the tissue of the liver? Do you suppose those oysters — sluggish meat fattened on slime — don't load a man down with their mud-bred heaviness? Do you suppose the so-called "Sauce of the Provinces," that costly extract of rotten fish, doesn't scald the stomach with its salted putrefaction? Do you suppose those festering delicacies, swallowed practically flaming from the kitchen fire, are quenched in the digestive tract without doing any harm? Then how foul and how unhealthy their belches are — and how men disgust themselves, breathing out the fumes of yesterday's debauch! You may be certain their food is not being digested. It is rotting.

I remember once hearing gossip about a notorious dish into which a cookshop, on its way to a well-earned bankruptcy, had piled together everything the epicures love to linger over: two kinds of mussels, and oysters trimmed round to the edible line, set off at intervals by sea-urchins, the whole thing flanked with mullets, filleted and served boneless. These days we are ashamed of foods served one at a time; people mash many flavors into one. The dinner table now does the work the stomach ought to do. I fully expect, next, to see food served pre-chewed — and how far off is that, really, when the shells and bones are picked out for us and the cook performs the office of the teeth?

"It's too much trouble," they say, "to take our luxuries one at a time. Let's have everything served at once, blended into a single flavor. Why should I reach for one dish at a time? Let many arrive together; let the dainties of the separate courses be combined and confounded. Those who used to claim this was all done for show and vanity should understand: it is not for display — it is a solemn offering to our sense of duty! Let the dishes usually served in sequence come drenched together in one sauce. Let there be no distinctions: let oysters, sea-urchins, shellfish, and mullets be mixed up and cooked in the same pan." Vomited food could not be jumbled together more thoroughly. And as the food is confounded, so the resulting diseases are complex, unaccountable, manifold, variegated — and medicine has had to arm itself against them with many weapons and many rules of treatment.

Now I tell you the same holds for philosophy. It was once simpler, because men's sins were on a smaller scale and could be cured with modest effort; but in the face of this moral upheaval, every remedy must be tried. And if only the plague could be beaten even so! We are mad — not just individually, but nationally. We check manslaughter and the murder of individuals; but what about war, and the celebrated glory of slaughtering whole peoples? Our greed knows no limits; neither does our cruelty. And as long as such crimes are committed quietly, by single men, they are less harmful and less monstrous; but now cruelty is practiced by decree of senate and popular assembly, and the state orders publicly what it forbids privately. Deeds that would cost a man his life if done in secret, we applaud because generals in uniform carried them out. Man, the gentlest species of all by nature, is not ashamed to revel in one another's blood, to wage war, and to hand war down to his sons — when even the dumb beasts, even the wild ones, keep peace with their own kind. Against this overpowering, epidemic madness, philosophy has become a heavier labor; she has had to gain strength in proportion to the strength of what she fights.

It used to be easy to scold the men who were slaves to the bottle and hunted after fancier food; it took no great effort to lead the spirit back to a simplicity it had strayed from only a little way. But now

  One needs the rapid hand, the master's craft.

Men hunt pleasure from every source. No vice stays within its own limits; luxury topples over into greed. Honor has fallen into oblivion. Nothing is shameful anymore, if it fetches a good price. Man — a sacred thing to man — is slaughtered for sport; men whom it was once a sacrilege even to train for the giving and receiving of wounds are now driven into the arena naked and unarmed, and a human being turned into a corpse is judged a satisfying show.

Amid this wreckage of morals, something stronger than the usual is needed — something to shake off ills grown chronic. To root out beliefs in error that have sunk this deep, conduct must be governed by doctrines. Only when we add precepts, consolation, and encouragement to those doctrines can they prevail; by themselves, precepts are ineffective. If we want to hold men fast and tear them free of the ills that grip them, they must learn what is evil and what is good. They must know that everything except virtue changes its name, turning now good and now bad. Just as the soldier's first bond is his oath of allegiance — his love for the standard and his horror of desertion — and just as, once the oath has been sworn, every other duty can easily be demanded of him and every trust placed in him: so it is with those you would lead to the happy life. The foundations must be laid first, and virtue worked deep into the man. Let men be held by something like a religious devotion to virtue; let them love her; let them long to live with her, and refuse to live without her.

"But wait," people say. "Haven't certain men reached excellence without any elaborate training? Haven't they made great progress obeying bare precepts alone?" Quite true — but they had lucky temperaments; they snatched salvation in passing, as it were. For just as the immortal gods never learned virtue — being born with virtue complete, goodness built into their very nature — so certain men are fitted with extraordinary gifts and arrive without a long apprenticeship at what is ordinarily a matter of teaching, embracing what is honorable the moment they hear it. Hence the choice minds that seize on virtue at once, or produce it from within themselves. But your dull, sluggish fellow, hobbled by bad habits, must have this rust scoured off his soul again and again. Now, since those inclined toward the good can be lifted to the heights more quickly, the weaker spirits, too, will be helped and freed from their false opinions if we entrust to them the settled principles of philosophy — and you may see how essential those principles are in the following way. Certain things sink into us and make us sluggish in some directions and headlong in others. These two faults — recklessness and sloth — cannot be checked or roused until we remove their causes, which are mistaken admiration and mistaken fear. So long as those feelings possess us, you may say to us, "You owe this duty to your father, this to your children, this to your friends, this to your guests" — and greed will hold us back, try as we may. A man may know he should fight for his country; fear talks him out of it. A man may know he should sweat out his last drop of strength for his friends; soft living forbids it. A man may know that keeping a mistress is the deepest insult to his wife; lust drives him the other way. So it is useless to hand out precepts unless you first clear away what will stand in the precepts' path; it does no more good than laying weapons beside a man and marching him up to the enemy with his hands tied. To deal with the precepts we offer, the soul must first be set free. Suppose a man is doing just what he ought: he cannot keep it up continuously or consistently, because he will not know why he is doing it. Some of his conduct will come out right through luck or through practice, but he will hold in his hand no rule to measure his actions by — nothing he can trust to tell him whether what he has done is right. A man who is good by accident gives no guarantee of staying good forever. Furthermore, precepts may perhaps help you to do what ought to be done; they will not help you do it in the right way — and if they don't get you that far, they don't lead you to virtue. I grant that a man who is warned will do what he should. But that isn't enough, because the credit lies not in the deed itself but in the way it is done. What is more shameful than a lavish dinner that eats through a knight's whole income? What deserves the censor's condemnation more than a man forever indulging himself and — as the gluttons say — his "inner man"? And yet many a careful man has spent a cool million on his inaugural dinner! The very sum called disgraceful when spent on appetite is beyond reproach when spent for official purposes. For then it isn't luxury — it's an expense sanctioned by custom.

A mullet of monstrous size was once presented to the Emperor Tiberius. They say it weighed four and a half pounds — and why shouldn't I tickle certain epicures' palates by naming its weight? Tiberius ordered it sent to the fish-market and put up for sale, remarking, "My friends, I shall be very much surprised if either Apicius or Publius Octavius doesn't buy that mullet." The guess came truer than he expected: the two men bid against each other, and Octavius won — earning a tremendous reputation among his set because he had paid five thousand sesterces for a fish the Emperor had sold and even Apicius had failed to buy. Paying such a price was disgraceful for Octavius, but not for the man who first bought the fish to present it to Tiberius — though I would blame him as well; still, at least he admired it as a gift he thought worthy of Caesar.

When people sit at the bedsides of sick friends, we honor them for it. But when people do it to angle for a legacy, they are vultures waiting for the carcass. The same act can be shameful or honorable: the purpose and the manner make all the difference. Now, every act of ours will be honorable if we pledge our allegiance to honor and judge honor, and what flows from it, to be the only good that can fall to a man — everything else being good only for a day. So I hold that there must be planted deep in us a settled conviction that applies to life as a whole: this is what I call a doctrine. As that conviction is, so will our acts and thoughts be; and as our acts and thoughts are, so will our lives be. When you are ordering an entire existence, advice about the details is not enough. Marcus Brutus, in the book he entitled On Duty, gives many precepts to parents, to children, to brothers; but no one will do his duty as he ought unless he has some principle to refer his conduct to. We must set before our eyes the goal of the Supreme Good — something to strive toward, something every act and every word can look to for its bearings — just as sailors must steer by a fixed star. Life without an ideal is aimless drifting; and the moment an ideal has to be set up, doctrines become necessary. You will admit, I am sure, that nothing is more shameful than hesitant, wavering conduct — than the habit of timidly backing out. And that will be our experience in everything, until we remove whatever checks the spirit, whatever clogs it and keeps it from committing itself wholly to the attempt.

Precepts are commonly given on how the gods should be worshipped. But let us forbid the lighting of lamps on the Sabbath: the gods have no need of light, and even men take no pleasure in soot. Let us forbid the morning levee and the crowding of temple doorways: those ceremonies flatter human ambition — God is worshipped by those who know him. Let us forbid the carrying of towels and body-scrapers to Jupiter, and the holding of mirrors for Juno: God seeks no servants. Of course not — he himself is mankind's servant; everywhere, and for everyone, he stands ready to help. A man may hear all about the proper limits of sacrifice, and how far to keep clear of burdensome superstition; but he will never make real progress until he has formed a right conception of God — as the one who possesses all things and allots all things, and gives them freely, without price. And what reason do the gods have for their kindnesses? Their nature. Anyone who thinks they are merely unwilling to do harm is mistaken: they cannot do harm. They can neither suffer injury nor inflict it — for to harm and to be harmed belong to the same order of things. Universal nature, glorious and beautiful in all her parts, has made those she lifted beyond the danger of evil incapable of dealing it out.

The first way to worship the gods is to believe in the gods. The next is to acknowledge their majesty — and their goodness, without which there is no majesty; to know that they are the powers presiding over the universe, ruling all things by their strength and standing guard over the human race, even though they sometimes take no notice of the individual man. They neither have evil nor give it; but they do chasten certain men, and restrain them, and impose penalties — and sometimes their punishment comes disguised as a blessing. Would you win the gods' favor? Be a good man. Whoever imitates them has worshipped them enough. Then comes the second question: how to deal with men. What is our aim? What precepts do we give? To abstain from bloodshed? What a small thing it is not to harm the very person you ought to help! High praise indeed, that a human being should be gentle to a human being! Shall we advise stretching out a hand to the shipwrecked sailor, pointing the road to the lost traveler, sharing bread with the starving? Yes — when I can first list everything that ought to be given or withheld. In the meantime, I can lay down for mankind one short rule covering all our duties to one another: everything you see, everything that contains both the divine and the human, is one. We are all limbs of one great body. Nature made us kin, creating us from the same materials and for the same ends. She planted in us love for one another and made us for fellowship. She established fairness and justice; and by her decree, it is more wretched to do harm than to suffer it. By her command, let our hands be ready wherever help is needed. Keep this verse in your heart and on your lips:

  I am a man; nothing that touches man
  Do I count foreign to me.

Let us hold things in common — for our birth is in common. Human society is like a stone arch: it would fall if the stones did not brace one another — and that mutual pressure is exactly what holds it up.

Next, after gods and men, let us consider how to make use of things. It is pointless to have rattled off precepts unless we begin by settling what opinion we ought to hold of each thing — of poverty, riches, fame, disgrace, one's country, exile. Let us shut rumor out and set a value on each thing for ourselves, asking what it is rather than what it is called.

Now to the virtues. Some will advise us to rate prudence very high, to cherish courage, and to cling to justice, if possible, more tightly than to any other quality. But this will do us no good if we do not know what virtue is — whether it is simple or compound; whether it is one or many; whether its parts stand separate or are woven together; whether the man who has one virtue has them all; and how, exactly, they differ from one another. A carpenter has no need to investigate his craft in terms of its origin or its purpose, any more than a pantomime dancer must investigate the art of dance; those arts understand themselves, and that is enough — they do not refer to life as a whole. But virtue is knowledge of other things as well as of herself: to learn virtue, we must learn everything about virtue. Conduct will not be right unless the will behind it is right, for the will is the source of conduct. And the will cannot be right without a right attitude of mind, for the mind is the source of the will. And that attitude of mind will not be found even in the best of men unless he has learned the laws of life as a whole, worked out a sound judgment on everything, and measured facts against the standard of truth. Peace of mind belongs only to those who have reached a fixed and unchanging standard of judgment; everyone else ebbs and flows in their decisions, floating between rejecting things and chasing them. And what causes all this tossing back and forth? Nothing is clear to them, because they consult the least reliable of all authorities — rumor. If you would always desire the same things, you must desire the truth. And no one reaches the truth without doctrines, for doctrines take in the whole of life. Good and evil, honorable and disgraceful, just and unjust, dutiful and undutiful; the virtues and their exercise; the possession of comforts; reputation and standing; health, strength, beauty, sharpness of the senses — all of these call for someone able to appraise them. You should be permitted to know what each item on the list is really worth; for you are often deceived into thinking things are worth more than they are — so badly deceived that what men rate highest of all — riches, influence, power — should really be marked down to a penny.

You will never understand this unless you have examined the actual standard by which such things are measured against each other. Just as leaves cannot flourish on their own — they need a branch to hold to and draw sap from — so your precepts, taken alone, wither away; they must be grafted onto a school of philosophy. Besides, those who abolish doctrines don't see that the very arguments they use to disprove doctrines establish them. For what are they saying? That precepts are sufficient to equip a life, and that the doctrines of wisdom — the dogmas — are superfluous. But that very claim is a doctrine — just as it would be if I now declared that precepts must be dropped as superfluous, that doctrines alone should be used, and that our studies should aim at doctrines only: by the very act of declaring that precepts should not be taken seriously, I would be uttering a precept. Some matters in philosophy call for admonition; others call for proof — and a great deal of proof, because they are complicated and can barely be untangled with the utmost care and the utmost skill in argument. If proofs are necessary, so are doctrines, for doctrines deduce the truth by reasoning. Some things are plain, some are obscure: plain, whatever the senses and the memory can take hold of; obscure, whatever lies beyond their reach.

But reason is not satisfied with the obvious. Its higher and nobler business is with hidden things. Hidden things require proof; proof cannot come without doctrines; therefore doctrines are necessary. What produces settled conviction — and perfected conviction too — is assured belief in certain facts; and if, for lack of that assurance, everything drifts loose in our minds, then doctrines are indispensable, for they give the mind the means of unswerving judgment. When we advise a man to value his friends as highly as himself, to remember that an enemy can become a friend, to cultivate love in the one and soften hatred in the other, we add: "This is just and honorable." But the just and honorable element in our doctrines is grasped by reason; so reason is necessary, for without it the doctrines cannot exist either. But let us join the two together. Branches are useless without their roots — and the roots themselves are strengthened by the growth they have produced. Everyone can see how useful the hands are; their help is out in the open. But the heart, which gives the hands their growth and power and motion, is hidden. I can say the same of precepts: they are out in the open, while the doctrines of wisdom lie concealed. And just as only the initiated know the innermost portion of the rites, so in philosophy the hidden truths are revealed only to those who have been received and admitted into her mysteries — while precepts and the like are familiar even to outsiders.

Posidonius holds that not only precept-giving (nothing stops me from using the word) but even persuasion, consolation, and encouragement are necessary. To these he adds the investigation of causes — and I don't see why I shouldn't dare to call it "aetiology," since the scholars who mount guard over the Latin language use the term as if they had every right to. He remarks that it will also be useful to give a portrait of each particular virtue; this science Posidonius calls "ethology," while others call it "characterization." It sets out the signs and marks belonging to each virtue and each vice, so that like can be told from like. Its function is the same as precept-giving. The man who gives precepts says, "If you would have self-control, do this and this." The man who draws the portrait says, "The man who does this and this, and refrains from that, is the man who has self-control." What's the difference, you ask? One gives the precepts of virtue, the other gives virtue in the flesh. These portraits — these samples, to borrow the merchant's word — do have their use, I admit; just put them on display with a good recommendation, and you will find men to copy them. Wouldn't you consider it useful to be given the marks by which to recognize a thoroughbred horse, so that you aren't cheated in your purchase or left wasting your time on a nag? Then how much more useful to know the marks of a surpassingly fine soul — marks one man may take over from another for himself!

  Straightway the foal of the noble herd, brought up in the pastures,
  Marches with spirited step, and sets down his feet with a light touch;
  First on the dangerous path he goes, and into the threatening river,
  Trusting himself to the unknown bridge, unafraid at its creakings;
  Neck carried high in the air, and a clean-cut head, and a belly
  Spare, and a rounded back, and a breast abounding in courage
  And muscle. And when the far-off clash of weapons resounds, he
  Cannot stand still: he pricks up his ears, and his limbs are all trembling,
  And out he pours the pent-up fire held close in his nostrils.

Vergil's description, though it refers to something else, might perfectly well be the portrait of a brave man; at any rate, I would choose no other image for a hero. If I had to describe Cato — unterrified amid the din of civil war, first to attack the armies already marching on the Alps, throwing himself face-forward into the civil conflict — this is exactly the bearing and expression I would give him. Surely no one ever "marched with more spirited step" than the man who rose against Caesar and Pompey at the same time and, while some backed Caesar's party and others Pompey's, threw down his challenge to both — showing that the Republic, too, still had someone on its side. For it is not enough to say of Cato "unafraid at its creakings." Of course he is not afraid! He does not flinch at real and present thunder; in the face of ten legions, Gallic auxiliaries, and a motley host of citizens and foreigners, he speaks the language of freedom, urging the Republic not to give up the fight for liberty but to hazard everything — declaring that it is more honorable to fall into servitude than to fall in step with it. What force, what energy is his! What confidence, in the midst of the general panic! He knows he is the one man whose standing is not in question — that men do not ask whether Cato is free, but whether he is still among the free. Hence his contempt for danger and for the sword. What a pleasure it is to say, in admiration of a hero's unflinching steadiness while the whole state fell in ruins around him:

  A breast abounding in courage and muscle!

It will help not only to describe the usual character of good men and sketch their figure and features, but to relate and retell the actual men there have been of this kind. We might picture Cato's last and bravest wound, through which Freedom breathed her last; or the wise Laelius, and the harmony of his life with his friend Scipio; or the noble deeds of the Elder Cato at home and abroad; or the wooden couches of Tubero, spread at a public feast — goatskins in place of tapestry, and earthenware vessels set out for the banquet before the very shrine of Jupiter. What was that but consecrating poverty on the Capitol? Even if I knew no other deed of his to rank him with the Catos, is that one not enough? It was a censorship, not a banquet. How pitifully the men who covet glory misunderstand what glory is, and how it should be sought! On that day the Roman people looked over the furniture of many hosts, and marveled at one man's only. The gold and silver of all the rest has been broken up and melted down a hundred times over; but Tubero's earthenware will outlast eternity.

Farewell.

Letters 96–98

Letter 96: On Facing Hardships

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Still grumbling? Still complaining, after everything? Don't you see that in all the evils you list there is really only one — the fact that you grumble and complain? If you ask me, nothing is miserable for a man unless there is something in the universe he has decided to call miserable. The day I find anything in my life unendurable, I will have become unendurable to myself.

I'm sick — that's part of my lot. My slaves have come down with fever, my income has dropped, my house is falling apart; losses, accidents, hard labor, and alarms have hit me from every side — well, that's a common story. No, that was an understatement: it's an inevitable story. Things like this come by assignment, not by accident. If you'll believe me, I'm opening my inmost feelings to you right now: whenever everything seems to go hard and uphill, I have trained myself not merely to obey God but to agree with his decisions. I follow him because my soul chooses to, not because I have to. Nothing will ever happen to me that I'll receive with a sour temper or a long face. There is no tax I won't pay willingly. And all the things that make us groan and flinch are simply the tax of living — things, my dear Lucilius, you should never hope to escape, and never ask to.

It was disease of the bladder that had you frightened; gloomy letters kept arriving; you kept getting worse. Let me come a little closer to the truth: you were afraid for your life. But come now — when you prayed for a long life, didn't you know that this was what you were praying for? A long life contains all these troubles, just as a long journey contains dust and mud and rain. "But," you cry, "I wanted to live, and to be immune from every ill at the same time." A cry like that does no credit to a man. Here is a prayer of mine instead — consider the spirit you should receive it in, because I offer it in not just a good spirit but a noble one: "May the gods and goddesses never allow Fortune to keep you in luxury!" Ask yourself honestly: if some god gave you the choice, would you rather live your life in a tavern or in an army camp?

And life, Lucilius, is a soldier's service. So the men who are tossed about at sea, who slog uphill and downhill over punishing crags, who draw the campaigns of greatest danger — these are the heroes, the front-rank fighters. The ones who loll in rotten luxury while other men sweat are turtle-doves: safe only because nobody thinks them worth striking.

Farewell.

Letter 97: On the Degeneracy of the Age

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You're mistaken, my dear Lucilius, if you think that luxury, the neglect of decent manners, and the other vices every man charges against his own times belong especially to our era. They are the vices of mankind, not of the times. No age has been free of guilt. In fact, once you start auditing the misconduct of any particular period, you will find — to humanity's shame be it said — that sin never walked around more openly than in Cato's own presence.

Would anyone believe that money changed hands at the trial of Clodius? He stood accused of secret adultery with Caesar's wife, and of profaning the ritual of that sacrifice which is offered on behalf of the people — the rite from which every male is banished so strictly that even pictures of male animals are covered up. And yet the jury was bribed; and — fouler still than the bribe — the seduction of married women and noble youths was demanded and thrown in as a kind of bonus payment. The charge contained less sin than the acquittal did: the defendant in an adultery case parceled out adulteries, and could not feel sure of his own safety until he had made the jurors criminals like himself. All this was done at a trial where Cato gave evidence — though that was his only part in the affair.

I'll quote Cicero's actual words, because the facts are too bad for belief: "He summoned them, made promises, pleaded, gave gifts. And more than that — merciful heavens, what a rotten state of affairs! — on several of the jurors, to round out their pay, he even bestowed nights with certain women and introductions to youths of noble family." The bribe itself isn't worth our shock; the extras were worse. "Would you like the wife of A., that solemn prig? Done. Or of B., the millionaire? I'll guarantee you sleep with her. Fail to commit adultery, and you're free to convict Clodius. That beauty you've been wanting will pay you a visit — I promise you a night in her company without delay, and my promise will be honored inside the legal period of postponement." Parceling out crimes like that means more than committing them; it means blackmailing the dignified married women of Rome. These Clodius jurors had asked the Senate for a bodyguard — a favor only a jury intending to convict could have needed — and the request was granted. Hence the witty remark of Catulus after the defendant got off: "Why did you ask us for the guard? Were you afraid of having your money stolen?" And amid jokes like these a man went unpunished who before the trial was an adulterer, during the trial a pimp — a man who escaped conviction more foully than he had earned it.

Can you believe any morals were ever more corrupt than those — when lust could not keep its hands off either religious worship or the courts of law, when in the very inquiry the Senate convened by special order, more crime was committed than investigated? The question on trial was whether a man could be safe after committing adultery; the answer turned out to be that he could not be safe without committing it. And all this bargaining went on in the presence of Pompey and Caesar, of Cicero and Cato — yes, the very Cato whose presence, they say, made the crowd give up demanding the usual routine of naked actresses at the Floralia, the spring festival. If you can believe it: men behaved more strictly at a festival than in a courtroom! Such things will be done in the future, as they have been done in the past; and the licentiousness of cities will sometimes ebb through discipline and fear — never on its own.

So don't believe that ours is the generation that has yielded most to lust and least to law. Today's young men live far more simply than the young men of an era when a defendant denied an adultery charge before his judges and his judges confessed to it before the defendant — when debauchery was practiced to secure a verdict, and Clodius, in favor thanks to the very vices he was on trial for, played the pimp during the actual hearing of his case. Could anyone believe it? The man whom one adultery would have condemned was acquitted because of many. Every age will produce men like Clodius; not every age will produce men like Cato. We slide into degeneracy easily, because we lack neither guides nor companions in our wickedness — and the wickedness marches on even without guides or companions. The road to vice is not just downhill; it is steep. And what makes so many men incorrigible is this: in every other craft, mistakes shame the good craftsman and gall the one who makes them, but the mistakes of life are a positive pleasure. The pilot does not rejoice when his ship goes over on her beam-ends; the doctor does not rejoice when he buries his patient; the lawyer does not rejoice when his client loses the case through his advocate's bungling. But every man enjoys his own crimes. A. delights in an affair — the difficulty was exactly what attracted him. B. delights in forgery and theft, and is displeased with his sin only when his sin has missed its mark. All of this is what perverted habit produces.

And yet — here is the other side, and it proves that even souls led into the most depraved ways keep a buried sense of right conduct, that men are not ignorant of what evil is but indifferent to it — all men hide their sins. However well things turn out, they enjoy the profits while concealing how they got them. A good conscience wants to step forward and be seen; wickedness is afraid even of the shadows. So I think Epicurus put it very aptly: "The guilty man may have the luck to remain hidden; the confidence that he will remain hidden he can never have." Or, if you think the meaning comes across more clearly this way: "Concealment does wrongdoers no good, because even when they have the luck of it, they never have the assurance of it." What I mean is this: crimes can be well guarded; they cannot be carefree.

This view, I maintain, is not at odds with the principles of our school, if it is explained properly. Why? Because the first and greatest punishment of sinners is the fact of having sinned. Crime may be decked out with Fortune's favors; she may protect it, take it under her wing — it can never go unpunished, since the punishment of the crime lies in the crime itself. But hard on the heels of that first punishment come the second ones: constant fear, constant dread, and distrust of one's own safety.

Why, then, should I let wickedness off with anything less? Why shouldn't I leave it hanging forever in suspense? Let us disagree with Epicurus on the one point — when he declares that nothing is just by nature, and that crime should be avoided only because the fear it breeds cannot be avoided. But let us agree with him on the other: that bad deeds are lashed by the whip of conscience, and that conscience suffers its worst torture because endless anxiety drives and flogs it on, and it can never trust the guarantors of its own peace of mind. For this, Epicurus, is exactly the proof that we shrink from crime by nature: even in perfect safety, there is no guilty man who does not feel afraid. Luck frees many men from punishment; it frees no man from fear. Why should that be, if not that nature has condemned certain things, and a loathing for them is bred into us? That is why even men who hide their sins can never count on staying hidden: their conscience convicts them and shows them to themselves. To be afraid is the very property of guilt. With so many crimes escaping the law and its prescribed punishments, we would have come off badly — except that those grievous offenses against nature must pay their penalty in ready cash, and where the punishment does not fall, the fear falls in its place.

Farewell.

Letter 98: On the Fickleness of Fortune

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Never believe that anyone who depends on happiness is happy. Delight in things that come from outside is a fragile prop; the joy that walked in will someday walk out. But the joy that springs wholly from within is loyal and sound; it grows, and it stays with us to the last. Everything else the crowd admires is a good for a day only. "What do you mean?" you reply. "Can't such things be useful and delightful too?" Of course they can — but only if they depend on us, and not we on them. Everything Fortune looks upon becomes fruitful and pleasant only if its possessor is also in possession of himself, and is not the property of his own property. For men make a mistake, my dear Lucilius, when they hold that Fortune gives us anything, good or evil. She gives us only the raw material of goods and ills — the beginnings of things which, in our keeping, will grow into good or into evil. The soul is stronger than any Fortune; by its own action it steers its affairs in either direction, and by its own power it makes its life a happy one or a wretched one.

A bad man turns everything bad — even things that arrived wearing the look of the best. An upright and honest man corrects the wrongs of Fortune; he softens hardship and bitterness because he knows how to endure them; he accepts prosperity with gratitude and moderation, and stands against trouble with steadiness and courage. A man may be prudent, may weigh every interest with balanced judgment, may attempt nothing beyond his strength — he will still never reach the good that is pure and beyond the reach of threats unless he is sure in his dealings with the unsure. Whether you prefer to watch other men (it is easier to make up your mind when the affairs you're judging are someone else's) or to watch yourself with every prejudice set aside, you will see and admit that none of these desirable, beloved things is any use unless you have armed yourself against the fickleness of chance and everything that follows in its train — unless, at every mishap, you repeat to yourself often and without complaint: "Heaven decreed otherwise." No — let us take a phrase braver and nearer the truth, one that will prop your spirit more safely. Whenever things turn out against your expectation, say to yourself: "Heaven decreed better."

To a man so poised, nothing will come as a blow. And a man will be so poised if he reflects on the possible ups and downs of human affairs before he feels their force — if he regards his children, his wife, his property as things he will not necessarily have forever, and things whose loss will not make him one bit more wretched. It is a tragic soul that dreads the future, that is wretched in advance of wretchedness, eaten up with anxiety that the things it delights in should stay in its hands to the very end. Such a soul will never be at rest; waiting for what is coming, it will lose the blessings in front of it — the ones it could actually enjoy. And there is no difference between grief over a thing lost and the fear of losing it.

Not that I'm advising you to be careless. No — steer away from whatever might cause fear. Whatever planning can foresee, foresee. Whatever is likely to hurt you, watch for it and turn it aside long before it happens. Your best help in this will be a confident spirit and a mind firmly resolved to endure all things. The man who can bear Fortune can also beware of Fortune. At the very least, calm seas throw up no breakers. And nothing is more wretched or more foolish than fearing ahead of schedule. What madness it is to run out to meet your troubles! In short, let me compress my thought and sketch you those busybodies, those self-tormentors: they are as unrestrained in the middle of their troubles as they were before them. He who suffers before it is necessary suffers more than is necessary. The same weakness that keeps such men from being ready for suffering keeps them from ever measuring it; and with the same lack of restraint they fondly imagine that their luck will last forever, and fondly imagine that their gains are bound not merely to continue but to grow. They forget the springboard on which all mortal things are tossed, and guarantee for themselves — of all people — that the gifts of chance will hold steady.

For exactly this reason I think the saying of Metrodorus is excellent — from the letter of consolation he wrote to his sister on the loss of her son, a boy of great promise: "All the good of mortals is mortal." He means the goods men run after in shoals. The real good does not perish; it is certain and lasting, and it consists of wisdom and virtue — the one immortal thing granted to mortals. But men are so wayward, so forgetful of where they are headed and of the point toward which every single day is shoving them, that they are surprised to lose anything — although one day they are bound to lose everything. Whatever you are listed as owner of is in your possession, but it is not yours; nothing weak has strength, nothing frail is lasting and invincible. We must lose our lives as surely as we lose our property — and this, if we understand the truth, is itself a consolation. Lose calmly; you must lose your life as well.

What resource is left to us, then, against these losses? Just this: to keep the things we have lost in memory, and not let the enjoyment we drew from them slip away along with them. To have can be taken from us; to have had, never. A man is thankless in the extreme if, after losing a thing, he feels no debt for ever having received it. Chance robs us of the thing, but leaves us its use and its enjoyment — and it is we who throw that away by the unfairness of our regret. Say to yourself: "Of all these things that look so frightful, not one is unbeatable. Each one has been beaten by many before now: fire by Mucius, crucifixion by Regulus, poison by Socrates, exile by Rutilius, death by the sword by Cato. Let us, too, beat something." And the things that lure the crowd with their look of beauty and happiness — those, too, have been scorned by many men, on many occasions. Fabricius refused riches when he was a general, and condemned them when he was censor. Tubero judged poverty worthy of himself and of the god on the Capitol when he set out earthenware dishes at a public feast — showing that a man should be content with what the gods themselves could still use. The elder Sextius turned down the honors of office: he was born with an obligation to take part in public affairs, and yet he would not accept the broad stripe, the senator's badge, even when the deified Julius offered it to him. For he understood that what can be given can also be taken away.

Let us, then, do something courageous of our own accord — let us take our place among the great examples of history. Why have we gone slack? Why do we lose heart? Whatever could be done once can be done now, if only we cleanse our souls and follow Nature. Stray from Nature, and you are condemned to crave, to fear, and to slave for the things of chance. We may return to the true path; we may be restored to our proper state.
