Let us be restored — so that we can endure pain in whatever form it attacks the body, and say to Fortune: "You are dealing with a man. Go find someone you can beat."

By these words, and words like them, the anger of the ulcer is being soothed; and I truly hope it can be reduced — either cured, or at least halted, left to grow old along with the patient himself. But I am easy in my mind about him. What we are discussing now is our own loss: the taking-off of a most excellent old man. He himself has lived a full life; anything added to it he craves not for his own sake, but for the sake of those who need his services. In going on living, he is being generous. Another man might have put an end to these sufferings; but our friend considers it no less shameful to flee toward death than to flee from it. "But," comes the objection, "if circumstances call for it, won't he make his exit?" Of course — if he can no longer be useful to anyone, if his one remaining occupation would be pain. This, my dear Lucilius, is what we mean by studying philosophy in practice, by drilling on the real thing: watching how much courage a prudent man has against death, and against pain, when the one draws near and the other bears down. What ought to be done must be learned from a man who does it. Up to now we have dealt in arguments — whether anyone can resist pain, whether the approach of death can bring down even great souls. Why argue further? Here is the fact itself, right in front of us: death does not make our friend braver against pain, and pain does not make him braver against death. Rather, he trusts himself against both. He does not suffer patiently because he hopes for death, and he does not die gladly because he is tired of suffering. Pain he endures; death he awaits.

Farewell.

Letters 99–100

Letter 99: On Consolation to the Bereaved

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I'm enclosing a copy of the letter I wrote to Marullus when he had lost his little son and was reported to be grieving with less self-command than a man should. In it I did not follow the usual rules of condolence, because I didn't think he should be handled gently — in my judgment he deserved criticism more than comfort. When a man has just been struck and is struggling under a fresh, heavy wound, you have to humor him for a while: let him grieve it out, or at least discharge the first shock. But people who have settled into grief as an indulgence should be called out at once, and taught that there is such a thing as foolishness even in tears.

"You're looking for comfort? Let me give you a scolding instead. You are taking your son's death the way a woman would — what would you do if you had lost a close friend? A son died: a little child, his promise still unknown; a small piece of time is gone. We go hunting for reasons to grieve; we'd even file unfair complaints against Fortune, as if Fortune would never hand us a just one! I honestly believed you had spirit enough to face solid troubles, let alone these shadowy ones that men moan over out of sheer habit. If you had lost a friend — and that is the greatest blow of all — your task would have been to work at rejoicing that you had him, rather than mourning that you lost him.

"But most men never add up their gains, never count their joys. Grief like yours has this evil among its others: it is not only useless, it is ungrateful. Was it all for nothing, then, that you had such a friend? So many years, such close companionship, such intimate sharing of everything that mattered — did all of it accomplish nothing? Do you bury the friendship along with the friend? And why lament losing him, if having him did you no good? Believe me, a great part of those we have loved stays with us, even after chance has taken their persons away. The past is ours, and nothing we hold is more secure than what has already been. We are ungrateful for what we've received because we are forever hoping for the future — as if the future, supposing any future is ours at all, won't quickly blend into the past. People who take pleasure only in the present put narrow limits on their enjoyment; the future and the past both serve our delight — the one with anticipation, the other with memory — but the one is uncertain and may never arrive, while the other cannot un-happen.

"What madness, then, to lose our grip on the surest thing we own! Let us rest content with the pleasures we have already drunk down — so long as, while we drank them, the soul wasn't pierced like a sieve, losing whatever it received. There are countless cases of men who have buried sons in the prime of manhood without a tear — men who walked back from the funeral pyre to the Senate chamber, or to whatever public duty, and got straight to work on something else. And rightly. First, grieving is pointless if grief brings you no help. Second, it is unfair to complain about what has happened to one man when it lies in store for all. Third, it is foolish to mourn a loss when the gap between the lost and the mourner is so slight. We should take these things with a calmer spirit, because we follow close behind the ones we have lost.

"Look at the speed of time — the swiftest thing there is. Consider the shortness of the course we run at full tilt. Watch this whole throng of humanity straining toward the same finish line, separated by the briefest intervals even when they seem longest: the one you count as lost has simply been sent ahead. And what is more irrational than to weep for the man ahead of you on a road you yourself must travel? Does a man weep over an event he knew would happen? If he never thought of death as part of the human bargain, he was only cheating himself. Does a man weep over an event he has been admitting all along is unavoidable? Whoever complains that someone died is complaining that he was human. We are all bound by the same terms: whoever is granted birth is scheduled for death. Stretches of time separate us; death levels us. The span between our first day and our last is shifting and uncertain: measure it by its troubles and it is long even for a boy; measure it by its speed and it is scanty even for an old man. Everything is slippery, treacherous, more changeable than any weather. Everything is tossed about and turns into its opposite at Fortune's whim, and in all this churning of mortal affairs only one thing is guaranteed to anyone — death. Yet everybody complains about the one thing in which no one is ever deceived. 'But he died a boy.' I'm not yet prepared to argue that whoever finishes life quickly has gotten the better bargain; so consider instead the man who has grown old. How little has he gained over the child! Set before your mind's eye the vast abyss of time; take in the whole universe; then compare what we call a human life with infinity — and you will see how tiny a thing we pray for, how little it is we strain to stretch. How much of that time is taken up with weeping, how much with worry! How much with praying for death before death arrives, how much with our health, how much with our fears! How much goes to the years when we were too green to use, or used badly! And half of the whole is spent asleep. Add our labors, our griefs, our dangers — and you will grasp that even in the longest life, actual living is the smallest part. And yet who will admit that a man is not better off for being allowed to go home early, his journey finished before it wore him out? Life is neither a good nor an evil; it is simply the place where goods and evils happen. So this little boy has lost nothing except a gamble in which loss was more certain than gain. He might have turned out temperate and sensible; with your care he might have been shaped into something better; but — and this fear is the more reasonable one — he might have turned out just like the many. Look at the young men of the noblest houses, whose extravagance has flung them into the arena; look at the men who serve their own lusts and other people's, whose days never pass without a drunken bout or some spectacular disgrace — and it will be clear to you that there was more to fear than to hope for.

"So you should not go inviting excuses for grief, or swell small burdens with indignation. I'm not urging you to strain and rise to some great height; I don't think so little of you as to believe you need to summon your whole stock of virtue for this trouble. This is not pain; it is a sting — and it is you yourself who are turning it into pain.

"Philosophy has certainly done you good service if you can bear bravely the loss of a boy still better known to his nurse than to his father! What then — am I now advising you to be hard-hearted? Do I want your face unmoved at the funeral itself, your soul not permitted even the pinch of pain? Not at all. That would be lack of feeling, not virtue — to watch the burial of your own dear ones with the same expression you wore when they were alive, and to feel nothing at the first bereavement in your family. But suppose I did forbid you to feel: certain feelings claim their own rights. Tears fall no matter how we fight to check them, and in falling they ease the soul. So what shall we do? Let them fall — but let us not command them to. Let us weep as much as emotion floods our eyes, not as much as imitation demands. Let us add nothing to natural grief, and not swell it to match other people's example. The performance of grief demands more than grief itself: how few men are sad in their own company! They wail the louder for being heard; people who are quiet and contained when alone break into fresh fits of tears the moment they see others near them. That is when they lay violent hands on themselves — which they could have done more easily with no one there to stop them; that is when they pray for death; that is when they throw themselves off their couches. Their grief slackens the moment the audience leaves. Here, as everywhere, we are stuck in the same fault: conforming to the pattern of the many, looking to convention instead of duty. We abandon nature and hand ourselves over to the mob — never good advisers in anything, and in this matter, as in all others, utterly inconsistent. People see a man bearing his grief bravely: they call him unloving and cold-hearted. They see a man collapse and cling to his dead: they call him womanish and weak. Everything, therefore, should be brought back to reason. And nothing is more foolish than to court a reputation for sorrow and give tears your official sanction; for I hold that in a wise man some tears fall by consent, and others by their own force.

"Here is the difference. When the first news of a bitter loss strikes us — when we hold the body that will soon pass from our arms to the funeral flames — then tears are wrung from us by nature's own necessity: the life-force, struck by grief's blow, shakes the whole body, and the eyes with it, pressing out the moisture that lies within. Tears like these are forced out, against our will. Different are the tears we allow to escape when we linger in memory over those we have lost. There is a certain sweet sadness in them — a pleasant voice remembered, a warm conversation, the busy devotion of the old days — and then the eyes loosen, as it were, with joy. This kind of weeping we permit; the other kind overpowers us.

"So there is no reason why, just because a group of people is standing in front of you or sitting at your side, you should either dam up your tears or pour them out. Held back or let flow, they are never so disgraceful as when they are faked. Let them come naturally. Tears can fall from eyes that are calm and at peace; they often flow without touching the wise man's authority — with such measure that they show no lack of feeling and no lack of self-respect. We can, I assure you, obey nature and keep our dignity. I have seen men worthy of reverence at the burial of their own dear ones — love written plainly on their faces even with the whole apparatus of mourning stripped away — with nothing in their bearing beyond what genuine emotion allowed. There is a gracefulness even in grief. The wise man should cultivate it: in tears, as in everything else, there is such a thing as enough. It is the unwise whose sorrows, like their joys, overflow.

"Accept what is inevitable with an unruffled spirit. What can happen that is past believing? What that is new? How many men at this very moment are arranging funerals! How many are buying grave-clothes! How many are mourning after you yourself have finished mourning! Every time you reflect that your boy has ceased to be, reflect also on man — who holds no sure promise of anything, whom Fortune does not necessarily escort all the way to old age, but lets go at whatever point she pleases. Do speak of the departed often, and cherish his memory as much as you can. The memory will come back to you all the more often if it comes without bitterness; no one enjoys spending time with a sorrowful companion, much less with sorrow itself. And whatever things he said, whatever little jokes — child as he was — gave you pleasure to hear, recall them again and again; tell yourself with confidence that he could have made good on the hopes you, his father, had for him. To forget the beloved dead, to bury their memory along with their bodies, to weep for them extravagantly and afterward scarcely think of them — that is the mark of a soul beneath a man's. That is how birds and beasts love their young: the affection flares up hot, almost to madness, and cools completely when its object dies. That does not suit a man of sense. Keep remembering; stop mourning. And I do not in the least approve of Metrodorus's remark — that there is a certain pleasure akin to sadness, and that at times like these one ought to hunt it down. Those are Metrodorus's actual words. I have no doubt how you will feel about them; for what is baser than to chase pleasure in the very middle of mourning — no, by means of mourning — and to hunt among your own tears for something that feels good? And these are the men who accuse us of too much strictness, who slander our teaching as harsh — because we say grief should either not be admitted into the soul at all, or should be shown the door at once. But which is harder to believe, which more inhuman: to feel no grief at the loss of a friend, or to go hawking after pleasure in the middle of grief? What we Stoics advise is honorable: when emotion has drawn a moderate flow of tears and has, so to speak, ceased to boil, the soul is not to be surrendered to grief. But what do you mean, Metrodorus — that grief should come with a blending of pleasure? That is the candy method of pacifying children; that is how we quiet a crying infant, by pouring milk down its throat.

"Even at the moment your son's body is on the pyre, or your friend is drawing his last breath, you will not let your pleasure pause — you would rather tickle your grief with it? Which is more honorable: to remove grief from your soul, or to invite pleasure in to keep grief company? Invite, did I say? No — chase after it, and out of grief's own hands. 'There is a certain pleasure related to sadness,' he says. We Stoics may say that; you may not. The only good you recognize is pleasure, and the only evil, pain — and what kinship can there be between a good and an evil? But suppose such kinship exists: is now, of all times, the moment to dig it up? Shall we ransack grief itself, to see what delight and pleasure might be found around it? Some remedies, beneficial for one part of the body, cannot be applied to another, because there they would be offensive and unfit; what would do good in one case at no cost to decency becomes shameful because of where the wound sits. Aren't you likewise ashamed to cure sorrow with pleasure? No — this sore spot needs harsher treatment. Say this instead: no sensation of evil can reach a man who is dead — for if it can reach him, he is not dead. Nothing, I say, can hurt a man who is nothing; if he can be hurt, he is alive. Do you think him badly off because he no longer exists, or because he still exists as somebody? No torment can come to him from not existing — for what can be felt by one who is not there? — and none from existing, since he has escaped the greatest disadvantage death carries: not existing at all.

"And let us say this, too, to the man who mourns and misses one taken young: measured against eternity, young or old, we all live equally short lives. Of all time, less comes to us than anyone could call least — for 'least' is at any rate some fraction, but this life of ours is next to nothing. And yet — fools that we are — we spread it out as if it were broad and grand!

"I have written you all this not because you should expect a cure from me this late — it is clear to me that you have already told yourself everything you will read here — but to rebuke you even for the brief delay in which you slipped away from your true self, and to brace you for what comes next: rouse your spirit against Fortune, and keep watch for her missiles — not as if they might come, but as things that are bound to come."

Farewell.

Letter 100: On the Writings of Fabianus

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You write that you read Fabianus Papirius's book, The Duties of a Citizen, with the greatest eagerness, that it fell short of your expectations — and then, forgetting you are dealing with a philosopher, you start criticizing his prose.

Suppose your claim is true — that he pours his words out rather than placing them. Let me tell you at the start that this trait has a charm of its own, a grace that belongs to a smoothly gliding style. It matters a great deal, I maintain, whether prose tumbles out or flows along. And there is a further difference here, which I'll make clear: Fabianus's words do not spill, to my mind — his words flow like a river, abundant without confusion, yet never slow. His style openly declares and announces that he has not spent long hours kneading his material and twisting it into shape. But even granting the facts as you'd have them: the man was building character, not sentences, and he wrote his words for the mind, not for the ear. Besides, had he been delivering them in person, you would have had no time to inspect the details — the whole would have swept you along. And as a rule, what pleases by its swiftness loses value when you take it in hand to read.

Even so, that very power of catching you at first sight is a great advantage, whether or not careful inspection turns up something to criticize. If you ask me, the writer who forces approval is greater than the one who earns it — though I know the second kind is safer; I know he can post surer guarantees for the future. A fussy, meticulous way of writing does not suit the philosopher. If he is timid about words, when will he ever be brave and steady? When will he ever show what he is made of? Fabianus's style was not careless; it was confident. That is why you will find nothing shoddy in his work: the words are well chosen but not hunted down; not wrenched out of order and inverted after the current fashion; and they carry distinction even though they come from ordinary speech. There you have honorable, splendid thoughts, not shackled into aphorisms, but spoken with room to breathe. Of course we will notice passages not pruned enough, not built with enough care, missing the polish that is in vogue nowadays; but survey the whole, and you will find no clever subtleties spun about nothing. There may be no variety of marbles, no water piped from room to room, no "poor man's rooms," none of the other devices luxury piles up when plain charm is not enough — but, as the common phrase puts it, it is a good house to live in.

Besides, opinions differ about style itself. Some want it filed smooth of every roughness; some take such pleasure in abruptness that wherever a passage happens to spread out smoothly, they would deliberately break it up, scattering the closing words so the sentences land where you least expect. Read Cicero: his style is all of a piece; it moves at a measured pace, gentle without going soft. Asinius Pollio's, by contrast, is bumpy, jerky, quitting where you least expect it. Cicero always brings things to rest gradually; Pollio drops them mid-stride — except in the very few places where he holds to a fixed rhythm and a single pattern.

You say next that everything in Fabianus strikes you as commonplace, lacking lift; I hold that he is free of any such fault. His style is not commonplace — it is calm, fitted to his peaceful and well-ordered mind: not low, but level. It lacks the orator's punch and spur, which is what you are looking for, and the sudden jolt of epigrams. But look, please, at the whole work — how well ordered it is. There is a distinction in it. His style does not possess dignity; it suggests it.

Name someone you would rank above Fabianus. Cicero, say — whose books on philosophy are nearly as numerous as Fabianus's own. Granted; but it is no small thing to be less than the greatest. Or Asinius Pollio, say. Granted again; I will content myself with answering: it is a distinction to be third in a field this great. Add Livy too, if you like — for Livy wrote dialogues that belong to history as much as to philosophy, and works that deal with philosophy outright. I will yield on Livy as well. But consider how many writers Fabianus outranks, if only three surpass him — and those three the greatest masters of eloquence.

But, you may say, he does not offer everything. His style is elevated but not muscular; it flows abundantly but lacks force and sweep; it is not crystal, though it is clear. "You would look in vain," you object, "for any savage denunciation of vice, any brave words in the face of danger, any proud defiance of Fortune, any scornful jab at ambition. I want to see luxury rebuked, lust condemned, willfulness broken. Give me the orator's edge, the tragedian's height, the comedian's fine touch." You want him to lean on the smallest thing of all — phrasing; but he has sworn allegiance to the greatness of his subject, and eloquence follows him like a shadow, without his aiming at it.

Our author will not, I grant, examine every detail, or drill into it, or weigh and press each separate word. I admit it. Plenty of phrases will fall short or fail to strike home, and at times the style will slide along lazily. But there will be light everywhere throughout the work; there will be long stretches that never tire the reader. And finally, he offers you this: he makes it plain that he meant what he wrote. You will understand that his aim was for you to know what pleased him — not for him to please you. All of it works toward progress and toward sanity, with no fishing for applause.

I do not doubt that his writings are just as I have described them, though I am reaching back for him rather than holding a firm memory; the general flavor of his work stays with me — not from a careful recent reading, but in outline, the way things do after an acquaintance of long ago. But certainly, whenever I heard him lecture, this is how his work struck me: not dense, but full — the kind that would fire young men of promise and rouse their ambition to become like him, without making them despair of surpassing him. And that seems to me the most helpful encouragement of all; for it is disheartening to awaken in a man the desire to emulate while taking away the hope of it. At any rate, his language flowed freely, and though you might not approve of every detail, the whole was noble.

Farewell.

Letters 101–102

Letter 101: On the Futility of Planning Ahead

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Every day and every hour show us what nothings we are. Some fresh piece of evidence reminds us of the frailty we keep forgetting — and forces us, right in the middle of planning for eternity, to glance over our shoulder at death.

You ask what this gloomy opening means? It means Cornelius Senecio, a distinguished and capable Roman knight — you knew him. He had climbed from small beginnings to real money, and the rest of the road ran downhill in front of him. Rank grows more easily than it gets started, and money comes slowest while it still lives next door to poverty; it limps along until it has crawled clear. Senecio was right on the border of wealth, carried there by two very powerful assets, either of which could have made him rich by itself: he knew how to make money, and he knew how to keep it.

He was a man who lived very simply and took care of his health and his fortune with equal attention. He called on me early in the morning, as usual. Then he spent the whole day, right up to nightfall, at the bedside of a friend who was seriously and hopelessly ill. He ate a pleasant dinner. Then a sudden, violent attack of quinsy seized him, and with the breath squeezing through his swollen throat, he barely lasted until daybreak. Within a very few hours of performing every duty of a sound and healthy man, he was gone. A man who was venturing money by land and by sea, who had entered public life and left no kind of business untried, was snatched from the world at the very peak of his success, at the very moment the money was pouring into his coffers.

  Graft your pear trees now, Meliboeus — set out your vines in their rows!

How foolish it is to lay out plans for a life when you don't even own tomorrow! What madness, to build long-range hopes! "I'll buy and build, lend money and call it in, collect offices and honors — and then, old and full of years, I'll retire to a life of ease." Believe me: everything is doubtful, even for the fortunate. No one has any right to promise himself anything out of the future. The very thing we're holding slips through our fingers, and Fortune cuts into the very hour we're cramming so full. Time does roll on by a fixed law — but it rolls in the dark. What good is it to me that nature's course is certain, when my own is not?

We plan long voyages, and homecomings postponed until we've wandered every foreign shore. We plan for military service, and for the slow rewards of hard campaigning. We angle for a governorship, then for the office after that — and the whole time, death is standing right beside us. But since we never think of it except as something that happens to the neighbors, the examples of mortality press in on us daily and stay in our minds only as long as they surprise us.

Yet what is more foolish than being surprised that something which can happen every day has happened on some particular day? There is indeed a limit set for each of us, fixed exactly where the unbending law of fate has fixed it — but none of us knows how close he stands to that limit. So let us set our minds in order as though we had come to the very end. Let us postpone nothing; let us balance life's books each day. The greatest flaw in life is that it is always unfinished, always with some part of it put off. The man who puts the finishing touches on his life every day is never short of time. And yet out of that shortage come fear, and a craving for the future that eats away at the mind. Nothing is more wretched than dangling over the question of how things will turn out. How much is left? What kind will it be? — and the troubled mind flutters with a fear it can't even account for.

How do we escape this churning? One way only: if our life stops straining forward and gathers itself into itself. The only man anxious about the future is the man whose present is doing him no good. But when I have paid my soul what I owe it, when a well-balanced mind knows that a day is no different from eternity — then, whatever days and whatever problems are still to come, the soul looks out from a great height and laughs heartily to itself at the endless parade of the ages. What disturbance can the shifts and instability of Fortune cause, if you stand certain in the face of everything uncertain?

So begin at once, my dear Lucilius, to live — and count each separate day as a separate life. The man who has prepared himself this way, whose daily life has been a rounded whole, is easy in his mind. But those who live on hope alone watch the immediate future slip out of their grasp again and again, while greed creeps in to take its place, along with the fear of death — a curse that lays a curse on everything else. Out of that fear came the most degraded prayer on record, in which Maecenas accepts weakness, deformity, and finally the torture of the cross, so long as his life is stretched out through all of it:

  Cripple my hand, cripple my foot,
  pile a hump on my crooked back,
  shake my teeth loose in my head —
  all is well, if my life remains.
  Keep it for me, I beg you,
  even if I sit on the sharp cross itself!

There he is, praying for the thing that would be the most pitiable fate in the world if it actually happened — and begging for an extension of his torment as though he were begging for life! I would consider him utterly contemptible if he'd been willing to live right up to the moment of crucifixion. "No," he cries, "break my body if you must, so long as the breath stays in the battered, useless wreck. Maim me — just give me a little more time in the world, misshapen and deformed as I may be. Nail me up, if it comes to that, and set my seat on the sharp cross." Is it worth pressing down on your own wound, worth hanging stretched out on a gibbet, just to postpone the one thing that is the cure for troubles and the end of all punishment? Is it worth weakening yourself to that point just to keep breathing — breath you'll only have to give up anyway? What could you wish for Maecenas except that heaven show him mercy? What is the meaning of such spineless, disgraceful verse? What is the meaning of cutting deals with panic? Of begging for life so abjectly? He can't ever have heard Virgil read the line:

  Tell me — is death really such a misery?

He asks for the extremity of suffering and — harder still to bear — for its prolongation, its extension. And what does he gain by it? Merely a longer existence. But what kind of life is a drawn-out death? Can you find anyone who would rather waste away in pain, dying one limb at a time, letting his life out drop by drop, than die once and be done with it? Can you find any man willing to be fastened to the accursed tree — already sick, already deformed, chest and shoulders swollen with ugly tumors — and draw breath after breath through long-drawn-out agony? He would have had plenty of reasons to die before he ever mounted the cross!

Deny it now, if you can: nature is very generous in making death unavoidable. Many men have stood ready to strike bargains even more shameful — to betray their friends so they could live a little longer, or to hand over their own children to disgrace so they could keep enjoying the daylight that had witnessed all their sins. We must shake off this greed for life, and learn that it makes no difference when your suffering comes, because sooner or later you are bound to suffer. The point is not how long you live, but how nobly. And often, living nobly means you cannot live long.

Farewell.

Letter 102: On the Intimations of Our Immortality

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

A man is annoying when he shakes you out of a pleasant dream — he's spoiling a pleasure that may be unreal but looks and feels exactly like the real thing. Your letter has done me the same injury. It pulled me back abruptly from a meditation I was absorbed in and ready to carry further, if I'd been allowed. I was taking pleasure in investigating the immortality of souls — more than that, in believing it. I was lending a ready ear to the great authors, who not only approve that most welcome doctrine but promise it. I was giving myself over to a noble hope. I was already weary of myself, already starting to look down on the broken fragments of this existence, feeling that I was bound for that infinity of time, that inheritance of eternity — when your letter arrived and woke me, and I lost my lovely dream. But once I've settled accounts with you, I'll go looking for it and get it back.

At the beginning of your letter you remarked that I hadn't worked through the whole problem — the one where I was trying to prove a doctrine of our school: that the renown which comes to a man after death is a good. You said I hadn't answered the standard objection: "No good can be made up of things that are separate and distinct; yet renown is made up of exactly such things." What you're asking about, my dear Lucilius, belongs to another chapter of the same subject, and that's why I had set those arguments aside — them, and others covering the same ground. As you know, logical questions get tangled up with ethical ones. So I handled the essential part, the part that bears on conduct: whether it's foolish and useless to concern ourselves with what lies beyond our last day; whether our goods die with us and nothing is left of the man who is no more; whether any profit can be gained, or pursued in advance, from something we won't be able to feel when it comes.

All of that bears on conduct, so it went in under its proper topic. But what the logic-choppers say against the idea had to be sifted out separately, and so it was set aside. Now that you demand answers to all of it, I'll examine their claims and refute them one by one. But my rebuttals will be impossible to follow without one preliminary remark. And what is it? Just this: some bodies are continuous, like a man. Some are composite — a ship, a house, anything that results from joining separate parts into a single whole. And some are made up of members that remain distinct, each one standing separate — like an army, a crowd, or a senate. The people who make up such bodies are united by law or by function, but by their nature they are distinct individuals. What else do I need to lay down beforehand? Only this: we hold that nothing is a good if it is composed of separate things. A single good must be held together and governed by a single soul; the essential quality of each single good must itself be single. This can be proved on its own whenever you like; for now it simply had to be stated, because our own weapons are being hurled back at us.

The opponents argue like this: "You say no good can be made up of separate things? Yet this renown of yours is nothing but the favorable opinion of good men. Reputation doesn't consist of one person's remarks, and disgrace doesn't consist of one person's disapproval; in the same way, renown doesn't mean we've merely pleased one good man. To constitute renown, the agreement of many distinguished and praiseworthy men is required. But that agreement is the verdict of a number of people — that is, of persons who are distinct. Therefore it is not a good.

"You say, again, that renown is the praise given to a good man by good men. Praise means speech; speech is utterance with a particular meaning; and utterance, even from the lips of good men, is not in itself a good. Not everything a good man does is a good. He shouts applause, he hisses disapproval — but no one calls the shouting or the hissing a good, even if his conduct as a whole is admired and praised, any more than anyone would applaud a sneeze or a cough. Therefore renown is not a good.

"Finally, tell us this: does the good belong to the one who praises, or to the one who is praised? If you say it belongs to the one who is praised, you're on as foolish an errand as claiming that my neighbor's good health is my own. But praising worthy men is an honorable action; so the good belongs entirely to the man doing the praising — the man performing the action — and not to us, the ones being praised. And that was the whole question under discussion."

Now let me answer these objections one at a time, and quickly. The first question is still whether any good can be made up of separate things — and there are votes on both sides. But does renown really need many votes? It can be satisfied with the verdict of one good man: it is one good man who decides that we are good. "What!" comes the retort. "Would you define reputation as the esteem of one individual, and disgrace as one man's spiteful chatter? Glory, as we understand it, is something broader — it demands the agreement of many." But "the many" and "the one" stand on different footing here. Why? Because if the good man thinks well of me, that amounts in practice to being thought well of by all good men — for they would all think the same, if they knew me. Their judgment is one and the same; truth stamps it identically. They cannot disagree, which means they would all hold the same view, being unable to hold different ones. "One man's opinion," you say, "isn't enough to create glory or reputation." In the first case, one judgment is a universal judgment, because all of them, if asked, would judge alike. In the other case, people of dissimilar character hand down divergent verdicts. You'll find tangled emotions there — everything doubtful, shifting, untrustworthy. Do you suppose the many could ever hold one opinion? Even one man among them doesn't hold one opinion. With the good man, it is truth that produces conviction, and truth has one face and one function; the crowd agrees only on unsound ideas, and the false are never steady — they are irregular and clashing.

"But praise," says the objector, "is nothing but an utterance, and an utterance is not a good." When they define renown as praise bestowed on the good by the good, what they're referring to is not an utterance but a judgment. A good man may say nothing at all; but if he decides that a certain person deserves praise, that person is praised. Besides, praise is one thing and praise-giving another; the giving requires the voice as well. That's why no one speaks of a "funeral praise" but of a eulogy — a giving of praise — because its whole function depends on speech. When we say a man is worthy of praise, we are promising him men's goodwill not in words but in judgment. So even the good opinion of a man who silently approves of someone good is praise.

Praise, as I've said, is a matter of the mind rather than of speech; speech merely brings out the praise the mind has already formed and publishes it to the crowd. To judge a man worthy of praise is to praise him. When our tragic poet sings that it is a wonderful thing "to be praised by a well-praised hero," he means a hero worthy of praise. And when an equally venerable poet says "praise nurtures the arts," he doesn't mean praise-giving — that ruins the arts. Nothing has corrupted oratory, and every other art that plays to an audience, so much as popular approval. Reputation cannot exist without words; but renown can be content with men's judgments and complete without a syllable being spoken. It is satisfied not only with silent approval, but even in the face of open protest. Here, in my opinion, is the difference between renown and glory: glory rests on the judgments of the many; renown, on the judgments of the good.

"But whose good is it," comes the reply, "this renown, this praise given to a good man by good men? Does it belong to the one praised, or to the one who praises?" To both, I say. It is my good, as the one being praised, because nature made me to love all men, and I rejoice in having done good deeds, and I congratulate myself on finding men who express their sense of my virtues with gratitude. That they are grateful is a good for the many — but it's a good for me too. My spirit is arranged so that I can count other men's good as my own — above all the good that I myself have caused. And this good belongs also to the ones who give the praise, for it is exercised through virtue, and every act of virtue is a good. My friends could not have gained this blessing if I hadn't been the right sort of man. So being praised when you deserve it is a good belonging to both sides — just as a just decision is a good both for the one who renders it and for the one in whose favor it is rendered. Do you doubt that justice is a blessing to its possessor as well as to the man who receives his due? To praise the deserving is justice; therefore the good belongs to both.

That's answer enough for these dealers in fine print. But our purpose shouldn't be to argue cleverly, dragging philosophy down from her majesty into petty quibbles like these. How much better to follow the open, straight road than to lay out a winding course for yourself that you'll have to retrace with endless trouble! Argumentation of that kind is nothing but experts juggling with each other.

Tell me instead how much in keeping with nature it is to let the mind reach out into the boundless universe. The human soul is a great and noble thing; it accepts no limits except the ones it shares with the gods themselves. First of all, it will not accept a lowly birthplace — Ephesus, or Alexandria, or any spot still more crowded with people and thickly packed with houses. The soul's homeland is the whole sweep that circles the height and breadth of the sky — the entire rounded dome under which lie land and sea, under which the upper air divides the human from the divine and joins them too, and where all the sentinel stars stand their turns on watch. And the soul will not put up with a narrow allowance of time. "All the years are mine," it says. "No age is closed to great minds; all time lies open to the progress of thought. When the day comes to separate the heavenly from its earthly mixture, I will leave the body here, where I found it, and take myself back to the gods of my own accord. I am not cut off from them even now — only held back, in a heavy earthly prison."

These delays of mortal existence are the prelude to a longer and better life. The mother's womb holds us for ten months, preparing us not for the womb itself but for the open world we are delivered into once we're finally fit to draw breath and survive in the open air. In just the same way, through all the years that stretch from infancy to old age, we are ripening toward another birth. A different beginning waits for us, a different state of things. As yet we can bear the light of heaven only in rare glimpses. So look ahead without fear to that appointed hour — the last hour of the body, but not of the soul. Look around at everything lying about you as if it were luggage in a roadside inn: you must travel on. Nature searches you as bare on the way out as on the way in. You cannot take away more than you brought — in fact, you must throw away most of what you carried into life. You will be stripped of this outer skin, your last protection; stripped of the flesh; drained of the blood that runs and circulates through your body; stripped of the bones and sinews, the framework of these frail and temporary parts.

This day, which you fear as your last, is your birthday into eternity. Lay down your burden — why hang back? — as if you hadn't once before left a body that was hiding you. You cling to it, you struggle; well, at your first birth too, it took your mother's great labor to set you free. You weep and wail — and that very weeping happens at birth as well, though then it was forgivable: you came into the world ignorant of everything and utterly unpracticed. When you left the warm, sheltering protection of your mother's womb, a freer air breathed on your face; then you flinched at the touch of a rough hand, and you stared in amazement at unfamiliar things, still soft and knowing nothing.

But now it is nothing new for you to be separated from what you were once a part of. Let these limbs go calmly — they're of no more use — and lay aside the body you have lived in so long. It will be torn apart, buried out of sight, and worn away. Why be downcast? This is what always happens: when we are born, the afterbirth perishes. Why love such a thing as if it were your own possession? It was only your covering. The day will come that tears you out and leads you away from this foul, reeking womb of a world. Withdraw from it now, as much as you can. Withdraw from pleasure, except what is bound up with the essential and important things of life; grow a stranger to it even now, and turn your thoughts to something nobler and higher. One day the secrets of nature will be laid open to you, the haze will be shaken from your eyes, and bright light will stream in on you from every side.

Picture the blaze when all the stars mingle their fires. No shadow will trouble that clear sky; the whole expanse of heaven will shine evenly — day and night take turns only in the lowest layer of the air. Then you will say that you lived your whole life in the dark, once you have seen the perfect light with your whole, perfected being — the light you now see only darkly, through vision cramped to its narrowest. And yet, far off as it is, you already gaze at it in wonder. What do you think that heavenly light will be, when you have seen it in its own home?

Thoughts like these let nothing mean settle in the soul — nothing low, nothing cruel. They insist that the gods are witnesses of everything. They command us to seek the gods' approval, to prepare ourselves to join them some day, and to plan for immortality. The man who has grasped this idea shrinks from no attacking army, isn't rattled by the trumpet-blast, isn't cowed by any threat. Why should a man feel fear, when he looks forward to death? Even the man who believes the soul lasts only as long as it is chained to the body, and scatters the moment it is released, still arranges his life so that he'll be useful even after death. For though he is taken from our sight, still

  again and again our thoughts run back to the hero,
  and the glory his line has won returns to the mind.

Consider how much good examples help us, and you will understand this: the memory of a great man is no less useful than his presence.

Farewell.

Letters 103–105

Letter 103: On the Dangers of Association with Our Fellow-men

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Why are you scanning the horizon for troubles that might come your way — and might just as easily never come at all? I mean fires, collapsing buildings, the kind of accidents that simply happen to us rather than plot against us. Watch out instead for the troubles that dog our steps and reach out to grab us. Accidents can be serious, but they're rare — a shipwreck, a carriage overturning. It is from man that man's daily peril comes. Arm yourself against that; keep your eye fixed on that. No evil is more common, none more persistent, none better at slipping past your guard. Even a storm gives warning before it breaks; houses creak before they crash; smoke announces the fire. But damage from a human being is instantaneous — and the closer it comes, the more carefully it is hidden.

You're wrong to trust the faces of the people you meet. They have the look of men and the souls of wild animals — except that with animals, only the first encounter is dangerous; they don't chase what they've already passed by. Nothing drives them to hurt you except necessity: hunger or fear forces them into a fight. But man delights in ruining man.

Still, think about the danger you face from man in order to work out the duty you owe as a man. Deal with others so as to do no harm, and you're less likely to be harmed. Share in everyone's joys, feel for them in their troubles, and remember what you should offer and what you should hold back. What will you gain by living this way? Not necessarily safety from their hands — but at least safety from their deceit. And as far as you can, take refuge in philosophy. She will hold you close, and in her sanctuary you will be safe — or at any rate safer than before. People only collide when they're traveling the same road. But you must never flaunt this philosophy of yours; worn with insolence and arrogance, it has been the ruin of many. Let philosophy strip away your faults, not sharpen your tongue for the faults of others. Don't let her stand aloof from the ordinary customs of mankind, or make it her business to condemn everything she herself doesn't do. A man can be wise without parading it, and without making enemies.

Farewell.

Letter 104: On Care of Health and Peace of Mind

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I've run off to my villa at Nomentum — fleeing what, do you suppose? The city? No: a fever that was quietly working its way into my system. It already had a grip on me. My doctor kept insisting that when the pulse is upset and irregular, throwing off its natural rhythm, the disease is under way. So I ordered the carriage brought around at once and insisted on leaving, over the protests of my wife Paulina, who tried to stop me. I remembered what my master Gallio said when a fever came over him in Achaia and he boarded a ship immediately: the disease, he insisted, was in the place, not in his body. That's what I told my dear Paulina, who is always urging me to take care of my health. I know her very life-breath comes and goes with mine, and so, in caring for her, I am beginning to care for myself. Old age has made me braver about enduring many things, but here I'm gradually losing that gift of age. For it occurs to me that inside this old man there is a young person too — and youth needs tenderness. Since I cannot persuade her to love me more heroically, she persuades me to cherish myself more carefully. And genuine feelings deserve to be indulged. Sometimes, even against weighty reasons, the breath of life must be called back and held at our very lips, at the cost of real suffering, for the sake of those we love — because a good man should live not as long as it pleases him, but as long as he ought. The man who doesn't value his wife or his friend enough to linger a little longer in life, who stubbornly insists on dying, is not a hero. He is self-indulgent.

The soul should give itself this order whenever the needs of loved ones require it: it should pause and humor those near and dear, not only when it merely wants to die, but even when it has already begun. It shows a great heart to come back to life for the sake of others, and noble men have done it more than once. But I think this, too, is kindness of the highest order: the greatest privilege of old age is the license to be careless about self-preservation and to spend life more daringly — yet you should guard your old age all the more carefully if you know that doing so is pleasing, useful, or dear to someone you love. There's no small joy and profit in it, either. What is sweeter than to be so valued by your wife that you become more valuable to yourself because of it? So my dear Paulina manages to make me answerable not only for her fears, but for my own.

You're curious how the travel prescription turned out? The moment I escaped the oppressive air of the city — and that dreadful reek of smoking kitchens which, once fired up, pour out a ruinous mess of steam and soot — I felt my health mending at once. And how much stronger do you think I felt when I reached my vineyards! Turned out to pasture, so to speak, I positively charged at my meals. I'm my old self again: no wavering weakness in my body, no fog in my brain. I'm getting back to work with all my energy.

But the place itself contributes little, unless the mind is fully its own master and can find seclusion at will even in the thick of business. The man who is forever choosing resorts and hunting for leisure will find something to distract him in every one of them. Socrates is said to have answered a man who complained that his travels had done him no good: "It serves you right — you traveled in your own company!" What a blessing it would be for some people, if they could only wander away from themselves! As it is, they are their own source of worry, vexation, demoralization, and fear. What's the profit in crossing the sea, in moving from city to city? If you want to escape your troubles, what you need is not a different place but a different self. Say you've reached Athens, or Rhodes; pick any city you fancy — what does its character matter? You'll be bringing your own.

Suppose you believe wealth is a good: then poverty will torture you, and — most pitiful of all — an imaginary poverty. You may be rich; but because your neighbor is richer, you'll consider yourself poor by exactly the amount you fall short of him. Suppose you believe public office is a good: you'll be galled every time another man is made consul, or made consul again; you'll burn with envy whenever you see a certain name repeated in the public records. Your ambition will be so frantic that if anyone at all is ahead of you, you'll feel you're running last. Or suppose you rate death as the worst of evils — when there is really nothing evil in it except what comes before it: fear. You'll be scared out of your wits by dangers imagined as much as real, tossed forever on a sea of illusion. What good will it do you to have

  threaded your way through all the towns of Argolis,
  a fugitive slipping through the thick of your enemies?

Peace itself will supply you with fresh alarms. Once the mind has taken a shock, it loses confidence even in the midst of safety; once it acquires the habit of blind panic, it can no longer provide even for its own preservation. It doesn't avoid danger — it runs from it. And we are more exposed to danger with our backs turned.

You may judge the loss of someone you love to be the heaviest of blows; yet that would be as foolish as weeping because the trees that delight your eye and beautify your home drop their leaves. Look on everything that pleases you as a plant in flower: enjoy it while it's in leaf, for one plant after another must fall and die, each in its season. But just as losing leaves is a light matter, because they grow again, so it is with losing those you love, the delights of your life: they can be replaced, even if they can't be reborn. "But the new friends won't be the same." No — and neither will you. You change with every day and every hour. In other people you see readily enough what time steals; in yourself the theft is hidden, because it doesn't happen in plain view. Others are snatched from our sight; we are being secretly stolen from ourselves. You won't consider any of this, won't put any salve on these wounds; instead you'll sow yourself a crop of worries by hoping one moment and despairing the next. If you're wise, mix the two: never hope without a dose of despair, never despair without a dose of hope.

What has travel, by itself, ever done for anyone? It has never restrained pleasure, bridled desire, checked bad temper, or crushed the wild assaults of passion; it has never rid the soul of any evil. It doesn't give us judgment, doesn't shake off our errors; it merely occupies our attention for a moment with novelty, the way children stop to gawk at something unfamiliar. What's more, it aggravates the instability of a mind that's already sick; the very motion makes it more restless and jittery. That's why the places we sought most eagerly, we abandon more eagerly still — like birds that flit off as soon as they've landed. What travel will give you is acquaintance with other nations. It will show you mountains of strange shape, unfamiliar stretches of plain, valleys watered by everlasting springs, the peculiarities of some notable river — how the Nile rises and swells in summer, how the Tigris vanishes underground, runs through hidden channels, and reappears at full force, or how the Maeander, that well-worn theme and plaything of the poets, coils in loop after loop, winding so close to its own channel before it flows on. But none of this information will make us better men, or sounder ones.

We should be spending our time in study instead, and with the masters of wisdom, learning what has been investigated but not yet settled. That is how the mind is rescued from the most wretched slavery and won over to freedom. As long as you don't know what to avoid and what to seek, what is necessary and what is superfluous, what is right and what is wrong, you are not traveling — you are wandering. All this rushing around will do you no good, for you travel in the company of your emotions, and your afflictions follow you. If only they were following you! They'd be farther away. As it is, you're not leading them — you're carrying them. So they press in on you from every side, chafing and galling. It's medicine the sick man should be shopping for, not scenery. Suppose someone has broken a leg or dislocated a joint: he doesn't board a carriage or a ship for foreign parts; he calls in a doctor to set the fracture or work the joint back into its socket. Well then — when the spirit is broken or wrenched in so many places, do you think a change of address can heal it? The trouble sits too deep to be cured by a journey. Travel makes no one a doctor or an orator; no art is learned simply by living somewhere.

Where does that leave us? Can wisdom, the greatest art of all, be picked up along the road? Believe me: travel as far as you like, and you will never get beyond the reach of desire, beyond the reach of temper, beyond the reach of fear. If you could, the whole human race would have set out on pilgrimage to the spot long ago. As long as you carry the causes of these ills around with you, they will weigh you down and wear you to skin and bone across every land and sea. Are you surprised that running away doesn't help? What you're running from is inside you. So fix yourself; lift the load off your own shoulders; and hold within safe limits the cravings that ought to be removed altogether. Scrub every trace of wickedness from your soul. If you want to enjoy your travels, cure your traveling companion. As long as that companion is greedy and grasping, greed will stick to you; keep company with an arrogant man, and your own swagger sticks too. Live with an executioner and you'll never shed your cruelty; make an adulterer your clubmate and he'll fan your basest appetites. If you want to be stripped of your faults, get far away from the models of those faults. The miser, the swindler, the bully, the cheat — the ones who would damage you just by standing near you — are inside you.

Move to better company, then: live with the Catos, with Laelius, with Tubero. And if you like Greek company too, spend your time with Socrates and Zeno — the one will teach you how to die if it becomes necessary, the other how to die before it becomes necessary. Live with Chrysippus, with Posidonius. They will introduce you to things earthly and things heavenly. They will order you to work at something more than neat turns of phrase mouthed for the entertainment of an audience; they will order you to harden your heart and rise above threats. The only harbor safe from this life's seething storms is this: contempt for the future, a firm stance, and a readiness to take Fortune's missiles full in the chest — not skulking, not turning your back. Nature brought us forth with brave spirits. Just as she planted ferocity in some animals, cunning in others, timidity in others, so she gave us a soaring, aspiring spirit that seeks the most honorable life, not the safest one — a spirit most like the soul of the universe, which it follows and imitates as far as mortal steps allow. This spirit pushes itself forward, sure of winning praise and esteem. It stands above everything, master of all it surveys, so it should bow to nothing: no task too heavy for it, nothing strong enough to weigh down a man's shoulders.

  Shapes dread to look upon, of toil and death

are not dreadful in the least to anyone who can face them with an unflinching gaze and pierce through the shadows. Many a thing held up as a terror by night is turned to ridicule by day. "Shapes dread to look upon, of toil and death": our Vergil put it perfectly. These shapes are dreadful not in reality but only "to look upon" — that is, they seem terrible; they are not. What is there in these apparitions, I ask you, half as fearsome as rumor makes out? Why on earth, my dear Lucilius, should a man fear toil, or a mortal fear death? I can think of countless men who assume that whatever they themselves cannot do is impossible, who insist that we Stoics talk bigger than human nature can deliver. But how much more highly I think of these men than they do! They can do these things — they simply decline to. Ask anyone who ever actually tried: whom have these tasks ever failed? Who didn't find them easier in the doing? It is not the difficulty that drains our confidence; it is our lack of confidence that creates the difficulty.

If you want a model, take Socrates: a long-suffering old man, storm-tossed through every hardship, yet unconquered by poverty (made heavier by burdens at home) and unconquered by toil, including the drudgery of military service. He was sorely tried at home too — think of his wife, with her rough manners and shrewish tongue, or his children, whose unruliness proved them their mother's sons more than their father's. And consider the times he lived in: war, or tyranny, or a democracy crueler than wars and tyrants. The war lasted twenty-seven years; then the state fell prey to the Thirty Tyrants, many of them his personal enemies. Last came that final condemnation on the gravest of charges: they accused him of corrupting the state religion and corrupting the young — of teaching the youth, they said, to defy the gods, defy the council, defy the state itself. After that, the prison, and the cup of poison. And all of it changed the soul of Socrates so little that it didn't even change his face. What a marvelous, rare distinction! He held that bearing to the very end: no one ever saw Socrates too elated or too downcast. Amid all the turbulence of Fortune, he was untroubled.

Do you want another case? Take the younger Marcus Cato, whom Fortune handled with even more hostility and more persistence. He withstood her at every turn, and at the end, at the point of death, he showed that a brave man can live in spite of Fortune and die in spite of her. His whole life was spent either in civil war or under a regime that was breeding one. And you may say that he, no less than Socrates, pledged himself to liberty in the middle of slavery — unless you happen to think Pompey, Caesar, and Crassus were liberty's allies! No one ever saw Cato change, however often the state changed. He kept himself the same in every circumstance: as praetor, in defeat, under accusation, in his province, on the speaker's platform, in the army, in death. In the republic's hour of terror — Caesar on one side with ten battle-ready legions and whole foreign nations at his call, Pompey on the other, content to stand alone against all comers, and the citizens leaning toward one or the other — Cato alone founded a party for the Republic. If you want a mental picture of that moment, imagine on one side the people and the whole mob, hungry for revolution; on the other, the senators and knights, the chosen and honored men of the state; and left standing between them, just these two: the Republic, and Cato.

I promise you, you will marvel when you see

  Atreus' son, and Priam, and Achilles, angry at them both.

Like Achilles, Cato scorns both factions and disarms them. And this is the vote he casts on the pair of them: "If Caesar wins, I kill myself; if Pompey wins, I go into exile." What was left to fear for a man who, in victory or defeat, had already sentenced himself as harshly as his most furious enemies could have? So he died by his own decision.

You see that toil can be endured: Cato led an army on foot through the African desert. You see that thirst can be endured: he marched over sun-scorched hills, dragging the remnants of a beaten army with no supply train, going without water, wearing a full weight of armor — and always the last to drink at the few springs they happened to find. You see that honor can be despised, and dishonor too: on the very day Cato lost the election, they say, he played a game of ball. You see that a man can be free of fear of the powerful: Cato attacked Caesar and Pompey at the same time, in an age when no one dared cross the one without currying favor with the other. You see that death can be scorned as easily as exile: Cato sentenced himself to exile, and finally to death — and to war the whole time.

And so, if only we're willing to pull our necks out of the yoke, we can keep just as stout a heart against terrors like these. But first and foremost we must refuse pleasures; they make us soft and feeble, they demand a great deal from us, and they drive us, in turn, to demand a great deal from Fortune. Second, we must spurn wealth: wealth is the badge of slavery. Give up gold and silver and whatever else burdens our richly furnished houses. Liberty cannot be had for free. If you set a high price on liberty, you must set a low price on everything else.

Farewell.

Letter 105: On Facing the World with Confidence

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I'm going to tell you some things to keep in mind if you want to live more safely. And I suggest you listen to these precepts exactly as if I were advising you how to keep your health at your country place in Ardea.

Consider what it is that goads man into destroying man: you'll find it is hope, envy, hatred, fear, and contempt. Of all these, contempt is the least harmful — so much so that many people have actually hidden behind it as a kind of cure. The man who despises you does you an injury, true, but then he moves on; no one goes on hurting, deliberately and persistently, a person he despises. Even on the battlefield, soldiers lying on the ground are passed over; men fight with those still on their feet. As for the greedy hopes of the wicked, you can avoid those as long as you own nothing that stirs another man's appetite, nothing that stands out. People will covet even small things, if they catch the eye or come along rarely.

You'll escape envy if you don't push yourself into public view, if you don't boast about what you have, if you know how to enjoy things privately. Hatred comes either from giving offense — and you avoid that by provoking no one — or it comes unprovoked, and there common sense will keep you safe. Yet unprovoked hatred has been dangerous to many; some people have been hated without ever having made an enemy. As for not being feared: a moderate fortune and an easygoing nature will see to that. Let people know you're the kind of man who can be wronged without danger, and that reconciling with you is easy and sure. Being feared is as much trouble at home as it is out in the world — as bad to be feared by a slave as by a free man, for everyone has strength enough to do you some harm. Besides, whoever is feared, fears: no one has ever managed to inspire terror and live with a peaceful mind.

That leaves contempt. The man who has made contempt part of his own equipment — who is despised because he chooses to be, not because he has to be — holds its measure in his own hands. Any inconveniences on that score can be dispelled by honorable pursuits, and by friendships with men who have the ear of a man of influence. Engage with such men, but don't get entangled — or the cure may cost you more than the risk. Nothing, though, will help you so much as keeping quiet: talk very little with others, and as much as possible with yourself. There is a certain charm in conversation, something subtle and coaxing, which — like wine or love — draws our secrets out of us. And no one keeps to himself what he hears. No one repeats only as much as he was told. The man who tells the tale will tell the name too. Everyone has someone he trusts with exactly what was entrusted to him. Even if each one curbs his chattiness and settles for a single listener, he'll end up with a nation — and what was a secret a moment ago becomes common talk.

The greatest contribution to peace of mind is this: never do wrong. People without self-control lead disturbed and stormy lives; their crimes are matched by their fears, and they are never at rest. After the deed they tremble; they are trapped. Their consciences won't let them attend to anything else, and keep hauling them back to answer for themselves. Whoever expects punishment receives it — and whoever deserves it expects it. A guilty conscience may find safety in something, but it finds ease in nothing; such a man assumes that even if he isn't caught yet, he may be caught soon. His sleep is troubled. Whenever he mentions another man's crime, he thinks of his own — never sufficiently blotted out, he feels, never sufficiently hidden. A wrongdoer sometimes has the luck to escape notice — never the assurance of it.

Farewell.

Letters 106–107

Letter 106: On the Corporeality of Virtue

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

My slowness in answering your letter was not because I was busy. Don't accept that excuse — not from me, not from anyone. I am free, and so is everyone who wants to be. No man is at the mercy of his affairs. He tangles himself up in them by his own choice, and then flatters himself that being busy is proof of a full and happy life. So why didn't I answer sooner? The question you asked me was being woven into the fabric of my book. You know I'm planning to cover the whole of moral philosophy and settle every problem that touches it. So I hesitated: should I make you wait until the subject came up in its proper place, or hand down a ruling out of order? In the end it seemed kinder not to keep someone waiting who has come from so far away. So I'm going to pull this question out of its sequence — and I'll also send you, without waiting to be asked, whatever belongs to the same family of problems.

What family is that? Questions where the knowing is pleasant rather than useful. Your question, for instance: is the good corporeal — a physical body? Well, the good acts, because it does us good; and what acts is a body. The good stirs the mind and, in a way, molds and shapes what is essential to it. The goods of the body are bodily; so the goods of the soul must be too, because the soul itself is a body. And so man's good must be a body, since man himself is one. I am badly lost if the things that sustain a man, that preserve or restore his health, are not physical — and therefore his good is a body. You won't doubt, I'm sure, that the emotions are bodies (let me wedge in one more topic that wasn't on the agenda) — anger, love, sternness — unless you doubt that they change our faces, knot our foreheads, relax our features, spread a blush, or drain the blood away. Well then? Do you think marks that show so plainly on the body are stamped there by anything but a body? And if the emotions are bodies, so are the diseases of the spirit — greed, cruelty, and all the faults that harden in our souls until they pass the point of cure. So evil is a body too, with all its branches — spite, hatred, pride. And so are the goods: first because they are the opposite poles of the bad, and second because they show you the same physical symptoms. Don't you see how courage makes the eye flash? How prudence pulls the face into concentration? How reverence produces a settled calm, joy a serenity, sternness a stiffness, gentleness an easing of the features? These qualities are bodies, then — they change the tone and shape of physical stuff, each exercising its power in its own kingdom.

Now, all the virtues I've named are goods, and so is everything they produce. And can you doubt that whatever can touch is a body?

  Nothing but body can touch or be touched,

as Lucretius says. The changes I've described could not affect the body without touching it; therefore they are bodies. Furthermore, anything with the power to move, force, restrain, or control is a body. Well? Doesn't fear hold us back? Doesn't boldness push us forward? Courage spur us on and give us momentum? Restraint rein us in and call us home? Joy lift our spirits, sadness drag them down? In short, everything we do, we do at the command of vice or virtue. And only a body can control or force another body. The good of the body is a body; a man's good is bound up with his bodily good; therefore it is a body.

Now that I've humored you, I'll say your line before you can: "What a board game we're playing!" We dull our fine edge on superfluous puzzles like these; they make men clever, not good. Wisdom is a plainer thing than that. In fact, it is clearly better to use our learning to improve the mind — instead of squandering philosophy itself the way we squander everything else, on things we don't need. We suffer from excess in everything, and we suffer from excess in learning too: we learn not for life but for the lecture hall.

Farewell.

Letter 107: On Obedience to the Universal Will

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Where is that good sense of yours? Where is that skill of yours at sizing things up? That greatness of soul? Have you really been reduced to torment by a trifle? Your slaves saw that you were absorbed in business and took it as their chance to run away. Fine. If your friends had deceived you — and by all means let them keep the name we wrongly gave them, so that their shame is the greater for failing to live up to it — if your friends, I say, had deceived you, all your affairs would be missing something. As it is, you have merely lost men who were damaging your own work and making you a burden to your neighbors. Nothing in this is unusual or unexpected. To be upset by such things is as absurd as complaining that you got splashed in the street or spattered with mud. The program of life is the program of a bathhouse, a crowd, a journey: some things will be thrown at you, and some will hit you by accident. Life is not a dainty business. You have set out on a long road: you are bound to slip, collide, fall, wear out, and cry "I want to die!" — which is to say, you are bound to lie. At one stage you will leave a comrade behind; at another you will bury one; at another you will be afraid for one. It is across stumbling ground like this that the rugged journey has to be traveled.

Does someone wish for death? Let the mind be prepared to meet everything. Let it know it has arrived at the heights where the thunder plays; let it know it has come to the place where

  Grief and avenging Cares have made their beds,
  where pale Disease lives, and gloomy Old Age.

These are the housemates you must spend your days with. Avoid them you cannot; despise them you can. And you will despise them, if you think ahead often and rehearse the future. Everyone walks bravely toward a danger he prepared for long in advance, and stands up even under hardship if he has practiced meeting it. It's the unprepared who panic at the smallest things. We must see to it that nothing takes us by surprise. And since everything is heavier when it is unfamiliar, constant reflection will guarantee that whatever the trouble is, you won't face it like a raw schoolboy.

"My slaves have run away from me!" Yes — and other men have been robbed, blackmailed, murdered, betrayed, trampled underfoot, attacked with poison or with slander. Name any trouble you like; it has happened to many. Beyond that, there are missiles of every kind hurled at us. Some are already planted in us; some are in the air and on their way at this very moment; some that were aimed at other men graze us instead. We should not be surprised at any of the conditions we were born into — conditions no one should complain about, because they are ordained equally for all. Yes, I say equally: for a man might have suffered even the thing he escaped. An equal law is not one that everyone has felt, but one that is laid down for everyone. Prescribe this sense of fairness for your own mind: let us pay the tax of our mortality without complaint.

Winter brings the cold: we must shiver. Summer comes back with its heat: we must sweat. Freak weather shakes our health: we must fall ill. In some places we will meet wild beasts — or men more destructive than any beast. Flood will cost us something, or fire will. We cannot change this order of things. What we can do is acquire stout hearts worthy of good men — hearts that endure chance bravely and fall into step with Nature. And Nature steers this world-kingdom you see around you by means of change: clear skies follow cloudy ones; after a calm comes the storm; the winds blow by turns; day follows night; part of the sky is rising while part is setting. Eternity is made of opposites.

It is to this law that our souls must adjust themselves; this they should follow, this they should obey. Whatever happens, assume it was bound to happen, and do not volunteer to rail at Nature. What you cannot fix, it is best to endure — and to attend, without complaining, on the God under whose guidance everything moves forward. It is a bad soldier who grumbles while following his commander. So let us take our orders briskly and cheerfully, and never desert the march of this most beautiful universe, into which everything we will ever suffer is already woven.

Let us address Jupiter, the pilot of this world-mass, in the words of our great Cleanthes — those most eloquent lines, which I will allow myself to render in Latin, following the example of the eloquent Cicero. If you like them, make the most of them; if they displease you, you'll know I was only doing what Cicero did:

  Lead me, Zeus, and you too, Destiny,
  wherever you have decreed that I shall go.
  I will follow without flinching. And if I falter
  and turn unwilling, I will follow anyway —
  groaning, enduring in vice what I might
  have done in virtue. Fate leads the willing soul,
  and drags the unwilling along.

Let us live that way, and speak that way: let Fate find us ready and eager. That is the great soul — the man who has handed himself over to Fate. And the weakling, the degenerate, is the man who struggles, who bad-mouths the order of the universe, and who would rather reform the gods than reform himself.

Farewell.

Letter 108

Letter 108: On the Approaches to Philosophy

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

The question you ask belongs to that class of subjects where the knowing is the whole point — we want the knowledge for its own sake. Still, because it does concern us, you are in a hurry. You are not willing to wait for the books I am arranging for you at this very moment, the ones that cover the whole field of moral philosophy. I will send them right away. But first let me write to you about how this eagerness to learn — and I can see you are on fire with it — ought to be managed, so it does not trip over its own feet. Knowledge is not to be grabbed at random, and subjects should not be gulped down wholesale in a greedy rush; you arrive at the whole by mastering the parts. The load should be fitted to your strength; do not shoulder more than you can properly carry. Take in not everything you want, but everything you can hold. Just get yourself a sound mind, and then you will be able to hold everything you want. The more a mind receives, the more it expands.

This, I remember, was the advice Attalus gave me back in the days when I practically laid siege to his classroom — first to arrive, last to leave. Even while he paced up and down, I would draw him into one discussion after another; for he did not merely make himself available to his students, he came out to meet them halfway. His rule was: "Teacher and student should share a single purpose — the one aiming to help, the other aiming to improve."
