Whoever studies under a philosopher should carry home one good thing every day: he should return each evening a sounder man, or at least on the way to becoming one. And he will, because it is one of philosophy's powers to help not only those who study her but even those who just spend time in her company. A man who walks in the sun gets a tan, whether or not that was the plan. A man who lingers in a perfume shop, even briefly, carries the scent out with him. And a man who keeps a philosopher's company is bound to pick up some benefit, even if he is careless about it. Note the word: careless — not defiant.

"What's this?" you say. "Don't we all know men who sat at a philosopher's feet for years without picking up the faintest tint of wisdom?" Of course I know such men — very persistent gentlemen, too, who really stuck at it. I don't call them students of the wise; I call them squatters. Some of them come to hear, not to learn, just as we are drawn to the theater to please our ears with a speech, a song, or a play. This crowd, you will find, makes up a large share of any philosopher's audience — people who treat the lecture hall as a comfortable place to park their leisure. They are not there to drop any of their faults, or to pick up a rule of life to measure their character against; they only want to give their ears a treat. Some even arrive with notebooks — not to take down the substance, but the phrases, so they can repeat them later to others with as little profit as they got themselves. Others are swept up by the ringing sentences and mirror the speaker's feelings with eager faces and eager minds — exactly like those Phrygian priests who work themselves into a frenzy on cue the moment the flute begins to play. But the true listener is seized and stirred by the beauty of the subject itself, not by the jingle of empty words. When some brave word is flung in the face of death, or some defiant jab at Fortune, we take delight and itch to act at once on what we have heard. Men are moved by such words, and they become what the words command — if only the impression stays in the mind, and if the crowd, that great discourager of honorable things, does not immediately ambush the noble impulse. Only a few manage to carry home the state of mind they were inspired with. Yet it is easy to rouse a listener to crave what is right, because Nature has laid the foundations and planted the seeds of virtue in every one of us. We are all born with these advantages; add the spark, and the good in us stirs as if it had been let out of chains. Haven't you noticed how the theater roars whenever some line is spoken whose truth we all recognize and confirm with one voice?

  The poor man lacks much; the greedy man lacks everything.
  The greedy man does good to no one — and most harm
  Of all to himself.

At verses like these your meanest miser claps and cheers, delighted to hear his own sins abused. Now imagine how much stronger the effect when such things come from a philosopher's mouth — when he weaves verses in among his wholesome teachings so they sink deeper into a beginner's mind. Cleanthes used to say: "Just as our breath makes a louder sound when it is driven through the long narrow tube of a trumpet and escapes through the flared opening at the end, so the tight constraints of verse make our meaning ring out clearer." The very same words are received more carelessly and strike us less when spoken in prose; but add meter — compress a noble idea into regular rhythm — and the selfsame thought comes hurtling at you with all its force. We talk endlessly about despising money; we give the longest speeches urging mankind to believe that true riches live in the mind, not in the bank account, and that the man who fits himself to slender means and makes himself wealthy on a little is the man who is truly rich. But our minds are struck harder by one verse like this:

  He needs but little who desires but little.

or:

  He has his wish, whose wish is only for
  What is enough.

When we hear words like these, we are driven toward admitting the truth.

Even men who think nothing is ever enough will marvel and applaud at such lines, and swear eternal hatred against money. When you see them in that mood, strike home. Keep after them, press this duty on them, and drop the double meanings, the syllogisms, the hair-splitting, and all the other sideshows of pointless cleverness. Preach against greed, preach against luxury; and when you see that you are making progress and getting through to your hearers, press harder still. You cannot imagine how much good a speech of that kind can do when you are bent on curing your listeners and wholly devoted to their best interests. A young mind is easily won over to love what is honorable and upright; truth, given a decent advocate, lays strong hands on those who can still be taught — the ones only lightly spoiled.

I can tell you, when I used to hear Attalus denouncing sin, error, and the evils of life, I often felt pity for the whole human race, and I looked on Attalus as a noble and majestic being, standing above our mortal height. He called himself a king; to me he was more than a king, because he was qualified to pass judgment on kings. And when he began to praise poverty, and to show what a useless and dangerous burden everything is that goes beyond our need, I often wanted to walk out of that lecture hall a poor man. Whenever he lashed our pleasure-chasing lives and praised a clean body, a modest table, and a mind free of unnecessary pleasures — never mind unlawful ones — I felt the urge to put limits on my food and drink. And some of those habits have stayed with me, Lucilius. For I had laid out my whole life in a set of grand resolutions; and later, when I returned to a citizen's duties, I did keep a few of them. That is why I have sworn off oysters and mushrooms forever: they are not food at all, but relishes designed to bully a full stomach into eating more — the darlings of gourmands who stuff themselves past what they can digest: down it goes quickly, and up it comes quickly! That is why, all my life, I have avoided perfume: the best scent for the body is no scent at all. That is why my stomach is a stranger to wine. That is why, all my life, I have shunned the baths, convinced that stewing the body and sweating it thin is as useless as it is soft. Other resolutions I have broken — but broken in such a way that where I gave up total abstinence, I have kept to a limit that sits right next door to abstinence. Perhaps that is even harder; for some things it is easier for the will to cut off completely than to enjoy in moderation.

Since I have started telling you how much hotter my rush toward philosophy was in youth than my pace has been in old age, I will not be ashamed to confess the passion Pythagoras once lit in me. Sotion used to explain why Pythagoras refused to eat animals, and why Sextius, in later days, refused as well. Their reasons were different, but each was a noble reason. Sextius held that a man has food enough without resorting to blood, and that cruelty becomes a habit once butchering is practiced for pleasure. He added that we ought to shrink the sources of our luxury, and he argued that a varied diet works against health and is wrong for our bodies. Pythagoras, on the other hand, held that all beings are related, and that there is a system of exchange among souls, which pass from one bodily shape into another. If you believe him, no soul ever perishes or even pauses in its work, except for the tiny interval while it is being poured from one body into the next. We may leave open the question of when, and after how many changes of station, a soul returns to human form after wandering through so many dwellings; in the meantime, the doctrine made men afraid of guilt — afraid of parricide — since without knowing it they might be attacking a parent's soul, wounding it with knife or with teeth, if the kindred spirit happened to be lodging for a while in that piece of flesh. When Sotion had laid out this teaching, and filled it in with proofs of his own, he would say: "You don't believe that souls are dealt out to one body after another, and that what we call death is merely a change of address? You don't believe that in cattle, in wild beasts, in the creatures of the deep, there may linger the soul of what was once a man? You don't believe that nothing on this earth is annihilated, but only changes its neighborhood — that animals too have their cycles, and their souls travel an orbit of their own, no less than the heavenly bodies wheeling in their fixed circuits? Great men have believed this. So keep your own opinion if you like, but hold the whole question open in your mind. If the doctrine is true, abstaining from flesh is purity; if it is false, it is thrift. And what does it cost you to believe it? All I am taking from you is the diet of lions and vultures."

I was soaked in this teaching, and I began to abstain from animal food; and at the end of a year the habit was as pleasant as it was easy. I began to feel that my mind was quicker — though today I would not swear to you whether it really was. You ask how I came to give it up? It happened this way. My young days fell in the early part of the reign of Tiberius Caesar. Certain foreign rites were then being stirred up, and abstaining from particular animal foods was counted as evidence of devotion to the strange cult. So at the request of my father — who did not fear an accusation, but who hated philosophy — I went back to my old habits; and, to be honest, it did not take much persuading to get me to dine more comfortably.

Attalus used to recommend a pillow that does not give way beneath the body; and old as I am, I still use one so hard it takes no imprint from your weight. I have told you all this to show you how fiercely beginners charge toward the highest ideals, when someone does his part by urging them on and fanning the flame. But things do go wrong — partly through the fault of teachers who train us to argue rather than to live, and partly through the fault of students who come to their teachers to sharpen their wits rather than their souls. And that is how the study of wisdom has turned into the study of words.

Now, it makes a great deal of difference what you have in mind when you come to a subject. If a future language scholar is examining Virgil, he does not read that noble line

  Time flies away, and cannot be recalled

in this spirit: "We must wake up. Unless we hurry, we will be left behind. Time rolls swiftly on and rolls us along with it; we are swept forward, ignorant of our destination; we lay all our plans for the future, and lounge at our ease on the edge of a cliff." No — he points out that whenever Virgil speaks of the speed of time, he uses the word "flies":

  The choicest days of our unhappy life
  Fly first; disease and bitter age press on,
  And toil, till death's hard hand sweeps all away.

The man who reads these lines as a philosopher takes the words in their proper sense. "Virgil never says time goes," he comments, "but always time flies, because flying is the fastest of all movements — and our best days are always the first to be snatched away. Why then are we so slow to spur ourselves on, to try to match the pace of the swiftest thing there is?" The good flies past, and the worse takes its place. Just as the purest wine pours from the top of the jar while the heaviest dregs settle at the bottom, so in our human life the best comes first. Shall we let other men drink off the best, and keep the dregs for ourselves? Let this line fasten itself to your soul; let it satisfy you as if an oracle had spoken it:

  Each choicest day of our unhappy life
  Flies first.

Why the "choicest" day? Because what is coming is unsure. Why the "choicest"? Because in youth we can learn — we can bend minds still supple and ready toward nobler purposes; because this is the season for work, for keeping the mind busy with study and the body busy with useful effort. What comes after is more sluggish and short of spirit — closer to the end.

So let us strive with our whole heart, ignoring the attractions along the roadside; let us struggle with a single purpose, or we will discover too late — after we have been left behind — just how fast that flying time moves which we can never stop. Let every day, the moment it arrives, be welcomed as the choicest, and be made our own property. What is fleeing must be caught. But the man who reads those same lines with a scholar's eye never reflects that our first days are the best because disease is on the way, because old age is already pressing down and hanging over our heads while we are still daydreaming about our youth. He notes instead that Virgil always sets disease and age side by side — and, to be fair, rightly so, for old age is a disease without a cure. "And besides," he says to himself, "look at the epithet Virgil attaches to age: he calls it bitter" —

  Disease and bitter age press on.

And elsewhere Virgil says:

  There pale disease has made its home, and bitter age.

There is no reason to marvel that each man harvests from the same field material suited to his own pursuits: in one and the same meadow the cow finds grass, the dog hunts the hare, and the stork hunts the lizard. When Cicero's book On the State is opened by a philologist, a language scholar, and a follower of philosophy, each man runs his investigation his own way. The philosopher is astonished that so much could be said in it against justice. The philologist opens the same book and notes this: that there were two Roman kings, one with no father and one with no mother — for no one can settle who Servius's mother was, and Ancus, Numa's grandson, has no father on record. He also notes that the officer we call dictator, and read about in our histories under that title, was in the old days named the magister populi, "master of the people" — the name survives today in the augurs' records, and the proof is that the man the dictator chose as his second-in-command was called the magister equitum, "master of the horse." He will remark, too, that Romulus met his end during an eclipse; and that even under the kings there was a right of appeal to the people — so the pontiffs' register says, and Fenestella, among others, agrees. When the language scholar unrolls the very same volume, he enters into his notebook the forms of words: he notes that Cicero uses reapse for re ipsa, "in actual fact," and sepse just as often for se ipse, "he himself." Then he turns to shifts in usage. Cicero, for instance, writes: "since his interruption calls us back from the very calx" — for the finish line in the racecourse that we now call the creta, the chalk, was in the old days called the calx, the lime. Next he collects verses of Ennius, especially the ones about Africanus:

  A man whom neither friend nor foe could pay
  The worth of all his labors and his deeds.

From this passage the scholar declares he can infer that the word opem once meant not merely "aid" but "effort" — for Ennius must mean that neither friend nor foe could pay Scipio a reward equal to his efforts. Then he congratulates himself on discovering the source of Virgil's lines:

  Over whose head the mighty gate of heaven
  Thunders,

remarking that Ennius stole the idea from Homer, and Virgil stole it from Ennius; for preserved in this same book of Cicero's, On the State, is a couplet of Ennius:

  If it is right for a mortal to climb to the regions of heaven,
  Then for me alone the sky's great gate swings open.

But I must not slip into playing philologist or language scholar myself while I am busy with another errand. So here is my advice: all study of philosophy, and all reading, should be applied to the goal of living the happy life. We should not go hunting for archaic or far-fetched words, for eccentric metaphors and figures of speech; we should look for teachings that will help us — brave and spirited sayings that can be turned into fact on the spot. We should learn them in such a way that words become deeds. And I hold that no man has done mankind a worse turn than the one who studies philosophy as a trade to be sold — who lives one way and teaches another. Men like that, guilty of every fault they denounce, walk around as advertisements for useless training. A teacher of that sort can help me no more than a seasick pilot can help in a storm. The pilot has to grip the tiller while the waves are tossing him; he has to wrestle with the sea itself; he has to strip the sails when the storm rages — what good to me is a helmsman who is terrified and vomiting? And how much greater, do you think, is the storm of life than any storm that ever tossed a ship! One must steer, not talk.

Every word these men utter and juggle before an admiring crowd belongs to someone else. Plato said it, Zeno said it, Chrysippus said it, or Posidonius, or one of that whole company of Stoics, as numerous as they are excellent. I will show you how men can prove that the words are their own: let them do what they have been saying. And now, since I have delivered the message I wanted you to have, I will satisfy your eagerness — but I will save the complete answer to your question for a fresh letter, so that you do not come tired to a subject that is thorny, and that demands an attentive and painstaking ear.

Farewell.

Letters 109–111

Letter 109: On the Fellowship of Wise Men

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You wanted to know whether one wise man can help another. After all, we say the wise man is fully stocked with every good — he has reached perfection. So how could anyone help a man who already possesses the Supreme Good?

Good men are good for each other. Each gives the other's virtues a workout and keeps his wisdom in fighting trim. Each needs someone to compare notes with, someone to investigate things alongside. Skilled wrestlers stay sharp by sparring; a musician is spurred on by a player of equal skill. The wise man, too, needs his virtues kept in motion — and just as he prods himself into action, he can be prodded by another wise man. What does one wise man actually do for another? He quickens his impulses. He points out openings for honorable action. Beyond that, he can work out some of his own ideas in company and share what he has discovered. For even the wise man will always have something left to discover, some territory where his mind can push into new ground.

Bad men harm bad men. Each drags the other lower — stoking his anger, applauding his rudeness, praising his pleasures. Bad men hit bottom when their faults have thoroughly blended, when their wickedness has been pooled, so to speak, into a partnership. Flip that over, and you see how a good man helps a good man. "How?" you ask. He brings the other joy. He strengthens his confidence. Each looks at the other's calm, and the delight of both grows. Besides, they teach each other things they know — because the wise man is not all-knowing. And even if he were, someone might still devise shortcuts and point them out, ways of getting the whole business across more easily. The wise man helps the wise man not by his own strength alone, mind you, but by the strength of the man he's helping. The other could, it's true, develop his own gifts by himself — but even the swiftest runner is helped by a pacesetter.

"The wise man doesn't really help the wise man; he helps himself. Here's the proof: strip the second man of his own powers, and the first accomplishes nothing." On that logic you might as well say sweetness isn't in the honey — since it's the eater whose tongue and palate must be built for that kind of food, so that the flavor delights him instead of putting him off. There are people so altered by disease that honey tastes bitter to them. Both men need to be in good health: one to give the help, the other to be fit to receive it.

Again they say: "Once a thing has reached its highest heat, adding more heat is pointless. Once a man has reached the Supreme Good, a helper is pointless. Does a fully stocked farmer beg supplies from his neighbors? Does a soldier armed head to toe for battle need more weapons? Neither does the wise man — he is equipped and armed for life." My answer: even a thing heated to the highest degree needs continued heat to stay there. And if you object that heat maintains itself, I say you're comparing very different things. Heat is one single thing; helpfulness comes in many kinds. And heat doesn't need more heat added in order to be hot — but the wise man cannot hold his mind at its own high level without the company of friends of his own kind, people to share his goodness with. What's more, there is a kind of mutual friendship among all the virtues. So the man who loves the virtues of his peers, and shows his own to be loved in return, is being helpful. Like delights in like — especially when the likeness is honorable and the approval runs both ways. And besides, only a wise man can prompt another wise man's soul intelligently, just as only a human being can prompt a human being rationally. As reason is needed to prompt reason, so perfect reason is needed to prompt perfect reason.

Some say we're also "helped" by people who hand us the so-called indifferent things — money, influence, security, and all the other prized or essential props of living. Argue that way, and the biggest fool in town will count as a helper of the wise. But helping really means prompting the soul in accordance with Nature — by the excellence of the prompter and the excellence of the prompted alike. And this can't happen without profit to the helper too, for in training another man's excellence you necessarily train your own. But set aside the supreme goods and their causes; wise men can still be useful to each other. For a sage merely to discover another sage is itself worth wanting, since everything good is naturally dear to the good man. One feels at home with a good man the way one feels at home with oneself.

To prove the point, I have to move from this question to a neighboring one. People ask whether the wise man will weigh his opinions alone, or turn to others for advice. He is forced to do the latter whenever he takes up public or household business — everything, so to speak, that is mortal. In such matters he needs outside advice, just like the doctor, the pilot, the attorney, the courtroom pleader. So the wise will sometimes help the wise; they will persuade each other. But even in the great matters — the divine matters, as I've been calling them — the wise man can be useful too: by discussing honorable things in common, by contributing his thoughts and ideas. Besides, it accords with Nature to show affection for our friends and to rejoice in their progress as if it were absolutely our own. If we don't do that, even virtue — which stays strong only by exercising our perceptions — will not stay with us. Now virtue tells us to arrange the present well, to think ahead about the future, to deliberate and apply the mind. And a man who brings a friend into the council can apply his mind and think a problem through more easily.

So the wise man will seek out either the perfect sage or someone who has progressed to the very border of perfection. And the perfect sage will help us further if he backs up our deliberations with ordinary good sense. They say men see farther in other people's affairs than in their own. In ordinary people that's a defect of character: self-love blinds them, and fear in the hour of danger robs them of a clear view of what's useful — a man only begins to be wise when he is at ease and free of fear. But even wise men see certain things more clearly in another's case than in their own. Moreover, the wise man, together with his fellow sage, will make good on that sweetest and most honorable of proverbs — always desiring the same things, and always refusing the same things. It is a noble outcome when two draw the load with an equal yoke.

There — I've answered your demand, though it really belongs in my volumes On Moral Philosophy. Reflect, as I keep telling you, that there's nothing in topics like these for us except mental gymnastics. For I come back again and again to the same thought: "What good does this do me? Make me braver now, more just, more restrained! I haven't yet had the chance to use my training; I still need the physician. Why ask me to carry useless knowledge? You promised great things — test me, watch me! You assured me I'd be unterrified though swords flashed around me, though the blade's point grazed my throat. You assured me I'd be at ease though fires blazed around me, though a sudden whirlwind snatched up my ship and carried it across the whole sea. Give me the course of treatment that lets me despise pleasure and glory. Afterward you can teach me to untangle knotty problems, settle doubtful points, see through what's obscure. For now, teach me what I need to know!"

Farewell.

Letter 110: On True and False Riches

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

From my villa at Nomentum, I send you greetings and this instruction: keep a sound spirit within you — in other words, win the blessing of all the gods. For the man who has become a blessing to himself is assured of their grace and favor. Set aside, for now, the belief certain people hold that each of us has a god assigned as a kind of attendant — not a god of full rank, but one of the lower grade, one of those Ovid calls "plebeian gods." But while you set the belief aside, remember this: our ancestors who held it were being Stoics without knowing it, for they assigned a Genius or a Juno to every individual. Later on we'll investigate whether the gods have time on their hands to manage the affairs of private individuals. In the meantime, know this: whether we're allotted special guardians or abandoned to Fortune, you can lay no heavier curse on a man than to pray that he be at war with himself.

Not that there's any reason to ask the gods to turn hostile toward someone you think deserves punishment. They are hostile to such a man already, I maintain — even when he seems to be rising by their favor. Look carefully at how our affairs actually stand, not at what people say about them, and you'll understand that our so-called evils are more likely to help us than to hurt us. How often has "misfortune" been the source and beginning of happiness! How often have privileges we received with deep thanksgiving built themselves into a staircase up the side of a cliff — lifting still higher men who were already eminent, as if they had previously stood somewhere they could fall from in safety! Yet even that fall holds nothing evil, if you look at the end of it, beyond which nature lays no man lower. The universal limit is close; yes, close by is the point where the prosperous man is toppled — and the point where the unfortunate man is set free. It is we who stretch both distances out, lengthening them with our hopes and our fears.

If you're wise, though, measure everything by the human condition: keep your joys and your fears alike on a short leash. In fact, it's worth not rejoicing over anything for long, so that you won't fear anything for long. But why am I narrowing the scope of this evil? There is no reason to think anything is to be feared. Everything that rattles us and keeps us aflutter is empty. Not one of us has sifted out the truth; we've simply handed our fear along to one another. No one has dared to walk up to the thing that frightened him and get to know his fear — even the good hidden inside it. That's why falsehood and vanity keep their credit: nobody refutes them. Let's decide it's worth looking closely. Then it will become clear how fleeting, how unsure, how harmless the things we fear really are. The confusion in our spirits is just what Lucretius diagnosed:

  Like boys who cower, frightened, in the dark,
  we grown-ups fear things in the light of day.

Well then — aren't we more foolish than any child, we who are afraid in daylight? Except you were wrong, Lucretius: we are not afraid in the daylight. We have turned everything into darkness. We see neither what harms us nor what helps us; all our lives we blunder ahead, and we neither stop nor step more carefully for that. You see what madness it is to charge forward in the dark. And yet we're bent on it — on making ourselves harder to call back, from farther away. We don't know where we're going, but we rush at full speed in the direction we're straining toward.

Yet the light can begin to dawn, if we're willing. Only one thing can bring it about: acquiring, through knowledge, a familiarity with things divine and human — not just splashing in it but steeping ourselves in it; reviewing the same principles over and over, even when we understand them, and applying them again and again to ourselves; investigating what is good, what is evil, and what has been falsely labeled one or the other; investigating, finally, honor and baseness, and Providence. And the range of human intelligence isn't fenced in even there. It may explore beyond the universe itself — where it is headed, where it arose, and the ruin toward which all nature is hurrying so fast. We have dragged the soul away from that divine contemplation and down into mean, low work: to slave for greed; to abandon the universe and its frontiers and, under the orders of masters who will try anything, to pry beneath the earth and dig up whatever evil it can find there — unsatisfied with what was laid out in front of it for free.

God, the Father of us all, placed whatever was meant for our good ready at our hands. He didn't wait for us to search; he gave it voluntarily. What would harm us, he buried deep in the earth. We have no one to complain about but ourselves: against the will of Nature, who hid them, we hauled up into the light the materials of our own destruction. We have signed our souls over to pleasure — and serving pleasure is the beginning of every evil. We have surrendered ourselves to ambition and reputation, and to other aims equally idle and equally useless.

So what am I urging you to do? Nothing new — we aren't hunting cures for new diseases — but first of all this: figure out for yourself what is necessary and what is superfluous. What is necessary will meet you everywhere; what is superfluous always has to be hunted down, and hunted hard. But don't congratulate yourself too much for despising gilded couches and jeweled furniture. What virtue is there in despising useless things? Admire yourself when you've come to despise the necessities. It's no great feat to live without royal pomp, to feel no craving for thousand-pound boars or flamingo tongues or the other absurdities of a luxury so jaded with whole roasted game that it picks different bits from different animals. I'll admire you when you've learned to scorn even plain bread; when you've convinced yourself that grass grows for the needs of men as well as cattle; when you've discovered that the treetop can fill the belly — that belly into which we cram precious things as if it could keep what it receives. We should feed the stomach without being fussy about it. What does it matter what the stomach takes in, when it must lose everything it takes? You delight in the carefully arranged delicacies caught on land and sea — some prized when they reach the table fresh, others when they've been fed and fattened so long they nearly melt and can barely hold their own grease. You like their subtly engineered flavors. But I promise you: once those choice, elaborately seasoned dishes have gone down, one and the same corruption overtakes them all. Do you want to despise the pleasures of eating? Then look at where eating ends. I remember some words of Attalus that drew applause from the whole room:

"Riches deceived me for a long time. I would catch some gleam of them here and there and stand dazed. I assumed their hidden power matched their visible shine. Then, at a certain elaborate entertainment, I saw embossed work in silver and gold equal to the wealth of an entire city; colors and tapestries contrived to match objects worth more than gold or silver, brought not only from beyond our borders but from beyond our enemies' borders; here files of slave-boys picked for their training and their beauty, there throngs of slave-women, and everything else a prosperous and mighty empire could put forward after taking inventory of its possessions. What is this, I said to myself, but a deliberate stirring-up of human cravings that are inflamed enough already? What is the point of this whole parade of money? Did we gather here to learn what greed is? For my part, I walked out with less craving than I walked in with. I came to despise riches — not because they are useless, but because they are petty. Did you notice how that whole program, slow-moving and carefully staged as it was, played itself out inside a few hours? Has a business filled up this entire life of ours that could not fill up one whole day?

"Another thought struck me too: the riches seemed as useless to the men who owned them as to the men who watched. So now, whenever some spectacle of that sort dazzles my eyes — whenever I see a splendid palace, a polished corps of attendants, handsome bearers carrying a litter — I say to myself: Why wonder? Why gape? It's all show. These things are shown to you, not given to you — and even while they please, they are passing away. Turn instead to the true riches. Learn to be content with little, and call out with courage and greatness of soul: 'We have water, we have porridge — let us compete in happiness with Jupiter himself.' And why not, I ask you, make that challenge even without the porridge and the water? To hang the happy life on silver and gold is disgraceful — and to hang it on water and porridge is just as disgraceful. 'But what would I do without them?' someone says. You want the cure for want? Hunger ends hunger. Otherwise, what real difference is there between being enslaved by big things and being enslaved by small ones? What does it matter how little it is that Fortune can refuse you? Even your porridge and water can fall under someone else's control. Freedom belongs not to the man over whom Fortune has little power, but to the man over whom she has none at all. Here is what I mean: you must crave nothing, if you would go head to head with Jupiter — for Jupiter craves nothing."

That is what Attalus told us. If you're willing to think on these things often, you will work not to seem happy but to be happy — and to seem happy to yourself, rather than to others.

Farewell.

Letter 111: On the Vanity of Mental Gymnastics

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You asked me for a Latin word for the Greek sophismata. Many people have tried to pin a term on them, but no name has stuck — naturally enough, since the thing itself never won general acceptance among us, and so the name met resistance too. The word Cicero used seems the best fit to me: he called them cavillationes — quibbles. A man who surrenders himself to them weaves plenty of clever little traps, but he makes no progress toward actual living. He doesn't come out braver, or more restrained, or loftier in spirit.

But the man who has practiced philosophy as his own cure becomes great-souled, full of confidence, unbeatable — and bigger the closer you get to him. It's the same with high mountains: from far off they look lower than they are, and only when you approach do you see plainly how tall the peaks stand. That, my dear Lucilius, is the true philosopher — true in deeds, not in tricks. He stands on high ground: admirable, lofty, genuinely great. He doesn't stretch himself or walk on tiptoe like people who cheat their height, wanting to look taller than they are; he is content with his own size. And why shouldn't he be content, when he has grown to a height Fortune's hands can't reach? He is above earthly things and equal to himself in every situation — whether the current of life runs smooth, or he is tossed on rough and desperate seas. That kind of steadiness can never be won by the hair-splitting I just mentioned. The mind plays with quibbles and profits nothing; all it does is drag philosophy down from her heights onto the flat ground. The real philosopher climbs; the quibbler just walks in circles at sea level.

I wouldn't forbid you to practice such exercises now and then — but save them for a time when you want to do nothing. Their worst feature is this: they generate a charm of their own, gripping and holding the soul with a show of subtlety — while matters of real weight are calling for us, and a whole lifetime is barely enough to learn one single principle: to despise life. "What? Surely you meant 'control,' not 'despise'?" No. Controlling life is the second task. No one has ever controlled his life rightly who has not first learned to despise it.

Farewell.

Letters 112–113

Letter 112: On Reforming Hardened Sinners

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Believe me, I want your friend shaped and trained just as much as you do. But he has come to us in a very hardened state — or rather, and this is the harder problem, in a very soft state, broken down by bad habits of long standing.

Let me give you an illustration from my own hobby. Not every vine will take a graft. If the vine is old and decayed, or thin and feeble, it will either refuse the cutting outright or fail to feed it; it won't take the graft into itself and make it part of its own body, won't adapt itself to the new wood's quality and nature. So what we usually do is cut the vine off above the ground, so that if nothing comes of the first attempt, we can try our luck a second time and graft below the ground.

Now, this man you've written to me about has no strength. He has pampered his vices. He has managed to go flabby and hard at the same time. He cannot receive reason, and he cannot nourish it. "But," you say, "he wants it. He asks for it of his own free will." Don't believe him. I don't mean he's lying to you — he genuinely thinks he wants it. Luxury has upset his stomach, that's all; give it a little while and the two of them will be reconciled. "But he says he's disgusted with his old way of living." Very likely. Who isn't? People love their vices and hate them at the same time. The right season to pass judgment on him will come when he has given us a guarantee that he really hates luxury. As things stand now, he and luxury are merely not on speaking terms.

Farewell.

Letter 113: On the Vitality of the Soul and Its Attributes

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You want my written opinion on a question our school likes to kick around: whether justice, courage, foresight, and the other virtues are living things. It's quibbles like this, my dear Lucilius, that have convinced the public we sharpen our wits on useless targets and fritter away our free time on debates that will profit no one. Still, you asked, so I'll lay out the question as our school sees it. For myself, I confess to a different creed: I think some topics are best left to the sort of man who goes around in white shoes and a Greek cloak. But the arguments that stirred the old masters — or that the old masters stirred up — I will explain to you.

The soul, everyone agrees, is a living thing, because it is what makes us living things, and because "living things" take their very name from it. Now, virtue is nothing but the soul in a certain condition; therefore virtue is a living thing. Again: virtue acts, and no action happens without impulse; and whatever has impulse must be a living thing, because nothing except a living thing has impulse. The comeback to this is: "If virtue is a living thing, then virtue itself possesses virtue." Of course it does — it possesses its own self! Just as the wise man does everything by means of virtue, virtue does everything by means of itself. "In that case," they say, "all the arts are living things too, and all our thoughts, and everything the mind grasps. It follows that many thousands of living things dwell in the cramped quarters of a man's heart, and that each of us is — or at least contains — a whole crowd of living creatures."

Stuck for an answer? Here it is: each of these will be a living thing, but they will not be many separate living things. Why not? I'll explain, if you'll lend my words your subtlety and your concentration. Each separate living thing must have a substance of its own. But all the things I just listed share a single soul; so each can be a living thing without their being many. I myself am a living thing and a man, but you can't say there are two of me on that account. Why? Because to be two, they would have to be two separate existences — one would have to be split off from the other to make a pair. Whatever is manifold inside a single whole falls under a single nature, and so it is single.

My soul is a living thing, and so am I, but we are not two persons. Why? Because the soul is a part of me. A thing will only be counted as something in its own right when it stands by itself. As long as it is part of something else, it cannot be regarded as separate. Why? I'll tell you: because what is separate must be its own thing — personal, whole, and complete in itself.

I've put myself on record as holding a different opinion. For if you accept this doctrine, then not only the virtues will be living things, but so will their opposite vices, and the emotions too — anger, fear, grief, suspicion. Push the argument further, and every opinion and every thought becomes a living thing. That is simply not admissible; for not everything a man does is the man himself.

"What is Justice, then?" people say. Justice is a soul holding itself in a certain attitude. "So if the soul is a living thing, Justice is one too." Not at all. Justice is a state of the soul — a kind of power the soul has — and that same soul shifts into various likenesses without becoming a different kind of animal every time it acts differently. Nor is what the soul produces a living thing.

If Justice, Courage, and the other virtues are actually alive, do they stop being living things and then start life over again, or are they living things forever? Virtues cannot cease to exist. Then there are many — no, countless — living things lodging in this one soul. "Not many," comes the answer, "because they are all attached to the one soul, parts and limbs of a single whole." Then the picture we're painting of the soul is a many-headed hydra, each head fighting and destroying on its own. And yet no head is a separate living thing; each is the head of a living thing, and the hydra itself is one single animal. Nobody ever believed the Chimaera contained a living lion or a living serpent; those were parts of the whole Chimaera, and parts are not living things. So how do you conclude that Justice is a living thing? "Justice is active and helpful," they reply. "What acts and helps has impulse, and what has impulse is a living thing." True — if the impulse is its own. But Justice's impulse is not its own; it comes from the soul.

Every living thing remains what it began as, until death. A man is a man until he dies, a horse a horse, a dog a dog; they cannot change into anything else. Now let's grant that Justice — defined as "a soul in a certain attitude" — is a living thing. Suppose it so. Then Courage is alive too, being "a soul in a certain attitude." But which soul? The one we just defined as Justice? The soul is kept inside the first creature and cannot cross over into another; it must live out its existence in the being where it began. Besides, one soul cannot belong to two living things, much less to many. And if Justice, Courage, Restraint, and all the other virtues are living things, how will they share one soul? They must have separate souls, or else they are not living things. Several living things cannot have one body — our very opponents admit that. Now, what is the "body" of Justice? "The soul," they concede. And of Courage? "The soul again." And yet there cannot be one body for two living things. "But the same soul," they answer, "puts on the guise of Justice, or Courage, or Restraint." That would work if Courage were absent while Justice was present, and Restraint absent while Courage was present. As it stands, all the virtues are there at the same time. So how can the individual virtues be living things, if you grant there is one single soul — and one soul cannot make more than one single living thing?

Again: no living thing is part of another living thing. But Justice is part of the soul; therefore Justice is not a living thing. By now I seem to be wasting effort on an acknowledged fact — this is a topic to laugh out of the room, not to debate. And no two living things are equal. Look at the bodies of all creatures: every one has its particular color, shape, and size. Among all the reasons to marvel at the genius of the divine Creator, I count this one: in all that abundance, nothing repeats. Even things that look alike turn out, on comparison, to be different. God has made the whole multitude of leaves we see, and each is stamped with its own pattern; all the many animals, and no two the same size — always some difference. The Creator has set himself the task of making things distinct — unlike and unequal. But the virtues, by your own argument, are all equal. Therefore they are not living things.

Every living thing acts on its own; but virtue does nothing on its own — it must act in partnership with a man. All living things are either rational, like men and gods, or irrational, like beasts and cattle. The virtues are certainly rational; yet they are neither men nor gods; therefore they are not living things. Every rational living thing sits inactive until some external impression stirs it; then impulse comes, and finally assent confirms the impulse. Let me explain what assent is. Suppose I ought to take a walk: I do walk, but only after giving myself the order and approving my own opinion. Or suppose I ought to sit down: I do sit, but only after the same process. Now, this assent is no part of virtue. Say the virtue in question is Prudence: how is Prudence going to assent to the opinion "I must take a walk"? Nature doesn't allow it. Prudence looks after the interests of its possessor, not its own; Prudence can neither walk nor sit down. So it has no power of assent, and it is not a rational living thing. And if virtue is a living thing, it must be rational; but it is not rational; therefore it is not a living thing.

If virtue is a living thing, and virtue is a Good — then isn't every Good a living thing? It is; our school professes exactly that. Now, saving your father's life is a Good; giving a judicious opinion in the senate is a Good; handing down just verdicts is a Good. Therefore the act of saving your father is a living thing, and so is the act of giving sound opinions. We have pushed this absurdity so far that you can't keep from laughing out loud: wise silence is a Good, and so is a frugal dinner; therefore silence is a living thing, and so is dining. And I will never stop tickling my own mind and making sport for myself with this dainty nonsense. Justice and Courage, if they are living things, are certainly earthly ones. And every earthly living thing gets cold and hungry and thirsty. Therefore Justice shivers, Courage goes hungry, and Kindness could use a drink!

And why stop there? Shouldn't I ask our honorable opponents what shape these creatures have? A man's? A horse's? A wild beast's? If they give them a round shape, like a god's, I shall ask whether greed and luxury and madness are equally round — for these, too, are "living things." If I find they've rounded those off as well, I'll go on to ask whether a modest walk is a living thing. By their own argument they'll have to admit it — and say that a gait is a living thing, and a round one at that!

Now, don't imagine I'm the first man in our school to speak from his own judgment instead of the rulebook. Cleanthes and his pupil Chrysippus could not agree on what walking is. Cleanthes held it was spirit transmitted from the ruling essence to the feet; Chrysippus, that it was the ruling essence itself. So why, following Chrysippus's own example, shouldn't every man claim his freedom and laugh down all these "living things" — so numerous that the universe itself can't hold them?

Someone might say: "The virtues are not many living things, and yet they are living things. Just as one individual can be both poet and orator without being two men, so these virtues are living things without being many. The soul is one and the same; it can be at once just and prudent and brave, holding itself in a certain attitude toward each virtue." The dispute is settled; we're agreed. For the moment I'll grant that the soul is a living thing — reserving my final vote for later — but I deny that the soul's acts are living creatures. Otherwise all words and all verses would be alive. If prudent speech is a Good, and every Good is a living thing, then speech is a living thing. A prudent line of poetry is a Good; every Good is a living thing; therefore the line of poetry is a living thing. And so "Arms and the man I sing" is a living thing — though they can hardly call it round, since it has six feet!

"This whole business we're discussing," you say, "is a tangled web." I split with laughter whenever I reflect that solecisms and barbarisms and syllogisms are living things, and, like a portrait painter, I give each one a suitable face. Is this what we discuss with knitted brow and wrinkled forehead? After this I can't say, with Caelius, "What melancholy trifling!" It's more than trifling; it's absurd. What all this buys you, I can't see. Why don't we discuss something useful and wholesome instead — how we may attain the virtues, and what road will take us to them?

Teach me, not whether Courage is a living thing, but that no living thing is happy without courage — without the strength, that is, to stand against hazards and to master every stroke of Fortune by rehearsing and anticipating her attack. And what is Courage? It is the impregnable fortress of our mortal weakness. The man who has walled himself inside it holds out, free of anxiety, through life's whole siege, because he is using his own strength and his own weapons. Here let me give you a saying of our philosopher Posidonius: "Never think yourself safe because you carry the weapons of Fortune. Fight with your own! Fortune supplies no arms against herself; men equipped against their enemies stand unarmed against Fortune."

Alexander, to be sure, harried and routed the Persians, the Hyrcanians, the Indians, and every other nation the East spreads out to the edge of the Ocean; but he himself, as he killed one friend and lost another, would lie in the dark mourning now his crime, now his loss. The conqueror of so many kings and nations was laid low by anger and grief! For he had made it his aim to win control of everything except his emotions. What enormous mistakes possess men who long to push the borders of empire beyond the seas, who count themselves most prosperous when their armies occupy many provinces and they can stitch new territory onto the old — and who know nothing of the kingdom that stands equal to heaven itself in greatness! The greatest command is command of yourself.

Let self-command teach me what a holy thing Justice is — the virtue that always looks to another's good and asks nothing for itself except the chance to be exercised. It should have nothing to do with ambition and reputation; it should be its own satisfaction. And before everything else, let each man convince himself of this: "I must be just without reward." And that's not enough; let him convince himself of this as well: "May I take pleasure in devoting myself, of my own free will, to this noblest of virtues." Let all his thoughts turn as far as possible from personal advantage. Don't look around for the reward of a just deed; a just deed in itself pays a still greater one. Drive deep into your mind what I said a moment ago: it makes no difference how many people know of your integrity. Those who want their virtue advertised are working for fame, not virtue. Aren't you willing to be just without being famous? In fact, you will often have to be just and disgraced at the same time. And then, if you're wise, let a bad reputation, well earned, be a delight to you.

Farewell.

Letters 114–115

Letter 114: On Style as a Mirror of Character

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You've been asking me why certain periods produce a rotten style of speaking — how a whole generation's talent can slide into the same vices, so that in one era the fashion is prose all puffed up and straining for power, and in another it's prose that minces along, modulated like a piece of concert music. You wonder why one age applauds ideas so bold you can hardly believe anyone dared them, while another goes in for choppy, insinuating phrases where you have to read in more meaning than actually reaches your ear. And why some epochs claimed the right to use metaphor shamelessly, as if there were no such thing as going too far.

Here is the answer, and you already know it — it's a saying you hear on the street, one the Greeks turned into a proverb: men's speech matches their lives. Just as each individual's way of acting speaks for him, so a period's way of speaking reproduces the general character of the time, whenever public morals have slackened and the public has given itself over to softness. Decadence in speech is proof of decadence in the city — provided it's popular and fashionable, not just the quirk of one or two individuals.

A man's talent cannot be one thing and his soul another. If the soul is healthy, well ordered, serious, and restrained, the talent is likewise sound and sober. When the soul goes bad, the talent is infected too. Haven't you noticed that when a man's soul has gone sluggish, his limbs drag and his feet shuffle? That if the soul is soft, you can spot the softness in the very walk? That a keen, confident soul quickens the step? That madness in the soul — or anger, which is madness's near relation — speeds the body's movements from walking to rushing?

And how much more must all this affect one's talent, which is woven through and through with the soul — shaped by it, taking its orders from it, receiving its laws from it.

How Maecenas lived is too well known to need retelling here: how he walked, how soft he was, how he loved putting himself on display — how determined he was that his vices should not go unnoticed. Well then: doesn't the looseness of his prose match his loose, ungirt clothing? Are his habits, his entourage, his house, his wife, any less on display than his sentences? He could have been a man of great powers, if he had gone at his work by the straight road — if he hadn't shrunk from being understood, if he hadn't been just as slack in his style as in everything else. What you find in his eloquence, you will see, is a drunk man's talk: winding, wandering, sprawling without limit.

Is there anything more unbecoming than this: "a stream and a bank all shaggy with long-tressed woods"? Or look how "men plow the channel with boats and, churning up the shallows, leave gardens in their wake." Or this: "he crimps his lady-locks, and bills and coos, and falls a-sighing, like a lord of the forest who offers prayer with drooping neck." Or: "an unredeemed crew, they hunt out folk at feasts, and lay siege to households with the wine-cup, and by hope exact death." Or: "a Genius could scarcely stand witness to his own festival"; or "threads of slender tapers and crackling meal"; or "mothers or wives robing the hearth."

Reading through words like these, can't you instantly picture him — the man who was forever parading through the city with his tunic hanging loose? Even when he was standing in for the absent emperor, he was in undress when they came to him for the day's password. The man who sat as judge on the bench, or spoke in public, or appeared at any official function with his cloak pulled up over his head, only the ears sticking out, like the millionaire's runaway slaves in the farce. The man who, at the very moment the state was locked in civil war — the city in crisis, under martial law — went about in public attended by two eunuchs, both of them more man than he was. The man who had only one wife and yet was married a thousand times.

Those sentences of his — so faultily joined, so carelessly tossed off, arranged in such deliberate defiance of common practice — announce that their author's character was equally strange, equally unsound, equally off the track. He does get high praise, and deserves it, for his humanity: he was sparing with the sword, he shrank from bloodshed, and the only field where he showed what his power could do was his self-indulgence. But he spoiled that genuine credit with these ridiculous fussy affectations of style. Look closely and you see he was not really gentle, but soft — the proof is in his twisted word order, his upside-down expressions, and those startling thoughts of his, which often contain something great but go nerveless in the telling. Too much success, you would say, had turned his head.

Sometimes the fault lies in the man, sometimes in his times. When prosperity has spread luxury far and wide, people begin with more careful attention to how they look. Next they go crazy over furniture. Then it's the houses: how to spread them over more ground, as if they were country estates; how to make the walls gleam with marble shipped across the seas; how to gild the ceiling until it matches the shine of the inlaid floors. From there the exquisite taste moves to the dinner table, where the game is to win applause by novelty and by scrambling the customary order of the courses — serving first what used to come last, so that departing guests get the dishes that once greeted them when they arrived.

Once the mind has gotten into the habit of despising the usual, of finding the customary cheap, it starts hunting for novelty in speech as well. Now it dredges up obsolete, antique words and shows them off; now it coins words nobody has heard, or mangles existing ones; now a bold, incessant use of metaphor becomes the signature of style, according to whatever fashion has just come in. Some writers chop their thoughts short, hoping to impress by leaving the meaning in doubt and making the listener suspect his own dullness. Some dwell on a thought and stretch it out. Others go right up to the edge of a fault — which a writer really must do, if he wants grandeur — but these actually fall in love with the fault itself. In short, wherever you see a corrupt style winning applause, you can be sure the character of the age has also strayed from the true standard.

Just as extravagant banquets and elaborate clothes are symptoms of a sick state, so a slack style — if it's popular — shows that the mind, which is where words come from, has lost its balance. And don't be surprised that corrupt speech is welcomed not only by the grubby crowd but by our cultivated set as well; the two differ in their clothes, not in their judgment. What you may more fairly wonder at is that approval goes not just to the products of vice, but to the vices themselves. It has always been so: no talent has ever won acceptance without something being pardoned. Name me any famous man you like; I can tell you what his age forgave in him, what his age deliberately overlooked. I can show you many men whose vices never hurt them, and more than a few who were actually helped by them. I can show you men of the highest reputation, held up as models for our admiration — and yet if you set out to correct their faults, you erase them, because the vices are so entangled with the virtues that they drag the virtues down with them.

Besides, style has no fixed laws. It is changed by the usage of the crowd, and never stays the same for long. Many orators go raiding earlier centuries for their vocabulary and talk in the language of the Twelve Tables — Rome's oldest law code. To them, Gracchus, Crassus, and Curio are too polished and too modern; so it's back to Appius and Coruncanius! Others, determined to use nothing but worn, everyday expressions, sink into flatness. Both classes are degenerate, each in its own way — and it's every bit as degenerate to use only words that are showy, high-flown, and poetical, avoiding whatever is familiar and in common use. The one fault, I'd say, is as bad as the other: the first crowd grooms itself beyond all reason, the second neglects itself beyond all reason; the former shave their legs, the latter won't even shave their armpits.

Now let's turn to the arrangement of words. Here — what an endless catalogue of faults I could show you! Some writers are all for abruptness and rough edges, deliberately jumbling anything that threatens to flow smoothly. They want a jolt at every transition; whatever lands unevenly on the ear they call strong and manly. With others it isn't so much an arrangement of words as a setting of them to music, so coaxing and soft is the glide of their style. And what shall I say of the arrangement where words are held back and back, until, long awaited, they barely slip in at the sentence's end? Or of that gently closing style, Cicero-fashion, easing down its slope with the same poised descent every time, and always to the same familiar rhythm? Nor do the faults stop at the shape of the sentences — thoughts too can be petty and childish, or cheap, or more daring than decency allows, or flowery and cloying, or built to end in nothing, delivering sound and nothing more.

Some one individual makes these vices fashionable — whoever rules the eloquence of the day — and the rest follow his lead and pass the habit around. So when Sallust was at his height, phrases were lopped short, words stopped where you least expected, and obscure brevity passed for elegance. Lucius Arruntius, a man of rare plainness of character, who wrote a history of the Punic War, was a card-carrying member of the Sallust school. Sallust has the phrase exercitum argento fecit — literally "he made an army with silver," meaning he raised troops with money. Arruntius fell in love with this and planted the verb "make" all through his book. In one place he "makes a rout" of our forces; in another Hiero, king of Syracuse, "makes a war"; in another the news "makes" the people of Panormus surrender to Rome. I only want to give you a taste — the entire book is stitched together out of stuff like this. What Sallust kept for occasional use, Arruntius made a constant, almost continuous habit — and no wonder: Sallust's phrases came to him; Arruntius went out hunting for them. That is what comes of copying another man's vices. Again, Sallust wrote of "wintering waters." Arruntius, in the first book of his Punic War, has a storm that "suddenly wintered"; elsewhere, wanting to say a year was unusually cold, he says "the whole year wintered"; elsewhere he sends off sixty light transports with only soldiers and essential crew aboard "while the north wind wintered" — and he keeps propping up passage after passage with the same metaphor. Somewhere Sallust says that amid civil war a man "sought fame for fairness and goodness"; Arruntius cannot hold himself back from announcing at once, in book one, that there were vast "remindings" of Regulus.

Faults like these, stamped into a style by imitation, are not necessarily signs of slack standards or a debased mind. A man's true faults are personal, homegrown, and you can read his temperament from them: an angry man talks angrily, an excitable man in a flutter, a soft man in a style with no push-back in it. You see the same tendency in the men who pluck or thin their beards, who shave the upper lip close while carefully cultivating the rest, who wear cloaks in outrageous colors and see-through togas, and who will not lift a finger to do anything that might escape notice. They want to provoke attention, to catch the eye; they'll accept even disapproval, so long as they get looked at. That is Maecenas's style, and the style of everyone else who strays off the road not by accident but knowingly and on purpose.

This comes from a great sickness in the soul. Just as with wine, the tongue doesn't stumble until the mind buckles under the load and gives way or betrays itself — so with this drunkenness of style (what else can I call it?): it troubles no one unless the soul has started to totter. So take care of the soul. From the soul come our thoughts, from the soul our words, from the soul our bearing, our expression, our very walk. When the soul is sound and strong, the style is vigorous, energetic, manly; when the soul falls, everything else comes down in the ruins.

  Keep the king safe, and one mind rules the swarm;
  lose him, and the bees break faith.

The soul is our king. While it is safe, all the other functions stay at their posts and obey orders; let the soul wobble even slightly, and they wobble with it. And once the soul has surrendered to pleasure, its powers and its actions go slack, and everything it undertakes comes from a source that is limp and unsteady.

To keep on with my comparison: the soul is sometimes a king and sometimes a tyrant. A king, when it honors what is honorable, watches over the welfare of the body in its charge, and gives that body no shameful, no degrading orders. But a soul that is unrestrained, ruled by desire, gone soft, turns kingship into that most hateful and dreaded thing — tyranny. Then it falls prey to the passions, which dog its steps: exhilarated at first, like a mob gorged on a public handout that will be its ruin, pawing at what it cannot consume. But when the disease has gradually eaten the strength away, when luxury has worked into marrow and sinew, such a soul gloats over the very limbs its overindulgence has made useless. Barred from its own pleasures, it watches other people's instead; it becomes the procurer and the spectator of the appetites that its own excesses have left it unable to feel. Its heap of delights is less sweet to it than bitter, because it can no longer push all the old delicacies down its overworked throat and stomach, because it can no longer whirl through its maze of eunuchs and mistresses — and it grieves that so large a share of its happiness is shut off by the limits of the body.

Isn't it madness, Lucilius, plain madness, that not one of us stops to reflect that he is mortal? That he is fragile? That he is, after all, one single person? Look at our kitchens — the cooks scurrying among all those fires. Is it for one belly, do you think, that all this bustle of food preparation goes on? Look at our vintage wines and the storehouses stacked with vintages of many generations. Is it for one belly, do you think, that we lay down wine sealed with the names of so many consuls, gathered from so many vineyards? Look how many regions of the earth are under the plow, how many thousands of farmers are tilling and digging. Is it for one belly, do you think, that crops are sown in Sicily and Africa? We would be sane, and our wants would shrink to reason, if each of us would take stock of himself — measure his body's needs while he's at it, and grasp how little he can actually consume, and for how short a time. But nothing will help you toward moderation so much as this frequent thought: that life down here is short and uncertain. Whatever you are doing, keep death in view.

Farewell.

Letter 115: On the Superficial Blessings

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

I wish, my dear Lucilius, that you would stop fussing so much over words and their arrangement; I have bigger things to commend to your care. Ask what to write, not how to write it — and ask even that not for the sake of writing it but of feeling it, so that you can press what you have felt deeper into yourself and, so to speak, set your seal on it. Wherever you notice a style that is too careful, too polished, you can be sure the mind behind it is equally absorbed in petty things. The truly great man speaks casually and easily; whatever he says, he says with assurance rather than with effort.

You know the type — the young dandies, beards and hair just so, fresh out of the bandbox. From them you will never get anything strong, anything solid. Style is the clothing of thought: if it has been trimmed and dyed and worked over, it tells you the mind has its flaws and blemishes too. Elaborate elegance is not the dress of a man.

If we were granted the privilege of looking into a good man's soul — oh, what a face we would see: how fair, how holy, how magnificent, how gracious and shining! Radiant here with justice and self-control, there with courage and wisdom. And besides these, thrift, moderation, endurance, refinement, kindness, and — hard as it is to believe — love of one's fellow men, that Good so rare among men: all shedding their glory over that soul. And forethought joined with fine taste, and rising out of them all, greatness of soul, the noblest virtue of the lot — heavens, what charm it would add, what authority and dignity! What a marriage of sweetness and power! No one could call such a face lovable without also calling it worthy of worship. If anyone ever did behold such a face, more exalted and more radiant than mortal eyes are used to, wouldn't he stop dead, as if struck by a visitation from above, and breathe a silent prayer — "May I be forgiven for having looked on this!"? And then, drawn on by the encouraging kindness of its expression, wouldn't we bow down and worship? After long gazing at a countenance so far above us, so far beyond anything we're used to seeing — gentle-eyed, yet flashing with living fire — wouldn't we then, in reverence and awe, come out with those famous lines of our poet Virgil:

  O maiden, what shall I call you? For your face
  is more than mortal, and your voice rings sweeter
  than any human tongue...
  Be gracious, whoever you are, and lighten
  this load we carry.

And such a vision will indeed be a present help and a relief to us, if we are willing to worship it. And that worship does not mean slaughtering fattened bulls, or hanging up offerings of gold and silver, or pouring coins into a temple treasury. It means a will that is reverent and upright.

There is not one of us, I tell you, who would not burn with love for this vision of virtue, if only he were allowed to see it. As it is, too much stands in the way — our sight is either pierced by excess glare or clogged with darkness. But if, just as certain drugs are used to sharpen and clear the eyesight, we are willing to clear the eye of the mind of what obstructs it, then we will be able to make out virtue even when it is buried in a body — even with poverty standing in front of it, even with lowliness and disgrace blocking the road. We will see that beauty, I say, though it be smothered in rags. And by the same token we will see straight through to evil and the deadness of a sorrow-laden soul, despite the broad glitter of riches flashing all around it, despite the false light of high office on one side and great power on the other beating hard against the eye.

Then we will finally be able to understand how contemptible the things we admire really are. We are like children, who count every toy a treasure, who hold necklaces bought for a penny dearer than their parents or their brothers. And what is the difference, as Aristo says, between us and the children — except that we grown-ups go crazy over paintings and sculpture, and our folly comes at a higher price? Children delight in the smooth speckled pebbles they pick up on the beach; we delight in tall columns of veined marble, hauled in from the sands of Egypt or the deserts of Africa to hold up some colonnade or a dining hall big enough to seat a city. We admire walls veneered with a thin skin of marble — knowing perfectly well what defects the marble is hiding. We cheat our own eyes; and when we have plated our ceilings with gold, what are we delighting in but a lie? For we know that under all that gilding lurks some ugly wood.

And this kind of surface decoration isn't confined to walls and ceilings. All those famous men you see strutting about with their heads in the air — their prosperity is gold leaf too. Look underneath, and you'll learn how much evil sits beneath that thin coating of titles.

Take the commodity that keeps so many magistrates and judges busy — that makes magistrates and judges: money. Ever since money came to be honored, the true honor of things has been in ruins. We become, by turns, merchants and merchandise; we ask not what a thing is but what it costs. We do our duty if it pays, and neglect it if it pays; we hold an honorable course as long as it feeds our expectations, ready to swing to the opposite tack the moment crooked conduct promises more. Our parents drilled the admiration of gold and silver into us; the craving, planted young, settled deep and grew as we grew. Then too the whole nation, at odds on everything else, agrees on this: this is what they respect, this is what they beg for their children, this is what they dedicate to the gods when they want to show gratitude — as if it were the greatest thing a human being can possess. And finally public opinion has sunk to this: poverty is a hissing and a byword, despised by the rich and hated by the poor.

Add to the account the verses of the poets, throwing fuel on our passions — verses in which wealth is praised as if it were the one credit and glory of mortal life. The immortal gods themselves, to hear the poets tell it, can neither give anything better than wealth, nor own anything better:

  The Sun-god's palace rose on towering columns,
  ablaze with gold.

Or look at the Sun's own chariot:

  Gold was the axle, gold the pole,
  gold the rims around the circling wheels,
  and every spoke was silver.

And finally, when they want to praise an age as the best of all, they call it the Golden Age. Even in the Greek tragic poets there are characters who trade innocence, health, and good name for profit:

  Call me a scoundrel — only call me rich.
  We all ask how much wealth he has; no one asks
  whether he's good.
  Nobody asks where your fortune came from, or how —
  only how big it is.
  Every man is worth exactly what he owns.
  What is shameful for us to own? Nothing.
  Rich, I choose to live; poor, I'd rather die.
  A man dies well who dies while making money.
  Money — that colossal blessing to mankind! —
  no mother's love can match it, no small child's
  lisp, no father's claim to honor. And if Venus's
  face has half its sparkle, no wonder Love sets
  gods and men on fire.

When these last lines were spoken at a performance of one of Euripides' tragedies, the entire audience rose as one man to hiss the actor and the play off the stage. But Euripides jumped up, claimed a hearing, and asked them to wait and see what end was in store for this admirer of gold. Bellerophon, in that drama, was to pay the penalty — the penalty that every man pays in the drama of life. For greed never goes unpunished; though greed is, all by itself, punishment enough. What tears and toil money wrings out of us! Greed is miserable in what it craves, and miserable in what it gains. Think, too, of the daily worry, which torments every owner in proportion to what he owns. Having money is a greater agony than getting it. And how we grieve over losses — which fall heavy, and seem heavier still. And finally, even if Fortune takes nothing away, whatever we fail to gain on top counts as sheer loss.

"But," you'll say, "people call that man over there happy and rich; they pray to someday own as much as he does." True enough. And so? Can you imagine a more pitiable condition than owning misery and hatred into the bargain? If only those doomed to crave wealth could compare notes with the rich man! If only those doomed to chase office could confer with the ambitious men who have reached the top of the honors ladder! They would soon change their prayers — because these great men are forever gaping after some new gain while cursing everything already behind them. There is no one in the world satisfied with his own prosperity, even when it comes at a run. Men complain of their plans and of how their plans turned out; what they failed to get always looks better than what they got.

So philosophy can settle this problem for you, and give you what I count the greatest gift there is: never having to regret your own conduct. That is a happiness that holds; no storm can shake it. And no finely woven words, no smoothly flowing language, will steer you into that harbor. Let words go as they please — provided only that the soul keeps its own sure order; provided the soul is great, and holds steadily to its convictions, pleased with itself precisely for the things that displease everyone else; a soul that measures its progress by how it lives, and reckons that it knows exactly as much as it neither desires nor fears.

Farewell.

Letters 116–117

Letter 116: On Self-control

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

People have often debated the question: is it better to have moderate passions, or none at all? Our Stoic school throws the passions out; the Peripatetics keep them on a leash. I, for my part, don't see how a disease in moderation can be healthy or useful. But don't be afraid — I'm not going to confiscate anything you'd hate to give up. I'll be kind and easygoing about the things you chase — the ones you consider necessary for life, or useful, or pleasant. What I'll strip away is the vice in the chasing. After I've put desire itself under a ban, I'll still let you want those same things — but fearlessly, and with sharper judgment; you'll actually feel the pleasures more than you do now. Why wouldn't they come more readily when you call, once you're their master instead of their slave?

"But," you object, "it's natural to suffer when I lose a friend. Grant a few tears their right to fall! It's also natural to care what people think, and to feel low when their opinion of me is unfavorable. Why won't you allow me such an honorable dread of a bad reputation?"

There is no vice that can't produce a character reference. There is no vice that isn't modest and reasonable at the start; the trouble spreads from there. If you allow it to begin, you cannot guarantee it will stop. Every passion is weak at first. Then it wakes itself up and gathers strength as it goes: it is easier to keep it out than to throw it out. Who denies that all the passions flow from a source that is, in a sense, natural? Nature gave us a concern for our own well-being; but that very concern, overindulged, becomes a vice. Nature mixed pleasure into the necessities of life — not so that we would chase the pleasure, but so that the extra sweetness would make the indispensable things attractive to us. Let pleasure start claiming rights of its own, and its name is luxury.

So let us resist these faults while they are standing at the door, because, as I said, it is easier to refuse them entry than to show them out. And if you protest, "A person should be allowed a certain amount of grief, a certain amount of fear" — I answer that the "certain amount" has a way of stretching on, and it will not stop on your schedule.
