The wise man can safely be his own supervisor: he can halt his tears, and his pleasures, at will. We can't retrace our steps so easily, so for us the best plan is not to take the first step at all. I think Panaetius gave a very neat answer to a young man who asked him whether the wise man should fall in love. "As for the wise man, we shall see," he said. "But you and I, who are still a long way from wisdom, had better not trust ourselves to a state that is disordered, out of control, enslaved to another person, and worthless in its own eyes. If our love is returned, the kindness inflames us; if it is scorned, our pride does. An easy love hurts us as much as a difficult one: we're captured by the one that yields, and we batter ourselves against the one that resists. So, knowing how weak we are, let's stay quiet. Let's not expose this unstable spirit to drink, or beauty, or flattery, or anything else that coaxes and pulls."

What Panaetius said about love applies, I believe, to all the passions. As far as we can, let's step back from the slippery ground; even on dry footing it's hard enough to stand firm. At this point I know you'll hit me with the standard complaint against the Stoics: "You promise too much and demand too much. We are mere miniatures of men; we can't deny ourselves everything. We'll grieve, but not too much; we'll desire, but in moderation; we'll get angry, and then we'll be talked down." Do you know why we can't reach the Stoic ideal? Because we refuse to believe we can. No — the truth is, something else is at work too: we are in love with our vices. We stand up for them, and we would rather excuse them than evict them. Nature gave us strength enough — if only we would use it, if only we would gather our powers and make them all fight for us, or at least not against us. Unwillingness is the reason; inability is the excuse.

Farewell.

Letter 117: On Real Ethics as Superior to Syllogistic Subtleties

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You are going to manufacture a lot of trouble for me — and drag me, without meaning to, into a long argument and considerable bother — if you keep sending me these petty little questions. To settle them, I can't disagree with my fellow Stoics without hurting my standing among them, and I can't sign on without hurting my conscience. Your question is whether the Stoic doctrine is true: that wisdom is a Good, but that being wise is not. I'll lay out the Stoic position first, and then I'll be bold enough to give you my own.

We Stoics hold that the Good is corporeal — a body — because the Good is active, and whatever acts is a body. What is good, helps. But to help, a thing must act; and if it acts, it is a body. The Stoics declare that wisdom is a Good; it follows that they must call wisdom corporeal too. But being wise, they think, doesn't rate the same treatment. It is incorporeal — a mere attendant on something else, namely wisdom — and so it doesn't act or help in any way.

"What!" comes the reply. "Then why do we say that being wise is a Good?" We do say so — but only by referring it back to the thing it depends on, wisdom itself. Before I build my own creed and plant myself entirely on the other side, let me tell you how other philosophers answer this. "By that logic," they say, "not even living happily is a Good. Like it or not, your school has to answer that the happy life is a Good, but that living happily is not." And this objection gets thrown at our school as well: "You want to be wise. Therefore being wise is something to be desired. And if it is to be desired, it is a Good." So our philosophers are forced to twist their terms — to wedge an extra syllable into the word "desired" that our language does not normally allow. With your permission, I'll wedge it in anyway. "What is good," they say, "is a thing to be desired; the desirable extra is what falls to us after we have attained the Good. It is not sought as a Good itself; it is a bonus added on once the Good has been won."

I don't share this view. I judge that our philosophers have sunk to this argument because they are already shackled by the first link in the chain, and so they aren't free to change their definition. We usually give great weight to what all men take for granted; for us, universal agreement is evidence of truth. For instance, we infer that the gods exist partly from this: every human being has some idea of deity planted in him, and there is no tribe anywhere so far beyond law and custom that it doesn't believe in gods of some sort. When we discuss the immortality of the soul, the general opinion of mankind — who fear, or worship, the spirits of the world below — carries real weight with us. Well, I'll cash in that same universal belief: you will find no one who doesn't hold that wisdom is a Good, and being wise a Good too. But I won't appeal to the crowd like a beaten gladiator throwing himself on the audience's mercy. Let's fight at close quarters, with our own weapons.

When something affects an object, is it inside the object it affects, or outside? If inside, it is just as much a body as the thing it affects — for nothing can affect another thing without touching it, and what touches is a body. If outside, then it withdrew after doing its work; withdrawal is motion, and what moves is a body. You expect me, I suppose, to deny that "a race" differs from "running," that "heat" differs from "being hot," that "light" differs from "giving light." I grant that each pair differs — but not that the two fall into separate classes. If health is an indifferent thing, so is being healthy; if beauty is indifferent, so is being beautiful. If justice is a Good, so is being just. If baseness is an evil, then being base is an evil — exactly as, if sore eyes are an evil, having sore eyes is an evil too. Neither member of the pair, you can be sure, can exist without the other. Whoever is wise is a man of wisdom; whoever is a man of wisdom is wise. The one so exactly matches the other that some people regard them as one and the same thing.

But here is a question I'd love to put: granted that all things are good, bad, or indifferent — into which class does being wise fall? People deny it's a Good; it obviously isn't an evil; so it must be one of the "middle" things. But by the "middle" or "indifferent" we mean what can fall to the bad man as easily as to the good — money, beauty, high social position. Being wise can fall to the good man alone; therefore it is not indifferent. It isn't an evil either, because it cannot fall to the bad man at all. Therefore, it is a Good. What only the good man can possess is a Good; being wise belongs only to the good man; therefore it is a Good. "It's merely an accessory of wisdom," says the objector. Fine, I answer: this thing you call being wise — does it actively produce wisdom, or does it passively tag along with wisdom? Either way it's a body: for what is acted upon and what acts are both bodies, and if it's a body, it's a Good. The only thing that could disqualify it would be being incorporeal — and it isn't.

The Peripatetics hold that there is no distinction at all between wisdom and being wise, since each implies the other. Can you imagine anyone being wise without possessing wisdom? Or anyone possessing wisdom without being wise? But the old masters of logic do distinguish the two, and from them the classification has come all the way down to the Stoics. What kind of classification, I'll explain. A field is one thing; possessing the field is another — obviously, since "possessing the field" refers to the possessor rather than to the field itself. In the same way, wisdom is one thing and being wise another. You'll grant, I suppose, that these are two separate ideas — the possessed and the possessor: wisdom the thing possessed, the wise man its possessor. Now, wisdom is Mind perfected — developed to its highest and best; it is the art of living. And what is being wise? I can't call it "Mind perfected"; rather, it is what falls to the man who has a perfected mind. So a good mind is one thing, and having a good mind, so to speak, is another.

"There are," the argument runs, "certain natural classes of bodies: we say 'this is a man,' 'this is a horse.' Attending on these bodily natures are certain movements of the mind that declare something about the bodies. And these declarations have an essential quality all their own, cut off from body. For example: I see Cato walking. The senses report it; the mind believes it. What I see is a body, and it is on a body that I fix my eyes and my mind. But then I say, 'Cato walks.' What I say" — so they continue — "is not a body; it is a declarative statement about a body — call it an utterance, a declaration, a proposition. So when we say 'wisdom,' we mean something bodily; when we say 'he is wise,' we are making a statement about a body. And it makes a real difference whether you name the person directly or make a statement about him."

Suppose for the moment that these really are two separate conceptions (I'm not ready to give my own verdict yet): what prevents there being a third thing — which is nonetheless a Good? I said a moment ago that a "field" is one thing and "possession of a field" another; naturally, because possessor and possessed are of different natures — one is land, the other is the man who owns the land. But in the question before us, both are of the same nature: the possessor of wisdom, and wisdom itself. Moreover, with the field, the thing possessed is one thing and the possessor another; here, possessed and possessor come under the same heading. The field is owned by legal right; wisdom is owned by nature. The field can change hands and pass to a new owner; wisdom never leaves its owner. So there's no point trying to compare things so unlike.

I had started to say that these can be two separate conceptions and yet both be Goods. Wisdom and the wise man, for instance, are two separate things, and you grant that both are good. And just as there is no objection to counting both wisdom and the possessor of wisdom as Goods, there is no objection to counting as a Good both wisdom and the possession of wisdom — in other words, being wise. The only reason I want to be a wise man is in order to be wise. And really — isn't a thing a Good when, without it, a certain other thing can't be a Good? You'll surely admit that wisdom offered without the right to use it is not worth accepting. And what is the use of wisdom? Being wise. That is its whole value; take it away, and wisdom is useless. If torture is an evil, then being tortured is an evil — with this proviso, in fact: strip away the consequence, and the instruments aren't evil at all. Wisdom is the condition of a perfected mind; being wise is the employment of a perfected mind. How can the employment of a thing not be a Good, when without employment the thing itself is not a Good? If I ask you whether wisdom is desirable, you admit it. If I ask whether the employment of wisdom is desirable, you admit that too — since you say you wouldn't accept wisdom if you weren't allowed to employ it. Now, what is desirable is a Good. Being wise is the employment of wisdom — just as making a speech is the employment of eloquence, and seeing is the employment of the eyes. Being wise is the employment of wisdom; the employment of wisdom is desirable; therefore being wise is desirable; and if it is desirable, it is a Good.

For years now I have been condemning myself for imitating these men in the very act of prosecuting them — for wasting words on a point that's perfectly obvious. Who can doubt that if heat is an evil, being hot is an evil too? Or that if cold is an evil, being cold is an evil? Or that if life is a Good, so is being alive? All these questions live on the outskirts of wisdom, not in wisdom itself — and wisdom itself is where we should make our home. Even if the mind fancies a ramble, wisdom has large and spacious grounds of its own: we may investigate the nature of the gods, the fuel that feeds the stars, all the varied courses of the heavenly bodies; we may ask whether our affairs move in step with the stars, whether the impulse to motion flows from there into every mind and body, and whether even the events we call chance are bound by strict laws — whether nothing in this universe is unforeseen or off schedule in its rounds. Such subjects have been dropped from moral instruction these days, but they lift the mind and raise it to the scale of what it studies. The questions I was discussing a moment ago do the opposite — they grind the mind down and wear it thin, not whetting it, as you and your friends maintain, but weakening it. And I ask you: are we really going to squander the study we owe to greater and better themes on a question that may well be wrong and is certainly worthless? What will it profit me to know whether wisdom is one thing and being wise another? What will it profit me to know that the one is a Good and the other isn't? Let me take a gamble and put up this prayer: "Wisdom for you, and being wise for me!" We'll come out even.

Show me instead the road to those greater ends. Tell me what to avoid and what to seek; by what studies I can shore up my tottering mind; how I can beat back the waves that hit me broadside and drive me off course; how I can face down all my evils, and how I can be rid of the calamities that have crashed in on me and the ones I have crashed into myself. Teach me how to carry sorrow without a groan, and prosperity without making anyone else groan. Teach me not to sit waiting for the final, inevitable end, but to make my own retreat, of my own free will, when it seems right to me. Nothing seems to me more shameful than praying for death. If you want to live, why pray for death? And if you don't want to live, why ask the gods for what they gave you at birth? That you must die some day has been settled, whether you like it or not; when you shall wish to die is in your own hands. The one is a necessity for you; the other is a privilege.

I recently read a most disgraceful sentiment from — the more shame to him — a learned gentleman: "May I die as soon as possible!" You fool, you are praying for what is already yours. "May I die as soon as possible!" Perhaps you grew old in the act of saying it! In any case, what's stopping you? No one is holding you; leave by whatever road you please. Pick any part of Nature and tell it to supply the exit. The elements by which the world does its work — water, earth, air — are no more the causes of life than they are the roads to death. "May I die as soon as possible!" And what exactly do you mean by "as soon as possible"? What day are you setting for the event? It may come sooner than your prayer asks. Talk like this comes from a weak mind, one fishing for pity with its curses; the man who prays for death does not want to die. Ask the gods for life and health. If you have resolved to die, death's reward is this: you are done with praying.

These, my dear Lucilius, are the problems we should be dealing with — these are the problems on which we should be shaping our minds. This is wisdom; this is what being wise means: not batting empty subtleties around in idle, petty debates. Fortune has set you so many problems that you haven't solved yet — and you're still splitting hairs? How foolish, to practice fancy exhibition strokes after the signal for battle has sounded! Throw away the practice weapons; you need armor for a fight to the finish. Tell me how to keep sadness and fear from shaking my soul; tell me how to shrug off this load of hidden cravings. Do something! "Wisdom is a Good, but being wise is not a Good" — the payoff of talk like that is a verdict that we aren't wise, and a whole field of study made into a joke for wasting its effort on useless things. Would you believe this question is debated too: whether future wisdom is a Good? As if anyone could doubt it! Do barns feel the weight of the harvest that is still to come? Does boyhood sense approaching manhood in its muscle and force? The sick man, in the meantime, gets no help from the health that is on its way — no more than a runner or a wrestler is refreshed by the rest period that comes months after the match. Who doesn't know that what is yet to be is not a Good, for the simple reason that it is yet to be? What is good necessarily helps; only things that exist now can help; what doesn't help isn't a Good; and if it helps, it already is. I shall be wise some day, and that Good will be mine when I am — but in the meantime it doesn't exist. A thing must first be, and only then be of some particular kind. How, I ask you, can what is still nothing already be a Good? What better proof could you want that a thing does not exist than to say, "It is yet to be"? What's still on the road obviously hasn't arrived. "Spring will follow" — so I know it is winter now. "Summer will follow" — so I know it is not summer. The best evidence that a thing isn't here yet is that it's still coming. I hope to be wise some day, but in the meantime I am not wise; for if I had that Good, I'd already be free of this evil. Some day I shall be wise — and from that very sentence you can tell that I am not wise yet. I cannot live in that Good and in this evil at once; the two don't mix, and good and evil don't share the same person.

So let's rush past all this clever nonsense and hurry on to what will actually help us. No man running frantically for a midwife while his daughter is in labor stops to read the praetor's edict or the schedule of the games. No one racing to his burning house pauses over a checkerboard to work out how the trapped piece might be freed. But good heavens — the news is pouring in on you from every side: your house on fire, your children in danger, your country under siege, your property plundered. Add shipwreck, earthquakes, and everything else there is to dread. Battered by all this, you're setting aside time for intellectual entertainment? You're asking the difference between wisdom and being wise? Tying and untying knots while that ruin hangs over your head? Nature has not been so generous and open-handed with our time that we can afford to waste any of it. And notice how much gets lost even by the careful: one man's time is stolen by his own poor health, another's by illness in the family; now private business swallows the hours, now public business; and all the while sleep splits our lives with us, half and half.

Out of this time — so short, so swift, carrying us off in its current — what is the sense of spending the greater part on useless things? Besides, our minds have gotten into the habit of entertaining themselves instead of curing themselves — of making philosophy an aesthetic pleasure, when philosophy should really be a remedy. What the difference is between wisdom and being wise, I do not know; but I do know this: it makes no difference to me whether I know it or not. Tell me — when I have found out the difference between wisdom and being wise, will I be wise?

Why, then, do you keep me busy with the words of wisdom rather than its works? Make me braver. Make me calmer. Make me Fortune's equal — make me her superior. And I can be her superior, if I turn everything I learn to that end.

Farewell.

Letters 118–119

Letter 118: On the Vanity of Place-seeking

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You have been demanding letters from me more often. But if we compare the account books, you will not come out on the credit side. Our agreement, in fact, was that your part came first: you were to write the first letters, and I was to answer them. Still, I won't be difficult about it. I know you're good for the money, so I'll pay in advance. And even so I will not do what the eloquent Cicero tells Atticus to do: "Even if you have nothing to say, write whatever comes into your head." I will always have something to write about, even if I skip all the kinds of news Cicero stuffs his letters with — which candidate is in trouble; who is campaigning on borrowed money and who on his own; who is running for consul leaning on Caesar, or on Pompey, or on his own strongbox; what a merciless moneylender Caecilius is, out of whom his own friends can't pry a penny at less than one percent a month.

It is better to deal with your own ills than with someone else's — to sift yourself, to see how many empty offices you are secretly a candidate for, and to cast your vote for none of them. This, my dear Lucilius, is a noble thing; this is what brings peace and freedom — to campaign for nothing, and to walk straight past all of Fortune's elections. When the voting tribes are called together and the candidates are sweating in their favorite temples — one promising cash handouts, another doing his business through an agent, another wearing his hand raw with the kisses of men he won't touch with one finger after he's elected — when everyone is waiting breathless for the herald's announcement, how can you call it anything but delightful to stand idle and watch this whole vanity fair without buying and without selling? And how much greater is the joy of the man who watches, untroubled, not just the election of a praetor or a consul, but the great election underneath it — where some are running for offices that last a year, others for permanent power, others for a triumph and a lucky war, others for riches, or a marriage and children, or the safety of themselves and their families! What a great-souled thing it is to be the one person campaigning for nothing, petitioning no man, and saying: "Fortune, I have no business with you. I am not at your service. I know that you snub men like Cato and promote men like Vatinius. I ask you for nothing." That is how you demote Fortune to the ranks.

So these are the things we can write each other about — the ever-fresh material we can keep digging out as we look over the restless mobs of men, all of them struggling through evil after evil to reach something ruinous, chasing what they will soon have to run from, or find sickening once they have it. Was anyone ever satisfied, once he got it, with the thing that loomed so large while he was praying for it? Happiness is not the greedy thing people imagine. It is a lowly thing — and that is why it never glutted anyone's appetite. The things you're chasing look lofty to you because you are down low and far away from them; to the man who has reached them, they look cheap. And I am much mistaken if he isn't itching to climb higher still. What you take for the summit is only a rung on the ladder. The trouble with everyone is ignorance of the truth: misled by common report, men chase these things as if they were good, and then, after winning them and suffering plenty on the way, they find them evil, or empty, or smaller than advertised. Most people admire whatever fools them at a distance; to the crowd, good things and big things are the same thing.

To keep that from happening to us, let's ask: what is the Good? It has been explained in various ways; different thinkers have described it differently. Some define it like this: "The Good is what attracts and calls the spirit to itself." But the objection is immediate: what if it attracts you straight to ruin? You know how seductive many evils are. The true differs from the plausible; and the Good is bound up with the true — it isn't good unless it is also true. But what merely attracts and entices is only plausible: it steals your attention, demands your interest, pulls you in. So some have defined it instead: "The Good is what stirs desire for itself, or rouses toward itself the impulse of a striving soul." The same objection applies: plenty of things rouse the soul's impulses, and the chase does the chaser harm. This definition is better: "The Good is what rouses the soul's impulse toward itself in accordance with nature, and is only to be sought when it becomes thoroughly worth seeking." By that point it is already an honorable thing — for that is what is completely worth seeking.

The topic prompts me to state the difference between the Good and the honorable. They share one quality that blends into both and can't be pried out of either: nothing can be good without some element of the honorable in it, and the honorable is necessarily good. What, then, is the difference? The honorable is the perfect Good; the happy life is fulfilled by it; through its influence other things become good as well. I mean something like this: there are things that are neither good nor bad — military service, a diplomatic post, the pronouncing of legal judgments. When such things are conducted honorably, they begin to be good; they cross over from the "indifferent" column into the Good. The Good comes from partnership with the honorable; the honorable is good in itself. The Good springs from the honorable; the honorable springs from itself. What is good might have been bad. What is honorable could never have been anything but good.

Some have offered this definition: "The Good is what is according to nature." Now follow my point carefully: what is good is according to nature, but what is according to nature does not thereby become good. Many things harmonize with nature but are too petty to deserve the name of good; they are trivial, fit to be ignored. There is no such thing as a tiny, contemptible good — for as long as it is scanty, it is not good, and when it begins to be good, it stops being scanty. How, then, is the Good recognized? Only when it is completely according to nature.

"You admit," someone says, "that what is good is according to nature — that's its special property. And you admit that other things are according to nature which are not good. How can the one be good and the other not? How can the essential character change, when both share the same distinguishing mark of agreeing with nature?" By sheer magnitude. It is nothing new for things to change as they grow. A person who was a child becomes a youth; his essential character is transformed, for the child could not reason and the youth can. Some things don't merely get bigger as they develop — they grow into something else. "But," comes the reply, "getting bigger doesn't mean becoming different. It makes no difference whether you pour wine into a flask or into a vat; the wine keeps its character in both. Honey tastes the same in small amounts and large." You are citing different cases. Wine and honey have a uniform quality; enlarge the quantity all you like, the quality stays the same. Some things do persist in their kind and character even when they are enlarged.

But there are others which, after many additions, are transformed by the last one: a new character is stamped on them, different from what they were before. One stone makes an archway — the one that wedges the leaning sides and holds the whole arch together by sitting in the middle. Why does the last addition, tiny as it is, make so much difference? Because it does not increase; it completes. Some things, as they develop, shed their old shape and are altered into a new one. When the mind has dwelt on some idea a long time and grown weary trying to grasp its size, the thing comes to be called "infinite" — and then it has become something very different from what it was when it seemed vast but finite. In the same way, we think of something as hard to divide; at the very end, as the task grows harder and harder, it turns out to be "indivisible." So too, from what could barely be budged we advance step by step to the "immovable." By the same reasoning, a certain thing was according to nature; its magnitude has changed it into a different character altogether, and made it a Good.

Farewell.

Letter 119: On Nature as Our Best Provider

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Whenever I make a find, I don't wait for you to shout "Halves!" — I say it to myself on your behalf. If you want to know what I've discovered this time, open your pocket: it is pure profit. What I am going to teach you is how to get rich as fast as possible. You're all ears now, and rightly so: I am going to lead you by a shortcut to the greatest wealth there is. You will need a loan, though. To do business, you must go into debt — but I don't want you borrowing through a middleman, and I don't want the brokers gossiping about your credit rating. I will supply you with a ready lender: Cato's famous one, who says, "Borrow from yourself!" However little it is, it will be enough, if we can only make up the shortfall out of our own resources. For it makes no difference, my dear Lucilius, whether you don't crave a thing or you have it. In either case the essential point is the same: freedom from worry.

I am not advising you to refuse nature anything. Nature is insistent; she cannot be beaten down; she demands what she is owed. But you should understand that anything beyond nature's wants is an "extra" — not a necessity. If I am hungry, I must eat. Nature doesn't care whether the bread is coarse or the finest wheat; she doesn't want the stomach entertained, only filled. If I'm thirsty, nature doesn't care whether I drink water from the nearest tank or chill it artfully in heaps of snow. Nature's one order is that the thirst be quenched. Whether the cup is gold, or crystal, or precious carved stone, or common clay from Tibur, or my own cupped hands — it doesn't matter. Look to the end of a thing, in every case, and you will throw the superfluities away. Hunger calls me: I reach for whatever is nearest; my hunger itself has made whatever I can grab look appetizing. A starving man despises nothing.

Do you want to know, then, what has delighted me? This noble saying I've discovered: "The wise man is the keenest seeker after the riches of nature." "What!" you say. "You hand me an empty plate? What is this? I had my strongboxes arranged. I was already scouting for some stretch of water to send trading ships across, some public revenues to farm, some merchandise to buy up. This is a swindle — promising me riches and showing me poverty." But, my friend, do you call a man poor who lacks nothing? "Yes — thanks to himself and his endurance, not thanks to his fortune." And you hold that such a man isn't rich — precisely because his wealth can never run out? Tell me: would you rather have much, or enough? The man who has much wants more — proof that he doesn't yet have enough. The man who has enough has arrived at something no rich man ever reaches: a stopping point. Or do you refuse to call this riches because no one was ever put on the execution lists for owning it? Because no wife or son ever poured poison down a throat to get it? Because in wartime it goes unplundered? Because in peacetime it means leisure? Because it is neither dangerous to own nor troublesome to invest?

"But a man possesses too little, if all he's free of is cold and hunger and thirst." Jupiter himself possesses no more. Enough is never too little, and not-enough is never too much. Alexander was still poor after conquering Darius and the Indies. Am I wrong? Look at him: hunting for something he can call his own, probing unknown seas, sending fresh fleets out over the Ocean, breaking down — you might say — the very bars of the universe. What is enough for nature is not enough for man. There have been men who craved something more after obtaining everything — so blind are our wits, so easily does each man forget his beginnings once he is under way. The man who only yesterday was the disputed lord of one obscure corner of the world reaches the edge of the globe — and is gloomy, because now he must march home through a world that belongs to him. Money never made anyone rich; all it does is infect its owner with a greater craving for itself. You ask the reason? The man who has more has begun to be capable of more.

To sum it up: haul out for inspection any of the millionaires whose names come up whenever people talk of Crassus and Licinus. Let him bring his credit rating, his current holdings, and his future prospects, and add them all together. By my reckoning, such a man is poor; by yours, he may become poor someday. But the man who has arranged his affairs by the measure of nature's demands is free of the fear of poverty and of the feeling of it too. And just so you know how hard it is to narrow one's wants down to nature's limits — even this man we're describing, the one you call poor, owns something superfluous. Wealth dazzles and draws the mob: a great pile of ready cash carried out of a man's house, walls crusted thick with gold, a retinue of attendants picked for their handsome build or handsome dress. All that kind of prosperity faces the public; but the man we have snatched out of the hands of the people and of Fortune is happy from the inside. As for those in whose lives busy, bustling poverty has stolen the title of riches — they have riches the way we say we "have a fever," when really the fever has us. We're used to putting it the other way around: "A fever grips him." And in the same way we should say: "Riches grip him." There is no advice I would rather give you than this — and of this advice no one can have too much: measure everything by nature's demands, for those can be satisfied either free of charge or very cheaply. Only don't stir any vices into the demands. Why ask how your food should be served, on what sort of table, with what sort of silver, by what well-matched, smooth-cheeked serving boys? Nature demands nothing but food.

  When thirst is burning in your throat, do you demand a golden cup?
  When hunger comes, do you turn up your nose at everything
  but peacock and turbot?

Hunger is not ambitious. It is quite content to stop, and it doesn't much care what stops it. All those other things are the tools of a luxury that has nothing to do with happiness — a luxury that studies how to stretch hunger out past the point of fullness, how to stuff the stomach rather than fill it, how to reawaken a thirst that the first drink already quenched. So Horace's words are excellent: it makes no difference to your thirst, he says, how costly the goblet is or how elegantly the hand that pours the water. If you care how curly-haired the boy is who serves you, or how transparent the cup he offers — you are not thirsty.

Among her other gifts, nature has granted us this special blessing: sheer necessity is never picky. Only the superfluous things allow for choosiness. "That's not suitable"; "this doesn't come well recommended"; "that offends my eye." The Builder of the universe, who laid down the laws of life for us, arranged that we should live well, not that we should live in luxury. Everything that serves our well-being is prepared and lying ready to hand; what luxury demands can never be scraped together without misery and anxiety.

So let us use this blessing of nature and count it among the great ones. Let us reflect that nature has no better claim on our gratitude than this: whatever we want out of sheer necessity, we accept without turning up our nose.

Farewell.

Letters 120–121

Letter 120: More About Virtue

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Your letter wandered through several small problems but finally settled on one and asked me to explain it: how do we come to know the good and the honorable? Other schools treat these as two separate things; our school only distinguishes them. Here is what I mean. Some people believe the good is whatever is useful — so they hand the title to riches, horses, wine, even shoes. That is how cheap the good becomes for them, how low they let it stoop. The honorable, for them, is whatever squares with right conduct: caring dutifully for an aging father, helping a friend out of poverty, showing courage on campaign, giving prudent and balanced advice. We make the good and the honorable two things, but out of one substance: only the honorable can be good, and the honorable is always, necessarily, good. It would be superfluous to add the fine distinction between the two — I have drawn it so many times. But I will say this one thing: we count nothing as good that can be put to bad use by anybody. And you can see for yourself what bad uses many men make of their riches, their high positions, and their muscles.

Back, then, to your question: how did we first acquire knowledge of the good and the honorable? Nature could not teach us this directly. She gave us the seeds of knowledge, not knowledge itself. Some say we simply stumbled on it — but it is unbelievable that a vision of virtue ever presented itself to anyone by accident. Our view is that it came from inference and observation, from comparing acts that occurred again and again; our school holds that the good and the honorable were grasped by analogy. And since Latin scholars have granted the word "analogy" citizenship, I don't think it should be deported — I think it should be welcomed into the full citizenship it can justly claim. So I'll use the word, not as a tolerated resident but as an established one.

What is this analogy? Let me explain. We understood what health of the body was, and from that we deduced that there must also be a health of the mind. We knew bodily strength, and from that we inferred a sturdiness of soul. Certain kind acts, humane acts, brave acts had amazed us, and we began to admire them as if they were perfect. Underneath lay plenty of faults, hidden by the shine of a few conspicuous deeds — and to those we shut our eyes. Nature tells us to magnify whatever deserves praise, and everyone polishes fame beyond the truth. So from deeds like these we drew our picture of some enormous good. Fabricius refused King Pyrrhus's gold, judging it greater than a crown to be able to scorn a king's money. And the same Fabricius, when Pyrrhus's own physician promised to poison his master, warned Pyrrhus to watch out for the plot. One and the same man had the strength of will neither to be bought by gold nor to win by poison. So we admired him — a hero who could not be moved by the king's promises, or by promises made against the king; a man who held fast to his ideal; a man who (is anything harder?) waged war without sin, for he believed there were wrongs one could commit even against an enemy; a man who, in the extreme poverty he had made his glory, recoiled from taking riches just as he recoiled from using poison. "Live, Pyrrhus, thanks to me," he cried, "and be glad, for once, of the very thing you've been grieving over — that Fabricius cannot be bribed!"

Horatius Cocles blocked the narrow bridge alone and ordered his own retreat cut off, so that the enemy's way in would be destroyed with it; then he held off his attackers until the crash of the collapsing beams rang in his ears. When he looked back and saw that his country, through his own danger, was out of danger, he shouted: "Whoever wants to chase me, come this way!" — and plunged in headlong, taking as much care to come out of that churning channel with his weapons as to come out unhurt. He returned with the glory of his conquering arms intact, as safely as if he had walked back across the bridge.

These deeds and others like them showed us the face of virtue. Now let me add something that may surprise you: evil things have sometimes worn the look of what is honorable, and the best has shone out through its opposite. For there are, as you know, vices that live next door to virtues; even what is lost and degraded can resemble what is upright. The spendthrift passes himself off as a generous man — though it matters a great deal whether a man knows how to give or simply doesn't know how to keep. Believe me, my dear Lucilius, there are many men who don't give their money away; they throw it away. I don't call a man generous for being on bad terms with his own cash. Carelessness looks like ease, and recklessness looks like courage. This resemblance is what forced us to watch closely, and to tell apart things that look alike on the surface but are at war underneath. And in watching men whom some noble effort had made famous, we began to notice which of them had done a deed with high spirit and lofty impulse — and had done it exactly once. We saw one man brave in war and a coward in politics, bearing poverty with courage and disgrace with a hangdog face; we praised the deed, and we despised the man. We saw another man kind to his friends and restrained toward his enemies, running his public and private affairs with scrupulous devotion, patient wherever something had to be endured and prudent wherever something had to be done. We saw him giving with an open hand when payment was owed, and, when hard work was called for, digging in resolutely and lifting his body's weariness by sheer will. More than that: he was always the same man, consistent in everything he did, right not only by judgment but by habit so ingrained that he not only could act rightly but could not help acting rightly. In such a man we formed our conception: here perfect virtue exists.

We divided this perfect virtue into its parts. Desires had to be reined in, fears suppressed, right actions put in order, debts paid where owed — so we marked off self-control, courage, prudence, and justice, and assigned each its special job. Then how did we form our idea of virtue itself? From that man's order, his propriety, his steadfastness, the complete harmony of all his actions, and a greatness of soul that rose above everything. From there we derived our conception of the happy life — the life that flows along with a steady current, entirely under its own control. And how did we discover that? I'll tell you. That perfect man, the man who had attained virtue, never cursed his luck, never met what chance brought with a fallen face. Believing himself citizen and soldier of the universe, he accepted his labors as if they were orders. Whatever happened, he did not spurn it as some evil blown in on him by accident; he took it as an assignment. "Whatever this is," he said, "it is my lot. It's rough and it's hard — so let's get to work on it."

Necessarily, then, he showed himself a great man — he never groaned in evil days, never complained about his destiny. He gave many people a clear picture of what he was; he shone like a light in the dark and turned everyone's thoughts toward himself, because he was gentle and calm, and equally ready to obey the orders of men and of God. He had a perfect soul, developed to its utmost height — a soul that nothing outranks except the mind of God, from which a portion flows down even into this mortal chest of ours. And the chest is never more divine than when it reflects on its own mortality — when it understands that a man was born to finish his life, and that the body is not a permanent home but the soul's inn — and a short stay at that — to be left behind the moment you see you have become a burden to your host. The greatest proof, my dear Lucilius, that the soul comes down from somewhere higher is this: it finds its present quarters cramped and low, and it is not afraid to leave. For the man who remembers where he came from knows where he is going. Don't we see how many discomforts plague us, how badly this flesh of ours fits? We complain now about our heads, now about our digestion, now about our chests and throats. One day it's the nerves, another day the feet; then diarrhea, then a cold; sometimes too much blood, sometimes too little. Now this troubles us, now that, and we're told to move along — which is exactly what happens to people living in someone else's house.

And yet we, who were allotted these decaying bodies, still set eternity before our eyes. We reach in our hopes for the furthest stretch a human life can possibly be spun out to, satisfied with no income and no influence. Could anything be more shameless, more foolish? Nothing is enough for us — for us, who must die someday; who, in fact, are dying already. Every day we stand nearer the edge, and every hour shoves us toward the drop we must go over. Look how blind our minds are! What I keep calling the future is happening at this very minute — and a large part of it has already happened, for it consists of all the life we've lived. We're wrong to fear our last day: every day contributes just as much to death's account. The stumbling step doesn't cause the exhaustion; it announces it. The final hour reaches death; every hour approaches it. Death wears us away; it does not sweep us away.

For this reason the noble soul, conscious of its better nature, takes care to behave honorably and diligently at the post where it is stationed — yet it counts none of these surrounding things as its own. It uses them as a loan, the way a traveler passing through uses what he finds, hurrying on. When we see steadfastness like that in a man, how can we help sensing the image of an extraordinary nature — especially if, as I said, its consistency proved the greatness genuine? For it is consistency that lasts; what is false doesn't hold up. Some men are a Vatinius one day and a Cato the next. One moment Curius isn't stern enough for them, Fabricius isn't poor enough, Tubero isn't frugal enough or content with plain enough things; the next moment they're rivaling Licinus in wealth, Apicius at the table, Maecenas in soft living. The surest proof of a sick mind is wavering — this endless seesaw between pretending to virtue and loving vice.

  He'd keep two hundred slaves on hand — then ten.
  One hour his talk was kings and potentates
  and nothing short of greatness; the next hour:
  "A three-legged table, a dish of clean salt,
  and a coarse-woven gown to keep the cold out —
  that's all I ask." Yet hand him a cool million,
  that sparing, that contented man, and in
  five short days there'd be nothing in his purse.

The men I'm describing are of the stamp Horace sketches there — never the same man twice, never even resembling himself, so far does he swing between opposites. Did I say many men are like this? Almost all of them are. Everyone changes his plans and prayers by the day. Now he wants a wife, now a mistress; now he wants to reign, now he grovels lower than any slave; now he puffs himself up until everyone hates him, now he shrinks and flattens himself below the genuinely humble; now he flings money about, now he steals it. That is how a foolish mind gives itself away most clearly: it appears in one shape after another and is never like itself — and nothing, to my mind, is more shameful. Believe me, it is a great role — playing one man. Nobody but the wise man can play a single person; the rest of us keep changing masks. No man can wear one face in private and another in public for long. One moment you'll think us thrifty and serious; the next, wasteful and idle. We keep swapping characters, taking up a part opposite to the one we just threw off. So demand this of yourself: the character you assumed at the beginning, keep it to the final scene of life's drama. Make it possible for people to praise you — or, failing that, at least to recognize you. As things stand, a man can fairly ask about someone you saw only yesterday: "Who is this?" That is how great the change has been.

Farewell.

Letter 121: On Instinct in Animals

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

You'll take me to court, I'm sure of it, when I lay out today's little problem — one we've already fumbled with long enough. You'll shout, yet again: "What does this have to do with character?" Shout away — but first let me name you some other defendants for your lawsuit, Posidonius and Archidemus; let them stand trial. Then I'll add this: whatever deals with character doesn't necessarily produce good character. A man needs one thing for his food, another for his exercise, another for his clothing, another for his instruction, another for his pleasure. All of it relates to his needs; not all of it makes him better. Character, likewise, is served by different things in different ways: some things correct it and keep it in line; others investigate its nature and where it comes from. And when I ask why Nature produced man, and why she set him above the other animals, do you suppose I've left the study of character behind me? Wrong. How are you to know what character is worth wanting unless you've discovered what suits man best — unless you've studied his nature? You can find out what you should do and what you should avoid only when you've learned what you owe to your own nature.

"What I want," you say, "is to learn how to desire less and fear less. Strip me of my unreasoning beliefs. Show me that this thing people call happiness is fickle and hollow — a word just one prefix away from unhappiness." I'll give you what you want: I'll cheer on your virtues and flog your vices. People may decide I'm overzealous and reckless on this point, but I will never stop hounding wickedness, bridling the wildest of the emotions, blunting the pleasures that end in pain, and shouting down men's prayers. Of course I will — for the greatest evils are exactly the things we've prayed for, and everything we've ever given thanks for has turned into something that needs consoling.

Meanwhile, allow me to chew thoroughly on some points that may seem, for the moment, rather far from the question at hand. We were once debating whether all animals have a feel for their own constitution — their basic makeup. The best proof that they do is the fitness and nimbleness of their movements: they handle themselves as though they'd been trained for it. Every creature is expert in its own line. The skilled workman handles his tools with an ease born of experience; the pilot knows how to steer his ship; the painter, quick of eye and hand, moves among the countless colors he has laid out for the portrait, back and forth between palette and canvas. In just the same way, an animal is agile in everything that involves the use of its body. We tend to marvel at skilled dancers, whose gestures fit every meaning and emotion of the piece, whose movements keep pace with the speed of the words. But what art gives the performer, nature gives the animal. No animal fumbles with its own limbs; none is at a loss for how to use its body. They do this from the moment of birth. They arrive in the world with the knowledge; they are born fully trained.

"But," people answer, "the reason animals use their limbs so deftly is that moving them wrongly would hurt. On your own school's theory, they're compelled — it's fear, not will, that steers them right." Wrong. Bodies driven by compulsion move slowly; motion that comes from within has snap to it. And the proof that fear of pain isn't the prompt is this: even when pain checks them, they keep struggling toward their natural movement. So the child who is working on standing, getting used to carrying his own weight, no sooner starts testing his strength than he falls — and gets up, in tears, again and again, until through painful practice he has drilled himself into what nature demands. And certain animals with hard shells, flipped onto their backs, twist and grope with their feet and rock from side to side until they right themselves. The tortoise on his back feels no pain; but he is restless for his natural position, and he does not stop heaving himself about until he stands on his feet again.

So all these animals have a consciousness of their own constitution, and that is why they manage their limbs so readily; and we have no better proof that they're born equipped with this knowledge than the fact that no animal is clumsy at using its own body. But here comes an objection: "According to you, a constitution is 'the ruling power of the soul standing in a certain relation to the body.' How is a baby supposed to comprehend anything that intricate and subtle — something I can barely explain even to you? Every living creature would have to be born a logician, just to understand a definition that's over the heads of most Roman citizens." A fair complaint — if I had said that animals understand the definition of a constitution, rather than their own constitution. Nature is easier to understand than to explain. That baby doesn't know what "constitution" is; he knows his own constitution. He doesn't know what "a living creature" is; he feels that he is one. And even that constitution of his he grasps only confusedly, sketchily, in the dark. We, too, know that we have souls; but what the soul is, where it sits, what it's like, where it comes from — that we do not know. And just as we are conscious of our own souls while knowing nothing of their nature or their address, so all animals are conscious of their own constitutions. They cannot help feeling it, because the very faculty they feel everything else with is the same one; they cannot help having a feel for the thing they obey, the thing that governs them. Every one of us understands that there is something that stirs his impulses; what it is, he doesn't know. He knows he has a drive in him; what it is or where it comes from, he doesn't know. In the same way, even children and animals are conscious of their ruling part — just not clearly, not in sharp outline.

"So you're claiming," says the objector, "that every living thing starts out adapted to its own constitution, and that man's constitution is a rational one — so man is attached to himself not merely as a living being but as a reasoning being? For man is dear to himself precisely in the part that makes him a man. Then how can a baby, who doesn't yet have reason, be adapted to a rational constitution?" But each age has its own constitution — one for the infant, another for the boy, another for the old man; each is adapted to the constitution it currently finds itself in. The infant is toothless: he is fitted to that condition. Then his teeth come in: he is fitted to that condition too. The plant that will become grain and fruit has one constitution when it is young and barely peeking over the furrow, another when it has gathered strength and stands on a stalk soft but sturdy enough to carry its weight, another still when it turns yellow, hardens in the ear, and promises the threshing floor — whatever constitution it comes into, it keeps it and conforms to it. Infancy, boyhood, youth, and old age are different stages; but I, who have been infant and boy and youth, am still the same I. So although each thing has a different constitution at different times, its adaptation to its constitution is the same. Nature doesn't assign boyhood or youth or old age to me; she assigns me to them. So the child is adapted to the constitution he has right now, in childhood — not to the one he'll have as a young man. Even if there is some higher phase waiting for him to grow into, the state he's born in is according to nature too. First and foremost, the living thing is adapted to itself — for there has to be a standard that everything else gets referred back to. I seek pleasure. For whom? Myself. So I am looking out for myself. I shrink from pain. On whose behalf? My own. So I am looking out for myself. If I gauge everything I do by my own welfare, then my own welfare comes before everything else. This exists in every living being — not grafted on, but inborn.

Nature raises her own offspring; she does not cast them off. And because the most dependable protection is the protection closest at hand, each of us has been entrusted to himself. That is why, as I've said in earlier letters, even young animals, fresh from the womb or the egg, know at once and on their own what will hurt them, and steer clear of what deals death. They even flinch at the shadow of a hawk gliding overhead.

No animal enters life free from the fear of death. "How," people ask, "can an animal at birth understand what is wholesome and what is deadly?" The first question is whether it understands, not how. And that it does understand is clear from this: even if you added understanding, the animals would act no more competently than they already do. Why doesn't the hen fear the peacock or the goose, yet run from the hawk — a far smaller bird, and one she has never even met? Why do chicks fear a cat and not a dog? Clearly these birds carry a foreknowledge of harm that rests on no experiments — they avoid the thing before they could possibly have experience of it. And so you won't put it down to chance: they don't shrink from other things you'd expect them to fear; they never once forget their watchfulness; and they all possess, equally, the knack of avoiding what would destroy them. Besides, their fear doesn't grow as their lives go on.

From this it's plain enough that they didn't reach this condition through experience; it comes from an inborn drive for self-preservation. What experience teaches is slow and uneven; what Nature hands down comes to everyone alike, and comes at once. But if you insist on an explanation, shall I tell you how it is that every living thing tries to understand what would harm it? It feels that it is made of flesh — and so it perceives what flesh can suffer: cutting, burning, crushing — and which animals are armed to inflict the damage. From these it forms its idea of the harmful and the hostile. The two tendencies are joined: every creature at one and the same time looks out for its own safety, reaching for what helps it and recoiling from what would hurt it. Impulse toward the useful and revulsion from its opposite are according to nature; without any reflection to prompt them, without any instruction, whatever Nature has prescribed gets done.

Don't you see how skillful bees are at building their cells — how completely harmonious in sharing and enduring the work? Don't you see the spider's web, too fine for any human hand to copy — what a task it is to arrange those threads, some running straight to the center to make the web solid, others circling round and thinning out, spread to tangle and net the smaller insects for whose ruin the web is hung? This art is born, not taught — and that is why no animal is more skilled than another. Look: every spiderweb is equally fine; every opening in a honeycomb is identical in shape. What art passes along is uncertain and uneven; what Nature deals out is always uniform. She has handed down nothing except the duty of self-care and the skill to carry it out — which is why living and learning begin at the same time. No wonder creatures are born with the one gift whose absence would make being born pointless. This is the first equipment Nature issued them for survival: attachment to themselves, and love of themselves. They could not have survived without wanting to. The wanting alone wouldn't have saved them; but without it, nothing could have. In no animal will you find contempt for itself, or even neglect of itself. Dumb beasts, sluggish at everything else, are clever at living. So you will see that creatures useless to everyone else never fail themselves.

Farewell.

Letters 122–123

Letter 122: On Darkness as a Veil for Wickedness

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

The days have started to shrink. They've lost a good deal of light already, but there's still plenty of time in them — if you get up when the day itself does. The man who's awake to meet the dawn is more productive and, frankly, a better man. But the one who lies snoring while the sun stands high overhead, who only comes to at noon — and for plenty of people even noon feels like the middle of the night — that man is a disgrace to himself.

Some people have gone further and swapped the jobs of light and darkness entirely. Their eyes, still swollen from yesterday's binge, don't open until night is coming on. They're like those peoples Vergil describes, whom Nature has tucked away on the far side of the world, directly beneath our feet:

  When Dawn with her panting horses breathes down on our faces,
  for them the red evening is just lighting its late lamps.

Except with these men it isn't their country that's on the opposite side of the world — it's their life. There are Antipodes living right here in our own city who, as Cato put it, "have never seen the sun rise or set." Do you think people like this know how to live, when they don't even know when to live? Do they fear death — these men who have already buried themselves alive? They're as unnatural as owls and night-birds. They may pass their dark hours surrounded by wine and perfume; they may spend the whole of their backwards waking life eating dinners of course after course, each cooked separately; but that isn't a banquet. It's a funeral service they're holding for themselves. And at least the dead get their banquets in the daytime.

To a man who's actually doing something, no day is long. So let's lengthen our lives — because the duty of life, and the proof of it, is action. Trim the night short; carry some of it over into the day's work. Birds being fattened for the table are kept in the dark, so that they'll plump up without moving. It's the same with men who vegetate: no exercise, and the idle body drowns in its own flesh; in their smug retirement, the fat of laziness settles on them. That's why the bodies of these sworn servants of darkness are so repulsive to look at. Their color is more alarming than an invalid's — pale, limp, bloated, walking corpses while they're still alive. And that, believe me, is the least of their troubles. How much deeper the darkness is inside them! Such a man is dazed at the core; his inner sight has gone dim; he could envy the blind. Whoever had eyes so that he could see in the dark?

You ask how the soul acquires this perversion — this habit of turning its back on daylight and handing its whole existence over to the night. All vices rebel against Nature; every one of them deserts the appointed order. Luxury's motto is: enjoy the unusual. It isn't enough to stray from the right path — luxury wants to get as far from it as possible, and finally to plant its flag on the exact opposite side. Tell me: don't men live against Nature when they drink on an empty stomach, pouring wine into empty veins and arriving at their food already drunk? Yet that's a favorite trick of the young set — building up their capacity so they can start drinking practically in the doorway of the bathhouse, among the naked bathers, and then swig wine and immediately scrape off the sweat they've worked up with round after round of hot drinks. To them, a glass after lunch or dinner is hopelessly middle-class — that's what country squires do, men who don't understand pleasure. What delights them is the neat wine with no food to cushion it, the way it goes straight to the muscles; the whole joy of the binge is the empty stomach.

Don't men live against Nature when they trade clothes with women? Don't men live against Nature when they chase a fresh, boyish look at an age when nothing about them is boyish? What could be crueler, or more pitiful? Will he never be allowed to grow up — held forever in an artificial boyhood? Don't men live against Nature when they crave roses in winter, and force a spring lily to bloom in the cold with hot-water pipes and rigged temperatures? Don't men live against Nature when they plant orchards on top of walls? When whole forests wave on the roofs and towers of their houses, the roots starting at a height the treetops would be showing off to reach? Don't men live against Nature when they sink the foundations of their bathhouses into the sea — and can't enjoy a swim unless the heated pool is being slapped by storm waves?

Once men have decided to want everything the wrong way around, they end by abandoning Nature's way altogether. "It's daylight — time for bed! Everyone's resting — now for our workout, our drive, our lunch! Look, dawn's coming — dinner time! We can't do what ordinary people do. Living the usual, conventional way is cheap and common. Let's give up the standard-issue day. Let's have a morning that's ours alone, custom-made!" Men like this are as good as dead. Living by torches and lamps — aren't they attending their own funeral, and ahead of schedule?

I remember when this way of life was actually fashionable. Take Acilius Buta, a man of praetorian rank, who burned through an enormous fortune; when he confessed to Tiberius that he was broke, the emperor answered: "You've woken up too late!" Or take the day Julius Montanus was giving a public reading of his poetry — a middling poet, famous for being Tiberius's friend and then for losing that friendship. Montanus loved to stuff his poems with sunrises and sunsets. So when somebody complained that his reading had gone on all day and swore no one should ever attend one again, Natta Pinarius said: "I'll make him the fairest offer he'll ever get: I'm willing to listen to him from sunrise to sunset!" Montanus read on and reached these lines:

  Now the bright morning begins to spread its clear-burning flames;
  the red dawn scatters its light, and the sad-eyed swallow returns
  to her nestlings, carrying food to the chatterers, sharing it out,
  serving them with her sweet bill.

At that, Varus — a Roman knight who trailed around after Marcus Vinicius, a sponger who earned his seat at elegant dinners with his nasty wit — shouted: "Bedtime for Buta!" Later, when Montanus declaimed:

  Look, now the shepherds have folded their flocks, and the slow-moving
  darkness begins to spread silence over the drowsy, sleep-lulled land,

the same Varus called out: "What, night already? I'll go pay my morning call on Buta!" Nothing, you see, was more notorious than Buta's upside-down life. And as I said, for a while that life was the fashion.

Why do some men live this way? Not because they think night itself has any special charm, but because what's normal gives them no pleasure. Light is a bitter enemy to a bad conscience. And to a man who prices every desire and every contempt by what it costs him, illumination that comes free is beneath notice. There's more: the luxurious man wants to be talked about his whole life long. If people are silent about him, he feels he's wasting his time. It actually pains him whenever one of his stunts goes unnoticed.

Plenty of men devour their fortunes; plenty keep mistresses. To make a name in that crowd, you need a program not just of luxury but of notoriety — in a city this busy, garden-variety scandal doesn't even register. I once heard Pedo Albinovanus — the most delightful storyteller — describing life in his apartment above the townhouse of Sextus Papinius. Papinius belonged to the tribe that shuns the light. "Around nine at night I hear whips cracking. I ask what's happening: Papinius is going over his accounts. Around midnight, furious shouting. I ask what's wrong: he's doing his voice exercises. Around two in the morning, the sound of wheels — what's that? He's going out for his drive. And at dawn, total uproar — slaves being summoned, butlers running, chaos among the cooks. I ask about that too: he's back from the bath and has called for his cordial and his appetizer. 'Did his dinner run past the day?' Certainly not," said Pedo. "He lived very frugally — the night was the only thing he spent freely. So if you believe the people who call him a tightwad and a miser, go ahead and call him a slave of the lamp as well."

Don't be surprised to find so many varieties of vice. Vices are endless; they come in countless shapes; no catalogue could hold them all. Doing right is simple; doing wrong is complicated, with infinite room to swerve. The same goes for character. Follow Nature, and character is easy to manage, free, and varies only in small shades. But the sort of men I've described are warped through and through — out of tune with everything, including themselves. The main cause of this disease, I think, is a fastidious disgust with ordinary life. Just as these men set themselves apart by their dress, by the theatrical staging of their dinners, by the elegance of their carriages, so they want to be special in the way they carve up the hours of their day. They refuse to be wicked in the conventional way, because the whole payoff of their wickedness is notoriety. Notoriety is what every such man is after — every man who is, quite literally, living backwards.

So, Lucilius, let's keep to the road Nature has mapped out for us, and never leave it. Follow Nature, and everything is easy and open; fight her, and life is nothing but rowing against the current.

Farewell.

Letter 123: On the Conflict Between Pleasure and Virtue

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

Worn out — more by the discomfort of the trip than by its length — I reached my villa at Alba late at night and found nothing ready except myself. So I'm shaking off my fatigue at the writing table, and getting some good out of my cook's and my baker's slowness. In fact, I'm talking the matter over with myself, and the topic is exactly this: nothing is heavy if you pick it up lightly, and nothing needs to make you angry — provided you don't add anger to your pile of troubles. My baker has no bread; but the overseer has some, or the steward, or one of my tenants. "Bad bread!" you say. Just wait: it will turn good. Hunger will make even that loaf soft and fine as bakery's best. The trick is not to eat until hunger gives the order. So I'll wait, and I won't touch food until I either get good bread or stop being fussy about bad. You have to train yourself to a slim table. Time and place throw up obstacles everywhere — even the rich man, fully rigged for pleasure, will run into them and be pulled up short. To have whatever he wants is in no man's power. What is in his power is this: not to wish for what he doesn't have, and to make cheerful use of whatever shows up. A good-humored stomach — one willing to take rough handling — is a long stride toward freedom.

You can't imagine how much pleasure I'm taking in the way my tiredness is settling down all by itself. No masseurs, no bath, no cure of any kind — except time. What work has piled up, rest carries away. This supper, whatever it turns out to be, will give me more pleasure than a banquet celebrating some new consul. I've put my spirit to a surprise test — and that's the simpler and truer kind. When a man has made his preparations and formally ordered himself to be patient, you can't tell how much strength he really has. The surest proofs are the ones given on the spot: looking at your own troubles not just fairly but calmly, without tantrums or arguments; making up your own shortfall by simply not craving the thing you were owed; and reminding yourself that a habit of ours may go unsatisfied — we ourselves never truly lack anything. How many things are superfluous we never realize until they run out. We were using them not because we needed them but because we had them. And how much do we buy only because our neighbors bought it, or because most people own one! Half our troubles come from living by the pattern — steering not by reason but by convention.

If only a few people did a thing, we'd refuse to copy it; but let the majority start, and we fall in line — as if being common made a thing more honorable! Once a wrong idea becomes widespread, it passes for the standard of what's right. Everyone now travels with Numidian outriders in front and a squad of runners to clear the road; it's a disgrace to have no attendants elbowing crowds out of your way, no great cloud of dust announcing that Somebody Important is coming. Everyone now has mules loaded with crystal cups and myrrhine vases carved by famous artists; it's a disgrace if your baggage is the kind that can rattle down the road without danger. Everyone's page-boys ride with their faces slathered in ointment so heat and cold won't trouble their tender complexions; it's a disgrace if a single boy in your entourage shows a healthy cheek not covered in cosmetics.

Avoid conversation with all such people. These are the men who pass their bad habits from hand to hand, grafting them onto everyone they meet. We used to think the worst of the breed were the ones who paraded their fine words; but there are men who parade their vices. Their talk does real damage. Even when it doesn't convince you on the spot, it leaves seeds of trouble in the soul, and the evil follows us around after we've left them, sure to spring up later with fresh strength. People who've heard a concert carry the melodies home in their heads, and the sweetness of the tunes keeps intruding, making serious thought impossible. In just the same way, the talk of flatterers and cheerleaders for vice sticks with us long after we've heard it. A catchy tune is hard to evict from the memory — it stays, it lasts, it comes back when you least expect it. So shut your ears against evil talk, and do it at the first note. Once that talk has gotten inside — once the words are admitted and settled in the mind — they grow bolder. And then we begin to talk like this: "Virtue, Philosophy, Justice — a rattle of empty words. The only happiness is treating yourself well. Eating, drinking, spending — that's real living; that's how you remember you're mortal. The days are running out; life is racing away and can't be refunded. Why hesitate to wise up? Life won't take pleasures forever; meanwhile, while it can, while it's begging for them, what's the point of forcing frugality on it? Get ahead of death — whatever death is going to steal from you, spend on yourself now. You have no mistress, no favorite slave-boy to make a mistress jealous; you show up in public sober every day; you dine as though Father were going to audit your account-book. That isn't living — that's minding someone else's life. And what madness, to look out for your heir's interests and deny yourself everything, until the sheer size of the inheritance turns your friends into enemies! The more your heir stands to gain, the harder he'll celebrate your exit. As for those sour types who set themselves up as schoolmasters to the world, judging everyone else's life while making an enemy of their own — don't give them a second thought, and don't hesitate to choose good living over a good name."

These are the voices you must flee the way Ulysses fled the Sirens — he wouldn't sail past until he was lashed to the mast. These voices are every bit as powerful. They lure men away from country, parents, friends, and every honest habit; they wreck them, with hopes that are shameful — or at best doomed — on a shameful life. How much better to hold a straight course and steer for the harbor where "pleasant" and "honorable" finally mean the same thing! We can get there once we understand that there are two classes of things: those that attract us and those that repel us. Riches, pleasures, beauty, ambition — everything seductive and smooth — pulls us in; toil, death, pain, disgrace, a stricter table — these push us away. So we must train ourselves out of fearing the one and desiring the other. Fight the battle in reverse: retreat from what entices, and charge what attacks.

Notice how differently men walk downhill and uphill? Going down a slope, you lean back; climbing a steep grade, you lean forward. To throw your weight forward on the way down, Lucilius, or backward on the way up — that is to side with vice. The pleasures run downhill; the road to what is rough and hard runs up. Downhill, hold the body back on the rein; uphill, throw it forward.

Do you think I'm saying the only men who ruin our ears are the ones who preach pleasure and teach us to dread pain — pain, which is frightening enough on its own? I think we're also injured by men who dress up in the Stoic uniform and coach us into vice all the same. They boast that only the wise and learned man knows how to be a lover: "He alone has the science of this art; the wise man is also the best-trained at drinking and feasting. The one question worth studying is: up to what age does the bloom of love last?" Let all that be written off as a concession to Greek ways. We should be turning our attention to words like these instead: "No man is good by accident. Virtue has to be learned. Pleasure is a low, small thing, worth nothing — we share it with the dumb animals; the tiniest and meanest of them go fluttering after it. Glory is empty and fleeting, lighter than air. Poverty is an evil to no one — unless he kicks against it. Death is not an evil. What is it, you ask? The one law of mankind that plays no favorites. Superstition is a lunatic's error: it fears the very beings it should love, and insults the very beings it worships. What difference is there between denying the gods and defaming them?"

Principles like these you should learn — no, learn by heart. Philosophy has no business writing excuses for vice. When a doctor tells a sick man to live recklessly, the patient is past saving.

Farewell.

Letter 124

Letter 124: On the True Good as Attained by Reason

Greetings from Seneca to his friend Lucilius.

  Many an old-time precept I could hand you,
  if you did not flinch, ashamed to study
  lessons that look so lowly.

But you do not flinch, and no fine point of study scares you off. Your mind is too well trained to take up serious questions in a casual, easygoing way. I like your method: everything has to count toward some real measure of progress, and the only thing that annoys you is when the utmost subtlety accomplishes nothing at all. I am going to take some pains to show that this is not one of those cases. Our question is this: is the Good grasped by the senses, or by the understanding? And a corollary comes with it: the Good does not exist in animals or in small children.

Everyone who sets pleasure on the throne as the supreme ideal holds that the Good is a matter of the senses. We Stoics say it is a matter of the understanding, and we assign it to the mind. If the senses were the judges of what is good, we would never turn down a single pleasure — there is no pleasure that does not attract, none that does not please. And by the same token we would never voluntarily accept any pain, because there is no pain that does not grate against the senses. What's more, the people who are too fond of pleasure, and the people who fear pain above everything, would deserve no blame at all. Yet in fact we condemn men who are slaves to their appetites and their lusts, and we despise men who are too afraid of pain to dare anything worthy of a man. But what wrong would such men be doing, if they were simply consulting the senses — the very judges to whom you and your school have entrusted the whole test of what to seek and what to avoid?

Surely reason is the governing power in a question like this. Reason made the ruling about the happy life, about virtue, about honor; reason has made the ruling about good and evil too. With our opponents, the vilest part of us is allowed to pass sentence on the better part, so that the senses — dense, dull things, and more sluggish in human beings than in the other animals — get to pronounce judgment on the Good. Imagine someone trying to make out tiny objects by touch instead of by eyesight! To tell good from evil we would need the subtlest, sharpest faculty we possess — nothing cruder than the eye could even attempt it. So you can see in what ignorance of truth a man spends his days, and how flat he has knocked down the lofty and the divine, if he thinks the sense of touch can pass judgment on the nature of the Supreme Good and the Supreme Evil!
